Answering a ring is hit or miss, sometimes it takes so long to get the ring that the person is already gone.5. Even if you manage to answer a ring, often one party or the other has trouble hearing or communicating with the other. Not sure if this is a microphone issue, a speaker volume issue, or a connection issue, but the few times I've talked to someone when away from the house it has always been problematic.6. Video quality is terrible. Adjustment to light conditions takes way too long, and resolution is just way too low even for a $99 device (and I paid $200 for it when this thing was brand new).7. The service isn't really worth paying for. With all the other flaws associated with the device, I got tired of paying a monthly fee just to see what my doorbell was capturing, especially when so many videos were either of cars driving by or people walking away, or of such low quality that the video was useless.8. The ring tone. It's dumb. It's not customizable. You're stuck with it.9. Still no volume control of any kind whatsoever.10. The Ring app stinks, it's very limited in scope and is somewhat buggy. And it's also the only way you can operate the doorbell. It hasn't changed much, if at all, as far as I can tell, since Ring first started selling these devices.Since I first bought and reviewed this, the product has had very few minor improvements. There was at one point a problem with audio feedback that was corrected, and live viewing was a feature that wasn't originally available (much to my disappointment). The ability to connect and view a live feed is certainly welcome, but not nearly enough to overcome the problems. Some issues are inherent to the hardware, like the low resolution. I don't understand why Ring continues to sell these when they have such poor video quality.The connection delay between the Ring and the app is unacceptably long and has not improved at all since the first release. Ring seems more concerned with partnering with police (another reason why I stopped paying for their service, I have no idea what they're doing with my personal footage) than fixing the many bugs of their devices. For anyone with privacy concerns, Ring is a no-go. The neighborhood reporting that they added to the app (also advertised as the "Neighbors" app, which I believe is available as a standalone app for non-Ring users) is somewhat useful for neighbors posting videos and reporting things, but is also filled with alerts about local law enforcement seemingly designed to scare you into buying more Ring products.Honestly, I still wish after all this time that I hadn't wasted my money. I can think of only 2 instances since I got this thing years ago that it came in handy. I would rate this somewhere between 1 and 2 stars. I think I gave it 2 stars initially because the concept was relatively novel at the time but this device is very poor at what it does. It's not worth $99. By now I would have to believe that there are better products out there. Ring is clearly making too much money things like subscriptions and law enforcement partnerships to spend time fixing broken features.PREVIOUS REVIEW BELOW, EDITED FOR BREVITY:Don't plan on using the Ring for remote monitoring. For some strange reason, the people at Ring still haven't given us the feature we want the most, the ability to view live video whenever you want. They claim to be working on adding this feature, but I've already owned this thing for about 6 months, and I'm not nearly the first person to buy one of these. You can imagine how disappointed I was when I learned that one of the major reasons I paid $200 for this thing was a feature that it doesn't have. No idea why it's taking them so long, but I see that they're busy trying to sell me another product, so my guess is that it's not going to happen. Because live viewing doesn't make them money.They're more than happy to charge you for their cloud service, though. But before you spend the money on it, you might want to consider how valuable the service is. Here's the problem: because the device doesn't work quite as well as it should, the vast majority of videos you're going to be looking at are completely worthless. And honestly it's not all that often that people come to my door, let alone ring the bell. There are many more problems with it that I will explain, but bottom line is that it's definitely not worth a monthly fee for me, and it may not be worth it for you, either.The Ring doorbell masquerades as a security device, and that's why I bought it, but it doesn't handle the job. The second-best feature behind remote monitoring is the proximity sensor which incorporates heat sensing to accomplish the task. This is important because passing vehicles will generate enough heat to set the damn thing off. I live on a small street and my house is well past 30 feet from the road, but I still get at least a dozen false alarms a day due to passing cars. I've tried turning the sensitivity all the way down and it's exactly the same. It's like the app settings don't really change anything at all. I've tried to talk to Ring about this but their answer is basically to tell the thing to ignore the motion sensor. Which defeats the purpose of having a motion sensor.2-way communication is awesome, my third-favorite feature of a device like this. The problem is, I never use it, because like the motion sensor, it doesn't work properly. I get constant feedback when the microphone is enabled. No idea why or how, but I could never get it to work properly without a ton of feedback. I think the main problem is that the speaker is too loud and there somehow is no way to adjust the volume. Seriously, an electronic doorbell in 2015, now 2016, has no volume control whatsoever. WHAT THE ****?You can't change the doorbell sound. You're stuck with the garbage Ring sound. I can only imagine the reason for doing this is to kind of push a trademark sound. I can buy a cell phone for basically nothing that I can change the ring tone on, but for some reason this $200 doorbell can't do that.The app has a slick interface and almost no features. You can check your Ring's battery level on the app, which is basically the only thing that I use it for. You can't view live video, you can't change the volume or ring tone. You can toggle call alerts (when someone presses the button, the app responds allowing you to see the video), toggle motion alerts (same thing but with the motion sensor), and set the sensitivity and the zones for the motion sensor. That's about it. On top of that, the app is laid out rather poorly, the labeling of some things is rather odd, and overall the app feels like it was designed more for form over function. The help portions don't really help all that much (actual support is non-existent on their web page, by the way). You can access your videos from a PC but the website isn't all that hot, either, and there's no PC application that alerts you when someone is ringing the bell like there is for my phone. I'm used to much more flexibility and than this. I just checked my recent activity and I have 11 motion events (I'm sure they're all cars driving by). I have the proximity sensor set as low as it goes, 5 feet.It takes forever for the app to work. I understand there will be some lag, but this really sucks because what usually happens is that the person will leave by the time my phone starts to ring. Another problem I've had is that people will ring the bell and I won't get any ring on my phone at all. In fact, I haven't had the app ring me on its own for so long that I just assumed it was broken. I just tested it and it seems to be working. I'm wondering if there's an issue with getting rings when you're away from home on a different network. In other words, if you're away and only have your cellular network for data, you're probably not going to be able to answer the door before the person leaves. The pizza guy two days ago ringed my doorbell and I answered it before the app responded (because I heard the ring). The app never alerted me. The stupid speaker is so loud that I can hear it in any room of my admittedly tiny house every time it's pressed, which makes the video moot if it's not going to show up on time, which means that this is ultimately a very expensive, and somewhat fancy doorbell.I think that their hearts are in the right places and that they truly wish to deliver the device that we want, I'm just not sure that they understand what they need to do to deliver on that. After many months of owning a Ring, it's not a device that I could recommend to anyone. The app is confusing to use and is missing features anyone would want. Motion sensing just plain doesn't work as advertised, and there's very little support available. Ring seems more interested in selling you more products than improving the ones you currently own. Like I said, I don't think remote monitoring will ever become a feature because I think they'd rather sell you a monthly service than provide a device that does what you want it to.

title: Things you might not know about the Ring Doorbell and should know before buying it!!!!!!!!!!
body: [[VIDEOID:d7bd5b41c81dfcba565ec7451853ca34]] [[VIDEOID:fb3e72ddd4afedf2752d1e58ea19de9e]] the Ring doorbell is a great idea but is lacking some serious features in my opinion that aren't well advertised in the videos. So I wanted to bring the following facts to peoples attentions:#1) I bought the Ring doorbell for my new house and gave my parents the doorbell to install at my new house while I was down the road supervising the move out of the old place. When I got to the new house, I saw that my dad had followed the instructions inside the box. He said the instructions told him to mount it to the wall. It didn't tell him to SETUP the device first through the app. So now the device is mounted nice and tight to the exterior of the house, and I go to setup the device within the app, and it tells me to push the orange button on the back of the device to set it up but I can't push the button on the back of the ring device because its mounted nice and tight to the wall. So I had to remove the device from where it was mounted on the wall, push the stupid orange button on the back, setup the device through then app and then painstakingly try to mount it back on the wall. it would NOT snap into those 6 little prongs for the life of me. I wish the instructions would have told you to setup the device first before mounting. (I haven't personally read the instructions so I can't speak to this with 100% confidence). What a pain in my you-know-what! Wish the instruction booklet would have wrote something to the extent of 'please setup the device through your app BEFORE MOUNTING IT TO THE WALL!'#2) for months they did not offer a live video feed option.#3) It seems to work sometimes. But not always. I had people coming and going to my house throughout the night (on Christmas night) and I would get notifications sent to my phone saying "Motion Detected". I'm not sure if this is important to the story but the weather was about 50 to 60 degrees out. Then 2 days later, around 11am on a cloudy day, I walk outside, stand in front of my ring device which is mounted on my front porch, and I got zero notifications sent to my phone saying it detected motion. Then I pressed the doorbell and it didnt light up blue and send the signal to my phone for me to answer to the doorbell call. Then maybe a few hours later I walked past it and it sent a signal to my phone saying motion detected. Ok so it didnt even sense my motion around 11am but it did a few hours later? (Yes I have my motion zones setup).#4) This one is minor but thought you should know that from the time the camera senses motion to the time it hits your phone... There's like a 5-10 second delay. For instance, when I had friends over during Christmas, I was down in the basement and I could hear the storm door upstairs opening, and then a few seconds later, I heard the 'chime' sound effect on my phone telling me that the Ring doorbell sensed motion.#5) This one kind of goes back to point #1.... Ok so the device has been mounted twice now. Well I decided today to change my wifi password. Now the device won't recognize the wifi signal any more, so in order to make it recognize the wifi signal, I have to choose the "add device" button within the app, and yup you guessed it, that "add device" button takes you through setting up the device again, which requires PUSHING THE ORANGE BUTTON on the back of the device. The website even says so itself: "In order to connect your Ring Doorbell to a new/different Wi-Fi network, all you need to do is complete the Wi-Fi setup through the Ring app. First, be sure that your phone/tablet is connected to the new (Wi-Fi) network that you'd like pair the Ring to, and then follow these steps: 2. Select Device Settings > Remove this device. 3. Choose to Add Device and follow the provided instructions" (the provided instructions in the app tell me to push the orange button, so that means I have to UNMOUNT THE DEVICE FROM THE WALL AGAIN JUST TO UPDATE THE WIFI PASSWORD FOR THE DEVICE?)6) The other night at work, my ring app said there was movement at mt front door. So I open the live video feed and saw it was just a truck. A few minutes later it said there was more movement at the front door. I tried pulling up the live video feed again. And it refused to show me live video feed. It showed me a black screen that said initializing, then it said securing connection. And the buffering arrow kept spinning and spinning. And It wouldn't show me the live video feed any more. It didn't even record that second motion to the activities log, almost as if it never happened. Got home and the live video feed still refusedto work. I emailed Support about it and they told me to push the orange button on the back of the doorbell . removing the doorbell from the mounting on the door (to reach the orange button is one thing), but trying to get the doorbell mounted back on the wall again is ridiculously tough. Ive sent quite a few queries to this company over the past year, about things not working with my doorbell. And Ring always tell me to push the orange button. it's so inconvenient and difficult to remove the doorbell from the wall. So I want to say this very clearly: POOR DESIGN!!!!! A product should never be this frustrating to remove and put back in its mounting. This is a serious flaw with the product And needs to be remedied to become more user-friendly and easy to do. Magically my doorbell's live video feed was working again the next morning without me pushing the orange button. But the outages are just as frustrating as the task of pushing the orange button. You have no idea how frustrating and infuriating it is every time Ring tells me to push the orange button. This product should not be making customers angry.! They need to take that kind of feedback into serious consideration. This orange button needs to be relocated in the next time revision of this product. Which I hope will be within the next 12 months. -10/31/16

title: Don't buy if you have a covered porch.
body: I installed this last night and there are major issues if you have an Android device. First, I can't answer my doorbell unless the app is running AND open on my screen. When I am simply at my home screen or in another app like email, I get the notification pop-up when the doorbell is pressed. I then tap on the notification to launch the app (or resume the app, it doesn't matter). The app will take me to the page that displays my device information but won't answer the doorbell. There's no way to answer the doorbell at all!Second, the motion activity video demonstrates that when you get a motion alert, you should be able to immediately see a live feed of what's happening outside. This does not work. I can only watch videos about two minutes after the activity has occurred.Lastly, I cannot initiate Live View. There simply isn't a Live View tile on my Ring main screen in the app. However, product videos for the Ring show the Live View icon.I contacted Customer Support:(09:47:05) Slip7: Asked via AnswerDash: "I don't have live view as demonstrated in the motion activity video."(09:47:46) Slip7: Plus I can't answer my doorbell unless the app is open and running on my phone screen already.(09:50:50) Slip7: Whenever I get a doorbell alert on my Android phone, I tap the notification on my screen and it takes me to the app but doesn't actually answer the call.(09:53:16) *** John joined the chat ***(09:54:18) John: I do apologize for the issue that you are having. It looks like there is something going on with the Android app, what it looks like I will have to do is get this up to my engineering team and they will be able to look further into this for you, they will email you if they have further questions or a resolution for you.(09:55:01) Slip7: So, are you saying that these features work correctly on an Apple device but not Android?(09:55:22) John: This does seem to be the case, I do apologize(09:55:58) Slip7: Do I require a certain firmware version on my Ring device? I just installed it yesterday.(09:56:37) Slip7: I have v 1.6.76(09:57:39) John: That is the most up to date Firmware for the Ring Video Doorbell(09:58:38) Slip7: Okay, so I basically have to wait for the developers to fix the android app and I should be good to go. There's nothing on the server end that needs to be enabled or anything.(09:59:03) John: That's it, I do apologize for the wait and for the inconvenience(10:00:28) Slip7: Is the Android issue a frequently reported complaint from Ring users? How long has this been noted?(10:01:48) John: This is not a frequent issue, this only happened recently and we are working to get this resolved as quickly as possibleI then checked Google Play to investigate reviews of the app to see if anyone had similar issues. Yes...most certainly, yes!I will update this review if/when they fix it but from what I have seen on various blogs, unless you have an Apple device, Ring will be a buggy product. Apple seems to get the development love first. This app was last updated March 23, 2016 for version 1.7.25. It is now 14 April. No fix.*************************JUNE 20 2016 UPDATE*****************************So, I'd like to follow up with what's happened since and current performance.First, let me get out front and say that Ring customer service is very responsive. Long story short, the issue I had above was software based and the Ring developers eventually pushed out a fix that restored the advertised functionality to the phone.Now...about the current performance.I've been living with the Ring device for two months after the fix and all I have to say is that it's only a so-so product. It has four MAJOR flaws.1. The light sensor can't cope with shaded porches. I have shaded entryway to my front porch that extends about 15ft. The Ring is only capable of seeing to the end of the porch. Anything beyond the porch steps get's washed out to completely white. This makes the Ring useless for viewing people as they walk up my driveway. It also brings up the next problem.2. The unit does not react quickly enough to capture someone at the door. I had a few solicitors place flyers on my door. The Ring only managed to capture the BACK of one of them as they walked down my porch steps. I never got to see them touch the door. Another example is when UPS delivers a package. The UPS driver will drop the package, hit the doorbell and then leave the porch quickly. The Ring will only catch his backside as he's leaving the porch.3. TONS AND TONS of false positives. So what do I do? I've tried "tuning" the Ring to varying distance. The only way to get the Ring to respond and capture people at all is to crank the sensor to the max but then it's capturing every single vehicle that drives by my street (which I can't even see because the light sensor washes out any viewing of the street itself). I can't strike a distance balance that doesn't give me false positives.4. Not always hi def video. I have a 200MB internet connection and my wi-fi sits about 15 ft from the front door in my media room. Despite this, the video captured on the Ring isn't always clear. At times it is grainy and clears where others it never sharpens. Other times, it's very clear. I'm not getting 720p quality each capture.Here's the latest false positive today that shows the light sensor issue:[...]Here's an example of the poor video quality along with the light washout[...]

title: It's a Doorbell, but Barely
body: Because of home renovations, I was looking for a battery powered doorbell to use temporarily. So when I saw this one, that was WiFi enabled AND with a built-in video camera, I immediately bought it. Now, after four months I'm still using it, but only because it's still working on batteries (sort of). This is also the reason I VERY generously give it two stars. Here are my reasons for not recommending this product.1. With the introduction of the pro model video doorbell, it's a safe bet that there will be no further updates to this product. So the deficiencies noted below will likely never be resolved. The Ring website also states there is no upgrade discount for existing users to purchase the pro model. Even a minor discount would show customer loyalty, especially given this model's well documented problems.2. I think the cost for the delivered functionality is way too high. This is true for both this unit and the pro model. It turns out that a regular doorbell paired with an ordinary two-way audio capable WiFi security camera will out perform either Ring product for less money. Read on for details.3. Maybe a minor complaint, but the ring sound is silly in my opinion (and 100% of my friends and family agree). But worse is the fact the sound can't be changed. I'm also fairly certain (I'm an engineer) that this feature could be added by a simple software/firmware update. For a couple of decades now, smart devices have allowed user customization of audio alerts. Why go backwards now?4. The slow video response time is a major deficiency. As others have noted, there is a lag between the time a person is within view of the camera and the time it begins recording. My unit is mounted inches from the front door, which is 32 feet from the street curb and 27 feet sideways to the driver's side of my truck. With the doorbell set to motion zone 6 and standard sensitivity, I can walk (quickly, not running) out my front door, lock the front door, open and then close a low 3 foot high) gate, and make it to the driver's side of my truck (that then hides me from the camera), WITHOUT the camera ever even seeing me. No alarm, no video, nothing. I can also walk briskly up to the door, unlock and open it, and still not be detected by the video camera. Once I snuck up with the orange screwdriver Ring provides, and removed the unit from the wall, without the camera recording any part of my body. The final test was when a delivery driver parked his truck at my curb (32 feet from the door remember), walked up, opened the gate, put the package at my front door, closed the gate, walked back to his truck, and drove off. And the motion detect video BEGINS with the truck driving away, already half way gone from view.5. No combination of zone coverage and/or sensitivity effectively reduces false positive motion detection of pedestrians on either sidewalk or vehicles passing on the street. Also, no video showed the faces of any drivers or pedestrians - all were moving out of the camera's field of view. This is ironic, given how easy it is to avoid detection at all in other situations.6. Battery life in my installation, even with motion detection turned off, is about two months. During the first two months, I was using motion detection. After the first recharge, I disabled motion detection to get a longer run time, but the change made no noticeable improvement.7. Again, this might seem like a minor thing, but the unit has no markings on its front, other than the "ring" logo. Until I put a label under the button that said "Push to ring doorbell", many people didn't push the button at all but knocked on the door instead. An embossed legend on the faceplate would help people to recognize that this device actually is a doorbell.8. Lastly, for those who do push the button, it takes a pretty firm press to make it work reliably. Some have told me that it didn't do anything when they pushed the button and so knocked on the door anyway.Do I have anything positive to say? Well, it's working as a doorbell, even if I have to charge it more often than advertised. And since it is, I can procrastinate replacing it, while I fight more urgent fires. Also, there was one time where I was actually able to talk to someone at my front door when I wasn't home. In the live video, I could see the person turned away from the camera, and flinch when he heard my voice asking him to wait a couple of minutes till I returned home. In the end though, it's not enough for me to be satisfied with this product.

title: Way too sensitive on what goes on 30' away, button not sensitive enough
body: Many people seem to have an issue with the doorbell button. If you push it dead center, it works, Off the side a bit, it does not seem to register. Maybe the button should be smaller, or the embossing around it removed.Maybe this is not so much a ring issue as I had this issue on my last two normal hard wired doorbell buttons too.I don't recall hearing of this problem in older generations, so maybe as a nation we've just become too weak to push the button hard enough? Or, is it just not possible to make a button sensitive enough to work when pushed softly or off center anymore?My door faces the street which is 30' away and slightly down hill, about a 5' elevation. I have set the sensitivity to the bare minimum, which shows in the app as the end of my 5' porch. Still cars driving by set it off. I also purchased an optional wedge kit and pointed the camera both comically down and up, which helped some, but not enough. Trucks trigger it, and on bright days the light reflecting off cars also sets it off. I need to keep the proximity alert of someone approaching my door disabled, or it just goes off all day long. I thought the People Only mode feature they released might be the answer, but no, cars, even the suns reflection from cars 30’ away well outside the minimum range I set still set it off. I have detection as restrictive as it can be. There is no lower setting left to try.Neither FedEx or UPS can seem to push the button hard enough to make it ring when a package needs a signature and leave. That proximity alert would be a nice backup to get them coming to the door if only I could leave it on...Food delivery during the pandemic has become a problem too. Too many cold pizza and Chinese food found on the porch long after delivery. Once so long a squirrel got into one meal and made a real mess. I can see in the video the delivery people make an effort to push the doorbell, but obviously off center or not pushing hard enough. I've resorted to using cash, but Dash, GrubHub and some places won't take that, so I had to change restaurants that are not as good. Still, they often wait, sometimes they get the bell to work on the second or third try, but most usually resort to knocking. The frequent delivery people now just ignore the Ring bell and knock.I like the camera to verify an expected or overdue package wasn't pirated, or to see how long my food was sitting out. But there's lots of video snips from cars driving by I need to get skip through, so it's not pleasant.I thought I'd leave the camera video up on an Echo to hopefully catch when people come up. But the power from the doorbell transformer is not enough to leave the camera on for extended periods. It acts more like a trickle charger, even though I have a large transformer that will support 3 bells/speakers. After 10-20 minutes the camera shuts off with a warning about the battery is going dead. Then I can't see the camera for a day while the battery charges. During the winter it takes a few days for the battery to charge back to full strength.When the video is not streamed for long periods, the battery still holds up. Even after nearly 3 years of summer heat waves and freezing winters here in eastern PA. I see the charge level dips a bit now and then where it used to be steadily 'full', so it may need a replacement in the next year. 3-4 years is expected, so no all good there.The app is good, I like it. The backyard camera works as expected, no issues there. The Ring alarm system works well, no false alarms from equipment, the monitoring station calls a minute or so when I set it off by accident. I like every other Ring product, if they could just fix the motion detection on the doorbell I’d be super happy. Reading reviews on the newer models does not make me optimistic that the issues have been addressed. After seeing reviews about this for three years, it seems like it may never be fixed.I'd try another brand, but I'm already heavily invested into a Ring home alarm and other cameras in the yard. I don't want yet another paid subscription to deal with. I guess when I move, I'll hope to find a house with a front door that does not face the street if they can't figure this out before then or try other brands within the 30 day return window Amazon has.

title: Many Caveats
body: First off, I must say that this review in no way reflects the team at their tech support. They are very friendly, professional and helpful. That being said, on with the review.The first problem I had is it would not reach my router on the second story. Even if it did, I found that the device really requires a strong signal to get smooth video and audio and good response time. If your signal is not strong, it will be choppy.I opted for an access point near the Ring Video Doorbell but Ring would not stay connected to my wi-fi. This brings up the next issue, there is no option to hard wire it to Ethernet. I then tried using a Repeater, a Linksys WRT54G, DD WRT firmware. Same problem. The Ring Video Doorbell would drop the wi-fi connection and not re-connect. No other devices, such as my wireless cameras have such problems on the same connection, only the Ring Video Doorbell. Tech support said to opt for an extender. I don't particularly like extenders as you will halve your bandwidth. It seems the problem is that the Ring Video Doorbell disconnects to save battery power and then re-connects on demand. The Ring Video Doorbell Engineers should recognize that if you have this thing hard wired you don't need to be concerned about conserving battery. But there is no option to keep the connection alive. According to tech support it does not go to "sleep" but just disconnects. This is a problem on two fronts. First off, since it really does not go into sleep mode, it may try to re-connect with the same assigned IP. While it was disconnected your DHCP server may have re-issued that IP to something else. The second problem is that it simply does not always re-connect anyway even when in the clear. This brings up the next problem. There is no status screen, so you don't know it's not working. I found myself testing it every time I got home and every time I left the house. But still never knew if it lost it's connection. If someone was out there ringing the doorbell I would never know it. Which brings up the next problem. You can't call up the video camera on demand. Someone has to push the doorbell or it has to detect motion for it to re-connect, (or try to re-connect). Which brings up the next problem. You only have a few seconds to answer the call on your smartphone, if you miss the window of opportunity, your out of luck, and have to just go open the door. The reason is because you can't call up the connection with the Ring Video Doorbell on demand. Which brings up the next problem. Because it disconnects and then reconnects only when it needs to, there is a delay in your notification. Most people will only wait 15 to 20 seconds at your door for an answer. It also affects the motion sensing videos. By the time it makes a record of the motion you will see absolutely nothing out there, or at best the back of someone walking away. Which brings up the next problem. If you do want to use what I think is a worthless motion detection video recording feature, it will cost you $3.00 a month.While this is not a review of the competitors, it is worth mentioning the Doorbird, also sold on Amazon. I have not gotten mine yet, but here is what the specs say. There is a status screen so you know it's still connected. There is a fast, dependable Ethernet connection option, with optional POE, no lag time. There is no monthly fee. You can call the video camera up on demand. There are also more but I think this will suffice.The Ring Video Doorbell is not quite ready for prime time. Ring does not subscribe to the fact that 90% or less functionality is not acceptable on the consumer market. Consumers demand 100%. It's just the nature of things. If you bought a TV that worked most of the time, or a coffee maker that worked sometimes but not always, you would return it. And that's just what I did.One note though, the Doorbird is expensive. Consider how much it is worth to you to have this concept work correctly in what will probably be a one-time investment for your house. On the other hand, if you want a cheap doorbell, opt for the Ring Video Doorbell, and that is what you will get...a cheap doorbell.And just one more thing that bothers me.Ring will continue to bombard you with spam once they get your email address.

title: I want to like this but I am disappointed :(
body: I have mixed feelings about the Ring doorbell.I originally purchased it on a friends recommendation and received it on Jan 20 2016I gave it a full charge and hardwired it into my existing doorbell power supply and at first it worked well.A lot of motion notifications, but I played with the settings and got them down quite a bit.Notifications for both motion and door rings came in a timely fashion and I was totally digging it!I also bought the one year subscription for cloud recording, felt it was worth it.Then I started having issues#1 I found that even though I had power to the Ring, it did not like the power because apparently my transformer for my hardwired doorbell was to old and after contacting customer support and going through a # of tests decided it didn't like the juice. - old doorbell never worked on the Ring from the get go, but I had purchased the wireless chime so I didn't worry about it and figured I could just run off the battery.#2 the battery started running down a bit fast, seems like I had to charge it every 2 weeks - was told because the weather in Indianapolis is too cold and I should hardwire it & I had excessive motion - no matter how I set it the Ring picks up cars that are way outside the 5' min motion sensitivity area. - I was willing to live with the excessive alerts, but the doorbell wasn't and my choices were no alerts or excessive#3 now I decide that I can live with charging the battery all the time until I can find the time to swap out my existing transformer - unit decides it will no longer take a full charge#4 we determined that the unit was bad with tech support - Customer service is very friendly but this is starting to eat a lot of my time that I don't have to spare - but they sent me a new one - Great!! Problems solved..... no :(#5 new unit has had numerous issues that I have been on the phone with customer service a lot more than I would prefer - again customer service people are nice - but my time is valuable and I don't have an hour a month to mess with my door bell not to mention the no doorbell time - new one lost wifi connection for what ever reason and I ended up having to re boot my network that was working for everything else but the Ring to get it to work again.#6 when we got the new unit, Ring cloud subscription did not remember that I had already subscribed which led to another phone call - this time with a somewhat surly rep who couldn't even figure out my subscription start date and acted like he was doing me a favor to get it started again??? seriously - waste my time to turn something back on that should have been automatic and then give me a tude??#7 soldiering on but again with the problems - Ring quits ringing anything including the door bell chime that I bought from Ring - had to do a hard reset - more phone time (no idea what my total time on the phone is, but could likely document that - this is starting to get annoying.#8 after the reset, had to repair with 2 phones - again subscription service is lost - has totally kicked my phone off the Ring notification and I have to make yet another phone call, as of this minute problems not resolved (I don't have time to keep servicing their device that is not performing properly and am pretty sure that I have a 2nd bad device)#9 forgot to mention that new unit does not notify in a timely fashion like the old one did and I am sure that they are going to tell me it is a problem with my wifi, which is working fine for everything from streaming movies on Prime & Netflix to you name it.FTR - customer service has been very courteous to me (WITH THE EXCEPTION OF THE CLOUD SUBSCRIPTION GUY) - but I have a very busy life and was hoping the Ring would help with that and not take away from my time like it has - almost 6 months down the roadI will update this after my next phone call which may be a few days, but at this point I am very frustrated with the product.

title: I'll keep looking for an acceptable exterior SIP Intercom solution.
body: Abysmally bad, bordering on useless.[begin edit, 27-Feb-2019] After Ring reached out to me, years later, I decided to not only tear mine down again, but tear down another belonging to a coworker, this unit dead from weather exposure, in order to prepare for the call and defend my statements.In the interest of intellectual honesty and completeness, I must note that, while what I say below is factual _for my unit_, the second unit I tore down does, in fact, contain 3x PIR on Kapton tape above the camera. Those are entirely non-existent in my original sample. The plastic casing is there for them, they just simply don't exist. QA defect? Perhaps so. But in 50% [n=2 ;-) ] of samples there are three PIR... I stand behind the observation of my unit lacking motion detection circuitry, and the remainder of the experience as well; but that portion of the review (which I discounted anyway) needs to be viewed as a possible assembly/QA defect rather than indicative of the generation itself). /end edit]I would go beyond questioning the claims they make regarding their motion detection technology, being someone that has implemented those technologies many times in the CCTV world. But leaving off the claims I think are hogwash - even then... it's bad. Worse. Awful.False alarms abound. A courier can make it to my doorstep, scan the package, place it, ring the doorbell, and make it to the end of my 40' driveway before recording even starts. Relying upon motion detection is futile as the logs start well after the person has walked away. But grass waving in the breeze at 20'? Gets that every time.30-45+ seconds is typical for a doorbell press to register on the remote device when it's on the same network segment. Remote? A minute-plus is not uncommon. Few hang-out at the door for a minute plus.My plethora of actual CCTV cameras on a real DVR catch the events unfolding, so I can precisely identify the time.So now I rely on the CCTV cameras to tell me if someone's there. Motion _at_ the door? I'm pushing alerts for that with a Maxbotix Ultrasonic sensor on a Particle Photon microcontroller (about six seconds average to get my push notification through, with a 20 second worst-case)"Oh it's your WiFi"... [Multiple] Enterprise-grade access points with zero handoff latency mesh networking. 50mbit/sec synchronous redundant connection to the backbone. Low two-digit packet RTTs. -42 to -54dBm signal strength to the device. I do this stuff for a living.My Photon with sensors is 25' further away, still outside, going over the same path with the same obstructions.If I've had to supplement the video and the motion - what's left? The SIP VOIP communications. That's why it gets two stars - if someone REALLY wants to talk to you and is willing to tolerate the initial latency and call setup times - it does work. It's an almost usable Intercom.Am I returning it? Indecisive. Go to the trouble to set up Asterisk and find or build a handsfree weather-tight intercom and return the Ring? Keep it for just that purpose and continue supplementing all the things it's supposed to be but isn't? Meh. I've got another week to decide...Would I recommend it? Authoritatively 'No'... Probably not even to someone I didn't like unless I were willing to change my name and phone number...Is there a better turnkey product today? I'm unsure. I would probably have given it four stars if they'd lose the camera, lose the product literature making wild claims, and lose the pseudo-motion-detection (which would be lost with the camera regardless).Maybe there's something better already. If you find it - let me know - otherwise I'll end-up building it ultimately...

title: Does not record in HD to the cloud. High compression kills the quality. Like filming with a potato.
body: The CMOS camera sensor in the Doorbell 1 is 720p, however the compression level is very lossy, resulting in pixilated footage especially in color, but also somewhat in night vision (grayscale footage). My WIFI is strong with a high end router close to the doorbell with a 55Mb/s/5Mb/s down/up bandwidth with nothing else using much bandwidth during my testing. Testing just a few feet away from the router with no walls between.The 0.5 to 1Mb/s upload bandwidth requirement of this device as shown in the web page on their site for this product, is not enough to stream HD content. You need 6Mb/s to stream HD content without visible compression losses. The 1-2Mb/s of the Doorbell 2 streaming quality is significantly better, but still not HD quality. Too much compression. The Doorbell Pro is supposed to be the same bandwidth as the 2.The camera will decide before turning on, whether to be in night vision mode, or color mode. Once it decides, it turns on whether from an alert or Live View. If i then put the Doorbell in a dark or light place (opposite of what environment it was in when it began recording, it will not adjust. As in, if I turn it on outside and starts recording in color, then I bring the doorbell in while still recording in Live View mode and go in a dark room, night vision mode will not engage. The camera has to shut off first and turn back on and it will not do this by itself. Same thing going from dark to light environment. Will be recording in grayscale a sunny day. But, this isn't so important because the Doorbell would be fixed. Only thought is an intruder trying to trick the Doorbell at night by shining a flashlight at it before it starts recording to it'll record nothing but black.Night vision, when the person's face is directly facing the camera, their face tends to be completely washed out, white. Not good for police to identify who it was. Night vision LEDS should be variable strength controlled by algorithms and video post processing to help maintain quality as much as possible.The fishbowl footage is the result of the camera lens being a dome shape. The Doorbell 2 and Doorbell Pro does not, it is full widescreen. However the viewing angle of the Doorbell 1 is wide as well, not sure what advantage the fishbowl has besides effectively cropping the corners of the camera sensor viewing angles.Its ability to stay connected to WIFI from outside the house doesn't seem to be as good as the Doorbell 2 but I haven't confirmed. Regardless in my situation, I would buy the Ring extender to plug in right by the front door.This device does have Live View, despite the product page on Ring's website not mentioning it. Live view connects in about 5 seconds after requesting it, pretty fast. However if I then travel away from the house with it still on, the video feed will stop, but the app will never say that communication has been interrupted. and if I get back in the house and wait a few minutes, Live View will not resume. I have to turn off Live View and turn on again. There should be some automatic retry/abort algorithms.The 2-way audio communication feature works well.I bought this Doorbell for a friend for the $99 deal and I love the idea and how it is disrupting the big boy home security companies. I will not be getting one until they have a product capable of streaming at 6Mb/s. Pocket camcorders have much better video quality than the best Ring Doorbell AFAIK.

title: OK With an Issue or two
body: UPDATE: 10 October -- I've downgraded this device to two stars despite my initial positive review and it working for about two weeks. Lately the Ring much of the time does not operate fully. The website fails to delete saved video. The Ring Doorbell connects and disconnects during the day. No it's not my network. At the doorbell I have 15 upload and 40 download speeds along with three wifi bars on my iPhone. When someone rings the doorbell I get an immediate notification on my iPhone but the app fails to connect, gives me an error or indicates connection is lost. I'm taking this thing down and replacing it with something that actually works before my 30 trial is up and I have to pay fees.Overall does what it advertises but not without issues. Video is good/audio breaks up occasionally. Using it with a dual band Asus high end router on a PC with Windows 10 and Apple iPhone 5S running latest IOS. Router is about 20 feet from the Ring and upload/download speeds at the Ring are several times the minimum required in the owner's manual.I live in a townhouse neighborhood with immediate neighbors and sidewalk/road about 20 feet from front door with a fair amount of foot traffic. This would be a true test of the motion sensitivity settings to avoid constant detection and notification. Installation through a wired doorbell was generally quick and easy. I have an uneven ribbed metal mounting surface so I had to modify installation using metal screws and caulking around the bottom plate for a good tight seal. This is important to keep out water and moisture.Software installation, app setup, and Ring doorbell to PC and IOS sync was straightforward following the instructions. No problems. Because of our townhouse environment motion detection settings were set at lowest setting to avoid spurious inputs. We get occasional hits from large vehicles but we've managed to tame this somewhat using the settings. Unfortunately this also means the system delays telling us someone is at the door. This delay is about 30 seconds from first detection until we can bring up a video/audio feed. We can live with this. Considering our complex detection environment we rate detection capability as average to good. We've never had a situation where the system noticed something that wasn't there. The Ring is hardwired so it activates our normal in-house doorbell chime when someone pushes the Ring button...a plus.Video and audio performance on a high speed network is average. Video is stable in both day and night mode. Audio tends to break up when conversing with someone at the door. This is not due to wireless network speed and it happens on average every two or three times the system is employed. Not a deal breaker but annoying. A recently added feature is Live video feed and we like this a lot. We can check the front of our townhouse from anywhere/anytime. The ability to ratchet down the detection range and limit the detection angle of the motion sensor are extremely important in our environment. This is essential for us and we rate this as excellent.It's clear Ring continues to evolve and improve their system and we are impressed. While we'd like to rate it higher the fact that audio dropouts and breakups occur on our high speed wireless network on a regular basis prevents a higher rating. If Ring can solve this, we'd give it four stars.

title: The hardwaere is nice, too bad their cloud service is unstable
body: So I'm a technophile, and in particular I'm an "Internet of things" embedded systems engineer... So I build things like Ring.The hardware itself is fairly solid. But since you are not allowed to use anything other than the Ring cloud for connectivity, you are 100% dependent on Ring having a strong Internet presence with reliable servers, and solid connectivity.From my house to the Ring cloud, everything works as it should. However when using a smartphone app to use the system, it tends to not work more than it works. So when your phone shows motion, or someone rings the bell, the app's connection to the ring cloud servers is CRITICAL.Problems I have seen:1. 50% of the time, connection to the cloud fails for a real-time event, such as answering a doorbell ring.2. 25% of the time, when a real-time event occurs such as motion, you will get a notice, but when you connect to the service the event does not show up for a half hour. That's right, it might take a half hour before the app shows the event in the event list. And during that time you cannot connect to the live view. So if someone rings your doorbell, 25% of the time you get the ring, but you can't get the video. The event notification seems to never be delayed more than a couple seconds, but the event itself is not available to view 25% of the time.3. 25% of the times during the day, when you try to access the live view, it can be unreachable for hours at a time. Event notifications come through, and events can be recorded, but the app tells you that your Ring is offline, even when you can prove it isn't.4. Different Internet connections appear to connect to different server clusters, so an event that is not showing on one phone might show up on another if it has a different path back to Ring.5. And since these problems are all in the cloud, being in the home on the same network as your Ring does not give you any better access, there is zero local connectivity, other than while performing the initial configuration.I have a variety of Internet connections I test with. My day job is running a lab where we manufacture Internet gateways for the largest ISP's, so we have 25 /different/ Internet connections, so when I have a problem I can test it from several different points of view. Since the problems are so frequent, it is easy to find anomalies and test them while they are happening.As someone with 25+ years experience with Internet applications and servers, it "feels" like Ring has internal data convergence issues, as if they are using Microsoft servers that just stop responding at inappropriate times, or they use an active directory forest with some problematic systems causing parts of their network to fail to connect to other parts. This isn't a latency issue, where we are off by seconds, this is a DOWNTIME issue where something is offline for dozens of minutes at a time. This tells me that there is some sort of architectural problem in their cloud service.It could be growing pains, but it is also clearly exposing a design flaw. I'm a "newish" user, so it is possible that Ring has simply had a bad couple of weeks, and that it is normally better. I will update this review as I spend more time with this system.

title: WARNING: They are programmed to CUT OFF recording just when you need it
body: This is an update to a previous review and I have raised the rating by 1 star only because, after talking to at least 4 or 5 tech support people (all but the last one of whom gave me erroneous information) someone at least told me the truth about the serious limitations of this device and refunded the purchase even though the return period was expired. It is really a well made device with very good components but has SERIOUS design limitations. Here is that old review:As an engineer I have to admit that this camera doorbell has some good engineering behind it. The image is good (though grainy when in color mode under dim light so I actually shielded the camera from peripheral ambient and overhead light to force it to monochrome which is very sharp). The battery is great in terms of mAh. It's a real shame they don't let you replace them (it looks like they're a pair of 18650s which are excellent). The size and outside design elements are better than good. Unfortunately, when the engineers finish their job the marketing people usually take over for commercialization. This is when technical sacrifices are made to meet price points (like $99.00) such as removing buffer memory to save a few bucks and including inadequate battery voltage regulation to allow the battery to fully charge from the doorbell current when available.The results are, from a usability perspective, pretty catastrophic. Buy something else. For example:1. With inadequate buffer memory to store new camera-captured data while already-captured data is being uploaded to the cloud they stop recording after 30 seconds or so and start uploading to the cloud. Yes, they stop recording right when you want to see what is going on because they don't have enough memory to buffer the stream. They have very good technical support people but they all finally say the same thing..."The reason they are no software selections allowing you to fully see what follows a motion alert is that they have to shut the camera down while they upload".2. Forget using this as a continuous view (or ANY VIEW OVER 10 MINUTES LONG). In addition to the above early cutoff, they cut you off after 10 minutes anyway (tech support will tell you they're protecting the camera but the CCD doesn't need protection; they're protecting the battery because they have no charging electronics on board). There is no software option to do a better job because a) they were too cheap to include electronics to charge the battery from the doorbell wire and b) they didn't even provide you access to external power (which would have required virtually no additional front end expense for them and would have provided a nice "wall wart" power supply cross-sale for them).3. As a further result you also have to keep going out to take the battery out and recharge it (which can take most of the day while your camera is useless) on a regular basis.There are many other frustrating elements that make this a terrible waste of time in addition to being a poor product but do NOT blame the engineers. I can assure you they wanted to provide a useful product. This one is not.Great design, quality components and terrible final product. Buy yourself something better. There is already enough grief in the world. Peace.

title: Major let down in motion detection features
body: If all you want is a video doorbell with a good camera that can be used to answer the door from anywhere, this is a great device, buy it and read nor further. If you have expectations that certain safety features, like motion detection, or the person at the door not being able to defeat the recording by covering the camera before it starts, stay away from this rather pricey doorbell.I’ve had the Ring for a couple of months now. If I could, I’d probably return it, but it’s too late. It has so much potential that is suggested in the product description, but that it doesn’t quite live up to.The motion detection feature is just too sensitive and trips too easily. I’ve got it set down to 3’ in the settings, but it still goes off every time a car goes by down the street, which is at least 40’ away from the Ring.Despite being set off too easily, the motion detection actually starts recording too slowly., it seems like maybe as long as 30 seconds or more before it starts recording sometimes. I’ve tested this myself by walking up to the doorbell from down the driveway and placing my hand over the camera. The Ring didn’t catch me coming up the driveway, it only caught my palm over the camera. This flaw 100% defeats any safety expectations of the Ring.If you’re thinking of using this as some sort of poor man’s security camera, forget it. True story here of how the device failed me. I had a UPS package stolen from my front porch one day. The Ring had detected motion on the front porch at least a dozen times that day, including at the exact time the UPS driver scanned my package as delivered. It then recorded 6 more motion alerts before I got home and discovered the package was missing. No problem, I thought, I can see who stole it and give a picture to the police. NOPE! The Ring camera only recorded an empty driveway every time the motion alert recorded something. It didn’t even catch the actual package delivery; the UPS guy was able to pull up to the house, run up the driveway, scan the package, drop it on the porch, run back down the driveway, and drive away all before the camera started its recording. I’ve watched many a UPS guy come up the driveway, and they can do all that in 30 seconds or so when they’re hustling. Apparently a package thief (known as “porch pirates” here) can get up the driveway, steal the package and leave before the Ring starts recording.All the disappointments aside, there are some good things about the Ring. The intercom and video works great, even over cellular connections. It was amazing to have a discussion with a door to door salesman while I was sitting in my parked car 15 miles away. I could see him, his reactions to my comments, and hear him very clearly. I’m sure the guy had no idea I wasn’t in the house.Other good things about the device: The product comes with all the tools and materials needed to install it, it’s a good looking doorbell on the wall, the video resolution is great, albeit it very fish-eye and distorted if someone gets too close to the camera, and the very reasonable price for online storage of everything the camera records.

title: Just the start of a lengthy, expensive and largely fruitless journey
body: First and foremost. I am an engineer with over 40 years of experience in designing this kind of crap. I'm not a noob at setting this kind of stuff up. Before you fall for the hype, find a friend that has one and find out the real truth about how much effort and expense went into getting it working and ask them for a demo to see what kind of quality and lag they get.We fell for the hype and ordered one of these for my 90+ year old father in law to keep him from having to get up and hobble across the house every time the doorbell rang. He had an Android smartphone and knew pretty well how to make use of it after a couple of years of working with it. The doorbell arrived and I went over to install it.Surprise 1. After trying to download the app from the play store I was informed that "my version of android was just too old and decrepit to run this app" Buried in the fine print it says you better have the latest and greatest Android OS or you're screwed. His phone of course could not be upgraded. After another $300 for a refurb Galaxy phone with "acceptable" OS and another week gone by I install the app and finally get the doorbell talking to the router and the app. This of course doesn't include the time to change phones, port the existing number, transfer contacts, photos, etc and show him how to use the new phone that is just enough different from the old one to make things difficult.Surprise #2. After finally configuring the app so it wouldn't alert whenever anyone with a ring doorbell within 5 miles was rung (and likewise we didn't wish to share every bodily function with the connected neighborhood) we were able to start testing it. Even sitting in the same room as the router with plenty of signal strength, the network delay between the doorbell, audio and video on the phone was horrible - on the order of seconds.Surprise #3. I finally install the Ring unit in place of the existing front doorbell button. We go to test it and it detects it only about 1 in 3 times the button is pressed, the lag is worse and video and audio are breaking up when they are there at all. I check the signal level - its -62-65db at the doorbell and only one room away outside. No re-orientation of the router helps and moving it is a non-starter. Soooo.... now its another $50 for a signal booster. Tab so far for this "$100 wonder" is $450. I'm awaiting the booster which may help the reliability ratio of rings to notifications but I doubt if will help the still present network lag. I guess you're just supposed to pretend you're talking to someone on the Moon and accept for the delay.In short, I didn't find this product to be anything it claims and should have just sent it back when I discovered the software incompatibility. To date it has cost my father in law some $450+ dollars and numerous hours of my time and at this point I believe it will be just another unused gadget because its poor performance makes it more of a hassle to use than just getting up and hobbling to the front door. I definitely won't be recommending it to friends or family.

title: Unable to Install on Home wifi network. Very frustrating app setup experience.
body: I REALLY wanted to love this product and make it part of our automated and aware home. Unfortunately, I was not able to complete the installation, due to inability to connect to our home wifi network.Here's what I was able to observe:Pros:1) Excellent inclusion of installation tools and parts in the box. Includes screwdriver, mini-leveler (really cute), and even a masonry drill bit! This is really a A+ in my book, as almost no other home automation product includes the complete set of tools you need.2) Good build quality, finish, and styling of the unit itself. (Though personally, I'd prefer that the logo not be plastered on the front of the case.)Cons:1) Setup process is done via the mobile app. The IOS app gets 2 stars in the app store.2) After creating an account, the very first setup step is to press a button on the back of the device, at which point the front ring is supposed to glow. My did nothing however. There was not a single link in the app to address this. "Having problems?" Or "Ligfht not glowing?". None of those. I had to just take an educated guess that maybe the battery was dead ... and then I had to charge it by plugging into the USB port on the camera, which was not yet identified or explained in any way in the app. This is really a major FAIL. If the app requires the battery to be charged in order to move forward with the setup process then either (1) you need to have a 100% fool-proof way that the battery is always charged when the customer receives the product, or (2) you need to have a clear step in the app process to identify and solve if this is your situation.3) Next step is to join the devices wifi network and input your home network details and password. Many other smart home devices take this same approach, and in my experience, it is OFTEN frustrating and problematic. (In contrast, for example, to the experience I recently had with the Rachio smart sprinkler controller, which I believe used bluetooth, and it was SEAMLESS/MAGIC.)4) Once I input the details of my home wifi network, the app stalled, I waited for a few minutes, and eventually it said setup had failed. I tried 4 more times. I confirmed the password was correct. I moved over and stood immediatley next to the router. I tried a whole different router. Nothing worked. Each time the wifi setup process failed, instead of giving you an easy way to try again (like re-enter the password, or move closer to the router press this button again) ... I had to re-start the entire setup process again from scratch. Very frustrating, and not at all best in class app setup experience design.So, unfortunately, I won't be able to use the Ring in our home. Hopefully, some of the above details will help the team make some setup process improvements.

title: It's OK don't replace your doorbell yet
body: So, I'm about two months in to using this device. As others have said the motion detection is fidigity and lacks the ability to detect motion quick enough for you to see someone as they walk up. I always get motion alerts with at least a 3-5 second delay. The path up to my front door is about 10 feet and there is a set of stairs up one level the camera has full view and even then it can't do the job.While this device is touted as a permanent doorbell replacement it is far from that. I have found it to have lag in the amount of time or even if the Ring chime will ring. The computers and phones ring no problem. They are all in the same location less the 10 feet from the wifi router which is all less then 10 feet from the door.They don't make an app for the Windows Phone environment. If you are the only other Windows phone user on the planet aside from me, you have to make due with the desktop app. It does work in the Windows 10 phone environment with limited functionality. Ring has no intention of changing this.I could look over these three points if the Ring worked like a normal door bell, but it doesn't. By that I mean you have to pull the thing off of it's cradle every 6-12 months, or so they say and charge it for 5-10 hours. I have actually just had to pull it off the wall to charge it after two months, not six and certainly not twelve. I get maybe 1 doorbell ring every two days. I get maybe 5 motion alerts a day, and I am already at the lowest setting. I will have to turn off motion detection if I can't get better battery life. I live in a city on a light to medium traveled street.Today I tried to connect the existing wired doorbell wiring to trickle charge the Ring. I wanted to have a permanent solution rather then something I had to take off the wall every couple of months. After wiring up the cradle as indicated I was getting no indication the device was charging. So I contacted them. To my surprise the trickle charge is not supposed to keep the Ring powered at all. It is there to prolong the battery life. So it is always intended to be taken off the wall.... That is not a doorbell, that's a battery operated camera button. They could have easily made a device that could be charged, but they chose not to. Either through planned obsolesce or sheer neglect. Eventually these are made to be thrown away as the rechargeable batteries die off. Booth through the constant change in temperature and the lack of a permanent power supply.9/5/2017 UPDATE: Ring died and would not take a charge. In the end the charge cycles were less then 3 weeks with motion detection turned off completely. Ring has actually offered to replace the device which is great, though I am skeptical as to the expectation I have for the life of this new device.

title: Ring's Biggest Issue: You.
body: I love the idea of this doorbell. I love the idea so much, that I lobbied my wife for months about spending $200 on a doorbell. Yet the powerful thing about Ring is it's not just some button, it brings the doorbell into the 21st century, and uses that space to keep an eye over who comes near your house, even when you're not home. It allows you to set motion alerts at specific sensitivities and should someone try to slink into your yard or garage under the cover of darkness, it detects it, alerts you, and records their every step. It's the end of the burglar. After a few nighttime break-ins around town, we got one.Installation was a snap (it comes with every tool you need) and in under 15 minutes we had a watchful eye over the house--day and night, awake or asleep, whether at home or at work. And the alerts work great. First it was just me and my wife fooling around, making each others' phones light up, with us being idiots in front the camera. That was fun. Then there was a car in our driveway at night...just a newspaper delivery. Phew.Then, within a week, the real problem with Ring dawns on you, the one that needs to be fixed right now: You. Currently Ring's motion alerts detect movement that occurs in front of the device (likely mounted at your front door), and the motion that occurs there, every day, morning at night, several times a day, is you--coming and going. And Ring alerts you and records it, every time. Bing Bong--it's you. Bing Bong--it's your wife. Bing Bong--you, again. Bing Bong--your child. Bing Bong--you, again. Bing bong--your wife. If you think that's annoying, that's what you'll be alerted to in just the first few hours.What Ring desperately needs is the ability to set the motion alerts for the hours you designate: likely when you're not home and in the wee hours of the night. Somehow this setting has been overlooked thus far. I don't see how: didn't Ring test this product out with any living families before releasing it? Because it's a glaringly obvious problem...and I presume a simple update could fix it, but the product has been out for awhile and still no fix. Ring doorbells across America are blaring alerts constantly about the coming and goings of one's own family.Or more likely, most people are doing what I did: shutting off the motion alerts.So now, my $200, 21st-century, day-and-night, ever-vigilant security system is simply a doorbell again.PS - Two weeks after I bought my Ring doorbell, the company announced a Ring Pro, which allows you to set very specific areas for its motion detection, but as far as I can tell, still does not allow you to set the times for detection. So the biggest problem persists.

title: So much to say
body: Where do I begin. I got this Ring video doorbell after we had some drunk / high stranger come to our front door around midnight one night. Out of the box, the doorbell worked okay. It took about a day and a half for the doorbell to sync up with my phone, even while it was on the same WiFi. Ring has a WiFi download / upload speed tester on their app, and so I ran that test to see if my WiFi was the problem, which it was not (according to Ring's own app). Finally, magically, the doorbell synced up with my phone and we were able to see live video footage as well as any recorded footage. So I attached the Ring to the front of the house, near the door, and started getting bombarded with notifications. I set the perimeter to the shortest possible distance so the thing would stop notifying me of every single car that drove by, yet it still notified me of everything. It alerts you so much, that you actually stop caring to bother checking on if there is actually a threat at your front door or not. Here's the worst part. After a one-month free trial of Ring's subscription service, you lose access to any pre-recorded videos and then you only have access to the live video footage as it occurs. I'm also convinced that in order to get you to subscribe, Ring also completely dumbs down the connection between your phone and doorbell camera. The thing literally became a $100 paperweight after the free trial of the subscription. We would get notified someone was at our door, we would click the notification to jump right into the app, and the app would take FOREVER to connect to the doorbell, EVEN if we were in our home, on the same WiFi as the doorbell. It got so ridiculous that my wife and I ran a test. I went out the back of the house, walked around to the front, up to the Ring doorbell. It took 15 seconds for the doorbell to notify my wife, I was at the door. She then tried to connect to the doorbell's camera through her phone and it took 45 SECONDS of a spinning circle and "connecting to camera" until she was able to see me. An entire minute had passed before she could see what was going on outside of the house. This doesn't sound like much time, but I guarantee you it is plenty of time to react if you see there is a burglar at your door. While we had access to the free-trial subscription, we were able to connect to our door within seconds from anywhere, even while out shopping. I understand that the subscription service is very inexpensive at $2 or $3 a month, but I was so annoyed at how manipulative this entire product and its service was, that I returned it. I will not be bait and switched into paying a subscription fee.

title: $200 Headache??
body: Having had this installed and working for several days I find it to be a source of frustration rather than anything else. I really like the concept and overall product appearance, but it seems to beworking out to be a $200 headache. Most of which centers around the motion sensor. People are correct in saying that the lag time from pushing the button to activation of recording creates videos that show ringers walking away. I tried to compensate for the lag time by using the motion sensing abilities of the device. The idea was to set the motion sensing to go off as one walks up to or approaches the Ring. Setting the Zones and Range caused most of the frustration. I was unable to actually set any of the zones to off; either singularly or whole. After a couple of calls I figured that the slider for zones had to be set out fully to 30 ft then pulled in, turned off or on, then saved by pushing the door bell button on the unit to actually save what I wanted. I could not save any setting by starting the sensitivity at the 5 ft zone (6) and moving outward. The settings I did set worked OK, but varied quite a bit as the sun traveled it's path across the sky and the unit move from shade to full sun. I currently have it set to all zones approx. 10-12 feet out. Set at this range I can walk up to the Ring and it consistently activates prior to me reaching the Ring. However, it is picking up cars traveling up and down the street at approx. 45 feet out. This is creating a lot of unwanted recordings that one would have to view later.During my first call to tech help the call taker basically gave up after realizing I had done all the things he was suggesting and ended by saying that portion of his system was currently down and he would call back later. He never called back. Emailing produced a response assigning me a ticket number and no other directions. I did get follow up emails later. The last call I just left my number and an operator did call back. While we were discussing how to save the zones settings I arbitrarily ended up doing the above process that worked. Both operators used an expression that "setting the motion detection is an art rather than a scientific process". I was on hold so long waiting for an operator that I printed out the return label via my Amazon.com account. I've since cancelled that return and decided to give it a little longer to prove to me that it is worth $214 and change.Note: In the attached photo the actual field test distance from the Ring is 10 to 12 feet. There is no numerical distance value on the second arc, but it I am getting a response at that distance.

title: I really wanted to like this product
body: TLDR: I really wanted to like this product. Bought it at xmas and despite excessive tweaking and even a replacement of my home router, it doesn't work as advertised.Long version:Bought this back at xmas. I was as very impressed with the packaging and the supply of all the required tools/screws, etc.Setup was easy. After getting the app and following the instructions, it was up and running. Great experience so far.Installation was reasonably easy but the screws were made of a softer metal than most screws. I installed into cladding and it required some force to turn the screws, and unfortunately the screws were soft enough that with some meaningful torque, the head twisted off one of the screws. That's going to be a nightmare to get out. But I'll take some responsibility for that as I probably could have drilled slightly bigger holes in the aluminum cladding and not required as much torque. So if you're installing in a material which requires some force, be sure to learn from my experience!Aside from that, the installation went smoothly.Then the problems started.The doorbell was hardwired and transmitted the button press quickly. But it would take forever (long enough for the person ringing the door to have left) for the image to come up. It was heavily compressed and choppy. Taking that long for an image to appear defeats the purpose of having a video doorbell in the first place.After reading online that this was likely a WiFi problem, I pulled the unit from its outdoor mount and stood next to my router, and pressed the button. The image came up slightly faster but was still slower than one would like and still heavily compressed. I mean if Apple can figure out FaceTime in HD on the same WiFi signal and have a useful, no-lag conversation with someone halfway around the world, why can't this doorbell broadcast across my local network?So, thinking this was still my fault (or at least that of my old, but not antiquated, router), just yesterday I installed a new fancy router (R7000 Nighthawk, for reference), pulled the Ring from outside and put it on the new WiFi network.Still no joy. Doorbell rings-through quickly but video still to slow and still too compressed.So, for its primary function, I'm still disappointed with the product and wouldn't buy again. Simply because the video lag is too long to be useful.Finally, despite the ring being hard-wired, it still requires charging. Not frequently -- once since original installation. This was disappointing as well.

title: Doesn't always work as advertised
body: I had this Ring doorbell about a week. It installed fairly easy and I set up the Ring doorbell to be hardwired into the wall. Once the set up was completed, the Ring doorbell went through it's routine to update to the latest firmware version (1.6.291). I am lucky enough to have our wifi router set up on the opposite side of the wall to the doorbell, so the wifi signal was 100% for the Ring doorbell. When we could get the Ring to record video, the video came out clear and did not lag at all. Unfortunately, this Ring doorbell we received only worked for the first full day on the wall. The first time I found it unresponsive, I removed it from the wall and had to put it on the charging cable for it to turn back on. It had completely died while hardwired to the wall with the 20VAC transformer wired to my mechanical doorbell. Each day this week, I tried every available trouble shooting instructions on the Ring website and each time, I was able to get the Ring doorbell to work for about 10 minutes. Feeling confident that I had resolved the non-responsive Ring doorbell issue each time I reset and reconnected....the Ring doorbell would end up not responding to the app when the first person would come to the door (neighbors or family). Whenever I pushed the doorbell button, the blue ring would light up and make the doorbell chime sound, but would not push the notification to any phones. However, today pushing the Ring doorbell button did not light up the blue ring light or make the Ring doorbell emit it's doorbell chime. The light on the Ring doorbell is solid white which indicates it is hardwired so it does, however, make my mechanical doorbell chime ring. As for the motion sensing feature of the Ring doorbell....we were able to set up the motion sensor areas within the app on the first day. The motion sensor worked in our initial testing on the first day to see how sensitive the motion was set. But of course, every day after the initial set up, no motion was detected for any of our neighbors who came up to our door or when we would pass by the doorbell on our way to and from work.
