Whenever I tapped on live view in the app, the app would fail to connect to the Ring doorbell. I don't think the first version of the Ring doorbell works as accurately or as consistently as many reviews here point out. I also came across several Youtube video reviews which pointed out the same issues I was experiencing. I am disappointed in this product and am hesitant to get the Pro version as I fear that the same problems will still be present. As of this review, I am boxing up this product and sending it back to Amazon.

title: Be warned: hard wired does not mean you won't have dismount and charge it regularly!!
body: I was given the Ring as a gift. The first couple of months I loved it. I could sit in my office and see when packages were dropped off or even "answer" the door while I was traveling. Yes, there are some flaws like the video quality isn't great and the motion detection often only kicks in after the person is walking away. These were not deal breakers for me. I just like knowing who was at my house all the time. Note: I have my Ring hard wired.Fast forward a couple months and Ring put out an update for Live View. Maybe a coincidence, but right around that time, my Ring disconnected from Wifi and stopped working. It would not reconnect. I came to learn that the battery was out and in that case, it won't connect to Wifi. I never got a low battery alarm and I wouldn't expect one as my Ring is hard wired. Even the App will NOT show you in battery information when hard wired implying the battery level is not important information anymore.I took the Ring off, charged it by USB and remounted it and things were working. 5 days later again it disconnects with drained battery. I go through this one more cycle and have been corresponding with Ring support. Why was this just now a problem after the Live View functionality? Why did I not get these battery drains for a couple months prior? Ring support offers no explanation.They told me that the hard wire only charges about 3% per day. They rate the battery with being able to last 6-12 months, but that assumes only 2-3 events (motion/ring) per day!!! What person in the world has 2-3 events? I have a family of 5 and a dog and Rung support noted I had 27 events on a particular day. Of course I do! What made Ring management think it was ok to market a set of features based on no use at all. I mean, a single person still gets mail and has to go/come from work and occasionally eat! That exceeds 2-3 right there. Typical silicon valley bubble thinking by millennials.So, now I have no information on the state of the battery, I know it will run down even though it is hard wired and I get no battery alerts. I probably have to dismount and charge this thing every single week and suddenly this doorbell just became a big pain in the butt. Not to mention the Support people are arrogant and make it like my life is the problem.I understand it is a low voltage line. I understand wifi and video and uploading take significant power. What I don't like is it is marketing and treated one way when he reality is much different. If only this thing didn't have to be removed weekly, then I would give it 4 or 5 stars.

title: Motion Detection Needs Work
body: 3 Stars = "It's okay" and that about sums it up. The Ring installed easily on my vinyl siding as the builder put a nice square panel behind my existing doorbell and it wired in to the existing mechanical chime with no problems. The Ring rings both my phone on the app and the existing doorbell box in the house. I live in a 3 floor townhouse so I absolutely cannot hear the existing doorbell chime in the 2nd floor living room from the 3rd floor bedroom/bathrooms. Usually I'm expecting company anyway but I've had neighbors drop by with missing mail or kids play ding-dong-ditch and I'm never sure which. The Ring fixes that problem! Now I know if it's annoying kids, a package delivery person, a package thief, or my friendly neighbors and I can tell them I'll be right down.Now for the frustrating part. It's been noted on tons of reviews already, this thing has ULTRA SENSITIVE motion detection. So sensitive that even though I have it set to 6ft, it's now given me 30 notifications while I've been at work of cars driving by that are at least 100 feet away. Ring support has only said to INCREASE the slider to 30 feet which results in even MORE notifications. So much for being proactive about getting motion notifications for when UPS doesn't ring the doorbell I get notified when every car, school bus, garbage truck, or any vehicle passes by on my street. They say that the further out I set the slider the smaller heat signature it looks for but somehow that makes it worse and worse. Even more frustrating is when it actually is a package delivery that doesn't ring the doorbell (looking at you UPS, FedEx, OnTrac, and FleetFoot!) Ring doesn't even start recording until the person is long on their way out the gate 30 feet away. That means the person walked 30 feet into my yard, onto my porch, set a package on a chair underneath the actual camera, walked 30 feet away THEN it started recording. Honestly while sitting here typing this I've gotten 2 notifications of cars driving by. This is a TWO HUNDRED DOLLAR product that has been out for over a year. I expect to have more refined software than this. I have 7 days left on the free cloud trial and I'm starting to think that maybe I will sign up and clog their servers full of videos of cars driving by.I was inspired to get Ring because a home 2 blocks away was able to submit Ring video to the police and they actually found the package thieves the video was so good.... wish it worked that well at my house. Right now I can tell you what time the school bus comes (8:35am) and I don't even have children.

title: 1st Gen Ring Doorbell Camera / Poor video quality / only 20 sec recordings
body: I just purchased the 1st Gen Ring Doorbell through Amazon. I have this unit hardwired to our doorbell and connected to our wireless router.The ring doorbell is approximately 10 ft. from the router and shows a RSSI -40 signal strength. The Live view and recorded playback of Live view, motion detection, or an activated doorbell is subpar. I’ve attempted resetting both the Ring doorbell and my router, reconnected the devices and the quality didn’t improve. I’ve also brought the Ring doorbell inside and placed it next to the router and the image quality was degraded and laggy. What can Ring do to improve these recordings? I’ve attached screenshots of the device health and recordings. These are definitely NOT 720p as advertised. I have 102.2 Mbps download and 19.47 Mbps upload according to Rings in app link to bandwidth place. Should this be enough to provide high quality security camera recordings?I’m subscribed to the cloud service which is nice however the quality (poor to bad) and length of the videos provided (20 - 30 seconds) is just terrible... Especially since I’m paying for a service. The camera stops recording after 20 or 30 seconds no matter what’s happening in front of it. Someone can approach the door and just hang out for 20 seconds then take something without the camera recording it.Motion detection; sometimes the camera doesn’t detect anyone until they are walking away, then I may only get a 20 second clip of them being there. Shouldn’t this device keep recording until the motion stops? Someone came up to my door and rang the doorbell, however I only received a 20 second clip of the event. After the 20 seconds they could have taken anything off the porch they wanted without being recorded. The bottom line is if there is motion ongoing within range of the device it should keep recording. If Ring is limiting the length of the recordings because Ring cloud can’t support anything longer than 20 seconds which doesn’t do us any good, then unlock the capability to locally store my recordings without Ring cloud. Not just download them, but capture and store them on a NAS. UPS delivered a package to the door yesterday and it didn’t show how it got there, the box just magically appeared...With these limiting factors it hasn’t taken my kids (or the neighborhood kids) very long to figure out how to defeat this device. They can bang on the door and remove tables and chairs from the front porch without being seen by the camera because of the 20 second recoding limit. This is a FAILURE on the Ring camera.

title: Not reliable, now a $200 paperweight
body: Disappointed and wished it worked. I work in the telecom industry and understand more than the average bear about how this technology works. First, you need good internet. I have a 50Mbps download x 10Mbps upload connection so you have a reference point. The router is commercial grade, but in our basement so I have an extender upstairs. WIFI quality is a combination of bandwidth, quality of router, distance from router, and how many devices you have online. If you don't have enough bandwidth, or a crummy router, are far from the router, or a modem or router over 2-3 years old, or a ton of things online this will all effect your experience. Also, every wall, floor, door between the doorbell and router will effect your experience. And the type of wall or floor will matter too. For example, getting through drywall will be WAY better than a brick wall. I read another post where they were upset about loss of signal through a "standard" brick wall. 1) what's a non-standard brick wall? 2) getting anything through a brick wall, let alone a wireless signal is unrealistic. WIFI is a best-effort service. You can do some things to ensure your best possible experience, but nothing compares to being hard wired, period. Even having your microwave running because they use the same frequency! All that being said, user satisfaction will vary from user to user based on all these variables. Knowing I had everything set up to be the most successful I was very disappointed in the lack of motion accuracy as other users state here, the live video feature worked maybe 50% of the time, and our door bell actually fried our doorbell wires in the house. The button got stuck in and my husband realized a couple days later that the doorbell hadn't gone off in a while. He went and pushed the button and it was pushed in and not working. People were ringing the doorbell but it wasnt working so we had no idea. Next day he noticed a smoke smell in our hallway. Determined it was the doorbell box in the hallway and was making an awful buzzing sound. Unhooked it and sent back the ring doorbell for a new one. New one came with a dent, probably refurbished. Plugged that in and that night woke to a horrible loud buzzing sound at 4am coming from the doorbell inside. Had to unhook the ring outside at 4am in freezing weather. Now stuck with a $200 paperweight. It fried all of the wires inside the house we built 13 years ago and zero problems until hooking up ring. SUPER DISAPPOINTED. Sending back.

title: Great Product, Bad Commercial Execution
body: I've seen this advertised on TV at nauseam and agree it's a great and practical idea even though I haven't personally bought one. So what gives me the right to write a review about something I haven't seen, touched, smelled or used? The fact that I'm a woman and a former black belt instructor and safety expert. One of the commercials haunts me so deeply that I had to write. It's the one where a woman answers the bell to the guy on the porch who says he's giving out free painting estimates. She then says, not now. "I'm bathing the children." I cringe every time it comes on.This answer in the real world is not a deterrent but rather an invitation to perverts to catch a woman in the vulnerable position of bathing naked children. Any whacko or pedophile would find that very temping to break in anyway. Did you know that there are more than 100,000 registered sex offenders in EVERY single major city in the US? And those are only the ones who have been caught.It appears that either the owner of the product or his marketing team have never been in the real world of creeps, robbers, rapists, home invaders, child molesters, people who would gladly like to hear an answer like that (true or not) as an open invitation to come in and take advantage of a woman alone on the floor, back to the door, handling slippery little naked kids. It is so offensive every time it comes on. Do your homework marketing folks at the number of women and children that are raped, murdered, abused and molested each year by the hundreds of thousands of child molesters out there. Just ask the Petit family from CT for example what they went through after a home invasion in which the mother/wife was raped and strangled to death, and her two teenage daughters were tied to beds, raped then burned alive. Killers caught, awaiting death penalty.Wake up dudes. It would be far better to have a woman answer and say, Not now, my husband and I are cleaning our gun collection." Anything, but not disclose one's self and kids in a very vulnerable position, even if the situation isn't real. You want answers that deter would-be bad poeple, not extend an open invitation to satisfy their perverse and sick behavior. Bad idea, and offensive to thousands of women and families who have experienced violence at the hands of strangers. You really need to rethink that commercial. Otherwise, product great idea. Just needed to get that off my chest.

title: Design flaw in the sensing and recording system means this system can become very unreliable.
body: I really wanted to like the Ring doorbell. It seemed like it would be an innovative solution to basic front door home security. But there is an interesting design flaw which made it a deal breaker for me. I installed our ring and over the course of a month spent many hours online with their support people trying to tune the sensitivity of the motion sensor. We either got a lot of false positives, or no detection at all. Many others have reported the same issue and I discovered through the continuous contact with the support people that a 5 to 10 seconds delay is what they consider typical. This isn't really an issue when someone rings the bell - people tend to hang around long enough for you to open the door or receive the ring and answer it. But for motion detection it can be really bad. Most of our clips were either nothing going on (whatever triggered it had gone before the recording started) or clips of people walking away.And this is where the design flaw is. The Ring doorbell appears to have no internal recording capacity. It has to connect to the Ring cloud service before it can start recording. This explains the delay in the video clip recording as well as the delay in sending alerts to phones connected to the account. 5 to 10 seconds is the time it takes to handshake with the Ring cloud service and commence recording. The only way to cure this would be for the unit to be redesigned to have onboard memory where it could start storing video the instant motion is detected (this is how Dropcam and Blink cameras work). The memory could then be dumped to the cloud as the service became connected.This also explains the poor video quality and sketchy audio problems others have reported. Ring is entirely dependent on the speed and quality of your internet connection. The further the unit is from your wifi router, the worse the problems become (I proved this empirically with the support team by putting the unit right next to our router, where it worked flawlessly. Then moving to the back of our separate structure garage which is the limit of our wifi range, where we ended up with a good 10 second delay in recordings and notifications).In the end we just found Ring to be too unreliable and returned it. I can't fault their support people but this design issue in the unit itself is a deal breaker. Without instantaneous recording to onboard memory, it's not the right choice for our house.

title: unreliable
body: works about 2 out of 3 times. Seems not very useful if it doesn't work all the time. To wide for my door. Two of the wholes hang over an edge. If it were half inch narrower I could make it work without modifying more door. The screws are made of extremely soft metal. Even with predrilled holes I stripped the head of my screws. It would be useful if there gave you some room between the back plate and the unit. If your screws did not go in on a right angle you will have trouble hooking the unit to the back plate. If your wires don't line up with the very center of the doorbell the plate and unit won't lay flat against the place you are mounting it. It stupid hard to hook the unit to the back plate even when it's not mounted. You have to make sure the back plate isn't bending what so over. If they made the backplate more rigid this would be easier. They need a better mechanism to hook the unit to the back plate. It fell several times onto the concrete several times on me. My unit is all scratched up. Be sure to place blankets on the concrete to avoid breaking it. Im the manual they don't suggest the doorbell line can charge up the unit but in fact it does not. Be sure to fully charge the unit (4-8 hours) on a usb cable before installing. You may have to to recharge every so often even if you have a wired connection due to power outages. They claim it can last a year on battery. It doesn't seem to be close to that from what I observed. I think a month tops. It would be useful if it would ring the doorbell if the motion sensor is activated. It would also be useful to have an osx desktop app. I'm not always near my cell phone/tablet. If it supported desktops I would almost always be near some way of accessing the video. I don't get why they have security screws. I suspect it's not actually proprietary. it's just an uncommon screw. This just makes the have to ship extra parts (screw driver) and more expensive screws. If someone were to still this they would be caught on video if you have their video service. I think most people won't want to steel this because they wouldn't be able to sell it for much. I'd rather them ship cheaper hardware the fancy useless stuff.For $100 this is reasonable. The hardware in this isn't worth nearly that much. If I were guessing there may be $50 in hardware. At $200 it's disappointing to me. I would expect a better thought out/stable product.

title: The Ring doorbell stopped during a possible burglary because it only records for 20 seconds.
body: I received a motion notice only to see a strange man who was not suppose to be on my property walk down my sidewalk in front of my house coming from the front door. He had a hat, sunglasses and "black rubber gloves" on and it appeared that he had something in his hand. He was walking very quickly to his car, got in, tossed something into his back seat then walked to the back of his car (license plate was not clear) and opened up his trunk. Then . . . the Ring Doorbell "timed out" at the 20 second mark leaving me frightened as to why was he there and what in the world was he doing with his trunk open. The Ring doorbell did not pick up any further motion (even though he was about 15' from the Ring. I had my father check the house yet . . .the front door was unlocked. I know when I left it was locked so . . .what the heck??? If the Ring Doorbell worked as I thought it would by picking up and RECORDING all the motion rather than timing out at 20 seconds, I could have more to give to the police. We have No Idea what happened! Did he put things in his trunk that we haven't discovered yet?? I called the Ring Doorbell (800) number and was told that the reason it "times out at 20 seconds was because my Ring was battery operated and if it was hardwired into my power . . . it would record until all motion stops, only to immediately pick up again if motion is sensed" Example: I have recorded my dad walk out the door triggering the camera only to stop, turn around and appear to walk back towards the house only to "time out at 20 seconds" as he was 10 " from the house. I have no idea as to what happen after that because the camera did NOT pick up the motion again immediately after it timed out even though he was feet away from the Rind doorbell. So, if burglar is aware of this, they might be able to work around this flaw. I am highly reconsidering using The Ring Doorbell as my main security camera. I need something that will record from beginning of motion to end regardless of how long and not one that only records 20 seconds (due to battery over hard wire power) leaving you wondering what is happening to your home / property after that. Knowing a strange man with black gloves, hat and sunglasses was walking around the front of my house . . who had opened up his trunk of his car before the recording stopped freaks me out!!! The Ring Doorbell needs to fix this!!!

title: Not worth the trouble
body: It took me more than two hours to set it up. The directions for syncing it with your wifi are a little confusing as they don't tell you to charge it first or that you have to leave the app and go to settings to click on wifi where you choose "ring" and then return to the app and continue. Once I figured that out, with the help of Maurice from the company, it still took forever because it kept saying my wifi was not on when it was on. Finally I read a review that suggested a hard reset - holding both buttons down for 30 seconds. After that I finally got it hooked up to my wifi. Then I installed it - the directions there were very clear and everything you needed was included in the package.Now the moment of truth. I rang the doorbell, holding my cell phone. First of all it didn't ring on my cell phone or in the house. Do you have to go to the app on your phone and click on it? Then it took a long time with the little circle going around and around before you could see anything. Finally I saw myself but not too clearly because I was facing away from a huge sky behind me towards the door and the camera had not compensated for the backlighting. When I said "Who is it" it was quite indistinct as was my answer. It really should have a ringer in the house so you know when someone has rung the bell without having to check your phone. So far I don't think it's worth the trouble. I love the Sado tech wireless doorbell - a cinch to set up and looks and works great. It's not a video doorbell though, and I thought I would like this one better for my front door because I don't have a peephole, but I think I'll just get another Sado Tech and give this to the thrift shop. But now I have to take it down, unscrew it, pack it up - more time wasted.Update: the company did contact me and offer to help after I published this review, but I had already taken it to the thrift shop. But today I visited a friend who has one and loves it (she does have the chime that rings inside the house). It seems to work perfectly. She had it installed by an electrician though, and maybe that's what I should have done but since I was reviewing it I thought I should include how hard or easy it was to install myself (a somewhat handy retired librarian) as part of the review. If I had been willing to spend another hour or two maybe I could have succeeded.

title: Cold weather beware!!
body: If you live anywhere that the temp ever gets down to freezing or below - read this! I used to be the BIGGEST supporter of this device. I told everyone about it. Showed videos all the time. Talked about how great it was. Then winter came.Let me first say that I purchased this device so I would know who's around my house when I'm not home. That was my first priority. The novelty of not needing to get up to see who's at the door when I AM at home was just a secondary benefit. The cute videos of my family and friends making jokes was of course a close third.Now, let's get back to the cold weather. I live in Wisconsin. What do we do in winter when it's zero degrees out? We go on vacation to somewhere warm. That said, on a recent vacation to a warm climate I attempted to view my videos from Ring. I was prompted by an alert when I clicked on the app, it was warning me my battery was critically low. How could that be? I wondered. My device is hard wired. I hard wired it so I would never have to worry about charging the battery. Charging the battery would require using the special tool to remove the device and then bringing it inside, charging it and then taking it back outside. Hard wired was definitely the way to go. Or so I thought. Anyways, I clicked on the warning and then realized not every video had footage or they were "frozen", kinda like everything else outside.Being concerned about the poor quality and the lack of a charge on a hard wired unit I sent an email to Ring. I've actually exchanged several with them. I've gotten a few different responses, lots of misunderstanding from them and no resolution that is satisfactory.Bottom line is this:They aren't meant for cold weather, even if they are hard wired. So, if you're going to go on vacation or ever leave your house in cold weather don't expect to count on this device as your eyes and ears while you are away.It's unfortunate. I otherwise really (did) like this. Thankfully I hadn't yet purchased the stuck up cams that I was going to use for each side of the house.I've included pics of one response from them on the topic as well as the faq from their site. Yes, the FAQ should have been a warning to me before I purchased it. The performance though seems to be even less than expected the email also gives lower expectations.

title: OK... but far from great
body: I've had Ring for 3 months now, and my final tl;dr analysis... Ring is not yet ready for prime time.The good:1. Very solidly built. It feels high quality2. Install is straightforward, and mounting and unmounting is mostly painless (more on that in the cons)3. Video quality is top notch with a wide field of view4. Interface to adjust sensitivity is intuitive5. Warranty if stolen is a good value, especially since its not permanently mounted, and can be removed with a simple tool.The bad:1. Ring is slow.. really slow. I have a very good home network, and WiFi signal near the doorbell is 4/5. So the issue is the Ring's responsiveness. Other reviews note, this is a problem,2. Ties to #1, many videos are of people walking away after ringing the doorbell (deliveries etc). It appears Ring takes at least 10 seconds to engage the camera and ring the bell.3. I also bought the Ring Chime, and this is also affected by the Ring's lag.4. When the Ring activates on your phone, unless you have it in your hand, and activate it quickly, you are locked out of it. You can not activate the camera to see who's standing there. so add this to the lag, and you may have people standing at your door for a min before in the end, you need to open the door to see who's there.5. It's 2015, how does Ring not have more than one chime sound??6. No PC access to videos7. Deleting old videos are a bit of a chore, especially if you have lots of activations8. Big one here for those who don't have power to the doorbell, the charge for the doorbell, even with low sensitivity, charge only lasts at best, 30 days. So you need to unmount, charge and remount every time.9. Tied to number 8, if your Ring discharges completely, you need to go through the entire setup for the Ring again. And this means, deleting the item from the interface and re-adding it. A bit of a tedious process considering the MAC address, wifi setup etc, all remain the same. Again issues you should not see on a 200$ doorbell.I'll update as I use it more.. but simply for an item that does 1 thing, it doesn't do it well. This maybe a simple patch at some future date which will correct the lag issues, but until that's resolved, you will have a rough go of it.

title: It Almost Works
body: I bought the Ring doorbell disregarding many negative reviews. I’m beginning to wish I hadn’t. Though there are many attractive features about the product, and I suspect that if it were working right, I’d love it, I haven’t been able to get it working right.First problem: cross-talk.Installation was easy, but on first testing, the doorbell to cell phone connection was unclear. Video was a bit blurred and it was impossible to understand the speaker on the other end. Definitely the router was too far away, so I bought a wifi repeater (another $40), which helped a lot. Many people seem to have this problem, and you should be aware that a solid connection with the router appears to be necessary.So now video is clear but very the audio is almost unintelligible. The problem is that there is a slight delay when talking and the microphone on the receiving end picks up the voice and sends it back to the original unit. Thus if you are talking into the doorbell unit, what you say is picked up by the cell phone and sent back to the doorbell. It’s as though both people are speaking at the same time, even though it’s actually just the person at the door talking.If you turn off the microphone at the cell phone while the doorbell person is speaking, you can hear just fine. But to then talk to the doorbell, you have to turn the microphone back on -- and turn off the speaker so you don’t hear back your own voice coming back from the front door. When one person is talking, the microphone on the other side should be squelched to prevent this from happening.Second problem: Ring support.I sent an email to Ring requesting help on updating firmware. There are instructions on how to get the firmware updated, but it doesn’t work when I follow them, so I asked for help.I received an automatic return email acknowledging my inquiry and asking for 24 hours to respond but assuring me of the strong desire of Ring to show me how great their support is.Three days later, I sent another email asking for attention to my first email. Another three days later I’ve heard nothing.Ok. So it seems like if it worked it would be great. But it doesn’t work as it should, and I don’t know what to do about it, and Ring doesn’t seem to care.

title: Trendy, has problems and doesn't work for my use case
body: It looks nice and trendy, has simple installation instructions and kind of works, but needs a lot more work to get it right. I say two stars and not one simply because of the innovative ideas. Hopefully, they fix all of their problems in the next version of the device.Now, to all of the serious issues with our Ring doorbell which no call to tech support is going to solve:1) You can set and save how far the motion detector works and even set the zones/sectors it will detect. However, if I reduce it to the bare minimum, it still picks up traffic on the street which is about 50 ft away. I had to turn off the motion sensor to keep my sanity because the thing goes off every 10 minutes whenever we have heavy traffic. Huge problem since the motion detector is one of the main features of the device.2) This doorbell has to authenticate with the server which sometimes takes seconds before it actually starts taking video. This is a problem on the back end and we have no control over how long authentication takes, no matter how fast our internet connection is. So, we get video of nothing most of the time. Doesn't matter, probably was just a car driving by... see number 1.3) The doorbell is large, so if your wall is not perfectly flat, then you'll have the screws that mount to the wall showing. Yeah, I can fix that with extra time and money, but they should have thought of that when designing such a large fixture. All they needed to do was add a shroud that fits around the doorbell to hide the gap or a flexible spongy backside on the mount (think yoga mat),4) The Ring software for some reason won't save my house location even though it says it's been saved. Every time I open up the app, I get an annoying location of "Lompoc, CA". I don't live anywhere near there!5) Our house is stucco on the outside, so we had to drill large holes to fit anchors and mount the device. Wouldn't be that big of a problem if the doorbell actually delivered, but in our case it's not all that. So, to remove it, I'll have to patch the wall and somehow match the paint in a highly visible area the will detract from curb appeal if I get it wrong I'm leaving the doorbell there as a trendy fixture.

title: Disappointing and very limited capabilities if you don't pay the cloud storage fee
body: This has been a disappointed since I received it. I had to return the first one they sent because it wouldn't work if I hard wired it. I had problems with the second one they sent as well, but was finally able to get it to function while being hard wired, and the I was able to get the live view function to work. But a few months after my purchase, they (ring) took away the ability to see video of ring and motion alerts, unless I pay $30 per year for cloud storage. So here's the problem: 1. Ring works great as long as someone pushes your doorbell, and you answer the alert and can get a connection fast enough to see who it is before they leave. (which in my case is almost never). If you can not answer fast enough, then you can not see who it was unless your paying $30 a year for the video storage. 2. If you receive a motion alert on your phone, its worthless because you can't see what set off the motion alert unless your paying the $30 per year for video storage. So here's a scenario. lets say a delivery person drops of a package to your porch and rings your doorbell. You should get a ring alert and If you can connect fast enough you might be able to see there was a delivery person. Five minutes later a thief comes up on your porch and takes the packages and runs away. Yes, you should get a motion alert, but there will be no video of the thief because he didn't push your door bell to activate the Ring video. It should have sent you a motion alert when the thief came on the porch, but if you aren't paying the $30 dollars then there will be no video of the motion or the thief. So what's the point? This door bell is useless as a monitoring device unless your paying for cloud storage. All you end up with is a door bell with live view capability (only if live wired) intercom capability, and a motion sensor with no video. It's not worth $200.I'll be looking for something else to replace it, because I have no doubt that the cloud storage will be $30 this year then $35 next year, an up and up every year.Talk about a coincidence, just as I'm writing this I got a "motion was detected at your front door" message Hit the icon to view what the motion was, and got nothing but a message to sign up for cloud storage! Hahaha...Ridiculous

title: Good idea, but pretty poor execution.
body: Got the ring door bell for my new house based on decent reviews. Installed the doorbell with hardwire option with no problems. Connected to WiFi with good signal.The video quality of the doorbell is fairly poor. I would compare it to early 2000's cell phone camera. Then I started noticing issues with the motion detection not working properly, which was one of the main attractions for getting this door bell.At first I noticed that no matter how sensitive and how far/close I make the setting, the motion detection would still be catching passing cars on the street that is about 80 feet away from my door, which should be beyond the furthest setting of the bell.Next I noticed that the motion detection is random at best with noticing people movement on my porch. First I noticed that a delivery guy was pulling away in his truck after dropping off my package on the porch before I got notification about motion (yes excellent wifi signal and 120mbps internet connection), then I started noticing that I can walk through my door back and forth and stand on the porch without any detection of my presence.So I contacted Ring about my issues. The (email) response time of customer support is fairly quick, but sometimes rep would either be not knowledgeable or think that you are type of person that looks for "Any Key" on your keyboard. In any case, after few weeks of back and forth of changing settings (which I already tried 10 times my self) I finally requested to have a replacement bell sent to me. Which I was kind of skeptical about fixing the issue...When finally receiving a new door bell, I installed it and noticed right away that the quality of the video was even worse than the first one. To the fairly low quality of video, now I have blurriness added and even poorer night vision quality. As for the motion detection, it became slightly better, but again, nothing that you can rely on. I can still walk out or stand on the porch for a while before it finally detects me (or not).Take a look at the attached picture of the video quality. Look how blurry it is with ghosting of the porch supporting poles, etc.Knowing how it performs, I would NOT buy this door bell.

title: It was so full of bugs and badly designed that I sent it back and then noticed the company completely rebranded the product and
body: I've have a RING doorbell for about 6 months now. A couple of years ago, I bought the "DoorBot" which was actually the first version of this product. It was so full of bugs and badly designed that I sent it back and then noticed the company completely rebranded the product and changed the design to get away from the bad publicity. The second time around, they still didn't fix all the bugs.The main things that I dislike about the new product (as MANY others have complained) is the very slow ability to start recording. By the time it starts recording via the cloud service, the person has already walked away and all you see is the back of them if you are even lucky enough to see that much. In most cases, they have completely left the area, so to me the cloud recording is worthless. Now the company is selling Wifi cameras and I will bet they are not any better in this regard. What I really want is a doorbell and camera that report to MY OWN router and NAS recording device, not an unreliable delayed recording cloud service that cost money. I have my own home NAS that backs up data to a cloud service, but my point is that recording to a local hard drive is much faster and doesn't depend on an internet connection or their company's cloud server to be running.Secondly is that their advertised ability to change the sensitivity and zones of motion detection is a joke. I have turned mine down to the minimum and still get motion notifications when a truck goes past the house 110 feet away! That feature simply does not work.Lastly is that I still have trouble getting my old home chime to ring as well as with the chime of the RING product. Being an electronics engineer, I've tried lots of different methods of using their "diode" on the unit to allow my regular doorbell to work. They should have designed in a separate pair of contacts with a reed relay to allow connection to any existing doorbell. That diode process does not work well.Bottom line is that for the price, this product is not worth it. The slowness of recording response and price is bad. Looks of the unit is fine and it does ring eventually and notify me on my iPhone ok, but that is about it.

title: GREAT IDEA RUSHED TO MARKET, WAIT BEFORE YOU BUY UNLESS YOU WANT TO INVEST $200 TO BE A PRODUCT TESTER
body: This product is not ready for prime time so expect to make lots of acquaintances in their tech support department. I bought one of these for my front door. After an hour or so with very pleasant tech support people it worked so well that I bought another for the back door. Both of them are hard wired to the present 20 year old doorbell wiring.Back door unit couldn't tell the difference between trees in the wind and someone at the door, then it takes forever to send a signal. Call tech support. Remove unit from wall, multiple resets, then I'm told I need a range extender. I buy the extender Ring recommends. Still get false reads and no reads, then random chimes on the doorbell. Disconnect transformer. Battery that was charged two weeks before and is supposed to last six months before recharge, dies within hours. Email tech support who says they have a problem with battery life in cold weather. Ring promptly replaces unit and it works OK from Saturday to Friday when the back door chime starts ringing every couple of seconds. Try calling tech support, get voicemail.Go on chat. Chat rep tells me that door chimes aren't standard (so why doesn't the other unit have a problem?), that the Ring unit wasn't really designed to be hard wired (despite the fact it's set up with terminals for hard wiring, and the admitted cold weather battery drain issue), and that they are willing to help me troubleshoot if I can give them all sorts of technical info about a chime unit that was on the wall since we bought the house 16 years ago.Asked if they would replace with one of their stick-up camera units since I have now tried two of their doorbell units with no success. No, we don't do that.Asked if they would refund money since they now have the unit I originally bought here. No, we can't send you money because you didn't buy the unit from us.This device was obviously rushed to market without enough testing under less than perfect real world conditions and Ring apparently expects their customers to spend $200 for the privilege of being their product tester. If you don't have the time or patience to do that, wait a while before you buy.

title: Help Further The Surveillance/Police State
body: It just came to light that Amazon, which bought Ring for a billion dollars like a year ago, has partnered with at least 225 law enforcement agencies around the country to promote and give discounts on Ring products. But, it gets much worse. Though, a private company working with law enforcement for monetary gains is bad enough, but it's worse than that.Doing this also allows law enforcement that have partnered up with Amazon/Ring, to view footage from every Ring camera you have in your home. "Oh but you first have to get a message from Ring and law enforcement asking if you approve to allow the police to view the camera footage from your home.". True, but what you might know is that the police have a workaround where they can go directly to Amazon/Ring and retrieve up to 60 days of footage from any Ring owner, WITHOUT your permission! So, the whole first asking the user's permission first point is moot and basically pointless because law enforcement can view any of your camera footage from any of your Ring cameras, without your permission and without a warrant.Buying this product, installing it all over your home and trusting your privacy will be kept is a terrible idea. Amazon/Ring and law enforcement are creating a surveillance state with Ring the likes which you've never seen.It's one thing to know when go out shopping, to any store, that there are cameras recording you everywhere, that's bad enough. But, what is going on here is recording you in your home, in your neighborhood! There will be no privacy anywhere! Avoid Ring at all costs.If you'd like to verify any of that claims I've made, assuming my review doesn't get taken down, search for Wired, Gizmodo, Forbes and Motherboard recent articles about Ring, Amazon and law enforcement. Since I'm posting this on Amazon and Amazon owns Ring and has partnered with law enforcement in this creepy and shady deal. Well, I would not be surprised if my review never sees the light of day, or if it does, not for long.I'd highly recommend reading those articles I mentioned and researching what I've talked about before putting your trust and privacy concerns in Ring.

title: *NOT SUITED FOR CONCRETE/STUCCO EXTERIOR HOMES*
body: I bought this Ring doorbell from Home Depot roughly 4 days ago, and now that I've had enough time to understand this product and correspond with Ring employees through email, I think it's time to leave an HONEST review. Does this product work? Yes, kind of. Do you get notifications for motion? Yes, barely. Has it met your expectations? NO. I was aware I was purchasing a 720p video doorbell for $99. I did not know that I would have subpar video quality worse than other 720p security devices I've owned. No where on homedepot.com or Amazon was it advertised in the description that "Concrete/stucco homes may have issues with this device". Also make sure your router is closer than 20-25 feet to where the camera stays or you'll have glitchy video resolution. I found out all of this AFTER hearing this stuff from a representative. Also for $99, you're buying a product that can only respond to 2G signal. The other thing I noticed, which after reading so many reviews I can relate to, is the doorbell activates to motion super slow. Extreme delay. I'll have my mailman walk 15 feet from the sidewalk to my front door, put mail in and as he is walking away, the doorbell captures his walk away from my door. The two way audio is only suitable for people who don't live near a busy street or else you'll have to scream into your phone for someone to hear you outside. Which at that point, I could scream a little louder from inside my home and they could hear me through the walls instead. I would return this product, but I can't afford a more expensive security item. Ring 2 has 1080p but will still be glitchy. I don't have wiring for the Ring Pro or Nest Hello. So if you're on the fence buying this product, make sure your internet router is closer than 20 feet to where you put your doorbell. Make sure your exterior walls are not concrete/stucco. And if you buy it anyway and a Ring employee asks you why you're having issues, you'll have them saying you should contact your internet provider to increase upload/download speeds which costs you additional money. Or have your internet provider drill multiple holes through your walls and run wire to get the router super close to your front door.

title: Consider other options for home monitoring
body: I have ring.com products since 2016. We have installed numerous products including 2 doorbells, 2 spotlight cams, 2 stickup cams and 3 floodlight cams. So we have these products up and running now for over 4 years and I can tell you if I had to do it all over again I'd select another option.First the good: The products are all fairly high-quality with easy installation. They connect seamlessly to the wifi. Basic customer support is adequate but not great. Night vision is outstanding. The newer cams have outstanding quality of videos. The overall cloud pricing is very inexpensive.Now the bad: The time it takes for motion to be detected to you being able to view it is completely unacceptable. You know something is there but you just can't see what it is for almost 2 minutes. If someone rings your bell it takes way too long to answer and be able to see and talk to them. In other words, Ring has A LOT of work to do on performance. I know it's not our network since we have a 1gig network with other devices that perform extremely well. Only Ring's performance is poor. Regarding motion, Ring still has a lot of work to do on false positives. I know they are tweaking the program but it is still very poor in identifying irrelevant motion from those you should be concerned about (people or cars versus a leaf blowing or squirrel running for examples). The biggest negative honestly though is customer support. I have had 2 unimaginable situations where in one case I waited 6 months to finally get the product I ordered. It was a total fiasco with no one seemingly to know what to do. Next I had another product issue that to this date has not been resolved. I opened the problem 8 months ago. No one responds to any of my calls or messages. I have since given up. It seems to have just fallen into a vortex in Ring customer support.One day I will ditch the ring.com products and move to another platform. I have Simplisafe as our security system and will just add cameras and doorbells to that system since I have found their products and customer support to be significantly superior.

title: Great idea for a product, POOR customer support
body: This is a great idea for a product, but it has underlying issues and poor customer service backing it.After using this for a couple of weeks, here are the issues that I have encountered with it:1 - Video/audio recording kicks in late after motion is detected. This means you see the backs of people walking away after deliveries are made (or if someone has made off with your delivery).2 - The recording on the cloud is mixed - sometimes it gets solid recordings, other times it will record for about 3 to 8 seconds, then just freeze. You will be able to see the video has recorded for 40 seconds, but it freezes after about 3-8. This freezing also occurs when you answer a doorbell ring or answer a motion detection while live. It just stops and freezes.3 - In broad daylight, anything that is in direct sunlight becomes whited out by the cameras light sensitivity. This is generally not a huge issue as the field of view that is 5 ft and under appears to be ok.4 - After reporting the issue to Ring's Community Support, the tech support guy ran through a gamut of excuses. At first, he tried to help by having me reboot the device by placing a screwdriver across the four contacts at the top. This didn't work. He then believed it was my internet or my cell phone data. Both of which weren't the case as the video on their cloud storage clearly shows the video being frozen. After I asked if he could do anything about this, he hasn't responded since. Ring customer support leaves a lot to be desired.I would give the product a 3.5 rating and the customer support a 1.5 rating, bringing the average to around 2 stars. It's unfortunate that Ring's customer service is bad. Presently I don't think I could recommend this product based on their lack of community support. The product is still usable and I would even say good. But the lack of customer service kills it for me. If you don't have issues with the product, then you are good. But if you ever have any issues and have to contact their customer service, you are out of luck and on your own.

title: Great at first, doesn't work anymore after 7 months
body: It was working great until recently.I have spent far too much time talking to customer service about this doorbell. It really worked great until last month. Since then I have had multiple emails with customer service, an hour long phone call, and other shorter calls.The doorbell keeps forgetting our network even though I have been assured the latest firmware release was supposed to fix that. We have to continuously bring it in the house and manually configure the doorbell. The power went out on Sunday, our Chime Pro was able to re-establish a connection but the Ring Doorbell would not even though it is the device that has a battery, so it certainly should be able to keep the wireless information stored in memory. Today there was no power or wifi outage, but the doorbell lost connection at some point and would not connect again. This is the only device we have to set back up in our house every time it loses the wifi signal. Our blu-ray players, video consoles, phones, security cameras, televisions, computers, etc., etc. can all remember the network and connect back up.It now makes a clicking noise that is new, but I can't figure out what triggers it. It seems to be motion, but that clicking noise was not audible until today. The digital ring (made by the doorbell itself) occasionally sounds garbled. The doorbell always says it is hardwired in, even when I am holding it in my hand, which isn't a big deal except that means I am able to access live view and drain the battery quickly when I should not be able to and customer service confirmed this behavior happened during one of my phone calls.I cannot get customer service to acknowledge that mine might actually possibly be defective as I know two other people that own this doorbell, have the same version of firmware and do not have the problems that I have. Customer service keeps blaming it on weak signal, but that doesn't explain why these problems didn't exist before. If anything the signal should be stronger given we now have a Chime Pro.

title: A Lot of CONS for this product.
body: CONS:1. Occasionally misses a ring. I even bought the extra chime product in case it was my phone's audio causing it. Still not 100%. Called support and they said it was probably the motion-detection, or that it was in sleep but it's not a fixable issue. Unless a firmware update fixes it, it will always miss a ring occasionally.2. To charge it you have to remove it and plug it in like a phone, so you are without a doorbell and its security abilities.3. I got a low battery alert by email which is good but I figured I'd have more than like 8 hours to get to it. When you get that alert you have to get right on it.4. This is the worst part. The subscription is a joke. They advertise that with the subscription that you can save your videos for up to 60 days. Who needs to save it for 60 days!? I just want to be able to see who was at my door 10 minutes ago when I was in the shower but you have to pay a $30 a year subscription JUST to do that. I have to pay for a feature of 60 day storage that I do not want just to see someone who was at the door 2 minutes ago. Please make a feature that offers like 10 days of storage for like $15 a year. It's definitely possible and should be given as an option.5. Motion detection alerts had to be turned off because no amount of adjusting kept it from notifying me when a car went by even though my house sits back about 35 feet from the street.6. If you hear a noise at your door and decide to use the app to see a live view of what's happening it takes 15-40 seconds very often to connect. I have a brand new top of the line router, I work on networks as a part of my living, so it's nothing wrong with my setup.8. I don't believe there's a way to switch the chime to something else. You should have a selection built into the doorbell as well as be able to create a custom sound, and for a product that claims to be so great, it's very surprising that it's not possible.Pros: Very easy to install. Looks pretty nice. Video quality is great. The app is alright.

title: Reviewed after 2 years, live answer doesn't work anymore even with paid service
body: I've had mine for 2 years now. I pay the $3/mo. I used to like it more than I have lately. The app seems to take longer than it used to when I answer a call. I try to click to answer and it takes so long it then becomes a past event and I have to wait to select the event then vs the live answer. By this point the person is probably gone. It didn't used to be this way, but they updated the app about a year ago (?) and it's been awful since. So for those of you complaining about a bait and switch thinking you need to pay to see who's at your door. IT DOESN'T HELP.My door faces a busy street, and so I set the motion detection to the minimum and still get alerts for large vehicles on the street about 50' away passing by. I disable the motion alerts because of how often it goes off.I power mine with the hardwired doorbell wires. The 1st year worked fine. The 2nd winter the battery had a hard time charging during a cold spell (-20F overnight and below 0F for days) and I had to pull the unit off the wall and charge it indoors. I've noticed too if the battery is low, it loads down the original doorbell and it won't ring. I bought the Ring Chime to address that issue, and it originally had problems maintaining a Wi-Fi connection. It seems to have been working better lately. I've only had to charge this once in 2 years, so that's not so bad.So, this seems to work as a portable doorbell ringer and security device to see who was at your door. It doesn't work so well anymore as an actual live view video doorbell for 2 way communication. Peaking out the window is faster! I originally bought this so if my son forgot his keys and had a dead phone when getting home from school he could still get ahold of me. I've had to let him know he needs to wait...wait...wait...wait...before I can answer him. At least it saves me a drive home since I have an app to open the garage door...If there's a decent competitor, I would consider replacing it,but in the meantime it sorta does the job.

title: Only works 50% of the time
body: The first one i received did not work. I was unable to get it to "phone home". I contacted support and the advised that they would sent me a replacement at no cost to me. The placement connected instantly.As far as the device itself they are so so. When you ring the doorbell it notifies my phone that is connected via wifi with in a second or two and generally the video displays quickly allowing me to see who is there. This portion i rate 2 out of 5 stars.My suggestions to ring.com first add the option to pull up live video whenever I want. Second let me program the dThe motion sensing i give 1 one out of 5 stars. I have played with the sensitivity trying to get it right. I hate that when u change the sensitivity range u have to ring the door bell. It should show me a still pic of my view and allow me to move a box around what i want it to ignore and watch for. Currently it goes off everyday when the sun shines on the front of the house and heats up the brick in its view which is about 10-12ft from the device. (Its set on 5 ft). Alsoit has never ever gone off with packages delivered. I tested it by standing in front of the door as if i was a creep looking through the window there. I said there for over 25 seconds before i waved my hand right in front of the camera and set it off. I got the call on my cell via the app but there was just a blank screen.I realize that this device is just a thin client and the processing and recording is done in the cloud. I hate that, id rather pay 100 bucks more and not have to do the subscription and deal with all that.evice via usb and not clunky wifi. Third allow me to totally customize the areas watched and ignored with the motion via a captured photo i can mark areas on. You can allow live feed and give the people an option for using your cloud storage or storing it on their own home network. In its current state i would NOT recommend this item to a friend.

title: So close......and yet
body: I wanted (needed) this to work and I am bummed it didn't. Especially as an Amazon Prime member who gets a lot of packages.... I tested the unit for two days. Install and setup wasn't too terrible. It took WAY too long for it to complete the network set up. Took several tries as it timed out a number of times before it finally decided to complete. I wasn't happy about having to alter some of my network (wireless router) security settingS for this to work. It requires that your SSID is broadcast! (tisk, tisk). Once it was finally on the network it didn't take long for me to realize that I had the same issues as reported by many other users. The motion detection worked about 30% of the time and when it did the delay was such that all I was able to see was the backside of the departing person. Defeats the purpose. Unless I had the sensitivity levels set to maximum range it didn't actually detect motion until I was less than 5 feet in. I stood 10 feet away waving my arms like an idiot and it didn't trigger motion. The doorbell function was plagued with delays. The notification took, on average, 3-5 seconds to trigger on my phone. Additionally there was long delays in the image resolving, such that the image was completely grey until it rendered in about 20- 30 seconds into the call. It needs to be instant, otherwise it's useless. I tested my network strength at the front door and it was full capacity. Not a big surprise as my router is only 20 feet from the doorbell location. Now all this said, I suspect that my issues may be related to my DSL service which doesn't stream 40-50mbps. So maybe upload speeds need to be good a rich for this to work well. So a big fail for me and a refund to boot (thank you Amazon for making that painless). I may give this another try in the future after they add some expanded network ranges and add some additional features. The concept here, is awesome. It will be nice once the glitches are addressed.

title: Cool concept, but unfortunately does not deliver
body: Installation: I had high hopes for this product, but right off the bat the drill bit is the wrong size. I used it anyways because I figured the makers would know what they're doing, but I regret it as now i'll have to get bigger wall-anchors and screws and redo it properly. Second the device never snapped completely into the bracket no matter what i tried and the proprietary screws on the bottom didn't even work properly with the provided screw driver. I had to hold them in place, put the screwdriver against it, and tap it with a hammer for it to even be remotely effective in tightening it. The new box also doesn't include any of the patching putty shown in their product videos to patch up holes in your wall from the previous doorbell.Wifi/Setup: Was able to get it connected to the internet in a matter of minutes with the iPhone app.Functionality: Motion detection set to 50% will indeed alert me of motion without false alarms. The problem is the time delay between being able to answer and if you miss the alert then there is no way for you to "call them back." This has been mentioned in several other reviews, but the basic feature to be able to view video from the camera at the press of a button is lacking and the only way for you to get a video feed is either by someone pressing the button or the motion detection. Picture quality for me has never reached clear 720p HD as of yet but that could be due to my internet which I will be upgrading to determine if that was the issue. The cloud recording feature is pretty cool, however, limiting the recordings to only mobile devices is quite a downgrade compared to a regular wi-fi camera.Summary:For a $200 device it is actually lacking in some very basic features you could easily find with a < $100 wireless camera setup. I'm going to keep it for a week or so and see if I can deal with its flaws otherwise I will be returning it.

title: not Worth it.
body: We were so excited to finally purchase this doorbell and for the most part have liked it. A MAJOR fact that we overlooked, however, is that we didn’t realize the doorbell (even when connected to the existing house wiring) won’t actually ring INSIDE the house. So the only way to “hear” the doorbell is if you are sitting next to your phone 24/7 waiting for the alarm to ring on it. You can also purchase for an alarming $30 a small “chime” that connects to the Ring doorbell via WiFi which plugs into an outlet. This was frustrating to learn since we weren’t prepared to spend even more on accessories to make this actually functional for a home.That being said, I do like the video quality on this bad boy and love being able to talk to whomever is at the door. I would only recommend this product if you’re also able to afford the chimes to put throughout your home otherwise it’s fairly useless as a doorbell and more useful as a camera.Update: After having this for a few weeks, I decided to adjust my 3 stars to two stars. I didn’t realize as well as having to purchase extra equipment in order to hear the doorbell throughout the house you also have to purchase a monthly subscription if you want to use the camera function- it will live stream but if you want to play back any motion you’re out of luck unless you want to pay $10/month for this. So if I happen to be away from my phone and there is motion detected (say a burglar trying to get in to my door) and I don’t happen to see it occurring at that very precise moment.... I canny rewind to see a face or anything of the sort. It kind of defeats the purpose of buying this for extra security. I wouldn’t buy this if I had known all of the extra costs associated with actually having a functioning system. You get a “free” trial for the 24/7 monitoring option but mine recently expired, I guess, so I discovered this little hiccup.

title: Brilliant concept, incomplete execution
body: This is a great concept. If it works out of the box, you're fine, but if not, you're screwed. The technical support is a huge problem when things go wrong.Here's what would be really helpful:1. Instructions to test the doorbell before you even configure it, and before you mount it.2. Instructions to understand the lights and hard reset the doorbell if necessary.3. Instructions for wiring variations (like common intercoms) so the DIYer can get it to work with "different" systems, beyond "try connecting a diode between the terminals."All these things prevented me from solving my own problem, and sent me to hours of technical support for what turned out to be a defective unit in the first place. This should be famous for working out of the box, not for being difficult to debug.Update: Called technical support, and after a long and flimsy first-tier survey, they escalated it to a tech who never called me back. I called again, and after a long and flimsy first-tier survey, and a two-day wait, I got a call back from a tech who was convinced that it was a battery problem. "Should it ring when I press the button?" I say. "Don't worry about that, try charging the battery" he says. "But I just charged it, the lights are flashing, and sometimes it rings" I say. "Oh. We'll get you a replacement with a new battery" he says.Update: I provided the proof of purchase information promptly, but didn't hear back for three days until I called to nudge them, then finally got a new unit shipped.Update: Tried it at a party. The unit would ring on both our phones, but when you answered it on one phone, it would keep ringing on the other. Also, the notification on the iPhone isn't fierce enough. It needs to be as commanding as a text, and make a sound when the sound is off.

title: Disappointed for now, but hopeful the company will improve the doorbell in the coming years
body: Why we bought the Ring:We live smack in the middle of a place where home invasions aren’t uncommon and wouldn’t even make news. As a matter of practice, we don’t answer the door if we’re not expecting somebody. But we also have lots of people ringing our doorbell: politicians who come by for meet-and-greets, our neighbors who want to pop in at unexpected times, and people peddling goods. We decided that because we don’t typically answer the door, we wanted to do two things: (1) see who is at the door without being obvious that we’re looking, and, depending on who is there, (2) let them know that we’re “home” whether we are or are not home (especially for the peddlers—we never know if they’re just in our neighborhood to see who’s home).In theory, the Ring doorbell is supposed to do these two things: if somebody rings your doorbell, a pop-up alert sounds on your iphone, ipad, i-whatever, which allows you to see a real-time feed of who is there. You can then have a conversation with the person through the microphone on the Ring. The entire interchange is recorded through Ring’s optional cloud service (free for 30 days then $3/month thereafter or $30/year). All of these features recently drove me to my local big box store to buy and install the Ring.Unfortunately, the Ring turned out to be much better in theory than in reality, so I’ve had to return it. Below, I’ll lay out what I think is great about the Ring and then why I returned it.What is great about the Ring:(1) The concept is awesome. For a geek like me living in a moderately-high crime area, I can’t think of anything cooler than being able to see who is at my door, being able to talk to that person, and being able to record our interchange.(2) The Ring is also supposed to send you a pop-up alert when motion detection is triggered. You can change the settings on your iphone or ipad or other devices to narrow this range and even to limit it to certain areas. Picture a half-circle – this represents the Ring’s 180-degree visibility due to its fish-eye lens. There are six zones in this half-circle: one smaller half-circle for the 5-foot area right in front of your door, and five more zones beyond that, extending out to 30 feet. So, if you want to shut off the motion detection for the left area of your yard/street because that’s a busy intersection, the Ring app allows you to do that. Again, very smart and very cool in theory.(3) Ring has done a good job of making the sound clear through the two-way noise reduction – even when I’ve been in my car, at work, or anywhere but home, it sounds remarkably clear and thus believable that I’m actually home. (This is according to my wife, who performed several tests of the Ring with me to see how it was working.)(4) You have two installation options. You can install it without connecting it to your existing wiring, but if you go this route, you will only hear the app alert and not your doorbell. However, Ring does have an add-on device called the Chime to solve this problem – for $30, the Chime plugs into a regular socket and makes a noise similar to what your doorbell would make. Alternatively, you can do what we did and install it straight into your existing doorbell system. Installation was a beastly, 3-hour task, but once finished, the Ring worked flawlessly with our existing doorbell system. Not only does installing the Ring this way allow you to hear your actual doorbell plus the alert on your Ring app, but there is an added benefit of conneting the Ring to your existing wiring: the wires will deliver a small trickle charge to the Ring so that you don’t have to take it down to recharge the battery through the provided USB cord (which Ring says has to happen about once a year, unless you live on a very busy street, in which case the battery will drain faster).(5) Intuitive, easy-to-use software. Multiple people in your household can download the free Ring app for their own devices and thus get alerts when somebody is at the door. The app is quite simple to use. Also, in case you were wondering (I certainly was), the alert on your iphone or other device will sound if you have your volume on, but will vibrate if you have silent mode + vibrate on your phone, or will make no sound if you have silent mode without vibration on. In other words, you will not have an embarassing moment during your work conference because your Ring app sounds.(6) Great customer service. I availed myself of several of Ring’s “how-to” videos during the installation process, and I felt (and still feel) connected to the brand. I believe in what they’re doing and I like their philosophies and promises (such as replacing the Ring if it’s ever stolen).Why we returned the Ring:(1) The motion detection did not work. It simply did not work. We tried multiple sensitivity settings (bringing it farther away, bringing it closer, restricting zones), but it never picked up any activity unless the person rang the doorbell. On various occasions, I even stood one foot from the Ring for several minutes, waving my arms, dancing around – and nothing. The importance of this cannot be overstated. While it’s quite useful to see who is there once that person rings the doorbell, I also wanted what Ring promised: seeing who was there when the person did not ring the doorbell. We have plenty of people who knock, and we have plenty of package-theft incidents. These are the types of things I was hoping to be able to record with the Ring. It’s simply not worth it to be to have the Ring alert “triggered” only if somebody rings the doorbell; for $199 (actually, for about half that price), I could put up a decent camera and get a companion app for my phone.(2) Along these same lines, the Ring had some lag in recording when somebody did ring the doorbell. So, when our friendly UPS man delivered a package recently, he rang the doorbell (as he always does), but then promply got back into his truck without waiting for a response (as he always does). The video feed, however, shows nothing – not even his truck driving away. Others have reported a similar lag time; I’m hoping that this is something Ring will fix in the future.(3) The Ring does not allow you to see what’s going on unless the Ring is triggered by motion or by the doorbell being rung (in our case, only by the doorbell being rung). Given that the Ring has a pretty big camera connected to your iphone or other device, it seems odd that this feature is not available. In perusing the internet about the Ring before I purchased it, I saw from several other people’s thoughts on the Ring that this was something people wanted. I’m hopeful that this will be available in future versions of or software updates to the Ring.(4) The picture quality is pretty grainy – 720 pixels. When we had a suspicious person come to our door during broad daylight and ring the doorbell, his face in the video was somewhat clear, but not enough that I’d be sure it was him if I saw him in person.Additional cons/things to be aware of (but not deal-breakers):(1) Before you buy this, know that this product is huge! It’s about as wide as a cigarette box and a little bit taller. Unless your existing doorbell is on a large slab of siding, there will be installation issues. You can remedy this by buying or creating a small slab of wood that sits behind the Ring and on top of your siding or next to your siding (i.e., you would drill that small slab of wood into the siding so that you now have a bigger “face plate” to install the Ring on). Or, you can do what we did and install the Ring with just two of the screws on (there are four screws – one in each corner). Surprinsingly, the Ring was still quite secure this way, but it’s an issue you should be aware of.(2) The mollies that come with the Ring are smaller than the drill bit that is provided with the Ring – so, you will be creating gaping holes in your siding with mollies that aren’t the right size. We ended up using our own mollies (which I highly recommend), but not without creating some serious holes in our siding.(3) The cloud feature is great, but several people out there have lamented that it should be able to be stored in one’s own cloud storage (like your Box or DropBox account) rather than in Ring’s proprietary cloud. I don’t so much have a problem with the fact that the videos are stored in Ring’s cloud – rather, I would just like more transparency with what security measures they take for their cloud. I wouldn’t just want anyone to be able to hack in and see the house. Also, given that you do have to pay $30 a year for the cloud service, you’ve now essentially identified your home address by linking up your credit card (billing address) with images of your house. For people who are in the market for this kind of doorbell (i.e., security-conscious people), this might be a sticking point. (The Ring does ask for your home address when you set up the app, but this is not required for the camera to work and was certainly not something I provided.)Overall, while the Ring has some great concepts, it didn’t deliver for us. I’m so disappointed that it didn’t work out for us – I had such high hopes. In sum, I believe there’s no reason that this product can’t be awesome with some tweaking. I think it’s just too early for the technology to be “all there,” so to speak, or there aren’t enough competitors to make this product as exceptional as I know it can be. Down the road, I will definitely be keeping my eyes open for later-offered doorbell cameras by Ring.

title: The "Living Review".
body: Meaning, I will modify this as I go because there's a LOT to talk about and I'll forget.UPDATE 8/27: I finally got a chance to try the 2-way feature on a couple of occasions - one when I was upstairs and didn't hear the doorbell (which I've since replaced), one when I had stepped out after waiting as long as I could tolerate. This worked extremely well - despite the video freezing, pixelating or otherwise being useless, audio seemed to be near instant from the moment the bell was rung. The downside to this is that the Ring Android app has a delay to open; I've estimated it at about 7 seconds. This is on an LG G2 which isn't a slow phone by any means. So yes, I like the 2-way but the app needs some work.UPDATE 7/18: I've noticed a rather disturbing trend and it's happened twice. OnTrac has dropped off packages, but the Ring didn't alert me to any motion whatsoever. Neither did Dropcam, but I did verify from a Dropcam playback that the guy walked up to the door, meaning the Ring would have seen him for at least 5 seconds, 3 of which are seconds that Dropcam can't see. But Ring shows no motion alert, and since it's not constantly recording, if there's something it missed you won't know. My guess therefore is that Ring has some sort of delay before it starts recording motion, similar to the [[ASIN:B00QH0Q8J4 Arlo Smart Home - 1 HD Camera Security System, 100% Wire-Free, Indoor/Outdoor with Night Vision (VMS3130-100NAS)]], but that delay has to be in excess of 5 seconds. That explains the false alerts. I've disabled it for now.UPDATE 7-14: Adjusting the zones down to the 5 foot level was pointless. It's still firing false positives from both the street and walkers-by. I'm getting less false positives, but realistically I shouldn't be getting alerts from a 5-foot zone when these motions are over 20 feet away.Second, Something changed to where I am no longer getting "Unknown Error" every screen. So that's good. There was also a trial of some kind enabled, which I'm assuming is the recorded history and viewing past video feature. That wasn't activated when I set this all up, so maybe that's why the "Unknown Error" was happening. Not sure. It's a decent feature but not sure I would pay for it. Given it's in the past, it seems to make better sense just to be emailed a photo with the alert telling me that someone was at the door, and their face. That's all I need to know.UPDATE 7-9: I noticed a lot of false alarms from the device about motion. Turns out that what it estimates as 30 feet has to be way off. My front door is approximately 50 feet from a busy street, and it throws motion alerts constantly for cars that drive by despite my zones being set to a max of 30 feet. So there's that, and I see no way to avoid that barring just dropping the zone down. I'm out of town so I haven't tried this yet. Seems a lot of headache though. If that doesn't work then it's got to be the various moths and spiders that hang out in my breezeway - and as we all know, there's not much you can do about those. So if it still throws false alarms I'll have to disable motion detection altogether...meaning I'm right back to depending on the Dropcam (and that subscription fee) for this.Second, the image quality improved, but as I got my first visitor, I noticed a significant delay (~5 seconds!) between what I saw on the video and what I heard on the audio. Dramatically out of sync, making it functionally worthless to use in this fashion.I ordered this spur-of-the-moment as I wanted to improve visibility to my front door. For the most part it's couriers that come to the door, I don't get a lot of visitors. But I recently had an incident where I almost didn't get a critical piece of hardware due to the driver allegedly ringing the bell and me not hearing it. Not going to argue whether that happened or not. Point is I wanted to know when they show up.
