I have a Dropcam pointed at the front of my house, but because the house is so large I don't have a vantage point where I can see the front door, only the walkway, by which time it's too late. I also didn't want to pay the subscription fee on Dropcam (but will have to anyway).First negative with this thing is the setup process. I give them kudos for using the adhoc wireless feature to set it up (which the [[ASIN:B00TO1QN9I Rachio Iro]] developers need to learn how to do), but their app is extremely buggy. The first time it didn't tell me it was creating an adhoc network for my phone to connect to. This isn't a big deal, but it would be nice to know what that orange button is exactly doing. Second, it refused to connect and never actually timed out. I hit back after some minutes passed so I could try again, but this time the app crashed and rebooted. Third, after it crashes it totally forgets everything you gave it (address, name, etc), so you have to start over again. This seems to be rather laughable, frankly.When I finally got past that I went in to set up the zones. After saving the zones it says to push "the button". It doesn't say WHICH button - the orange one? The ringer one? 50/50 shot, so I pressed the ringer. It proceed to ring and then "call" the app. Nice. No idea if the settings were saved or not.I backed out to see if there might be a log or something and went to Recent Activity. It then threw "Unknown Error". Repeatedly does so as I navigate through the app. Nice.So I went back and made a slight change on the zones, thinking I could "re-save" the change. This time I went for the orange button, but the device proceeded to call me again. Nice.Think I figured out the problem. Given I'm human and not a robot, I was holding the device normally, but this was causing my palm to trigger the camera. Now, in my mind the camera should not be active if I'm setting a program to the device, but whatever. I've still got the Unknown Error being thrown on every screen.So while calls do get through and it does appear to be detecting some motion I have no way of knowing if it's doing it right at all. Nice.I tried blowing it out and re-adding it, but it seems as though the knowledge of the device (i.e. history, etc) is stored locally, not in the cloud. When it was re-added it retained all of the history from before, so it's likely not a clean re-add. Still Unknown Erroring like mad. Nice.The other issue, and this is inexcusable, is that the display for the WiFi hotspots has an EXTREMELY short character limit. I would peg it at about 10-12 characters long. Obviously if my hotspot name is "johnson" that's fine, but I have three hotspots, differentiated by the suffix, and they're each lengthy. It perfectly truncates the part I need to know that it's on the right network. I had to guess based on the signal strength and I have no way of knowing if I got it right without going to my router's page. Not good.MOUNTING:WHEW, what a pain! This was to be mounted in place of an old doorbell from when the home was built over 30 years ago and frankly, in terms of wiring, I have no complaints. The stickers to help level, even the little plastic level device, all are nice touches. The problem is the housing itself. First, it doesn't nice and smoothly slide onto the bracket. You've got to bang on it from the top with your palm to get it to "snap" into place. Removing it requires you bang on it from the bottom to get it to slide up. This was unexpected. Second, if you need to do any sort of Wi-Fi adjustments whatsoever you have to remove the whole thing from the wall plate. You can't just go into the app and go to "Wireless Settings" and change the hotspot name or password. This is a HUGE negative. Back to the app's limitations again.Once I got it wired it worked beautifully, but I noticed the same picture quality issues that people have been talking about on here. And yes, I have 2MBs up, so it's not that. Besides, Dropcam is streaming 1080p without a hiccup, so it's not my connection, it's this device for some reason. On three subsequent tests, two of them resulted in a gray screen. The third picked me up but with no motion, just a still, even though audio was perfect. After I reset the wireless configuration thinking maybe it was a distance problem (it wasn't), it then picked me up, but apparently nobody thought to add algorithms for sunlight. If the person's back is to the sun you simply can't make them out, it's just a shadow with clothes. I'll share photos at some point. EDIT: This apparently only applies to black people, who show up like shadows. Caucasian, Hispanic, any other light-skinned person is reasonably visible. You still can't make out clear details of who exactly it is because of pixelation due to the struggling and hiccups.As others have mentioned it would also be nice to just engage the camera when I please rather than waiting for motion to pick it up or someone to hit the bell. Couriers like OnTrac don't even bother and they're not there fast enough. So I'll have to rely on both Dropcam and this in order to get a picture of what's going on.

title: Late Breaking News, read before buying!
body: Late Breaking News: I received an email today from Ring informing me that the next generation of the doorbell is here. If I wasn't going to return mine tomorrow, I certainly will be now. The new version is supposed to have the much desired Video On Demand capability as well as some other upgrades. Cost is around $250. Video on demand is also supposed to be available with your current Ring doorbell if it is connected to hard power and not just batteries. The email said the battery only one's would have VOD in about two months. As I said and for the reasons below, I am returning mine to Best Buy tomorrow and then will wait to see what the reviews on the new one are before deciding if to purchase or not. I am glad that Ring engineers listened to their customers and added the much needed improvements. Good on them************BREAK**********************Fellow shoppers, I continuously discover more and more value in going To Amazon.com and reading customer reviews prior to making any significant and sometimes insignificant purchases. This is my review of the Ring Doorbell and to be fair and in the hopes Amazon will post this review anyway, I did not purchase from Amazon. I made my purchase of the Ring Doorbell and companion Chime on kind of an impulse buy while shopping for a security camera system at Best Buy one Saturday. The young man at Best Buy seemed knowledgeable and I walked out with a Ring Doorbell, Arlo Wireless Security Camera System, and a Nest thermostat. Not sure if it would have impacted my decision to purchase the Ring but I did not make/take the time to research as much as I generally do. I guess that is why they call it an impulse buy. I do believe my review is consistent with other Amazon and internet reviews. Below is a copy of the email I sent to Ring earlier today.Question: does Amazon do price matching?*****************************Dear Ring DoorbellBottom Line Up Front (BLUF):Based solely on my personal experience over the last three weeks or so, I have found that the Ring Doorbell device does not operate as advertised and I will be returning it for a refund and waiting for the next generation to be designed prior to considering another purchase. Read further if you want more detail.Discussion:- Although Costco was selling the Ring/Chime as a package for about $30 less, I received a price match and purchased the Ring Doorbell and Chime packaged separately from Best Buy a few weeks ago and quickly returned home excited to install my new gadget. My wife gave me the typical wife "look" I am used to by now after returning from shopping and questioned my purchase but I assured her this would really look awesome and will be a nice addition to our modern and "connected" home. Unfortunately after using and testing it for nearly three weeks, I must now admit defeat to my wife and although not happy, I will be returning it back to Best Buy this week before my 30-day return period is up.- From my experience, most notably today, I will say and again solely based on my personal experience, that what I now own is a pretty awesome looking $200 toy/doorbell that does not seem to function completely as advertised. Here are my thoughts in the hopes that they may be useful.Pros:- Price: my initial thoughts were it was a little high but I guess reasonably priced for what it is advertised to do in this day and age.- Fairly easy installation to my masonry constructed home.- Easily understandable directions on use.- User Friendly App and quick notifications (I am connected to Suddenlink with a Netgear Router and one Netgear extender, Verizon is my cell carrier).- Fairly priced storage fee.- Crystal clear video.Cons:- The motion sensor is ultra-sensitive and claims to detect heat not motion if I understand it correctly. From the street to my front door is approximately 30 feet. I have slowly dialed the sensitivity back to what now is essentially zero and finally shut off all but the two immediate left/right views where it should only pick up someone actually stepping onto the concrete at my front door. The algorithm is also set to "light" and I believe I followed all written and video instructions. So the doorbell rings, app quickly sends notification with chimes to my iPhone, and indoor Chime sounds. All seems to work well but the motion sensor still picks up cars driving by on the street as well as from looking at the videos, branches blowing that are 5-10 feet away from the door. It is very annoying to receive dozens of "false" notifications a day and the device doesn't seem to "learn" as the setup video says.- I happen to currently be home somewhat immobilized while recovering from surgery. My wife needed to run some errands today and I said no problem because I had what I needed to get by for a few hours including a cell phone and laptop and of course my newly installed handy-dandy Ring Doorbell. My wife left and the next thing I knew the doorbell rang and my phone chimed notifying me someone was at my front door. I quickly accessed the Ring App and accepted the request. First, the guy outside couldn't figure out where the voice was coming from but I could see him very clearly. He couldn't manage, even though I verbally told him several times, to look at the doorbell and speak back to me. Now I admit this would have normally been entertaining but I didn’t have the time nor energy today. This is not necessarily a problem of Ring Doorbell but I thought it was worth mentioning. What is a problem is that once I accessed the app and started speaking, the picture became distorted and I could no longer see who I was talking to. Had this been someone with a package, I would not have been able to see them while instructing where to leave the package. Because I needed to talk to this guy and I am stubborn, I found a way to get to the door and deal with him even though I knew I would hear about it when my wife came home. So Ring failed at what I perceive is the very function it is advertised to perform.- To clarify the above, when I reviewed the video a few hours later, the video was clear and crisp, not distorted as it was when it was live.- I bring this up because it is when I reviewed the video with my wife several hours later that I discovered I had a missed-unanswered ring. Sure enough I have a clear video of my mail man ringing the doorbell and waiting patiently with a letter in his hand that required a signature. I now have video of him walking back to his mail truck, depositing the notification slip in the mailbox and driving away. I guess I will wait until tomorrow to see if I am being audited or something. Two failures here, first Ring didn't alert me to the mail truck when it pulled up but it sure did to the other 20 cars that drove by that same morning and second I didn't get a notification of the doorbell ringing. Now to be fair, I was on a call at about the same time the mailman rang the bell and I may not have heard the chime sound the alert but I am pretty sure my phone did not vibrate.- In the end, I just don’t have the confidence that I can rely on this and do not have time to further evaluate before the clock runs out on a return.Other suggestions/improvements:-Video on Demand would be a great feature.- My wife says the doorbell button is a bit hard to push. Perhaps lightning this up to meet that of a more typical doorbell might be an improvement.- Offer a way to download additional rings for the Chime. Same comment for the app notification although the “keys” sound is appropriate.I hope these comments are useful. I look forward to the next generation to come out so I can see what its advertised abilities are. This is a great idea and something that is needed and I honestly would look for a different brand but there isn’t anything close to Ring. In the end, it is just math in that the Pros do not outweigh the Cons.

title: Ring has some work to do, but I'm hopeful!
body: As an Amazon devotee, eBay seller, and frequent online shopper, I constantly have packages coming and going from my doorstep, so I bought the Ring Doorbell to act as another set of eyes making sure all is running smoothly at home. I love the motion detect feature and the video quality is pretty great! The Ring notifications arrive darn near instantly to my phone. For all those reporting lower video resolution and substantial lag, that's likely a product of a slow isp or weak wireless connection. Luckily my router is located just feet from my front door to begin with, and my overpriced cable internet is plenty fast.So why only 3 stars? Let's start with the price. $200 seems a touch steep for what is essentially a glorified webcam with motion detection and a speaker. Knock at least $50 off the price, and then we're in more reasonable territory. I also feel that such a pricey front door accessory could easily do without the gaudy "Ring" brand name scrawled across the bottom, but clearly classiness comes in at a distant second to consumerism in today's growth-driven world of tech brands. Additionally I wish the motion detect feature would work a bit quicker to activate the camera's recording. If someone knowledgable about the Ring's delay wanted to steal a package from my doorstep, I bet they could do so with the Ring ultimately only catching the thief's backside. I was also surprised to learn that the Ring does not allow you to pull up a live stream of your camera on demand. The Ring either has to detect motion first or have someone press the call button on the doorbell before you are able to access the video feed. Ring claims to be "looking into" a streaming feature, which makes me think they designed the doorbell to communicate with your smartphone on a one-way basis and they are now grappling with the technological challenges of reversing that one-way input securely. I am worried Ring will end up forcing me to buy a second generation of hardware to enjoy live streaming, which would be irritating.My biggest beef with the Ring however arose from the setup issues I faced. The night I received the product, I unpackaged my Ring with delight and dove right into the quick guide mounting instructions, which were pretty straightforward (you'll need a drill) but slim on details. I looked for a thicker manual booklet in the Ring's box to supplement the quickie foldout directions, but I guess I'm an old-fashioned square for thinking I'd find a detailed paper manual. A quick google for "Ring Doorbell setup directions" reveals that Ring's makers are sure we're all visual learners and that YouTube video directions are the way of the future apparently (for me, video tutorials are oft an absolute last-ditch source of info, but Ring's videos are pretty painless and quick-moving enough to be more helpful than annoying). Most of all, I was hoping to find some guidance on the best placement for the Ring i.e. "how high should I mount it?" but it matters so little with the wide angle lens that such questions remain largely unanswered by Ring.With the Ring's mount in the wall, I then downloaded the Ring App and followed the on-screen directions to connect the doorbell to my phone, and then to my home wifi network. Before I continue this review, I have to say that I own a veritable menagerie of connected home devices, from hue lights to an Amazon Echo, and I've NEVER had any trouble setting up all sorts of these unnecessary toys to my network/phone. The Ring however was not happy at all with my home wifi. I kept getting a "something went wrong" error page after my phone connected to the Ring and then tried to connect the Ring to my network. I tried all the normal electronic fixes of restarts, reinstalls, unplugging and everything in between.Resigned to my fate, I begrudgingly decided to reach out to Ring tech support (but had to wait until they were open the following day). I spent nearly two hours chatting online with a Ring associate, going back through the gamut of hard restarts and reinstalls until the associate ran to the end of his scripted "fixes" and declared that my problem was for "level 2" support. Frustratingly Ring must get a lot of "level 2" problems because you have to schedule a time to speak with a "level 2" technician ahead of time a la Apple Genius system. Most of the appointments were taken (you have to go through each technician's individual calendar to find an opening which is like adding insult to injury), but I eventually found one for the following day (although I had to run home during my work lunch hour to make the appointment as Ring support hours are more East Coast friendly than for us out here in CA).My "level 2" agent had to take control of my home computer and access my router settings, but he found the problem pretty quickly: the Ring was upset with the name of my home wifi network... Apparently I had a space after the last character of my SSID (network name) and that was enough to render the Ring incapable of joining the network... Really??? That was the problem??? As earlier stated, I have dozens of connected home devices, and not a single one of them found the space at the end of my home wifi network's name to be a FATAL ERROR. Seems like a known software glitch like this one would be the easiest thing for Ring to fix, but apparently they'd rather have their customers (and agents) suffer through hours of tech support and jump through several hoops instead. UNBELIEVABLE. It's not like I was typing my wifi password incorrectly (which all the Ring agents kept pressing me on incessantly as they felt that screwing up the password was pretty much the only way I could be getting the "something went wrong" page attempt after attempt). And my home wifi network name appeared on the list of wireless connections the Ring could connect to, so it's not like I was typing the name of my network and missing the final blank space character myself.Despite venting here about the Ring setup saga, I'm still happy about making the purchase. The Ring is pretty neat and handy. With time, I hope Ring's features can improve with on-demand streaming and faster recording start time after motion-detection trigger. I would also appreciate Ring compatibility with Amazon Echo, which I'm holding out for before purchasing the Ring Chime accessory, as I can envision Alexa notifying me after someone presses the doorbell button. Still, the pessimist in me says Ring would rather force me to buy their $30 speaker box before cozying up to a third-party solution with Amazon. Time will tell, but it's still an exciting world in this internet-of-things age the Ring represents!

title: BE CAREFUL OF FAKE REVIEWS / READ ONLY VERIFIED PURCHASED REVIEWS!
body: ***UPDATE 6/30/15***Levi @ Ring has been awesome since the posting of this review. I ended up returning my Ring, but he sent me one directly for no cost because of the troubles I was having with the one I bought here at Amazon. I am having much better luck with this one, and am still working out some kinks. As it stands, it will be staying at the door for now and am updating the review to 3 stars (from 1 star).My major criticisms now are how it works itself- it still is quite a process to unlock your phone (using Touch ID or a passcode) and then get the app to connect. Sometimes the app doesn't load when trying to answer the Ring alert, so you have to manually launch the app and wait for the connection to be made. When someone is standing at your door it can feel like eternity when this is happening.Another thing is, if your phone is on vibrate/silenced, the Ring alert only sends a quick vibrate - so if you miss this then you have no idea someone is standing at your door. Theres no way to make the Ring alert alone make a sound. Since I am using this in battery mode, I need a separate doorbell chime somewhere in the house. Fortunately, the Ring Chime device may make this possible and Levi already put me on the list to be a beta tester so I will be receiving one shortly to test out.***ORIGINAL 1-STAR REVIEW**Overall, I change my opinion on the support I have received and am blown away by Levi's responses. He even offered to come out to my house to troubleshoot the problems, which is above and beyond what I expected.This thing is NOT ready for primetime. Like many of you here, you are reading great reviews from people here on Amazon, many of the stellar reviews from NON-VERIFIED purchasers of the Ring Doorbell. I am definitely a legit GUINEA PIG / BETA TESTER for this garbage product.Apparently after they changed their name from DOORBOT to RING they wanted to clean up their image, I guess that image also includes writing BOGUS/FAKE reviews here! I am sure their fake accounts will down vote this review too, as they are really trying to fluff up this product after the EPIC FAILURE of the DOORBOT.Anyway, on to why this thing is a PIECE OF GARBAGE.First off, I am an IT consultant who owns my own IT consulting business and am a genuine techie- everything I have is the latest tech gadgets and devices, especially all of my smart home devices. I have been doing this type of thing for well over 17 years professionally.The Ring doorbell is very easy to setup, that I give them props for. If you can set up a Nest thermostat or Nest Protect, then this is a simple process.After that, it all goes downhill from there. I am currently using the battery installation vs hardwire because my wiring was too low in the stucco to use, in an awkward angle where the Ring can be installed. It is mounted directly on my stucco, which behind that wall about 15ft is a straight shot line to my current gen Apple Time Capsule which is the 802.11ac version. That is hooked directly to my cable internet connection which is 150mbps download and 25mbps upload.Standing outside next to the Ring doorbell with my iPhone 6+ and also my Macbook Pro I have full Wifi range and get the entire 150mbps down/25mbps up bandwidth, so clearly Wifi signal is not the issue.The video is HORRIBLY pixelated and the sound breaks up. The frames per second (FPS) bounce around between 1-2 FPS and also 4-5 FPS max. Faces are not legible as they are covered in pixels and have a ghost image and stutter thats so bad, it looks like a game of Minecraft, its horrible. Watching the video back on the cloud storage ($30 per year) is better than what you see live.Besides the atrocious and border line unusable audio and video is the connection time. You press the doorbell and you do get the notification in 2-3 seconds on your phone which is totally acceptable.. BUT... this is once again where it goes downhill. They demonstrate quick notifications in the videos and marketing information, but what they don't tell you about is the EXTREMELY LONG TIME IT TAKES TO CONNECT AND SEE VIDEO AND SPEAK WITH SOMEONE AT YOUR DOORSTEP. It can take 15-20 seconds for you to be able to see and talk to someone outside, by that time they may already be gone. In fact 2/5 times you will just get audio and NO video at all, just a spinning circle thing while it attempts to connect to the camera.The technical reasons behind the Ring doorbell itself seem to be the culprit. The doorbell DOES NOT connect directly to your phone or mobile device, but rather connects through the internet (or "Cloud" as everyone calls it now) to Ring's servers- which I doubt are anything high tech at all. That connection is established, then it acts as a liaison to your app on your phone and bridges that connection, so you are routing through a 3rd party system with lots of latency and bandwidth issues on their end. They can blame your Wifi all they want, but remember- you are sending all audio and video through Ring's "cloud" to get to your phone which results in very delayed and spotty video and audio. If there was a locally direct connection from the Ring doorbell -> your device, this would be a MUCH better experience, IMO.As it stands, this product is crap, just a rebranded failure of a device (The device previously known as the DOORBOT) and their shady way of cleaning their reputation is not going to make a defective and flawed product successful.On a side note, their technical support is a JOKE- I sent them an email when I first got it and received a quick response by a guy named "MIKE B" who said he made adjustments on their end to the doorbell, but I swear it made the problem worse and the video quality even crappier because of more compression artifacts and garbled audio. After I told him that didn't help, I received no reply- and I sent 3 more emails and they just ignored me after that.THE PRODUCT AND COMPANY SHOULD BE AVOIDED AT ALL COSTS. I AM INITIATING A RETURN RIGHT NOW THROUGH AMAZON. Do NOT be fooled by these fake reviews..!

title: Pretty good - but think twice before changing your WiFi name...
body: Update: I purchased a new torx screwdriver from Home Depot, and was FINALLY able to remove the doorbell so I could set it up again. I realized the purpose behind the special screws is to deter someone from removing the doorbell if they shouldn't be or to prevent someone from stealing it, but GEEZ! It shouldn't be THAT difficult!I bought the Ring doorbell back in December, and it worked pretty well up until a month ago when I setup a new WiFi network (because my old router died). I thought "Oh...I'll go into the app on my phone and update the WiFi info!" I found nothing in the app to update the WiFi info. Then I thought "Oh...I'll log into my account on the web site and update the WiFi info there!" I found nothing in my account on the web site to update the WiFi info. So I Googled how to update the WiFi info, and quickly found out how on the Ring web site. To update the WiFi info you have to delete the device from your account, physically remove the doorbell from the house, and go through the setup process like it's brand new. OK...so I go looking for the special tool to remove the doorbell from the house. Yes - there's a special tool, so some random person can't come up and take your $200 doorbell. Great...I can't find the tool. Now that I think about it, I think I put it back in the box, forgot that I put it in the box, and threw the box out with the tool in it. So I email Ring support and ask if there's any way I can get a new tool, and I offer to pay for it (because I screwed up and threw it away - but I didn't tell them that). They email back asking for all my info and tell me they'll send it out for free - no need to pay. Great! The tool shows up about a week later, and after looking at it I realize it's just a regular torx screwdriver - which I have in my computer toolkit. Oh well. So I delete the device from my account, and then go outside to remove the doorbell from the house. The screws did...not...budge...at...all. And guess what? The tool got stripped! Not the screws...the TOOL! So I figure if they're sending the tools out for free they're probably pretty cheap. Surely the torx screwdriver I have in my computer toolkit is better. I go in the house and get the torx screwdriver out of my computer toolkit, and go back out to take the doorbell off the house. Guess what? The exact...same...thing happened with the torx screwdriver from my toolkit. The screws didn't budge, and my screwdriver got stripped. So now I'm stuck with a VERY expensive $200 REGULAR doorbell on my house. Yay me! Thanks, Ring!!!What's that you say? Just change the name of my new WiFi to match the name of my old WiFi? Well...I *could* do that - but I've already removed the doorbell from my account, so it has to be setup again no matter what. Plus I have a TON of things that use WiFi in my house, and I had already gone around and changed everything to use the new WiFi before I realized the doorbell needed to be changed also. Even if I would have thought about that before I deleted the doorbell from my account, that would have been a LOT of things to go around and change AGAIN. Plus - if I ever sell this house (and that will eventually happen at some point), the new owners wouldn't be able to use the Ring doorbell unless I said "Hey - if you want to use this cool doorbell as it's intended you'll have to use this WiFi name with this WiFi password, or it won't work!" Yeah - no person in their right mind who has even the slightest concern about securing their network would do anything like that. And since it can't be removed from the house now (without probably destroying the doorbell in the process, there's no way for me or a new owner to set it up again. Yay! $200 down the toilet! Yay yay yay!Prior to all the above it worked pretty well. The only complaint I have about it is the motion detection feature - which you can turn on and off and *supposedly* adjust. WORST...MOTION...DETECTION I've ever seen in my life!! It seems like anything and everything sets off the motion detection. We have a flag on the front porch, and every time a breeze comes up and makes it move around the motion detection goes off. I even adjusted it down to the shortest possible distance (approximately 5 feet out from the doorbell) - and the flag STILL sets it off, even though the flag is well over 5 feet away (probably about 8 feet or more away). Plus - when it's set down to that lowest distance the motion detection NEVER gets set off by someone walking up on to the porch. The motion detection goes off when we walk out the door from inside the house - but someone outside walking up to the front door? Forget it. The motion detection gets set off by someone outside walking up if I set the distance out further - but not at 5 feet. And I know it's not just something funky going on with just my doorbell because one of my coworkers bought one too, and he has the exact same problems with motion detection. I even watched their video on how to best adjust the motion detection and made some changes based on what was in the video. Didn't help at all. Cars going up and down the street will set it off also, even though the street is WAY past the distance that I have it set for. The max distance is 30 feet (and I don't have it set that far out), and the street is WAY past 30 feet away from the doorbell. The alerts for motion are almost constant. Even when I set it to "Light" in order to receive fewer alerts, they can still come in as frequently as every 5 minutes. They really need to do something about the motion detection feature. Ugh.

title: Strong WiFi UPLOAD SPEED is critical at installation location + Some drawbacks for busy locations
body: What I like:1. The Ring Concept: Being able to see activity near the house and verbally respond, even when away from home, to someone who is at the door using the phone app is a great idea. Being able to adjust range and sensitivity of the motion sensors is a nice feature.2. RING Support: Although the first person you talk to may not know everything about the device, they can run you through some basic troubleshooting. Their Tier 2 and higher support is very knowledgeable. Also note: Support is based in the U.S. If they are very busy, you can have them call you back instead of "growing old on hold."3. Video Clarity - Nice when it works well, night vision feature is great and full-color (not green like night vision images on TV).What I do not like:1. We have found it difficult to find sensitivity and range setting that works well in our location, about 25 feet from a fairly busy street. The sensor is infrared and picks up heat from passing vehicle engines as motion, giving many nonpertinent motion alerts.2. Ring Device can only do one thing at a time: If "Motion Alert" is triggered, say from the mailman approaching the house, the video upload of that "event" prevents any other Wifi-dependent action by the Ring device until the upload is complete. This means that it cannot send a signal to the Ring "Chime" or Ring App device for a doorbell push "event."It takes around a minute for "events" to upload to the Ring cloud so they can be transmitted to your phone or tablet. If you have your Ring Chime or Ring App set to only notify you of doorbell push "events," you will not be notified that anyone is ringing the bell and the Ring "Chime" will not sound. This seems to defeat the purpose of the device, to see and speak to persons who RING your DOORBELL remotely or from safely indoors.(Note: If you connect the Ring Doorbell to a "wired in" doorbell chime, that WILL sound even if it is busy uploading motion alert video because that is a straight-up direct electrical signal.)3. The Ring "Chime" or Ring App can be set to alert Ring App/sound Ring Chime for motion events, doorbell push events, or both. Because we get so many motion alerts at this location, we have it set to alert/sound for Doorbell Push events only. If we set it to sound for Motion Alert events as well, it will sound frequently day and night for passing autos or sidewalk traffic.4. WiFi Setup Issues: In order for the Ring device to upload clear video/audio and allow you to respond via the speaker, it is ESSENTIAL that you have strong UPLOAD SPEED at the actual doorbell installation location. (Run http://www.bandwidthplace.com speed test on your tablet or phone at the exact proposed mounting location, with doors closed like they would be if you were not home.)Note: "Signal Strength" as displayed on most devices is based more on DOWNLOAD SPEED, but the Ring Doorbell needs UPLOAD SPEED of at least 1 MBPS to work and with little or no interference from other electronics, walls, wiring, metal lath in walls, etc. Ideally, it should be installed very close to an ethernet-connected router, which is set to the "least used" channels in your neighborhood. Sadly, there is no port to connect the RING device to ethernet cable directly.5. Power Supply: The advertised battery life is based on infrequent doorbell pushes or motion alerts. We had to go outside and remove the device and recharge it for hours every 4-6 days until we wired it into the electrical supply. (The first Ring device we had would not hold a charge even then.) Also: If you have a power outage or have to reboot your modem or router, you must repeat "Setup" steps to reconnect it to your WiFi. This includes going outside and removing the Ring device from its mount to press a button that is unreachable when mounted.6. Picture/Audio Quality: This really seems to be directly related to the upload speed and lack of interference mentioned above. We have still not managed to get consistently good video/audio, likely because of the WiFi setup that we have. Ring Tech Support was able to tweak a few settings at their end to help, but short of running ethernet connection nearer the device, so far it is just not working out for us.7. Lastly, if you order from Amazon, make sure you return your Ring device within the Amazon returns period (30 days from delivery?) if it is not working out. If you buy it direct from Ring, they seem to have more flexibility about refund periods. Nothing against Amazon, just good to know, perhaps especially if giving as a gift.UPDATE: We did decide to run Ethernet to a location near the front door and relocate out router (cable modem is upstairs and at the rear of the house, far from Ring doorbell). That GREATLY improved the video feed and speed in sending us alerts for motion or doorbell ring. Today, for the first time, I had video from the time a delivery driver exited his vehicle, made the delivery, and drove away with no freezing and minimal pixellating.NOTE: Ring Doorbell only communicates on 2.4 GHz Band, but works on N, G, or V mode.

title: Could be 5-Stars if the Motion Sensor Worked
body: I usually wait a while to jump on board with electronic gadgets, but with my recent first home purchase I've been really interested in the potential of the intelligent home idea. So when I first heard of the Doorbot I was intrigued, but stayed away because of the mediocre reviews. Once the new version came out (The Ring), I figured they had worked most of the kinks out and it was time to dive in. I've had mine about a month now and I'll say that it has the potential to be a 5-star product if they can just fix a few things.First off, the actual doorbell part works great. Note- my wi-fi router (the latest model Apple Time Capsule with 50mbps internet service) is about 5 feet from my front door, so the signal the doorbell gets is pretty solid. Because of this, I get notifications almost instantly on my iPhone when someone presses the button, and the video & audio are clear. Design-wise, I have the satin silver version and it looks pretty cool and I like how the button glows.Now for the issues. First off, on install it's great that they give you a tool kit with everything you need to install the doorbell, but the included wall anchors are way too small to work with the included drill bit. Be sure to use your own smaller drill bit or the included anchors will be useless. Second, the plastic mounting plate is way too flimsy. If you are attaching this to any uneven surface (stucco), you will either need to shim the plate out or not tighten it down super tight to keep it from warping. If the plate is not perfectly flat it makes attaching the actual doorbell unit to it virtually impossible. Another issue with the mounting plate- be sure to peel off the tape/gasket that is on it from the factory- with this in place it's impossible to mount the doorbell unit to it. There is no mention of removing this gasket in the instructions which seems really strange. I almost broke my unit trying to force it on with the gasket- once I peeled it off it went on perfectly. With it mounted, it does seem a bit precarious (it sticks out about an inch) and not very "snug" to the wall- like it could easily just be pried off and stolen. I hope they never get rid of their "free replacement if stolen" offer as I can see this happening as these get more common. As far as the proprietary screws holding this on to the plate, well all a thief has to do is buy their own Ring, and they'll have the tool to take these off. I notice this does have a unique MAC address, so maybe that will help these from getting stolen to be re-used? Anyway, I personally wouldn't have minded the form factor to be wider if that meant the depth could've been reduced.Now aside from all the mounting issues, the biggest issue I have is with the motion sensor. I've got it set for Zone 1 only (which shows as a 5 foot radius from the doorbell), but I'm constantly getting "false" alerts from cars driving down my street- my doorbell is a good 25-30 feet from the street. The worst part is, when the mailman came to my door, he didn't even trigger the motion sensor. I tried calling customer service but the line is always busy and after a while your only option is to leave a voicemail. I opted to just send them an email as they state they'll reply within 24 hours. Well, 3 days later I did get a reply, but so far none of their suggestions (latest was to do a hard reset of the device) has fixed the motion sensor. Based on all the other reviews, it seems the motion sensor is definitely the sore spot with this device right now.In closing, a few more random thoughts. As a few others have mentioned, it would be nice if I could turn on the camera whenever I wanted via the phone app just to check out my front porch/yard area. Also it's a bit confusing when setting the motion detection, at one point in the process you are supposed to press the doorbell, but once you do that you get the standard doorbell alert and are kicked out of the motion settings- until customer support told me this is normal it seemed very confusing and made me question if it was a bug and whether my motion settings were saved. Finally, the cloud service seems a bit overkill. I'm not a fan of "the cloud" in general, and don't really see the value in paying $30 a year to have access to your video recordings, when it all comes down to needing that one video (heaven forbid) of a criminal at your doorstep. There's really no reason the clips couldn't be saved to your mobile device, other than the fact that it seems every company wants to have a service-based income stream these days. I personally would rather they raised the price of the product to $250 or something and made it so the videos saved to your device. Or heck, cut that cloud price in half and this hater would probably sign up...

title: May SOMEDAY be the BEST doorbell money can buy -- just not yet.
body: Received a Ring Wi-Fi Enabled Video Doorbell as a birthday gift from my brother.1) Set up was easy, and it worked the first day seemingly as it is meant to work. However, after the first day, whenever a doorbell call was received, the video screen would open all black with a white circle spinning in the center of the screen. Even after accepting the call, there was no video for SEVERAL seconds. The time lapse was so long, if the caller had been UPS or FedEx they would have just left the package wherever they chose to and ran back to their truck and driven away by the time the video started. Once the video begins, there is nothing but static in the background making it difficult to understand the caller -- and possibly they can also hear the static, though no one has ever said so.2) I've only had the doorbell since October 15, when it was fully charged and hung up. Have only received up to 5 doorbell calls, but the battery is now at 50%. The included info said battery life would be approximately 6 months to one year depending on how much use. Which brings up item 3.3) I watched the video regarding setting the Zones and Range for motion activity. I initially tried approximately 10 feet from the front door, but vehicles passing in the street (which is approximately 30-40 feet from the front door) kept triggering motion activity. I only left that for a few days, and when I realized there were far too many motion activities, I reduced the range to 5 feet (the minimum) from the front door. Am still getting more motion activities from street traffic (which thankfully is low, since this is a quiet suburban street).4) When hanging the unit, it was difficult to get the unit securely onto the mounting rack. Luckily, my adult son was visiting and was able to help. I'm concerned if I will personally be able to remove the unit for charging and return it to the mounting rack without assistance. The locking screws are extremely tiny and would be lost until spring if they fell into a pile of snow. In spring I'd have to use my high power magnet to find them, if possible. I hope the company sells replacement screws in case anyone happens to drop one of those tiny things and cannot find it.5) Living in northeastern Ohio, we've been having some rather cold nights (30's and 40's) since I received my doorbell. When resetting motion settings, it's necessary to push the front button on the doorbell to save the settings. I've had difficulty making the button work. I do sincerely hope it's not because of the cold, because our last two winters we've had record low temperatures and record snowfalls.6) Sent an email to customer service regarding the slow video response and the static, and was told that it was necessary to have high speed internet -- at least 1 MBPS -- mine is 12 MBPS. Also the static might be because of the Wi-Fi's strength. I've tested Wi-Fi on my iPhone at the exact location of my doorbell and it works seamlessly without any problems.Summary) This product is an EXCELLENT concept. The idea of being able to answer your doorbell from ANYWHERE is priceless, because would-be unsavory characters will think you're home and perhaps move to another target. Also, the motion detection is a wonderful feature, in case someone is snooping around and decides not to ring the doorbell. They're unaware the doorbell is watching, but you're able to view the video and if their behavior is suspicious, you can contact authorities if you feel it's necessary. I believe the newness of this product is a major cause of the glitches. Nearly ALL new products need a break-in period to work out all the "kinks and bugs". I'm sincerely hoping that new software will be forthcoming in the very near future that may smooth out the wrinkles. If everything went as smoothly as it goes in the demo videos this would be a total MUST-HAVE product for all homeowners. Meanwhile, I'll try to keep the faith that things will improve. If not, mine came from Hammacher-Schlemmer and can be returned, refunded or exchanged.

title: Great Company, Great Camera, Poor Implementation
body: I purchased my Ring doorbell from Frys, but wanted to leave a review where I thought it would be most helpful.My first reaction, was Wow! I have never had a camera with this wide of a viewing angle. I am able to see the width of my yard, and can even see the screen-door handle which is right next to the Ring doorbell. The image is clear when the person starts to enter my yard. Far away, the person is too small to recognize unless you already know who they are. Can see just as clear at night as during the day, but that may be helped with my yard light.I mainly purchased this for my sister to be able to tell who was at the door without opening it. She is too short to see out the peephole. Unfortunately, this Ring doorbell does not work well for that. Before you can view the camera, Ring must detect motion, or someone must push the button. Then you must quickly answer the Ring doorbell. If you're not quick enough, you miss the opportunity to answer the door using the Ring app. The Ring doorbell cannot be activated on demand.For $30/year, Ring will record video to their cloud if motion is detected or the Ring button is pushed. It only records for 40 seconds if you don't answer the call. If you are quick enough to answer the call, it appears to record until you end the call.Challenges I ran into and overcame during installation:The first Ring unit I had, one of the security screws got mangled. Not sure how, as I thought I followed all the steps correctly. A call to Ring, and they offered to replace it for free. With the second unit, I made sure the security screws were loosened enough to clear the bottom mounting slots and only tightened them enough to be flush with the bottom of the unit.When I removed my old doorbell, I had 2 wires, same color, So which wire goes to Front and which to AC? Went to Frys and bought a non-contact volt detector. That did the trick.My router was using WEP 128 bit encryption. Turns out the Ring doorbell doesn't work with WEP 128 bit encryption. So I had to change my encryption and update wifi settings on 27 different devices. No big deal, but time consuming.All seemed good until Ring was uploading to the cloud while I was watching streaming videos. My streaming videos would freeze while Ring was uploading to the cloud and a lot of the Ring video files were blank. I then realized it was time to upgrade my old slow modem and router which I had been putting off until I actually started having issues. I now have no more freezing or blank videos.When I upgraded the router, I had to change the wifi settings on the Ring doorbell. This required me to remove the Ring doorbell from my account and remove the Ring doorbell from the bracket in order to push the orange reset button on the back of the unit. After loosening the security screws it came off easily. I pushed the reset button, several times and could not get it to show up in my wifi list to connect to again. I kept hearing click, click, click like it was in a loop mode detecting motion. Not sure though. Eventually, I held down the reset button until I saw the white ring. Success. I think if I have to do this again I will turn off motion detection before I remove the device from my account and wait to make sure no uploads are happening before proceeding. Once everything was set up again, I could no longer play any of the videos in my cloud account. Another call to Ring, and they fixed the issue right away and gave me another 30 day cloud trial for my troubles.In summary, the field of view is superb and the image/sound quality are very good. In addition, the added bonus of being able to store video clips to the cloud may help you catch a thief. The support from the company has been top notch. If they could overcome the problem of having to respond so quickly to the Ring, I would give this product 4 stars. If they can allow you to activate the Ring doorbell on demand through the app, then I would give it 5 stars.

title: Excellent idea, poor execution.
body: This doorbell has MAJOR flaws. I love the idea of it, but hate the execution. I will list the problems I've discovered in the couple weeks I've owned it below.1. The motion sensing feature detects "heat", not actual motion. It fails miserably as it does not alert to a 4000lb vehicle entering or leaving the driveway(within the manufactures 30ft effective range), but detects a landscaper 100ft away. It will VERY frequently miss people walking up to the door, with no reproducible reason why. Sometimes it will detect, sometimes it won't. Day or night, doesn't matter it will very inconsistently detect motion, many times missing it altogether. Sometimes when someone comes up and rings the doorbell, it literally doesn't record or detect any motion until the doorbell has already been rung. Meaning they were able to walk for 30 feet all the way to my door, passing my cars, passing my garages, passing my courtyard, and still nothing until they've pushed the doorbell.2. I've had instances where I've received alerts, but they didn't save in the cloud recording history. Or when I've received alerts and I've attempted to answer them/view the alert on my phone, it wouldn't load the live video of whatever set off my motion or allow me to answer when someone rang the doorbell. Again, like the motion sensing capability, very inconsistent at actually allowing you to receive or answer notifications, or actually storing motion from when the motion first happens. Many times I will try to play a video from a motion alert, only to see a car that was in front of my house leaving, but not the activity of them being there. They really should have it constantly recording and after each motion detection, store 10 seconds before and after to ensure nothing is missed.3. Trying to get a lot of the customer service is rough. You will be on hold for ever. I've been on hold for 40 mins straight only to be disconnected. Multiple times it's been longer then 30 mins. A call to customer service had me on the phone with a level 1 rep for over an hour to no avail, only to be told that a level 2 rep would have to call me the next day...and days went by and the call never came. I then reached out to customer service again and I found out the reason I never got a call was because it was canceled because the level 2 reps thought it wasn't an issue they needed to resolve, and that level 1 should've handled it, so instead of having level 1 handle it, they just canceled my ticket. The people I did actually speak to in customer service were excellent.The reason I'm giving them a 3 star rating is BECAUSE the Ring Pro is now available, and it is supposed to fix many pitfalls of this version. It isn't a replacement to the Ring, it's a more expensive($250 instead of $199) and better version with 1080p instead of 720, it takes photos every second or so and thats how it detects movement instead of with heat. It should be more accurate. It also has much more adjustability of motion zones. I have a pro on order, and I'm looking forward to trying that instead. I love the idea, but the execution is poor on this version. Hopefully the Pro will eliminate these problems and work as intended. Customer service did help me out as much as they could, because it was purchased at Amazon they couldn't accept it as a return but did everything in their power to keep me as a customer, which I really appreciated. I suspect they are going through some "growing pains" as they have really expanded quickly in a short time, seems like they are working on improving everything. Overall, It's a must have for every home...IF it works properly! Maybe mine was just a defective item? I hope so, because these 4 holes I drilled into my brand new homes door frame to mount this doorbell aren't going to be in vain!!!!! LOL. The new pro should be here any day now!

title: ... doorbell wired on our house so this was a nice option for me to avoid having to pay an ...
body: We didnt have a doorbell wired on our house so this was a nice option for me to avoid having to pay an electrician to come wire my house for a bell. The product works pretty well and is innovative but I have a few issues with it.First is the delay from when it picks up motion to when you receive an alert on your phone is considerable. Usually the mailman has already came and dropped the mail and is headed back to his mail truck by the time the Ring starts recording so all I see is his back. Not a big deal if it's just the mailman but if it's a burglar or someone shady, not picking up their face doesnt really help at all. Also, there is a considerable delay between the Ring recognizing motion, ending the recognition, and then being ready to recognize motion again. For example, I walked up the the ring (set on 30ft motion detection) and it recognized me and sent an alert when i was 2 feet away. I then walked away and headed another 20 yards down the street to get out of range, turned around and approached my door again. The Ring did not recognize me at all and sent no alert. I sat on the porch for a few minutes talking to Ring Customer Service and then repeated this exercise with them logged in so they could see the issue at hand. They said I had a faulty unit and sent me a new one. To their credit, I found their customer service technician to be helpful, thorough, and quick to try and solve my problem. I got the new unit but the issue persists. Before thinking this is an issue with my internet/wifi, please know that the signal strength always reads as "Very Good (RSSI-47)" on the app diagnostics.Second issue is the speed of the app. When I get an alert that there is motion or someone has rang my doorbell, I swipe into the app right away. From there it can be anywhere from 5-15 seconds for the app to startup, load the video, buffer the video, and connect. Sometimes it wont connect at all and just "times out". While I will admit that sometimes I'm at work with a less-than-stellar wifi connection, many times my connection has been good and the delay is still significant. I've tried keeping the app running on my phone to see if that would speed up the connection time but it really doesnt have much of an effect.Another issue with motion detection that I've had is the range. Even when I set the range to 5 feet away, I still get notified when a car drives by even though my Ring is every bit of 45-60 feet from the street. Not the biggest deal but it is annoying and definitely affects battery life.Which brings me to my last issue - battery life. There is no way you're getting 6-12 months on one charge as stated on their website. I had my Ring for less than a month when it needed to be recharged but seeing that I didnt initially charge it (nothing in the box/setup said I had to and the battery level read 80%) I figured a full charge would tell the true story. Well, I charged it about 10 days ago and it's already down to 77%. That's 23% degradation in 10 days which means I'll get about a month to a month-and-a-half usage out of it before I need to recharge again. It's easy to recharge but having to do so often is somewhat of a nuisance. The one unknown factor here is my wife has her phone connected to get alerts when the Ring goes off and I'm not sure if she's activating the video feed for each time she gets an alert and then letting it run. I imagine that could drain the battery faster but I'd venture a guess she doesnt do that as she never even answers when the bell rings.Overall, I give the Ring 3 stars because it's a nice tech piece to have, fills a void for homeowners who dont have a wired bell, and is somewhat useful security. The issues above keep me from rating it higher.

title: Great door bell but sucks as a security motion detector & poor battery life.
body: After having some minor vandlelism I deceided to install a security camera. It had to meet the following requirements:1. Preferably battery powered with long battery life, in case I wanted to relocate later, with easy set up.2. Great video especially in night IR mode, at a distance of at least 30'.3. Video saved to the cloud instead of a memory card/hard drive, in case it's stolen or destroyed. Videos must also be downloadable in case I need to provide to police/insurance.4. WiFi connectivity to my Android & iOS devices so it could alert me not only while home or away but others as well.5. Combined both IR & motion detection to eliminate false movement of our 2 flags.The Ring meets all of the above except #1* & #2 above. In addition its a door bell. An added benefit is free replacement of the unit if it's stolen. I'm not sure if that includes damage by vandals/burgulars.The battery is a built in rechargeable and Ring claims about 1 year life between recharges via its micro USB port. (Update: after 30 days my Ring's battery was down to 10% & needed to be recharged. So much for 1 year battery life.) Installation took just a few minutes to drill 4 holes to attach the backplate. Ring slides on to the backplate and is held in place with 2 hex screws on the bottom. Ring even includes a concrete drill bit, screws & screw drivers, however it does not include a drill bit for mounting to wood/metal. WiFi setup was very easy taking just a few minutes.Ring includes free 30 day cloud storage. After that it cost only $30 for a year of cloud storage. Videos can be retrieved for up to 6 months & can easily be download. I wish it was free like my Piper NT camera I use inside our house, but $30/year is reasonable.Apps are available for both Android & iOS devices. I prefer the app on my iPad over the Android app on my cell phone, because I can delete notices easier & see full screen video on the iPad. While I can view notices anywhere I have an Internet connection it takes about 30 seconds between the notice & its ability to see the video (guess it has to wait for the video to be stored to the cloud.). You can also share notices with others, example: you're on vacation so family can react in your absence.It uses dual IR & motion to prevent false notices. Our 2 flags flopping in the breeze do not set it off because while moving it has no heat. It is only set off if the moving item has a heat signature.Cons: Video quality is not adjustable like my Piper NT. Quality is good if someone is standing in front of the Ring during daylight but loses quality the farther away the subject is, especially off to the side. Ring claims it has a wide angle lens but based on objects I can see on both sides I'd estimate it's about a 160-170 degrees not 180 degree like my Piper NT. Ring uses a poor quality fish eye lens which distorts videos off to the sides. The IR night time video quality is marginal as is the distance it can see subjects. The battery life is not even close to the 1 year claimed, mine is only 30 days.Ring has 5 zones of detection which can be individually turn on or off. The detection range is adjustable out to 30'. Unfortunetly, I had to set the range to about 12' to prevent false detection of moving autos 50' out.Overall I'm happy with the Ring but would prefer they offered better video, especially night time. They could eliminate the expensive box & tools to use a better lens, etc.Update 10/24: submitted a suggestion to Ring to add remote viewing. In 20 minutes I had a reply thanking me for the great suggestion which they would pass on to their engineers. Wow...20 minutes...excellent service.

title: I found the Ring to be most useful for my needs
body: Updated review 3 stars 6/2016:The first few months or so were great. The Ring worked really well for me. It was pretty quick to notify mine and my wife's phones, it also recorded pretty quickly upon motion detection, and the video quality was decent enough. Sound and microphone needed some customized settings that Ring's cuatomer service took care of. By the way, customer service is pretty decent. They try to help and to explain things well.Unfortunately, after the first few months, things just didn't work out so well. I thi k there was an update which screwed things up. Basically, the notifications take upwards of 10 seconds to reach our phones, there is now a very long delay before it recognizes motion even though it is set at its highest sensitivity, the recorded video not only starts very late (like 6 seconds late) but it then records for 1 second then freezes for 13 to 16 seconds while audio continues then continues video then freezes again. Live View is also a new feature that allows a Ring owner to check a live feed without the Ring needing to be activated by motion or by someone pressing the doorbell. It also takes about 20 seconds for it to connect even while on the same wifi. Customer service sent me a new unit which mever solved the problem. I think they are at a lost but don't want to say that. Instead, they keep telling me that my speed or signal may be the problem but I have verified that numerous times with them that is not the case. The speed requirement is at least a quick ping, and 1 Mbs upload. Lol... My ping was 16 ms while download is about 95 Mbs and upload is about 16 Mbs all standing outside with the door closed right next to my Ring. My wifi router is about 10 feet away and in direct sight of the Ring minus the wooden front door. They did do someth4on their end and it fixed the issue greatly to the point that I was content once again, but 3 weeks later things just got bad again. I have a top of the line tp-link router and have confirmed so many times at all times of the day and night that my wifi is not the issue. I also have two wireless cameras in the house much further away from the router that have no issues whatsoever.Original review (5 stars) approximately 12/2016:And this is the one I pick!A few months ago I decided to get a wireless camera for my front door. I tried a number of IP wireless cameras including D-Link DCS-2132L, D-Link DCS-932L, iZtouch, Vimtag 361HD, Foscam C1, and Samsung SmartCam HD Pro. I set up the Ring video doorbell alongside some of the aforementioned cameras. Needless to say, I found the Ring to be most useful for my needs.I must say it now so you know, too. The Ring video doorbell was not designed to act as a security camera like the others. Comparing the features and quality of optics, etc. just doesn't make sense all that much. Sure, the optics could be better, but the Ring gets the job done. I can clearly identify people at my door.The number one feature that had me pick the Ring video doorbell over my other camera configurations is because it acted somewhat as an intercom between myself or wife and someone at the door. If someone goes to my door at night when I'm still at work and my wife is home alone with the kids, I can virtually answer the door. The person at the door has no clue if I'm home or not. On top of that, they know that I know what they look like and that there is a good chance they are being recorded.We've also had visitors meet at the house earlier than expected... I'll have to continue my review in a little while.

title: Changed rating from1 star to 3 as they are trying to fix bugs for me.
body: An item I WISH I could give good rating for. Started out "okay" but got progressively worse. Granted my DSL is not the fastest, so I didn't expect completely smooth "real-time" viewing, but firmware updates for LiveView, motion detection, door bell notification just got worse and worse. Now seeing black screen. Scheduled appointment with tech support last week. They cancelled 15 before start time. Rescheduled for last Tuesday. They cancelled. Finally got someone, spent a hour or so rebooting, reconfiguring, at the end, worse than before. Yesterday called and girl tried to walk me through "fix" but ended up crashing my firmware to previous version. Spent an hour or so today just trying to get newest firmware back - no luck. Now, nothing is working on it. Oh.. when I received it, some pins on back were slightly bent, but was told, no big deal (and it did work for a while) Great concept. Terrible execution. If you have fast connection should work better as far as recording, but device is suspect. I think they sent me a returned item and now too late to return. $200 paperweight. Some might have good luck with it but it's a roll of the dice. I think they over sold the item and their bandwidth isn't enough to support all the users. Still waiting for call back from tier 2 support.Okay, edit to add a few things. RING did finally contact me, several times, got firmware back and device working again. An important point is that you do need a decent DSL/broadband speed. ATT upgraded me from 1.5mbps to 3.0mbps last week. Improved the video a lot. Clearer, far less lag and appears to be more "real-time". Also, they sent me a new RING device to resolve any possible issue with bent pins. Again, it is a great concept, but there are still some bugs. They added a feature called LiveView, where you can at anytime open the video to see what is outside your door and that will stay on for about 10 minutes if you desire. HOWEVER, after installing the replacement unit and though I was upgraded to the latest version, the LiveView feature does not work. Was told today that there is a known issue with some customers on this (obviously I am one of those) Another bug is that when the motion detection goes off, the only alert I get is phone vibration. No audio notification working and thus far the tech support has not be able to guide me to make this work. Could be part of the issue that is affecting LiveView. So, it does work for the most part, but I am frustrated that the LiveView isn't working and I would really like to have an audio notification if there is motion detection. What's the point if it is later in the evening and I'm not carrying my phone and don't notice the vibration. It would be great to know that someone was hanging around my door. Hopefully they can resolve that for me. Saying all this, I still think it is a great concept, but you do need at least 3mbps and possibly may need to add a Wi-Fi extender for a better connection. Also, RING needs to get the bugs worked out. If I had LiveView working and an audio notification for motion detection I would give it 5 stars. As it is, they have tried to help me and contact me about once a day trying to get it working so I bumped it up from 1 star to 3. Again, it may be a roll of the dice for you, but if it works the way it is advertised, it is a nice item to have.

title: "This ticket will now be closed" William B,  Ring Community Support
body: I really wanted to like the RING but at this point it has been a complete let down.The overarching issue has been that it only recorded people as they were walking away. This became a problem in the past week when I was expecting a package. I had video of the mailman leaving but no video of him walking up and dropping anything off. The package was marked as delivered but nothing was there. There was also no recording of anyone coming after the mailman had delivered the package so I have no way of knowing if someone else retrieved it. After one frustrated chat session I ventured into another chat session which seemed a bit more productive. I was asked to check the wifi strength at the mounting location and again near the router. Everything checked out fine. I was walked through rebooting the device and updating the firmware and then tested the device notification by placing it in a different location to test motion. We adjusted the zones and set the notification frequency to frequent. After a few tests of walking up to the RING, this seemed to work and recordings/notifications were triggered. I also noted that perhaps my previous mounted location was too high and this could have contributed to the lack of detection. So, I relocated the RING, this time I hardwired it to where my doorbell used to be, I did this on Saturday. Today, I was sitting in my house at my computer when I heard the mail carrier arrive, park, walk up the steps to my door, and place mail in the mailbox which is right next to the ring. I received no alerts. Nothing was recorded. I then walked out the back of the house and around to the front and I managed to walk up, open the mailbox, retrieve the mail, and leave and again, no recording. I briefly attempted to chat about it again but the rep was asking me to once again take it off the mount and have it powercycle so it could be motion tested again with the zones and so forth but I just don't have time to go through that today. When I sent an email to RING outlining the above I received a response from William B. that said "Thank you for reaching out to us. I am sorry to hear that, it is our goal here at Ring to provide an exceptional overall experience for all of our customers.If there is anything that I can do to assist you in anyway please do not hesitate to contact me. This ticket will now be closed.Please feel free to reach out with any other questions, concerns, or comments.Best, William B Ring Community Support"this ticket will now be closed"??? I about died laughing except it's $200 of tears not counting the chime accessory.The whole point of having the RING was to be able to have some idea of movement on the porch and clearly this is not working.Update: Based on feedback from a comment to my original post I gave ring customer service a try via telephone and promptly reached someone. I laid out the sad tale including the most recent developments of no recording whatsover. The rep apologized for the experience I've had with the ring, processed a replacement, and said a prepaid label will be provided for me to return the current device and bracket. This was a no fuss exchange with the rep and a great relief. Changed my review from 2 to 3 stars and will update once I receive the replacement device.

title: Do your research before buying this product
body: In fairness, here is an update to my original review below: we had to order a new cable modem recently (thanks to badgering from my cable company about the old one being too slow and not supported anymore), and the faster speed of the modem seems to have solved the battery and lost connection issues. I just wish Ring had suggested this back in the fall when we were trying to figure out what was wrong. We might have been able to save the hassle of exchanging for a new one, if we had known it was the modem speed hindering us. I also fault Ring for not providing more specifics about what speeds of modems are acceptable. Our old one fell within their specs, so we didn't think that could be the problem.After I posted my original review, Ring customer support was very proactive about trying to help me solve my issues (as evidenced by the company's responses to the review). Through email, Ring put me in touch with a customer service rep who set up a time to call me. Unfortunately, the call was not at the scheduled time (the first time it was 15 min after the scheduled appointment, the second time it was 1 hour (!!!) after the set time). After the second time, I gave up with this method, since it didn't appear that my time was valued by the company. I appreciated their efforts, but I decided I would just try to solve the other issue (delayed motion detection) by myself...or as a last resort, I could call them instead.Original review (2 stars): I bought the Ring doorbell, because we don't have an easy way to see people at our door (no side window or peephole). However, I am strongly tempted to go back to our regular doorbell after all of the problems we have had with this. Just like other reviewers, we have the same problem that motion only engages once the person is walking away. Obviously, this is useless if we want to see the face of the person. There is also a seemingly long delay between the bell being pushed and the sound coming through on the phone or plug-in bell. The other two major problems are the battery life and loss of wifi connectivity. The wifi connection always reads as very good, but connection is lost at least once a week, meaning the doorbell has to be taken down and re-linked to the wifi. We often don't realize this until days have gone by with no motion or someone we are expecting tries the doorbell and has to text instead to let us know he/she is outside. Battery life is my biggest complaint. The Ring doorbell is marketed as having a 6-month battery life. Professional reviews said it would be more like 2 months, but we can barely get 2 weeks. We have limited motion/use of the doorbell, meaning we don't have a lot of activity at our door that would drain the battery. Motion settings are low too, reducing the drain. The first Ring we bought barely held a charge for a week. Ring exchanged it with another, after reviewing our records and agreeing we likely had a faulty battery. However, the second doorbell we received is no better, and it can hold a charge for maybe 8-10 days. But, with the constant loss of connectivity and battery drain, we are messing with the doorbell at least once a week. I don't recommend spending the money on this product.

title: Great concept, quality control issues, motion detection flawed, serious lag in calls
body: Update- After a few months of having it I can say that I am still having issues with the doorbell. I was previously having motion notification issues to which Ring sent me a new doorbell. The new doorbell has the same exact issue. It is my understanding that the motion detection is heat based. When you have large heat sources pass by it can give false readings. There is a setting where you tell Ring how far to "look" in terms of feet in front of the doorbell. I have mine set to 5 feet. I get notified anytime a car passes in front of the doorbell (the street is 35 feet away). Its frustrating to have the notification go off all day long.There is a serious lag in the call to your phone. I have NEVER been able to pick up a single call with it. The people are always walking away or getting in their vehicle by the time the app decides to let you accept the call.Also, the mounting bracket and doorbell need refinement. The mounting bracket has six hooks to which you hang the doorbell on once the bracket is secured to the wall. Those hooks are tiny and plastic. You then use proprietary screws to secure the unit to the bracket... If you screw down to hard which is easy, you will damage those hooks on the bottom. The doorbell itself is pretty large, its a little imposing from the street. I am sure they could fix these two nit picky items and make it a better product.I will keep it, but I really wish it was smaller, felt like it was securely mounted, had better motion detection and reduced the call lag.Update after two weeks of having it- I am having motion zone problems and I contacted customer service. They said a level 2 tech needed to help me and would call back in 48 hours. They did, we missed each other. He left a voicemail and email. Said to book a call back OR call him at a certain number anytime 9-5PM. I called back and they said I had to book the call, thus wasting more time. Its been REALLY tough to get this thing fixed and their customer support is frustrating me at this point. We shall see if they can fix it.A mixed bag so far.... Installation is rocky, product is not secure on the wall, slight lag in calls to the phone and motion detection is wonky.The doorbell function works fine. There is a little lag to the phone, but it works OK. You get a "call" on your phone to which you can accept or deny. Funny, you can't use it while on the phone. I believe it transmits over GSM. The motion detection settings in the app are pretty neat, very easy to use. Its just that they don't work. I have mine set to the absolute minimum, 5 ft of motion detection. I am getting alerts every time I drive out of my driveway (10 feet away) and when cars pass on the street (25 feet away). I called customer service and they want me to take the unit off the wall for trouble shooting, can't do that! It won't stay back on the wall (refer to comments above). Ugh.Aesthetically, its pretty large on the wall. I get whats all included inside the guts, but still, its large there by the door. I am almost certain I will keep it, but they have to fix the motion detection.

title: A BASICALLY GOOD PRODUCT LACKING THE FINISHING TOUCHES TO MAKE IT GREAT
body: So, I bought mine at Costco the other day. I try to buy most everything through Amazon. Our family has two Prime accounts, even. However, I had been hearing about the product on the radio, and there it was, at a good price, including a chime. In the kart. Done.I would have liked to give the product a "4", but this product needs both installation and setup.Therefore the instructions have got to be good, and the information on the web site has to be good as well.I am an engineer that does all sorts of fabrication in wood metal, plastic, etc., etc. I am essentially a master craftsman, so I'm not unfamiliar with tools and processes. I have also worked as a product engineer in a manufacturing environment, and I do expert testimony work, so I read and write well. Part of why this product has disappointed me.Here's my gripes:1. I had to re-setup the device after installation. The advice on line says you don't have to uninstall it to do so. WRONG. You must hit the setuo button on the back to enter setup. This means pulling it off the wall.2. This is related to No. 1. There are two screws that secure the unit on its backing plate. The installation instructions ONY MENTION THIS AS A VERY LAST STEP. They show the screws backed out in the literature. Mine were mostly screwed in. The mounting instructions state that you push in firmly and slide down. This is very hard to do with the screws already screwed in. The issue is what does "firmly" mean. I actually got it installed on the backing plate, but then couldn't remove it to repeat setup. The instructions should have stated somewhere - in the beginning - that the little screws on the bottom must be turned out for installation and removal.3. I went to a lot of trouble to use my existing wiring. I measured 20 VAC (yes, I have a multimeter). This meets their specs. The white light glows, but the unit insists it's on battery. WTF!
