This apparently will ultimately affect whether I can eventually use the feature which is being released that allows you to activate the video feature at any time - which I was surprised to find was not already in place.4. My unit is the Ring Doorbell. However the instructions for setup say to "select Video Doorbell Pro" during setup. Which is it? My unit does not look like the "Pro" as shown on the web site. FUBAR! (a technical term)5. Cheap fasteners. Come on guys, 3 cents more to buy decent screws?6. Power supply. The advertising says you can run it on battery - or use your existing wiring. Only when you get into the manual does it explain that is must be AC and not DC power. This stuff HAS TO BE stated up front.As I learned in business school, there is much more to a product than the product itself. How you distribute, how you package, and the supporting documentation is as much a part of the product as the hardware.Again, I'd like to rate this product higher, but the advertising is a bit too far ahead of the complete product.

title: Not Quite What The Ads Show You. Close... but not quite.
body: I want to give this a 5 star rating, but the Ring doorbell is still lacking in several areas of which I will echo many of the reviews already.1) Connectivity - it's assumed that when purchasing the device that your router will provide a sufficient amount of coverage from inside of the house to the outside by your front door. I have an Apple Extreme router located on my second floor that pushes up to 170Mbps all along that second floor when using the speedtest app. As soon as I go upstairs to the outside roof deck or downstairs to the main living/kitchen area and office I lose about half of that speed. As soon as I step out onto my front door and close the door... I lose nearly all coverage. Not saying that this is Ring's fault, but I'm stressing the point that before you purchase this device, ensure you have a strong wireless connection from the inside of your home to the outside front door. I ended up buying an Apple Airport Express to extend the coverage outside.2) Delays - Trying to keep this as short as possible... some people will walk up to the door (no less than 2 feet from the Ring doorbell) and the device will not consistently notify me. Sometimes it will... sometimes it won't. Some people will hit the Ring button and sometimes it will notify me, other times it won't at all. Let's say in those instances where the Ring does register motion or the fact that someone hit the Ring button I will receive a notification either right away or sometimes I will wait up to 5 seconds. Maybe even 8 seconds or more, but those instances are rare beyond 5 seconds. Then when those notifications come to my Apple iPhone 6, I have to navigate through the various prompts to finally get the camera to open up and let me view who is at the front door. That can take about 5-10 seconds easily for the video to even come up. And when it does for motion only... I'll be lucky if I am viewing video of the person's face. More times than not it's the back of the person because the delay took so long. And when it connects as a result of someone hitting the ring doorbell, I'm pretty sure that individual has been standing there for at least 10 seconds before I can even start talking to them. NOTE: I have adjusted the motion settings numerous times to close to 30 feet to as little as 5 feet, but the delays are still there.Quality - with regards to Video and Audio in "real time" the video quality is pixelated and not quite 720P from my end. Again, I have good coverage out there from the Extender. The Audio is pretty clear when it actually works. With regards to Audio and Video when viewing saved videos from the Cloud (under Recent Activity) the video will display first and then the audio can take up to 5-8 seconds before that comes on. Very rare do both come on in sync right away.I'm hopeful that with these types of review and feedback that Ring will continue to make improvements to these existing devices through firmware or software updates and NOT from putting out a whole new device that consumers will have to buy to see actual performance enhancements.

title: It works most of the time. Monthly subscription is a must
body: I've been using my Ring for over three weeks now and managed to sort out most of the issues.My ring is connected to a 16.5 vac power transformer as I don't have a doorbell.There are many reviews that cover most of the issue, however, there are few things to note:1. In order to connect the Ring directly to a transformer you need to add a resistor. Please read the instructions on Ring's website.2. Even when connected to power it still works off battery. The power trickle charges the doorbell. This means that (a) if you use it a lot during the day, the charge may be slower than needed and you will end up with a need to take it for an actual recharge using the micro USB cable. The instructions fail to tell you that before you install it you must fully charge it even if connecting to power. Mine lost charge after couple of days while connected because I didn't charge it before installation. If it misbehaves it may be due to low battery so check the level even if the device is connected by wire. (b) other than the ability to use live view, all the disadvantages of battery operations are still there - the device is sleeping most of the time so it needs to wake up when it senses events, making the notifications slower than they can be.2. Motion detection is a pain to set. I spent a lot of time during the past couple of weeks setting the motion accuracy until I managed to reduce the notifications for passing cars on my street to minimum. I've set the distance to half the maximum and it still picks some cars passing by. On the other hand, it will be late to detect and record people and cars coming into the driveway, I assume because it is easier for it to detect objects crossing it's path rather than object coming towards it (which is the case in my situation). It still detects a movement lasting more than few seconds and records a short part of it to let you know what happens but you will miss events.3. It is almost impossible to answer a motion alert quick enough to catch the action, unless the activity lasts for a very long period. This means that motion detection is useless without the monthly subscription that will allow you to see what happened. Please keep this in mind when purchasing.4. Not often, the doorbell won't ring at all when someone presses the button. It works 90% of the time, but the 10% it doesn't is annoying.5. Some of the recordings turn out to be black screen and many times they start static as a picture rather than video. Not sure why, I have a very good connection the to device (more than 50mbs with high priority set on my router).5. Support is very responsive though not always very knowledgeable. Do your online research as well if you are having issues.I've tested few other devices and so far, Ring is the best performing device. It may work better for some than others due to the angle of placement but for what it does and if you can live with it's shortcomings, it is a very cool and useful device.I bought the Ring Pro and will try to see if it works better. If so I will update my review.

title: Better now but still Underwhelmed
body: UPDATE 11/28/2019I finally figured out that part of my spotty experience - black video, no video, unable to view Live video was due to weak wifi. The signal at the doorbell seemed strong enough for other devices but the Ring needed a little more. Now that is all working better; I can always get live view or view videos.It still misses MANY motion activations that it should get, like someone walking right up to it, like the mail carrier or other delivery, etc. And it takes so long to activate, people are usually there and gone before video starts. Online and on TV I see a lot of videos of porch pirates approaching, grabbing packages, and walking away. All I get is the UPS guy's back when he is almost back to his truck.I finally set the motion to pretty much minimum, ie, that the street is 5-15 feet away when it is actually closer to 30. It no longer activates for every vehicle that drives by, but it still does it 2-3 times per day. Setting the sensitivity so close probably affects its ability to detect people approaching. Still, you would think it would detect the mail carrier 2 feet away, DIRECTLY in front of it for several seconds every day.I would love to try either an updated version or higher version, but none seem to get any better reviews overall. Even among more expensive, supposedly better brands, the reviews only seem to run about average. I figure I might as well stick with this one.ORIGINAL REVIEW:Others have reported similar experience to mine so I will be brief.PROS:Setup was pretty simple. I appreciate the fact that the doorbell lets you know when it needs to be charged.CONS:Motion detection sensitivity is a joke. As far as I have been able to tell you have a choice of sensing every vehicle that drives down the street or just big trucks or fast cars. A lot of days it does not even detect the mail carrier who has to walk right up to it. I would say more often than not, it fails to detect my wife or me entering the house, and anyone entering the house or leaving despite the fact that you practically brush it with your shoulder.The motion detector setting in the app is a bit of a joke, too. You use a little slide scale that is difficult to get to move, and you only have a graphic suggestion of distance so you don't really know what the setting is.Motion detection is way too slow to respond. Even when it does respond to someone like a UPS person, you get a video of them walking away.Live View is hit or miss. Occasionally I have actually seen the live image, but for a few days now it has shown nothing, ie, nothing happens when you tap the Live View play icon. It might start working again tomorrow. Who knows?I did not have an opportunity to install mine until a couple of months after I purchased it. Within a couple of weeks I realized it was pretty useless, but it was obviously too late to return to Amazon.The simple doorbell I had before was at least good at it's job.

title: Long term review: I recommend the Ring Doorbell 2 over this model
body: My wife and I bought our home in 2015. At the time, this model Ring Doorbell was the only one on the market that did not require a hardwire doorbell behind it to function. Ring's technology has proven reliable and we are pleased with both the performance and the pricing model of their cloud recording service. (Most other services charge per device; Ring's fee structure makes owning multiple cameras more economical) Because of this, we are loyal customers and use their doorbells and security cameras to keep an eye on our property.All this being said, unless your intent is to hardwire this particular doorbell, I strongly recommend the Ring Doorbell 2 over this model. The newer Ring Doorbell 2 has a better camera, but the real closer is THE BATTERY. The original Ring doorbell's battery is NOT USER SERVICEABLE. When it wears out, it's done. You know how your smartphone battery holds less and less charge as it ages to the point where you're constantly recharging it? The only way to fix it is to buy a new smartphone or pay a service to replace the battery. RING DOES NOT OFFER A BATTERY REPLACEMENT SERVICE. I've searched online trying to find the specific battery they use so I can swap it myself, but I can't find it. I've called Ring support about this problem and was told that once the unit is out of warranty, they won't do anything to support the device. I actually added their solar charger to extend my battery life. This was marginally effective, but my battery was already quite worn by then so it wasn't very helpful. I'm now at the point where my doorbell will simply not hold ANY charge. My options at this point are:1. Run a hardwire with a power adapter2. Buy another Ring doorbellIf I had known that I'd have to replace the unit after only four years, I'm sure I would have waited for the second generation unit. Or perhaps buy the original doorbell, but make the effort to run a hardline. With the exception of the battery, this is a fine product and I happily recommend Ring's doorbells and security cameras.A few other notes to consider before you purchase any Ring doorbell: - If you have a hardwire doorbell you plan to use in conjunction with your Ring doorbell, do some research. Some older doorbell systems do not provide the necessary voltage to power Ring's doorbells, which is why you see some negative reviews of this product. You may need some additional parts to step up the voltage to properly power your Ring (or any other brand) doorbell.- A strong, long range WiFi network is required for a reliable video connection. If you have a large home, you should have multiple access points or a mesh network like Eero or Google WiFi to be sure your doorbell can reliably reach the Internet. These use the 802.11ac standard, which is backward compatible with 802.11n devices. (Ring's current line uses Wireless N) If you're not network savvy, Ring's Chime Pro has a network extension feature to make sure the doorbell has a strong WiFi connection.I hope you found this helpful.

title: Installation was easy but shortly after getting it set up
body: Edited 11/21/16Ring gave me my money back after months of trying to get this to work without success so 3 stars for the eventual support that I received (After leaving the original review). 1 star for the product itself and the months of trying to get it to work wtih every level of tech support at the company.Thank you for the refund!------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------I was really excited about this product. Installation was easy but shortly after getting it set up, I realized that notifications were extremely delayed. On average, I get a notification to my phone 35-50 seconds after the button was pushed. By the time I either get to the door or open the app, the people are long gone. Thinking that it was something to do with the app, I deleted and reinstalled it only to be met with the same issue. I ran the obvious tests... Network speed tests at the doorbell (9mbps up), speed tests on my cellular network (25mbps down). I Even moved the doorbell inside the house to be closer to the router even though it was only 4 feet away from the router when it is outside. Literally nothing worked. Notifications were still delayed in the area of 35 seconds.At this point, I contacted tech support who had me do the speed tests, network tests, and were added as a user on my account. Somehow they were getting the notifications quicker than I was but couldn't explain why (I am using an Iphone 6S+ so it is a mainstream phone). They passed me along to level 2 tech support and it was much of the same. The following things were done:- New router- Redownloaded app- Speed Tests- Connection sharing- Went to Tmobile to get phone network settings adjusted- Several things on "their end"All in all, I have spent close to 10 hours working on this doorbell between the back and forth emails, going to get network settings changed, and phone calls. I am still not able to get a notification in less than 30 seconds.Here is the crazy part.... even though I started this process right after I purchased the doorbell, it has been such a long time that I can no longer return it to Best Buy (I wish I bought it from Amazon). It has been over 2 MONTHS of dealing with tech support to resolve the issue. I have requested to be refunded from Ring and they told me that there is nothing that they could do. If they had just told me from the start that this thing wasn't going to work, I would have brought it back but I was assured that the issue would be resolved. Here I am over 2 moths later with a $200 worthless doorbell along with a $30 door chime. All in all, I am out around $250 for something that is now sitting in a drawer and I am back to using my $25 honeywell wireless doorbell.Complete waste of time and money. Please... do yourself a favor and do not buy this. While tech support has been friendly, they aren't able to resolve issues and have no authority to issue replacements or refunds.

title: DO NOT BUY -- CRITICAL FLAW ** UPDATE - FROM 1 TO 3 STARS **
body: DO NOT BUY THIS MODEL!!! I purchased the original battery-powered Ring Video Doorbell at Costco, but found out after installing it per instructions that it sporadically quits and has to be reset by physically taking it off the mounting plate and pressing the internal orange button to reconnect to Wi-Fi. It does NOT reconnect itself automatically to the Wi-Fi network, like just about every other Wi-Fi device on the market. All the other models, including the Chime Pro, remote Chimes, and Video Doorbell 2, DO reconnect automatically. This seems to have been a basic design flaw that invalidates the entire reason one buys such a device.What happens if you're on vacation, there is a power outage, and you cannot be there physically to take the unit off the wall and follow the button-based reset procedure? You're out of luck. The Video Doorbell goes down and never comes back up, leaving your home unmonitored and unprotected. From chatting with their help desk, there is apparently no plan to upgrade firmware to allow you to reset the network connection remotely, which would have been the obvious fix. They do NOT mention this critical flaw on the website, nor do they offer any upgrade path to the Video Doorbell 2, which DOES reconnect automatically and therefore rectifies this glaring (and unacknowledged) shortcoming. I think this is shortsighted, customer-unfriendly company policy. The original Video Doorbell is seriously defective, and in a way that negates the entire reason for buying it. DO NOT BUY. If you still want to deal with Ring, go for the model 2 instead.-------------------------------------------------------Update 8/16/17: I am upgrading this review to 3 stars due to the exceptional customer service at Ring. After explaining the problem via Chat, I received a helpful and useful response from the Ring rep. They quickly sent a replacement unit, this time a Video Doorbell 2 (which fixes the Wi-Fi reconnect problem). However, the first replacement they sent had an issue with the setup, so we had to contact them again. To their credit they immediately sent a second Doorbell 2 unit that arrived soon after. This one installed perfectly without any issues -- exactly as described on their instructional video. They even provided prepaid postage to return the defective Doorbell 1 and 2. The new doorbell connected instantly to our Chime Pro unit, and all functions seem to be working well so far.I upgraded this review from 1 to 3 stars due to Ring's exceptional customer service on two separate occasions, which addressed our needs without much fuss or having to do much on our part. They did what every good company does: assume the customer is right, and seek to provide a remedy asap. I could not go higher than 3 because the Video Doorbell version 1 is not a good design. Go for the Video Doorbell 2 instead, whose only shortcoming for us at this point is the lack of the optional gold cover plate that Doorbell version 1 offered.

title: Cool but three problems
body: First off, the Ring is very cool. I really like it and many people that come to my front door (salespeople) know what it is immediately and don't bother me. There are three problems however, one very serious (in my opinion).1 - The motion function engages when people are walking away, not when they walk up. MANY of my videos are of people's backsides. I don't know if this is a software issue or what. Tried increasing the sensitivity to the point where I was grabbing cars driving down the street but still only got people leaving my front door. Weird.2 - This kind of goes along with #2. You cannot access the doorbell with security software like Blue Iris,et.al. Now, I get that they want to sell you their Cloud service and I'm ok with $30 / year to have an off-site backup. But if you could link the doorbell into your security system you would have an actual video of everything in your possession and you could access it from any computer in the world. That would be REALLY handy for police investigations and any legal hassles that may come your way.3 - If you do not answer the alert fast enough, you are locked out-- even though someone is standing at your front door. You cannot "turn on" the camera from your phone or tablet. If someone is there and you do not have an "ACCEPT" button on the screen, there is just no way to see or talk to them. This is obviously a software flaw. You should always be able to "wake" a camera remotely and talk to a person at your front door. Or maybe you just want to see the view outside your front door. You should be able to access the camera / microphone and enjoy your front yard.Hopefully these issues will be addressed in the future.Update 1/24/16 - I have found a fantastic use for the Ring Doorbell and Chime. My mother-in-law, now in her 90s, lives by herself. She has become hard-of-hearing and often does not hear the doorbell. By installing the Ring on her door, she now is bothered much less (solicitors tend to recognize it and don't seem to like it) and when someone rings the bell, the Chime, placed by her favorite chair, gives her a better chance of hearing someone at the door.I think the Chime would be MUCH improved if it had an ultra-brite LED on top (that you could turn on or off) that would flash when the bell rings. In addition, I REALLY like that I now know when people come to her door and I can talk to them before she answers the door. It gives me a much stronger sense of security for her.Update 3/24/16 - Ring has added “Live View” to their feature set. It allows you to access your Ring Doorbell any time you wish. This solves the problem with #3 as long as you are not on battery power. Obviously, it also lets you “dial in” any time you wish to check out your front or back yard (where ever your doorbell is). This is a feature that I like as every once in a while I just like to see if its raining or foggy at the house.

title: Fun, but Alexa integration is absolutely useless
body: This review is half negative, half positive. Negatives first:I purchased this for my small office on Prime Day along with an Echo Show because ring supposedly "integrates" with Alexa. The only integration is that when your Chime or your phone alerts you that someone's at the doorbell, you can ask Alexa to show the camera. Alexa doesn't tell you that there's movement on the camera or that someone's rung the bell; it will ring/alert on your smartphone app and/or a Ring Chime if you've purchased one, but then you have to ask Alexa to show you the camera (Echo Show). If you DO ask her to bring up the camera, you still can't talk to the person at the door or allow them to talk to you. I'm not sure I'll ever use that function, but it would be nice to have. Tapping the alert on your smartphone automatically brings up the doorbell camera, including the speaker, so the Alexa integration with this product is pointless.Further, after your free trial, it only records if you purchase a plan through them. You can still view the camera live, but you can't see video of anyone who rang the doorbell while you were away or didn't have alerts turned on. Plans are cheap - I personally only need the basic plan, which is $3/month or $30/year, and the professional plan is only $10/month or $100/year - but comparing it with other services (such as Arlo, which I also use), it's not something that's usually required.For the positives:As far as the product WITHOUT Alexa, it's nifty. You use your smartphone to be alerted to motion and/or when someone rings the doorbell, and there's an optional "chime" device that's required if you want it to ring elsewhere than your phone. Do not get this if you expect it to ring through Alexa. If you just need a doorbell camera that can be accessed remotely, my experience so far is that this is a pretty decent device.It can be wired into your current wired doorbell, or it can be used wirelessly. Our wiring is pretty scary, so I've had it on WiFi. I've been testing it by keeping it on and recording almost constantly since the day it arrived, and the battery is at 50%. I count that as a positive, because not only was it not fully charged when I installed it, my only comparison is a set of Arlo security cameras, which I've had to charge as often as every two days if there's been high traffic. Installation was easy, but looks like it could be more difficult if you're installing into brick or concrete.Overall, the statement that it "integrates" with Alexa is TECHNICALLY true but absolutely pointless. It works well with my iPhone 8, and I just ordered a Chime, which I'm excited to try out. I was extremely upset when I first figured out that it didn't work the way the description and reviews seemed to indicate, but it's grown on me and been very reliable so far.

title: Good idea but it needs more work.
body: I'm giving this a 3 star "its ok" rating but was toying with rating it as 2 star, "I don't like it". I really think this is a good idea but its not ready for prime time. My ring came with what must have been a few months of free cloud storage. I had become used to being able to review front door activity a few minutes or hours after the fact and just didn't understand that privilege was temporary. Today (after recharging the ring battery for the 3rd time in 3 months), I received a chime on my phone and was alerted to someone coming to my home. A few minutes later, I went to review the video and was surprised to find a new message telling me I no longer had access to video and that I needed to update my account. After further investigation, I find I must pay $3 a month to see any video the bell has recorded. Seriously? Cant you leave a video up for 24 hours for free and then delete it, how about 8 hours free? This thing is useless unless you pay for the cloud storage. Its just a noise maker with no information.My second complaint is the issue with the battery. I've had to find the special tiny screwdriver that comes with the unit, unscrew it, take it off the wall, into the house, hook to the computer, charge it, back outside, find the special tiny screwdriver that comes with the unit, re-install it onto the wall, then carefully put the tiny screwdriver back in its place, (I live in fear of losing the special tiny screwdriver) 3 times in 3 months. It doesn't even give you a warning when the battery is going to die, it just stops making the noise (the noise that gives you no further information or video unless you pay $3 a month). Yes, if you constantly monitor your app, you can check the health of the device and see the battery usage there, but I have a life, and constantly monitoring my doorbell seems to defeat the purpose of having an electronic unit that you can forget about until it alerts you to an event. Now I am constantly monitoring the system that is supposed to be constantly monitoring for me. Finally, this thing can be slow to send the video to you even when the event has just happened, I often get a warning noise or chime and I instantly try to view the activity, but I cannot see who is at the door until they have already left the package and walked away. Its a good thing no one has tried to steal a package yet, I would never catch them at it.In conclusion, its slow to record the video so you may or may not see it in real time, it wont let you see the "past tense" video it records even 10 minutes after the fact unless you pay for cloud storage, and the unit goes dead with no warning because the battery has to be uninstalled and charged every 30 days.If it weren't too late I would probably send it back. 3 stars might even be too much.

title: Glorified door bell
body: Got it from Amazon warehouse deal so saved 40% off new - good thing because it's not worth the full price (in my opinion). First thing you need to do is download the Ring app to your supported device and follow the simple instructions that comes with the unit. Wait half an hour for the firmware to update (that's if your internet connection is good enough). By the way, if the wifi test shows the signal is too weak at your installation location, return the unit, it'll do you no good. Best if you use "wired" connection using your existing doorbell. That way the unit battery continuously charges (very slowly) and have to worry less about the unit battery discharging too quickly. Recharging the battery is a hassle if not "wired" as the main unit need to be removed and connected to a power source using the included usb cable (a poor design). Wall mount the real panel using the supplied screws and the screw driver taking care to not overtighten as to warp the panel. This is very important as slight warp will prevent good electrical contact with the main unit and the "wired connection" will not be recognized ( problem I sorted thru). Camera is, at best, adequate with a round fish eye view of the world and poor resolution. Furthermore, the view is too wide, you're standing 4 feet from the camera and it shows the whole body so the most important part, the FACE, is TINY. It's not like I need a camera that takes in the whole front yard and the street, dahh..., you would think the manufacturer would be a lot smarter than this. As far as I can see, setting zones does very little in the overall view area. The motion sensor and video capture timeframe is slow and all the negative comments about video coming on too late is legit. Video is jerky with ghosting problems associated with slow connection to the internet. What would really make it much more useful is a snapshot feature where it captures photo shots at 10 second increments. This will amount to about four snaps per incident and Ring can store that in their cloud storage and offer it for free up to 7 days. Otherwise, after the trial period, you get NO video storage at all. Whether this product can serve as a security camera is highly debatable and my assessment is a big NO. It serves as a method of seeing who's at the door and telling sales or religious zealots to scram. Want real security features - get a decent security camera that doesn't cost a whole lot more. At least Ring now offers a one year complimentary video storage and notification service so I don't immediately have to decide whether subscription is worth it. I'll probably bundle this with the Ring home security system I'm getting then it'll be part of the $10 monthly plan. As long as you keep your expectations modest, this product is ok, just ok.

title: Takes a bit to get dialed-in, but worth it. (Updated after
body: After researching these devices ad-infinatum I purchased the Ring product. Almost caught the $160 (+ no tax) black friday sale price here on Amazon, but I snoozed and ended up buying at Best Buy for $179.Fortunately my wifi router has close proximity of the front door where it's installed (within 5 ft.). I also have the inside traditional doorbell unit (analog ding dong), and 12v AC button available at the front door. I had to dis-install this system first ---> [[ASIN:B000TY15G0 Swann SW244-BVD DIY BandW Video Doorphone]]], which never really worked correctly and was a pain to take out. Note that this Ring non-Pro version can take advantage of a lower voltage coming from a typical doorbell transformer (10-12v) than the "Pro" version (needs 16-25v).After the above dis-install, the time it took to install the Ring was about 45 minutes. Initially there was a problem with the iPhone (4S) App not recognizing the unit after syncing with my wifi. I had to reboot the iPhone a few times and eventually the Ring App did find the new doorbell. All seems to be working well at this point. It does take a few experimental trials in getting the motion activity settings dialed-in within the app. I have a fairly isolated front door not facing the front street, so for me it's working well. Funny... I'm only getting false motion notifications when large trash trucks roll down the street.All in all it's functioning as per my expectations and as reviewed here. For me after a front door button push there is the typical video delay other purchasers here have noted (6-10 seconds), but OK for my purposes. About the time a visitor is going in for a 2nd button push I'm speaking with them via the iPhone and unit.Interesting outcome... I have a grassy HOA access area in front of my townhome and always wondered who was allowing their dogs to relieve themselves and not cleaning up after. Now that I have video evidence I can confront the offending neighbor directly without making an accusation. That in itself is worth the price of the product.Update July 2017: Reduced to 3 stars. After several months of use I've found several annoying attributes... my close wifi (60MPS) is handled slow and inconsistent. If someone rings the bell it can take 2 minutes for a notice to come through on my iPhone. Most of the time the person just leaves before I can respond. The custom motion zones do not work, even when dialed down to the least sensitive setting. I'm still getting motion notices from trucks on my street. With the iPhone notifications, after deleting events there is a blinking vestige of the last one deleted, i.e., buggy.

title: I expect more refinement in performance for this kind of money
body: Update 2/26/16: I had a hard time reaching tech support but a guy, "Dierre", called me back and he provided EXCELLENT tech support. My unit is defective so they're sending me a replacement. He could see on his end that it was throwing tons of errors, and he had me try a factory reset but nothing happened which he suspected, but that confirmed that it's defective. I'll update once I've got the new one installed and tested. P.S. This time the battery charge was down to 25% just 3 days after I'd charged it.°°°°°°°°°°°°°°°°°°°°°°°°°So far I'm "meh" on the Ring, for several reasons. Firstly, i get a motion activation AFTER most activity at my door area is done - never, ever have i seen anyone walking up, aka from the front. If it's a delivery and it takes a few more seconds i only see the guy getting up from leaving a package and then walking away - I've never seen a face. What good is that if someone is rushing up and stealing packages? (It's of no use to me whatsoever..maybe someone who likes to watch backsides would really like it.)There is ALWAYS quite a delay between movement and the camera kicking in - i had people leaving my house, which takes a few seconds because i have to be careful that my cat isn't near the door, and the recording only caught their backs. The delay is simply unacceptable.The view when someone rings the bell is great, though. But you do have to have your phone immediately accessible, or as another reviewer wrote, you're SOL (paraphrasing) because there is little time allowed and you can't turn the camera/mic on at will/when needed/if it times out.Mine has a defect in that no light spins - in fact, the only time any light is on is when it's charging, which i had to do after only about a month, perhaps because my neighbor's new roommate comes and goes like 25 times a day and I'm in a townhouse so we use the same sidewalk. I've never seen the roommate, neither front nor back, he's already in the house or walking down the street by the time the camera comes on. i can't set the motion sensor farther or there would be no point in using it at all. All the incessant alerts for this invisible man coming and going are like some really bad musical.I might just turn the motion sensor off until a software upgrade is made, though it's annoying to have spent so much money and not have that feature. Other than the actual person-ringing- the-bell part it's pretty annoying to deal with anyway, and should be considered a beta version.I bought mine directly from the Ring website, my homeowner's insurance was giving something line a $30 discount.

title: Required a wifi extender
body: I got the Ring Doorbell after two of my friends told me how much they liked theirs. Everything about it is well done. It was easy to install where my old doorbell button was. The installation is slightly different if you have an existing “ding dong” bell or a bell that plays a melody like Westminster Chimes. The Ring Doorbell has a battery and also will run off of the power in your previous doorbell switch if you have one. So I don’t have to bother with recharging the battery periodically. However, during the initial setup, you might need to charge the battery a little so that you can get it connected to your wifi network before putting it on the wall.The Ring app is available for Apple, Windows, and Android devices. I found that the Android phone app was easier to use for setup than the Windows app. I needed to know the password for my wifi network.At first my Ring Doorbell worked fine. Then I stopped getting the picture for “live view.” I tried moving the wifi access point a couple of feet, but that was all I could move it due to the length of the cables. In the Windows ring app, you can select the device, click on the gear icon, and click on “Health.” It will tell you the strength of the wifi signal at your Ring Doorbell, in RSSI. I was getting about -70, which is poor. I bought a Chime Pro, which is a wifi extender plus sounds chimes and does other things. It is not very good as a wifi extender, and did not give me a picture in “live view.” I called Ring. The tech I talked to was real nice and knew what she was doing. After I gave her my email address she checked the signal strength and said that the Doorbell signal strength was too poor. She recommended that I get a more powerful wifi extender than the Chime Pro. So I bought a Linksys AC750 for fifty bucks. It was a bit of a chore to get connected, but when done everything worked. In the Windows app, there is not an option for connecting the doorbell to a different wifi network. I removed the doorbell from the wall, pressed the orange reset button long enough to reset, and used the phone app to setup with the new network.The Amazon page says that installation takes five minutes. Maybe that could be done if you had done a couple of them before and knew exactly what you are doing, but better plan on 20-30 minutes, assuming you don’t have signal strength issues like I did. I spent several hours, trying to get it to work, researching how to get better signal strength, going to the store twice, and setting up the Linksys extender.So after a little frustration and additional expense, it is working well.

title: Decent option for the price, but temper expectations.
body: [[VIDEOID:e93e13e37ced044d7075bf80be908152]] After having this doorbell installed for about five months, it finally recorded its first crime, revealing the true strengths and weaknesses of the device - at least as far as my purpose for it is concerned.TL;dr ... alerts to most motion, but starts recording late and does not record much detail during movement.Pros:The device generally does as advertised. It records video upon detecting motion and gives you the option of communicating with someone who rings the bell. It is nice looking and does not stand out too much next to the door. It is secured by a couple of screws, deterring theft, and is warranted against theft anyhow. The battery holds a charge pretty well. I've had to charge it once so far. The cloud recording service is relatively cost-effective at about $30/year, and I find that the peace of mind is well worth it. Whether for house-sitters, package delivery, or unexpected visitors, I like knowing who has been coming and going at my home.Cons:The sensor has two major flaws and a few minor ones.1. It detects motion late, no matter how much I adjust the motion sensor zones, which means video starts recording late. Many times I get video of people walking away from the door, or get an alert well after someone has rung the bell.2. The video/image quality falls dramatically when there is actual movement, especially quick movement, being recorded. Someone ran up to my door and stole a package. Because they were thoughtful enough to commit the crime quickly, the video picked up little detail of their face. This is frustrating. (I've posted that video below to demonstrate the problem.)Minor flaws: Because of the shape of my covered entryway, and the north-facing direction, the lighting tricks the sensor into "seeing" motion when it is not there; the exposure is skewed because of the same lighting issue - the sun is so bright near the driveway, that the area at the door seems dark in comparison, darkening the image and limiting detail; installation is a little troublesome with stucco, as drilling is required; and it does not integrate into any other "smart home" systems.Overall, I am happy with my purchase. I wish the above-mentioned major flaws were fixed because I could get much more benefit from the system, but it is better than other similar options I have researched and tried. Just don't trust the promotional images or videos Ring uses - they are far from accurate in everyday practical use.

title: Motion sensor sucks
body: I wanted both a doorbell and a camera because of package deliveries and previous garden flower theft right outside my front door. Even with all the bad reviews, I figured I'd give it a try since there weren't many options that didn't need wiring and had cloud storage. I have not been impressed.We rent a townhouse, so we don't have an option for doorbell wiring and can't change that. I set the motion sensors low to avoid getting all of the neighbors coming and going. Basically, I set it just to cover my front steps and my hydrangea (which had all of its flowers chopped off earlier this year while I was at work). Even with lower settings, it still alerts me to people walking way outside the range I have set. I have learned that our flower-stealing neighbors on one side come and go at least a dozen times a day. I figured it was worth it if the doorbell also caught people coming to our door. It does not. I can see my neighbor returning to and leaving his apartment, but I only see delivery people leaving my front step. It even caught someone standing on my front step facing the horrible neighbor's, but not his face because it only turned on after this person was already standing there. It does pick up us returning home, so I guess if someone tried to break in, we would have them on camera.The doorbell itself does work fine, when people use it. I have it connected to Alexa, so every room with Alexa gets a notification when someone rings the bell. The dog hasn't linked this together (yet), so that is an added bonus since it gives me a few extra seconds before she realizes there's a stranger. Unfortunately, it seems that people where I live are too stupid to press a doorbell, aside from some pizza delivery drivers. I had hoped package delivery people would ring the bell rather than drop/toss packages and leave without a single knock. They have not, and the video doesn't kick in in time to even see this happen. I also hate that there isn't a notification on the app that the doorbell battery is low. I get notifications probably 20 times a day for movement, but nothing when the battery was critical. I had turned off motion notifications, so we then went a few days before realizing the battery was dead simply because I didn't even think about it (a pizza delivery person told us).I do like having the videos stored on the cloud and having a doorbell for those intelligent enough to use it. I just wish the video sensor worked better and that it would let me know when the battery is dying.

title: Ring worth it, but less than perfect.
body: I found this product to be very useful and decided to keep it, although in some tough environments it does not perform very well.Here are some of the challenges I run into:1.- Installation is easy unless you are trying to drill into old tough brick. In this case you will need a hammer drill and a good masonry bit, which the one included with the package is not.2.- Heavy truck traffic will be detected by the motion detector. The ring was set to detect only at 5' distance, but kept being fooled by trucks going by my street, about 30' from the device. I tried many options and nothing worked. The situation was worsened by ongoing street work during this past month, as the gas line under the pavement was being replaced. I had to disarm the motion detection alerts as the great majority were false alarms.3.- The device misses people coming to the door sometimes. I think I have the cause narrowed down to the late afternoon sun hitting the ring and messing with its heat detection algorithm.On the plus side:1.- When somebody comes to your door and rings the ring bell, you are able to talk and see them without opening the door. This was important for us as we have a new solid door and did not want to make a peephole in it.2.- Every motion detected get logged to the web even with the alerts disabled, so if you are expecting a package you can check whether it was delivered or not. You could also get a picture of whomever it was that made out with your TV and other valuables. :-)3.- I went on a trip across the country and saw the mailman coming up the steps to my door and looking around. This was a surprise as I had the mail held during my absence, so I had to fire a complaint to the Post Office.4.- Still across the country, I was surprised by an alert that somebody had rung the "bell". I was able to answer many miles away and learned that the gas company wanted to reconnect my gas that day. I was able to tell them that I wasn't there and to come back in a couple of days. This was important as I wouldn't want the gas company to leave my house disconnected, as the only one on the street.5.- I found their support to be responsive. They solved all the problems they could and told me promptly when the problem was beyond the device capabilities.Overall, I found the device worth the money plus what I had to spend on a hammer drill, even though its performance was less than perfect in my environment. Others will probably like it even better.

title: Need a strong wi-fi signal or pixel city
body: I recently purchased this item hoping that it was a fix for all of our front door security needs. The reality is that it works quite well as a doorbell but the motion sensor and battery life leave much to be desired. This thing is a snap to install. I had it up and running inside of 10 minutes and that includes drilling the masonry for the mount. My wireless router is about 10 ft away from the doorbell and we seem to get enough bandwidth for a very clear picture day or night. When someone rings the doorbell the wide angle lens gives us an excellent view and the voice communication works quite well. The app is easy to use (except for motion detection protocols) and I have now answered my door from out of state (very cool).The downside of this device is the motion sensor and how frequently false alarms are logged. Even if I toggle off motion detection alerts all together the device still senses the motion and records it to the cloud. I live 40 ft from a busy city street and even when set to the lowest detection threshold the device still logs between 10 and 20 false detections per day. I normally wouldn't mind but I am using the rechargeable battery to power the Ring and these alerts are taking battery life from 10 months (as stated by the manufacturer) to 1.5 months (estimated since I haven't completed the cycle). I have had this device hooked up for 2 weeks and the battery is down to 55%.You would figure that with all of the false alerts that the motion detector would trigger rather easily when an actual person approaches your door, but this is not the case. My largest issue is the delay between someone coming to your door and the Ring triggering the alert to your phone and recording the incident to the cloud. When there is actually a person i.e. the mailman I get video of his keister as they are leaving the premises. This makes this device completely unusable as a security device (unless the police have posterior recognition software).That said I really like the concept and their customer service is fantastic. Although they couldn't solve the delays now they are working diligently to improve the software and make a better device. The staff was prompt in returning all correspondence and courteous while working with me to solve these issues. They even offered to cover return shipping should I wish to return the device. I decided to keep it so I will update this review as the software is updated and see if the device survives a Colorado winter.

title: Very nice concept but with some flaws!
body: I purchased and installed this item from an elderly family member. While it's a great device, there are a couple of issues that are very annoying.The good:- Nice quality video and very wide angle lens.- Great to monitor activity near your door and deter unwanted guests.- Relatively small design with good aesthetics.The bad:- Motion sensor gives off many false alarms. During sunrise the motion sensor goes off several times in the morning. The sun is in sight of the camera and it appears that the sun is setting off the alarm as there is no motion during those alarms. The motion sensor works by measuring thermal change. There is an option to block off zones for the motion sensor and adjust sensitivity. I have blocked off the region on the edge that the sun crosses during sunrise, and I have played with the various sensitivity settings. None have stopped the morning false alarms.- The mounting plate is flimsy plastic. This is not a problem if you are mounting the plate on a smooth surface such as brick or wood. However, on an uneven surface, such as common stucco, the plate will deform when you screw it to the wall and make it difficult or impossible to mount. A metal plate would be much better. I had to smooth out the uneven spots if the stucco to get is smooth enough for the mounting plate to work.- The security wrench is low quality. Do not tighten more than barely snug the two screws that hold the unit to the base plate, or you will strip the tool.- Finally, THE SOUND CANNOT BE ADJUSTED! This really bothers me. EVERYTIME there is motion a loud wind-chime sound is played from your phone. I called tech support to ask how to change the default wind-chime sound to something not as loud and more discreet. I was told the sound cannot be changed and the only option is to leave your phone on silent / vibrate mode. I want to know when people approach the house so that was not a viable option for me. The wind-chime sound is LOUD and gets obnoxious very quickly if using the motion detection option.Deducted 1 star for the remarkable lack of ability to change the motion sensor sound or door bell sound to less obnoxious sounds.Deducted 1 star for motion sensor false alarms.It is a great device and I would buy it again. However I am not happy with it due to the sound and motion sensor issues. Fix those two problems and it will be super.

title: Good quality image, but requires >1Mbit internet uplink, even for local use
body: What a great idea - see who's at the door and chat with them before getting out of bed or from the office or while on vacation. Those things would be awesome, but the fastest internet we can get at our house only offers a .5Mbit uplink. By specification (and verified with Ring support), the doorbell requires a minimum of 1Mbit uplink speeds on your internet connection to function optimally. Over our .5Mbit, we notice a good picture/video, but the audio is very delayed and breaks up, making 2-way communication impractical.Many of our other IoT devices (webcams, garage door, thermostat, Tablo DVR, etc.) are able to function without an internet connection at all (i.e., while connected to the in-house WiFi) and will continue to work or work well within or without. The Ring doorbell architecture requires an internet connection even if you are using the doorbell while connected to WiFi inside your house.Other negatives are that by the time we receive notifications that the doorbell has been pressed and can connect to the doorbell to see what's going on, the person has already left. Usually this is just package delivery, but it has happened with regular visitors. There is a subscription fee for recordings with no way to record the video using an app or computer program. $30/yr seems a little steep. I would think that $30 should cover it, forever.Other than that, the battery does seem to last a long time - at least 3 months. The documentation says that really cold weather can zap the battery. We found this to be true with many bouts of subzero temps earlier this winter. However, we installed it in September and didn't need to recharge until about Christmas. Haven't needed to do another recharge. We did NOT have the instant monitoring feature enabled (wherein you can open the app anytime to view the camera). This is said to run down the battery significantly faster when enabled.For $100, this camera is a good value, if not great, if you have more than 1Mbit uplink on your internet connection. The recording feature, otherwise known as 'the gift that keeps on costing,' as my dad would say, is a bit steep. A one-time fee would be much better there.Still, I would like to see a doorbell like this with camera and 2-way audio that is capable of full WiFi operation even without internet/fast internet (from within the house, anyway). Any ideas, please respond!

title: Nice features but a few drawbacks
body: I installed this and the remote notifier a few days ago.here is what I like....1. Clear video and awesome to answer on your phone or tablet.2. Having motion and being able to answer the door is a plus3. Product is compact and fairly easy to install except for connecting to WiFi - See below.4. USB charging the battery is a great feature. Not sure how long in between charges yet. Says 1 year but I'm already to 90% in 2 days with minimal use.Issues since installation...1. It took 7 tries to connect to my WiFi. My WiFi is 50 GB per sec and has tested OK from 50 yards away in the outdoors. Finds my WiFi signal quickly, enter the pass code and nothing. Have to start over.2. It also took 7 tries to connect the remote notifier also and it keeps losing the WiFi connection even though it's just a few feet away from the router.3. Installing on stucco is a challenge since it's an uneven surface. The 6 clips is hard to align the device and secure to the wall.4. The notifier has a mind of it's own. Sometimes it works, sometimes it doesn't, bases on luck. Have removed the device and re setup 3 times in 2 days. Then it drops the WiFi again although it's close to the router. very frustrating.5. In comparison, I have a Nest camera close to the Ring with no issues in detecting and recording motion.6. The recording is clear but it is slow to start recording. Usually get the back of the person so you don't know who rang your doorbell.7. The level in my package was not accurate. Use a better level if you want it straight.8. You get the notification before you get the video, perhaps that's why the delay in capturing the face.9. Lastly, I've had a difficult time deleting from the activity list. Would delete, confirm then get an error message to try again later but the activity remains on the list. The clip seems to be gone but it remains on the list. So far have not been able to delete anything.Again, the features on this product is amazing but until the WiFi and software issues mentioned above are resolved it will just a an expensive doorbell. Hopefully some of these issues can be corrected and it will be ready for prime time. I want to make sure this device works as it should before committing to the paid activity log.Hope this helps some of you that are considering buying this product.

title: This is great. A minute later repeating the test
body: *running off battery, hooked to a Linksys that is maybe 20 feet away tops. App on a Samsung S5. Tested when I had a very strong signal to my wi-fi on my phone.Pro: Packing is well done, I'm running on just the battery because I had no doorbell battery. It seems to last awhile. I've had it up for a week and have had lots of visitors with motion sensor on and it claims to still have 100% battery. The video and audio "Can" be clear when working right.Cons: I've had the app crash when trying to open during a doorbell ring (big problem), I've had the app crash while looking at it (not a huge problem), I've had the app lock up and the doorbell think its still connected when I forgot to hit the "End" button and just closed the app (forgot what I had to do to make the doorbell stop thinking it was connected, but took some fiddling.) (Not a huge issue, I just have to remember to use the "End" button after I let my guest in). The biggest problem is certainly the lag. I have fast equipment and strong wi-fi. While messing with the ring. I've pushed the button and had the app open ready to "accept" in a second or two. This is great. A minute later repeating the test, I wonder if the app is ever going to open (5-10 seconds later). This is wildly annoying. If someone rings the doorbell it might not be a huge deal, they are likely to hang out a bit. The major issue is the lag especially in recording. I am using the 30 day trail currently of video storage. I had the UPS guy show up, I got a notification of movement, but was working so I didn't answer. I went to the app to review the video. At the very start of the recording is just a box sitting at the door, mailmail has come and left and is totally out of the video, just a box. I send my wife a text we have a box. She goes and gets it. Again, I get a motion alert. I go to check out the recorded video. Nothing there. She had time to walk past the ring, pick up the box, open the door, go back inside, all before it started to record anything. Pretty much a worthless feature. I really want this to work. I'm also going to keep it. I'm going to buy the chime. I hope it will be more reliable than the video feed timing sent to my phone. I will not likely pay for the data storage since all the videos I'm getting are mostly a day late and a dollar short.

title: Few flaws which could be solved by Ring.
body: Due to bulk agreement, I purchased the unit directly from Ring.com. After 2 weeks of experience, I see few drawbacks with the unit - all could be fixed.a) For those houses with deep entrance, glare from outside area makes it impossible to view anyone approaching from the street.. Ring Support suggested to tilt the camera downward. It did not help. Or have light above the entrance 24/hr a day. That also not practical.I believe, Ring should add tweak to the s/w app on the smartphones, where users are able to adjust the glare manuallyb) As noted by others, the 2 - 4 sec delay between detection & display on smartphones allows would be stalkers enough time to examine presence of the camera-doorbell, and leave before the camera can record them. (Most of them return camouflaged and armed with spray paint defeat the camera and infrared. That delay exist, regardless of the proximity between the doorbell and the wifi router.Ring must fix this delay - it is paramount to safety of the occupants.c) provide an option to skip reminders for occupants of the residence. (No need to remind me that I opened my own doors).d) $30/ yr storage:. I do appreciate Rings business model and their objective to increase streaming revenue. The fact that, some cloud companies like dropbox, gdrive, and others offer limited cloud storage free of charge, most people would prefer on go that rout, save money and peace of mind that there are less chances of losing their data due to bankruptcy.Ring is a new company with a good concept, however it is subject to all market forces as other - and may go out of business.My advice to Ring. Price should include 10GB cloud storage - as long as you own the device. One of the most attractive element which convinces me and many others to buy this camera, is the free replacement, in case the device was ripped by burgles.By offering pay-one-time-enjoy-all-time, with 10GB free, Ring has benefit of direct contact with its customers. And with invaluable tool to send notices on new offerings. Also the opportunity to amass better (and credible) data about traffic, and usage of the cameras in given neighborhoods - w/o compromising the owners privacy. An invaluable tool to improve on existing devices, or design new devices which better serve consumers.

title: Good but could be improved
body: We are mostly happy with the doorbell but there are a few issues. The good: You can see and talk to anyone who rings your doorbell from anywhere as long as you have good Internet access on your phone. You are notified when there is motion at your door. It works on both our phones (iPhones) at the same time, both phones ring. It was easy to install and set up. It records, if you have cloud recording, the video and you can download it contrary to what some other reviewer's have said here. When I had a little bit of trouble setting it up the customer support representative was very cooperative and helpful and nice.Problems: For me, the biggest problem is that I can't access the camera at any time. So if you just want to see what's going on out there or access your "peephole" for any reason you can't, you have to wait for there to be motion or a ring at the doorbell. This is particularly annoying when you miss a ring or motion activation. Maybe you didn't push accept fast enough. Now you're completely locked out and you can't even review the video for several minutes. By the time you're able to review the video it's too late. Something's wrong with the motion sensor. I have it set to 5 feet and I get a motion notification for nothing, when I accep I just hear a little bit of wind and the tree which is easily 30 feet or more away moving, maybe a car or truck driving by. Another annoyance is you have to turn the phone to horizontal to accept and view video when you get a notification. That's annoying when most of the time I use my phone in vertical position. So you have to turn the phone to horizontal position and then tap the kind of small accept button. I wish two big buttons would come up on my phone in any position I happen to be using it in at the time so that I can quickly accept or deny the call . It doesn't come with an actual doorbell to place in the house. It should come with a ringing device to place in the house somewhere, at least one, or at least make it available on Amazon so I can get free shipping on it, or perhaps a package with both the doorbell and the ringing for the house a little cheaper. Except for the special screwdriver, the toolkit was mostly overkill, and probably added unnecessarily to the price.

title: OK product but needs an extender and the app needs work.
body: The physical installation of this product is a breeze and the documentation devotes too much time to that issue. The software for this product sucks. I don't know who wrote it but it's obvious that the app was not quality tested. There are too many decision points where the action required is described in a conflicting manner. For instance, the setup took me into the cloud purchase where I was tricked into entering my credit card info twice, yet there was no way to back it out or continue the setup.I couldn't even get the app to accept my address. The app asks for my home address, invokes a bot which shows my home address in a list, but when I'm forced to select from that list, the app inserts my neighbors address. I tried a dozen times and finally had to allow my neighbors address to be recorded. I hope I can amend this late.I did manage to get through the app and fix the credit card issue only to find out that the unit said my Wifi signal to the front door was weak. I was forced to buy a Wifi extender. Without the extender, the door bell rang but I could not get a 'live view' from the camera. Even with the extender (which is some 30 feet away) I still get poor signal quality notices from time to time.I purchased this product as a door bell with multiple chimes (since I have a 3 story hours). Being able to have a record of who came to the door is a nice feature. However, if you think you will be able to talk to someone who has rung your door bell or even approached your door, forget that idea. After the app notifies you because the infrared sensor has been tripped, it takes too long to invoke the camera so you can talk. The inherent latency is too great.Perhaps the 'Pro' model is more responsive. I liked the "Pro' model's form factor but it has to be hardwired. If there was a battery powered version I would have upgraded.UPDATE: Today I noticed my battery level is 88%, it was 98% two days ago. At this rate the battery will need to be charged within 3 weeks. I now know why the Pro model is hardwired. Wifi and the camera are power hogs. To fullfill the marketing claims, this video doorbell needs a stable power source; it needs to be hardwired.My recommendation: get the Pro model, don't screw bother with the battery powered cheaper model.

title: Good, not great
body: Works great as a doorbell. The motion detection needs some work. I have one at my house and I put one at my parents house as well. Theirs is wired directly into the existing doorbell so it rings inside as normal and never needs to be charged. I have an intercom system integrated into my existing doorbell, so I could not wire the ring doorbell into it. This also means I have to charge the Ring every month or two. I also bought a Chime (available from Ring for $20) so you can hear inside the house if someone rings the Ring video doorbell. It's great in that you can see and talk to anyone that rings the Ring doorbell.You can also see and talk to anyone that hangs out on the front porch long enough to trigger the motion detector. That is really the issue I have with the Ring. It does not seem to detect motion if something is coming directly toward it. When fedex or ups drop off a package, all I see is their back as they leave. I can walk up to the Ring and stand there and never get a motion notification until I move to the side as I go inside. And I've tried standing there for several minutes.They do continue to work on the firmware and phone app and I have noticed an improvement. (I used to not see video of people leaving until they were 10-15 feet down the sidewalk, it's now as you close the door). Their U.S. based customer service has always been very friendly and helpful. Only issue is they seem to think I'm not happy with the time lag. I was never able to get them to realize that I don't think it detects motion coming directly toward it. I also wonder if it's a temperature issue. The documentation says it uses a combo of heat and motion to prevent trees and etc from setting it off. The air temp has been 95-105 since I got it. Maybe it will improve when the weather cools down.Only other very minor issue I have is that the Ring rings at the unit. Outside. And you can't turn it off.Also beware you might need a wifi extender. It needs at least a decent signal to work properly. My parents and I already had extenders near the front door, so it wasn't an issue for me.If I just wanted this as a doorbell, I'd give it 5 stars. The motion detection was what i was more interested in. Since I never see a face on the video when motion is detected I dropped it.

title: A decent video doorbell
body: The Ring Wi-Fi Enabled Video Doorbell is a decent video doorbell. I guess as an infant industry these things still have a ways to go before someone comes up with the perfect setup.As a very doorbell that offers a video feature, the ring is adequate. Installation isn't entirely difficult but neither is it as easy as could be. The hardwiring coupled with the battery operation is a good feature design, allowing us some flexibility in installation. Although the battery option is there, it really does limit how the Ring functions. For somewhat full functionality we'll want to be hardwired, as having other features or certain parameters cranked up will zap battery life quite rapidly. The use of a diode means an extra few steps for installation (the diode is for certain types of ringers).Video functionality - For me, video functionality is very limited. One, it either acts as purely a video device to view ongoing "live" video or as a cloud enabled video viewing device. It cannot function on its own to view any type of archived video. There is no in-built video storage or the option to add some type of SD or MicroSD memory for local archiving. As a cloud based service, it requires price based functionality, no free cloud services (barring trial). The Ring functions more like a VOIP app than a video camera that is connected to your home network, and that's actually what it is. I'm not sure if this methodology of functioning is what is the main issues with what causes this delay, but this routing to the cloud in order to stream back to home may not be the most streamlined method. This means that its stated 720p may not always be 720p based on bandwidth issues. Your Ring is calling to your phone and your phone is calling to the Ring, not really a direct home network link.As for options, if you know what you're picking up and know the limitations of how the Ring works and are willing to work within those boundaries, the Ring may be a 5 star item to you, as an item that you might want some flexibility, you'll find it less so.The Ring's lifetime purchase protection is a nice bonus, even if there are certain requirements you must follow in order to have that honored.

title: Good idea, but the execution needs some work.
body: Ring is a very good idea, but the execution still needs a lot of work. There are a number of critical problems the company must solve before I will be satisfied with their product.1. First and foremost, I discovered the hard way that if the Ring falls off your Wifi network, the only way you will know is by the fact that you stop receiving motion and doorbell alerts. There is no notification at all on the smartphone application that it is no longer communicating with the doorbell. As a matter of fact, it will still show you the last battery status it received as if it were still connected. For 6 straight days I received no alerts while I was out of town and when I contacted the company to find out if something was wrong, they informed me that the Doorbell had apparently fell off my Wifi network (no idea why, of course). You can only reset the device locally, so if this happens, you are just out of luck until you return home. To me, this is the biggest problem with the device. There is no way for me to know that it is working and if it isn't your only clue will be a lack of alerts over time. 1. a. Related to the above, if the device falls off your network, it will also not sound the doorbell locally through the Chimes. So you won't get an alert and you won't get a doorbell ringing in your house.2. As noted in other reviews, the recording feature for motion alerts usually starts far too late to be useful. You either see nothing because the person has already left, or you see their back as they are leaving. When UPS, FEDEX, etc. drop off packages, I either don't see the driver at all or I see his back as he is leaving my driveway.3. Also as noted other reviews. There is no way to remotely access the device if you miss the alert. You cannot remotely turn on the camera and intercom if someone is standing at your door unless you caught the alert on time.Overall, I really want to like this product, but it still has some critical flaws that need to be fixed. The company seems very response and professional, so that is a positive. Hopefully they will work quickly to address some of these issues.

title: Good idea and useful product, but battery performance is terrible
body: I was glad for the LiveView feature, which I was initially disappointed to learn it didn't have when I first bought it, so adding it was a plus. However, the motion-feature is too slow to detect, as others have commented (it shows people walking away or sometimes there's nothing to show!), it's performance is not optimal, hence not the most reliable.My biggest gripe by far is the battery life. My house doesn't have a built-in door bell, hence no doorbell wiring so I appreciated the battery feature of ring. But that's where my 'appreciation' ends. Ring's official statement is that you could get up to 6 months of use but weather affects it (cold mostly, and perhaps hot too). After I heard the 6 month time frame, I mentally adjusted my expectation to say 3-4 months, but I've gotten 4 weeks at best! It was definitely worse during the winter (had to charge every week or so), but it's now July and I charged the battery on July 5th, and then got a warning about 2 days ago that it's critically low so less than 3 weeks? I am yet to go past 4 weeks without having to recharge it. So the 6 month time frame Ring gives is disingenuous at best, and dishonest at the least. If others have been able to get up to 6 months out of theirs running on battery, I'd love to know what part of the country (or the world) you live and what you've managed to do. And yes, I've adjusted the motion zones, sensitivity and everything I reasonably could (for instance, the bell rings for push events only, not for motion), and still get very crappy battery performance. I'm glad I have this somewhat inexpensive motion-based camera, but Ring has either got to put more effort in a more reliable battery or battery-operated performance or not sell it with the battery option at all, because the biggest nuisance of this is having to charge so often. Imagine if when I really need the features the most is when it is indoors charging. It is also not a reasonable expense or effort to install door bell wiring just for this and I certainly will look for a wired product (and a house with wiring) when I move to my next house but geez.

title: Good video doorbell - Motion detection doesn't work on homes with stoops
body: [[VIDEOID:f4cfcd2497f11da8833cd73e1d1f13d3]] The GOOD: - Video doorbell with battery power allows its use on those areas without wired doorbells. - Good communications and quick response on the phone when doorbell is rung - The software warns you when the battery needs to be recharged.The BAD: - Severe lag in detecting motion and recording it. More often than not, a fast moving individual was through the door or out on the sidewalk before the device started recording, leaving a video of a blank entrance (see video). - The motion detection appears to be focused straight ahead. On a house with stairs/stoop, it does not detect motion until the visitor is right through the door. Any attempt to extend the range only results in tall vehicles (SUVs, trucks, etc) being recorded. - While the battery option is great and provides for freedom of location, the claim of one year between charges is wildly exaggerated if you use the motion detection feature. Between all the trials to try to get the right motion detection and avoid all the passing cars, the bell asked for recharge a month into its first use. - Because the charging port is hidden in the back, recharging the battery requires physically unscrewing and removing the device from its location. - Battery charging takes a minimum of five hours.I think that the motion detection issues have been solved with the new Ring Pro (I dislike the term "pro". It implies that it is for professional use, when it's just a better model). Unfortunately, that doesn't do anything for someone who bought the original or who has problems wiring the location.I suppose that the new Ring Stickup cam with a solar panel could solve the motion detection issue and allow the Ring doorbell to have a much longer battery life but that adds a hefty price tag to your front door. The fact that the pickup cam would require a separate storage plan from Ring does not help matters.I'm waiting to see the reviews for the Zmodo Torch Pro - Smart Door Light and Connected Doorbell.

title: Way Too Sensitive
body: The good: I love being able to answer my doorbell with my phone. I don't normally answer my door unless I know the person, but now I can easily tell people to go away and not have to worry they were casing my house and think I'm not home. The video image quality is excellent and the sound is good. Set up and installation were very easy.The bad: Motion detection is more of a pain than it's worth. There is a setting that allows you to turn off certain zones and decrease sensitivity. With my settings at the absolute minimum I get videos of cars from down the block. Many times the cars are not even close enough to be in the picture yet still set off the motion detection. I know it's cars setting it off because I hear them in the videos. There is for me about a 10 second delay from the time the bell is rung to the notification on my phone. Sometimes by the time I find my phone, open the app and answer it the person is gone already. Not that much of a loss, but still a little annoying.The ugly: I sent an email to customer service telling them of my overly sensitive motion detection and how I had turned off all the zones except for the one directly on the porch and turned down the sensitivity as low as it would go. They sent me back an email telling me to watch the motion detection video to learn how to use it. When I asked if I was getting a message from a real person who actually read my first email they responded that yes indeed they were real and that I should reset my ring and reinstall it and see how that worked. I did that and there was no change. They then suggested that because I had the sensitivity on low ring was requiring a larger heat signature to detect motion and I should slide the detection slider all the way out. I did that and tripled the number of motion detections I was getting. After they suggested I adjust the slider a little at a time and see what happens I gave up. They clearly have no answer to my problem and are guessing at solutions. Their answer was to extend my free cloud recording trial by another month.

title: Great when it works - wish it would all the time.
body: I purchased this directly from Ring.com about a year and half ago. I live in a rural area and at first it worked great. When someone pulled into the drive (a long 150' one), I got a motion alert. When they rang the doorbell, I was immediately able to answer on my phone. I also got the chime for inside the house but it never worked - constantly said it couldn't find the internet connection. I also found that when the ground was warm and the wind blew, the tree branches moved and the motion detector went off every few minutes. I tried changing the motion setting so it wouldn't pick those up but it also meant anyone could walk into the yard on that side of the house and it wouldn't pick up that motion.By this last spring, it periodically wouldn't work. Ring.com service had me check the internet connection through a site they sent me. I never could get the app for the check to completely work so they said the doorbell problem was probably just a bad wifi connection.
