However, I have very a very good wifi signal for all the rest of the items in my house. When the doorbell is rung, it doesn't show up on my phone until after a delay. Unless the individual gets within a foot of the doorbell, I can't hear them. This past month or so the motion detector is picking up the mailman - not as he enters the drive, gets out, puts the mail in the box or a package on my porch (20' from the mailbox), or as he gets back in his vehicle but as he leaves the drive.I'm currently looking for another system to replace it. I want a unit that works all the time, not now and then or getting only the leaves in motion or the backside of individuals and cars.UPDATE: I removed the doorbell several months ago. There were problems charging the battery which has to be removed and brought inside to charge. It would take hours to charge and then only last for maybe a day. Because I have to have one with a battery I'm trying to find one that can be charged in place or more easily removed than this with the tiny screw that strip easily and are constantly being replaced.

title: I tested it out over battery power and it was really cool. One issue I noticed off-hand was pretty bad ...
body: UPDATE #3 January 12, 2017:Updating my review to 4 stars as my experience has definitely improved and the reliability of the doorbell has been consistently good. Motion-detection is OK and I do get a number of false alerts but overall it has been working well. I realize YMMV with these units also depending on how far away the router is - in my case, the router is maybe 15-20 feet away. Beyond the initial review and second updated, I haven't had issues accessing Live View or answering the door remotely except for one instance where the service appeared to be down.UPDATE #2 August 23rd:I've been using the replacement unit mostly without any qualms but I'm still having issues consistently connecting to Live View. Still working this out with support but aside from that there is another concern I have regarding the wireless network in the context of using this with an extender. Right now the Ring only "knows" about a single SSID, and you cannot easily change that from the app and have to physically remove the device and re-register it every time. I noticed on the web interface of the Ring (if you just go to the IP address) there are multiple entries for SSIDs but it doesn't seem like they are taking advantage of this. I think they should, especially in the case that there are multiple extenders/APs/routers in an environment - this way there would be a fallback for the Ring to connect to a different AP in case one goes down or offline. I still feel like I'm evaluating this technology - there are some big pluses and the idea is great but the execution and support behind it hasn't been the greatest.UPDATE #1 August 11th:I received the replacement unit today and it turns out that the support tech I spoke with who 'upgraded' my firmware to 1.6.76, actually misinformed me and likely hosed my unit. I found out today, that 1.6.279 is the latest/most recent firmware available. So basically, the tech who wanted to upgrade my FW, pushed an older one to me and screwed my unit up. Now, I don't know if the could have just rolled the 279 FW update back but I figure I'll just stick with the replacement unit at this point in time. Very confusing and would explain a lot in terms of why my original unit all of the sudden went bonkers. I am having another issue with Live View not working if "Ring Alerts" are disabled - I've tried this on multiple devices and it has never worked. Seems like a bug... other than that, I'm expecting to still be evaluating this and using the full length of time during the trial period to really see if this is worth it. Right now, I'm still kind of on the fence about it. When it works it's great. But when it doesn't it feels equally or more like such a letdown.ORIGINAL REVIEW:Writing an initial review based on a week of evaluation so far. I got my unit from Costco and was so excited to install it but had to wait for HOA approval before doing the hardwired installation.In the meantime, I tested it out over battery power and it was really cool. One issue I noticed off-hand was pretty bad pixelation and image quality when the video camera was activated. It got a little better several more seconds in but I haven't been too impressed with it. Same issue whether I'm 5ft away or 15-20ft away from the router. Oh, and my router is a newer AC router (Netgear Nighthawk X4 7500v2) and my internet connection is sufficient overall (150mbps down/10mbps up). Otherwise, the alerting was fine - I did notice a few seconds of delay. I setup IFTTT triggers to email me based on motion and rings as well, and that has seemed to work well too.As soon as I got the go-ahead to install from the HOA, I hardwired the unit. Installation was pretty simple and straightforward... the hardest part of drilling into the stucco/concrete walls. The unit is a bit difficult to snap into place but once it's in it feels secure. I do have concerns about the security screws really being *that* secure but I guess, assuming your internet/wifi connection isn't down, you can capture the perpetrator stealing your device and have it stored. I would like to note that a security device being fully dependent on wifi and the cloud for the monitoring/recording aspects is a huge point of failure. If internet connectivity is lost or internet goes down on the ISP side, you pretty much have a fancy-looking $200 doorbell assuming it's hardwired. If battery-powered, I think the only thing that will be heard is the built-in ring chime and that would be by the person ringing the doorbell. Point is, there needs to be more and or better integration with the local wifi network and provisions need to be made for local storage options and streaming everything locally within the network rather than purely through the cloud. It really feels like we, as customers, are beta-testing a product that is not so mature IMHO.Really great idea but the execution is lacking. To continue my review, after hardwiring, I started playing with the motion detection, only to realize that it isn't that accurate - as many others have been reporting, there is a delay between the motion detection and actual recording. This can't merely be an issue with poor/insufficient wifi connection or coverage - I think that's an oversimplification. I think it has to do with either hardware limitation of the motion sensor and or the logic on the software/firmware. Either way, I experienced the said delay as well even after making multiple adjustments to zones and range (e.g. Fedex guy drops a package off and motion detection and recording kicks off late and doesn't record anything except me picking the package up off the front porch). After continuing to test, at some point the door bell simply stopped working (well, the mechanical chime was fine but the unit was unresponsive otherwise). Even motion detection stopped working. I called support and they had me go through the usual steps of hard-resetting the device and running through setup again. It was also mentioned that a newer firmware push/update to 1.6.76 would occur, so I was thinking that would be great.. of course, I had to do another hard reset in order for that to happen. And that's when all the major problems started popping up - after updating to 1.6.76, the device just started acting funny. Major delays on the app from rings, and pretty much zero motion detection. Now, the big issue is that I get a blank/black screen when someone rings. I performed the hard-reset process over a handful of times and the problem still persists. I know this is not a wifi connection/speed issue because, incidentally, before the firmware push to 1.6.76 occurred the last time I went through the reset/setup process, a quick test of the door bell ring and video answer worked just fine and that was while the device was still on 1.6.236. It's possible that something is wrong with the device at this point as it's a bit weird that it keeps 're-flashing' the firmware to different versions. Every time I hard reset, the FW version shows up as 1.6.236 and goes through the process of updating to 1.6.76 - if this is not intentional or expected, then it does sound defective. Usually firmware flashes are a one-time thing, so my hunch is that this is not expected behavior and that something went haywire with the device... possibly the firmware image got corrupted.I called in and reported these issues, and a new device is on it's way out. So once I get it I'll be scrutinizing these same details during the setup. I'm hoping these issues won't come up as I test the replacement unit. If so, I think I'll have no choice but to lower the rating and return the unit to Costco. In the meantime, my rating is left at 3 stars due to the period of time that it was actually working before things blew up. I will be back for an update.

title: Great product for what it does, great customer service to boot!
body: I consider myself reasonably tech savvy and have been beefing up my home security with IP cameras , however setting up outdoor cameras were an issue. They are rather conspicuous and somewhat of an eyesore, need to have their locations carefully planned and thought out as well as might require running cables, both power and network as applicable, into the home.After coming to know of the Ring doorbell, I avidly read through reviews of this model as well as the newer Ring Pro as possible answers to our predicament. Locating a camera within the doorbell is a rather nifty solution since it is relatively unobtrusive and allows for surveillance while being conspicuous to those who know what the product is.My timeline for an upgrade was moved up when my old doorbell at the front door had the flimsy plastic switch cave in leaving the housing intact and the wires exposed and requiring me to swap it out.I did consider the Ring Pro as it's thinner dimensions seemed to fit my door frame better, however I ended up opting for the original Ring after carefully reading through all the earlier reviews and noting that the reported issues didn't all seem to have been fully addressed in the newer product.I'd viewed the installation video and it seemed simple enough.That said, when I received the package and took it outdoors, the old doorbell was barely an inch wide and the door frame has a step like pattern that meant that the left edge of the ring doorbell would protrude a little further out than its right edge since it was twice the width of my mechanical doorbell and spanned a couple of the layers.I had read that the unit needed to be flat and flush against the mount and had to compensate and adjust the depth of the screws along the right edge so that the mount didn't warp with them screwed in all the way. This was a an issue with my particular installation and if you have a largely flat surface, it shouldn't pose an issue for you. The provided tools and screws were perfectly adequate for the wooden door frame install.Luckily my old doorbell was wired which meant that it wouldn't run on battery. Per the reviews, I ensured that my signal strength at the door was good, and since my router is in the basement barely 15 feet from it, there have been no connectivity issues.One note of caution, I was surprised by the amount of vertical downward force it took me to have the unit clamp onto the mount, and almost feared I'd snap the clips before I felt it click into place, satisfyingly.My initial tests were great and we were up and running. The first time we had a FedEx delivery while away , and were able to speak through it and ask him to leave the package was an awesome feeling :)One of the criticisms for some users is that the unit needs to tilt downwards like the Stick-Up cam to adequately sense motion.In my case, however it seems to be working despite a walkway that rises up a couple of steps to my door and a slope down to the road which also means that passing vehicles don't trigger the alert needlessly but people walking up to my door, do.I tested the cloud functionality for about 20 days before opting to subscribe to it, however the option to record video locally would be much appreciated. The front door unit has been rock solid thus far, never dropped connections that I have noticed, my wife is very pleased with the convenience factor as well as the fact that it seems to deter unwanted visitors.Being able to attend the doorbell while on the go - Priceless!The camera has a fish eye lens view which while not great looking has the benefit of covering a much wider angle in the process than would otherwise be the case.I did notice that the other criticism of it recording people from the back seemed to be true initially but after tweaking the sensitivity somewhat, we seem to have found a happy medium between getting alerted just as someone rings the door. That said, the live view option was a tremendous value add, and I was very gratified that it was enabled shortly after reaching out to customer service.Being able to see who passes by and doesn't ring was great. The night vision works well and the unit appears to be charging without issue thus far.After our great experience with the front door unit, wifey asked me for one for the rear to overlook our patio as well. Sure enough, I ordered another.This one was installed on vinyl siding and I had to drill holes prior to being able to drive the screws in. While driving in the lower screws after mounting the unit, one of the two tiny screws fell and went right through a gap in my rear deck - yikes! Luckily the unit is firmly locked to the mount (it better be after the force required!) and the two lower screws just help keep it in place. That said - please be sure to keep something flat and non porous below the unit during install, to catch these runaway screws in case they fall out for you as well.Our rear doorbell also was a mechanical wired and allowed us to run this unit also in the same wired mode as the front. This one was further away from my main router so I set up a wi fi extender unit (The ASUS EA-N66 www.amazon.com/gp/product/B007ZT23ZE) right next to the patio door.I use the extender exclusively for the second ring unit and once it was up and running, I had no issues as before.Again, I made the request to customer support to enable Live View on this unit and it was quickly answered and enabled overnight.It has seen some very hot and very wet days thus far, I'm waiting to see how the winter treats it. I have read some concerns about weatherproofing and water seepage from behind the mount. One of the pictures for a 3D printed mount by a reviewer caught my eye - would that such a product were commercially available - a weather guard as well as a rear surface to protect the mount from water as well as be a flat surface to align the mount along, would greatly aid in mounting on uneven surfaces such as mine as can be seen in the pictures where the stepped nature of the door frame made some fine alignment necessary to ensure that the leads had good enough contact and my battery was charging from it.I now have two Ring doorbell units that complete my outdoor surveillance needs as well as double as smart doorbells with the ability to remotely answer the door. The cloud recording is adequate although my gripe about not allowing recorded video to be locally saved, still stands.Live View mitigates that complaint somewhat, but is currently available only to owners of wired units so could be a deal breaker for those who choose to run it in battery operated mode.I also wish there were discounted options for cloud recordings when there are multiple units within the same account, because while $30 a year seems reasonable, compared to say the Nest dropcam's cloud recording rates, shelling out an additional $30 for a second unit does feel a bit much.In summary - We are happy campers with our Ring doorbells and would recommend it highly but be sure to do your research about installation, wi fi coverage and operating mode to see if it works for you.

title: READ THE END of this review.  It will help!!!
body: Right after buying this, I posted a review that was very negative. My first impressions of the product were rather bad. But I have since learned a bit about the Ring Doorbell, how to configure the device and the app, and my opinion has changed significantly.I am disappointed that I had figure out all of these issues on my own. The documentation on Ring.com was out dated and not helpful.I first installed the "Ring Always Home" app on my phone. I later found there is "Rapid Ring" and "Neighbors". I find the "Rapid Ring" app more useful if i just want to check out the live feed, or answer the doorbell. The "Ring Always Home" app is still useful for seeing the motion event timeline or changing the motion detection settings.Pros:The doorbell came quickly (thanks to amazon prime), it was easy to install, and easy to setup.Cons:The night vision is easily defeated by a street light, house light, or yard light that is in the field of view. If you have a strong light source in the field of view, the doorbell camera thinks there is enough light to not use the night vision.The viewing angle is way to wide and the image quality is very poor. Unless a person puts their face right up to the camera, it's hard to recognize them, even if you know them.It wasn't easy to figure out how to turn off the constant motion notifications on my phone while still allowing the motion events to be recorded.Previous Comment: I don't know how I discovered this, certainly not from any of the documentation or instructions from RING, but I was able to turn off Motion Notifications in the APP Notifications Android settings. On Android, I went to "Settings" (the little gear icon in the pull down menu), "Notifications", I selected "See all" which revealed "Ring". I selected the "Ring" app. That brought up a window with "Show notifications", "App icon badges" and "Motion Notification". I then turned off "Motion Notification". With that setting off, I no longer get beeps, but I'm still able to see Motion Events in the camera timeline window.UPDATE (12-11-2019): I discovered a major design flaw in the Ring Doorbell. I'm pretty sure it applies to the Doorbell 2 as well. The camera is off and the unit is essentially blind until the motion sensor detects motion. The doorbell camera runs on the battery even when it's hardwired. In order to conserve battery power/life, the camera is off until the motion sensor determines there is something interesting to see. It takes about 2 seconds for the camera to turn on and adjust gain, white balance, etc. As a result, you may miss the action in a motion event. Many other devices are constantly on and buffering data. With those devices, it's possible to capture the few seconds before and after the motion event. Because the Ring Doorbell camera is off, you can only capture video after the motion event.Did you see where I said that the camera only draws power from the battery? The Ring troubleshooting page talks about the fact that the battery should allow for 1000 activations. That is under ideal conditions. If the temperature is too warm or too cold, the battery will last significantly less -- only about 40% (or 400 activations). In extreme cold or hot weather (above 80F, or below 0F) the battery efficiency is even worse, may not even work, and can be permanently damaged if you try to use it.Why should you care about the battery? Without the battery, you just have a doorbell -- no camera. If you mistakenly allow the battery to completely drain, you may have to go through the setup again. Even with a high speed charger, it takes about 4 hours to charge the battery. With a low speed charger, it could take up to 10 hours. During that time, you should post a sign saying "Please come back later". While you're charging the battery, the doorbell has to be removed from the mounting plate and connected to a micro-USB cable... So, it doesn't even function as a doorbell.UPDATE (12-12-2019): I read some of the Ring Community pages related to the battery and battery life. One of the pages claimed that your house doorbell AC transformer is trickle charging the battery. So, it would seem that if you want to use the Ring Doorbell without having to remove it and connect it to a USB charger, you can mess with the sensitivity settings so that the camera isn't activated as much. It would also seem that you may be able to recharge the Ring Doorbell without removing it (recharging it in-place), simply by putting the motion detection to sleep. I don't know how efficient the AC Transformer recharging is, or if it's faster than a USB charger. But if you don't have physical access (you're away from home) to your Ring Doorbell, it would seem to be a reasonable solution.Speaking of messing with settings to conserve battery life... I found a setting that seems to be on by default where the Ring Doorbell wakes up (activates) periodically without motion events just to show you what's going on in front of the camera. So that boring time lapse video you see between motion events counts against your camera activation count.UPDATE (12-22-2019): I spoke with a couple of people about my experience with the Ring doorbell and having to connect it to a USB cable to charge it. They suggested that my doorbell transformer may not be the recommended voltage or amperage. I checked. Sure enough, my doorbell transformer is 16V and 10VA. Ring recommends 24V and 30VA. I purchased a 24V, 40VA transformer from Amazon and installed it. I now hear a buz in the audio. I will have to see if the battery starts to charge.UPDATE (12-28-2019): Within a day of replacing the doorbell transformer, I could see that the Ring Doorbell was in-fact charging. On 12-22, the battery was at 58%. Today, the battery is at 98%. I've increased the motion sensitivity and increased the notification frequency... both to max. As the result, I'm seeing many more motion events, but the battery is staying charged. I've updated my rating to 4 stars. I feel that there should have been something more obvious telling me that I needed to upgrade my doorbell transformer. With the upgraded transformer, I hear a buz in the audio. That maybe noise on the power line, but I would have expected the Ring to filter that out. Also, there is a big delay between a button press and when Alexa, or my phone notifies me that the button was pressed.

title: Satisfied Customer... does everything I thought it would!
body: SUMMARY:- Physical setup started out frustrating (app couldn't find me), afterwards very smooth and quick- Using existing wiring of old doorbell- Set expectations accordingly: can't access camera at any time (without being alerted), recording lag- Have to unlock phone and go to app every time I get an alert but have been able to view video every time (never missed one that I tried to view)- Good picture quality including night/low light and soundIt does exactly what I expected it to do. I would have given this 5 stars if the app had found my address right away or let me input it manually and if I could see the view from the camera at any time through the app. Otherwise, I still love the device for the convenience and security it gives me.***LONG REVIEW - After using for 2 weeks***SETUP:The package is well thought out. You may need a drill, but otherwise they provide the screwdriver and the drill bit. The base template has a small level in it to ensure that it's installed straight. The app will direct you to a YouTube video that very clearly demonstrates the steps to install based on your wall type.The installation setup in the app was missing the option of Stucco as an exterior, which is what my house has. It does mention Stucco in the quick install guide. If you are installing on a stucco wall as well, you will have to figure out how to install the base so it's flat. If the base template is installed with some flex in it, then you'll have a hard time mounting the device to it. I pulled out my existing doorbell, drilled a couple of holes for the anchors and connected the base template to the wall using the existing wires.The app is required to install the device and I did experience some frustration with trying to get it to pick up the correct address via GPS. The app was very buggy as far as trying to manually correct the address, because for some reason it couldn't find my address and wouldn't accept it. It picked up both of my neighbors before finally finding me. This was easily the most frustrating part of this experience so far since it wouldn't let me continue the install without putting an address in first. It probably took about 20 minutes to finally find my address. They should have an option to bypass the address part and move on with the install. Once it did have the correct address, everything went smoothly and it took about 15 minutes to set up.MONITORING vs. SURVEILLANCE:This is a video monitoring device, so the camera is not always recording/live. As a monitoring device it needs to be triggered before the camera starts working. This will be true of any monitoring device. If you're looking for a surveillance camera, meaning it is always on and recording this is not for you. I understand the gripe about not being able to view through the camera at any time because by accessing the app you could essentially be triggering the camera. I don't know if they're ever going to enable the camera to "live view" on demand (without being triggered by an alert), but that seems to be the biggest thing missing and a great feature to add. Then again, I bought the device knowing this already and am fine with only viewing video when the motion sensor is triggered or the doorbell is pressed. I often accept the motion alert calls so I can zoom in and try to discern what triggered it. Not to mislead... the zoom function is very basic and has only 1 setting, but it is helpful when viewing. Zoom only works with the live video feed and not on the recordings.MOTION SENSOR:There are 6 zones on the motion sensor, Zones 1-5 equally split the 180 degree field, starting from 5ft away and adjustable to 30 feet. You can turn any of the zones off in case you don't want it to set off an alert (like a busy street next to you). Zone 6 is the entire 180 degree field under 5 feet from the device.You should expect to start out receiving plenty of motion alerts while you adjust the sensitivity. After a week, I think I have it pretty dialed in. There is a lag that I expected and in most cases I see people ring about a step away before they ring the doorbell. Most motion sensors don't immediately pick up so I expect this problem of anything that uses a motion sensor... not just the Ring doorbell.I also receive motion alerts when a big truck or someone with loud bass or a rumbling engine drives by. Basically, if the sound is enough to vibrate your house windows, you're probably going to get a motion alert too. I think that's one that will be very hard to compensate for, so I can live with it.VIDEO QUALITY:The 720p video quality is pretty clear and decent in low light. There are some times that the video recorded isn't smooth and might freeze for a second and then jump back to motion, but I can usually see the face of the person that set off the alert, which is mainly what I want. I have a 12 foot overhang approaching my door so the device is always in the shade. Due to the placement, during the day it is hard to see anything in the street because it's very bright in the background. People approaching the door under the overhang are always clear and in focus.CLOUD RECORDING:Optional for a monthly fee with a discount for buying the year in advance. $3 per month or $30 per year for 6 months of video storage is very reasonable. Without the storage option you will see the video when the camera is triggered and that's it. There is no micro sd card storage option.SMARTHUB INTEGRATION:I added this as a device to my Wink hub. It picked up easily and shows up under doorbell and also under sensors for the motion sensor. Showing up as a motion sensor in Wink as well is great because I can set the porch lights and entry lights to turn on when dark based on the motion sensor from the doorbell.Overall this is a nice, well thought out product and a great addition to my smart home.

title: It needs a little polishing, but overall a very good device
body: [[VIDEOID:4ee9b4677d95b7fbb31c55a59c44247]] SUMMARYI love my Ring video doorbell. It's not perfect, but it's pretty close and certainly a great product for the money. The ring was easy to install and generally works as advertised. There are occasional glitches, but nothing too serious. The app works well on both Android and iPhone. Video quality seems limited by wireless network signal.SETUPThe Ring was easy to install. In general, it was a simple swap with my existing hardwired doorbell. The hardest part was drilling holes into my unexpectedly hard stucco wall. (I think there was a metal plate just under the stucco.) Once the holes were drilled, it was a simple matter to mount the included base plate to the wall with a few mounting screws. The base is plastic and flexes a bit. Since my stucco wall was not perfectly flat, it took a little fiddling to get the plate flat enough for the doorbell to snap on correctly. It also took a fair amount of force to get the outer case to snap onto the plate. At first, I was too gentle and thought something was broken. Once I pushed a little harder, though, the case snapped on snugly.Connecting to the network was simple, but see my activation tip below.ACTIVATION TIPMy Ring arrived with a totally dead battery. Even when plugged into a charger, it behaved strangely and the activation instructions didn't work. It needed to charge for some time before it would activate correctly. So, make sure you get some charge into the battery before trying to activate. Once charged, it immediately hooked to my network. It's been great ever since with no connectivity issues.OPERATIONThe Ring generally works as advertised, with only an occasional hiccough. I get an alert on my Android phone whenever anyone comes to the door. I have it setup to alert my wife's iPhone as well. That's a bit of overkill, but we wanted to be sure we wouldn't miss anything important. It works fine on both Android and iPhone. The video feed is good, although sometimes there is pixelation. I blame that on my home wireless network which may have trouble getting a good signal to the outside of my home. The video is usually plenty good enough to recognize someone and see what's going on. With a porch light on, the video is also good at night. I haven't tried it at night without the light.The field of view is great. As you can see in the attached photos, I had to install the Ring perpendicular to my door, yet I still have a great view of people approaching on my sidewalk. I've also attached a few photos showing the range of quality under different lighting conditions. Sometimes we have severe backlighting due to the morning sun, but the picture is still fairly good even under those conditions.The Ring got a good workout recently when I was traveling out of the country for business. I received an alert when my daughter arrived home from school every day. I was able to chat with her as she was entering the house. It was comforting to know she had arrived home safely. Thanks, Ring.The Ring is also handy when I'm expecting a package, since most carriers no longer ring or knock. [Sidebar: I work at home and, until a few months ago, carriers always rang the bell when dropping off a package Then, all at once, they stopped. This includes UPS, USPS, FedEx, and DHL. I'm not sure why, but all the carriers stopped at about the same time. End of sidebar.]The Ring is reliable and rarely misses anyone at the door. I say rarely because sometimes it DOES fail: on occasion, there will be a flyer on the door but no alert and no video. But it's pretty good; I'd say it works 90-95% of the time.Sometimes I'll get an alert, but the video starts recording a few seconds after the alert. So when I look at the video, there's no one in the picture but I can hear someone walking away from the door. This seems to be the case if someone quickly passes through the frame of view. If there was a real issue, e.g., someone breaking into the house, they'd be in the frame plenty long enough to get captured, so it's not a concern.Because of the layout of our home, the doorbell also has a view of a neighbor's front door. The Ring alerts whenever someone comes or goes at their house, causing a lot of unnecessary alerts. This isn't the Ring's fault, it's just something to consider before you install. I understand the newer version of the Ring lets you block out parts of the frame. That might help alleviate the issue.CUSTOMER SERVICEOne of the reasons I bought this was because it has "live view" so you can look at the door whether or not there is an alert. I had read about this feature online, but there were no instructions on using it and the feature was not available in the phone app. So, I sent an email to Ring. It turns out that you have to ask Ring to activate this service for you. Once I sent the email, they quickly activated the service and the feature appeared in the app. Extra points to Ring for their quick response, although I'm not sure why this feature isn't activated by default.SUMMARYAll in all, this is a great device. It needs a little polishing around the edges, but it generally works the way it's supposed to. I would recommend it to a friend and I plan on adding another to our back door.

title: Provides A Huge Relief With Security For My House...Yearly Subscription Required To Record Events!
body: I bought this doorbell to fill a huge safety concern with our house. We live in a townhouse that has no windows near our door. Our door also doesn't have a peep hole. These two things left us very vulnerable when opening our door to see who was knocking, or just leaving the house. After researching security doorbell cameras, I choose this one because it works with Amazon Alexa. We have a Dot, Echo, and an Echo Show.The Ring App is very useful and makes installing and setting up your doorbell camera very easy. The hardest part is trying to drill into brick to secure it beside our door. After this tedious task, the rest of the installation went very smoothly. One huge benefit to using this doorbell is that it has the option to be both wired (using your existing doorbell wiring) or wireless! This leaves the placement of this device totally up to you letting you choose the best coverage for overall security.As far as features go...this doorbell has a lot!First, there is a built in motion sensor calibration feature. This lets you setup your motion areas automatically or you can determine how sensitive the three designated motion zones are setup. This would be better if you can scale back an individual zone that might have a flag or a decoration that moves in the wind. As of now you only have the ability to turn off each zone, which does help but it's not ideal to completely turn off a zone for obvious security reasons. The more expensive versions allow for these options, but it would still be nice to have this feature on the base model. You can also set motion "sleep" times that will turn off notifications for a set period of time. This is very useful when shoveling snow or kids playing in the front yard.Second, I love the fact that you get an alert on your phone whenever someone rings your doorbell, let alone walks up to your door. A simple tap on the alert takes you right to a live view of your doorbell camera allowing you to see, and either answer...or don't answer whoever is at the door. Also, there is a live view setting which enables you to view your front door at any time regardless if there is someone there or not. This does lower the battery life when using with a wireless install, but honestly the battery lasts for quite some time.Fourth, there is a nice little feature that lets you upload events to the "Ring Neighborhood" which is a collection of Ring devices in the surrounding geographic areas. You can only see these events when a nearby neighbor shares and event with the community. This provides a greater security network, rather than having one camera you have an unknown number of cameras that can capture events and be shared with everyone so you know what to look out for. For example, one of my neighbors posted a couple bears digging through their trash in the early morning. Took extra precaution the next few weeks by viewing my front door before I left for work. I couldn't imagine walking out and getting greeted by a hungry Yogi and Boo Boo without a picnic basket.Third, the battery lasts longer than expected. I bought this right before Christmas to make sure my Amazon gifts were delivered and didn't go "missing" before I got home. The alerts from these deliveries, watching my son get on his bus, and the few alerts of random cars that pass our house per day seem to have a very little affect on how long the battery lasts. Charging this battery requires you take the whole doorbell off of the mount. This is done by loosing two little screws at the bottom of the doorbell. You do not need to take them out completely, which is what I did the first time and almost dropped them on the ground.Finally, and probably the biggest feature that my family and I use, is the integration with Amazon Alexa. After enabling the "Ring Skill" in the Alexa App, we are notified of any motion or pressing of the doorbell with a verbal notification on all our Alexa devices. This lets us know whenever someone is at our door, and on our Echo Show we can tell Alexa to show us the front door and she will bring up the live view.The only downfall that I was surprised with was that Ring needs a subscription to save your motion/doorbell events. It's not much, I think around $37 for the year. You can still use your camera to see who is at your door or walking in front of your house, but after a free 30 day trial, your motion events are gone as soon as they are over. Since I bought it to make sure packages stayed on my stoop, I needed to be able to see if someone walked up and picked it up, or if it was even delivered at all. To be clear this is the only reason I gave it 4 out of 5 stars. I like all the features and the ease of use. I would buy this camera again, but the upgraded model. This one records in 720p and the newer one is in 1080p which does make a difference as things get further away. Also the newer one comes with a "Lifetime" replacement if your Ring Doorbell is ever stolen (But I'm sure there's some fine print in there somewhere). Taking this off the mount seems fairly easy, but with the videos going to the cloud and not being stored on the device itself can provide you with a video of this event. I hope this review helps...

title: Wish I had read the 1 star reviews before buying this product.
body: Update on 5/7/17After my review, I was contacted by a wonderful CSR from Ring Support division. She took care of all my issues. She sent me new Ring Pro and took care of the billing issues I had with Ring.com. I had waited to update my review because I had to install the new doorbell and it was extremely hard to get any electrician to even touch the product. After a month of going around in circles with the electricians, I gave up and solicited a coworker who had some electrical knowledge (my issue was mainly with the electrical wire coming out to the doorbell being too short--> which installs fine with Ring, but does not work well with Ring Pro). So far the Ring Pro is working extremely well.Thank you Jennifer at Ring Support. If there was a separate category for product and support, she would get 5 stars for the support.--------------With RING -->Do not buy until you read the reviews.I bought the package of Ring Doorbell, Stickup Cam and the solar panel. This came in highly recommended by friends. Wish I had read the critical reviews first before jumping in and buying the product. I have outlined what happened below. Before commenting, please know that I am very tech savvy. I run my own network in the house, have awesome wi-fi router with 150/150 from FIOS. Every corner of my house has strong wifi signal. The doorbell, outside, with the door closed has 85/85 speed on wifi. My doorbell is on the front door which has a covering on the top (patio). No direct sun or wind or rain can fall on it as the patio is 5x5 with solid roof on the top.Within installation of the ring doorbell, I realized that i was getting lots of false alarms with cars running on the street. My street is approximately 50 feet away from my door, just like most homes in the USA.I chatted with the support staff, and they gave me lot of BS about how their camera is Infrared and it picks up the heat signature of the cars etc. Their suggestion was to put some washers behind the plate so that the camera is pointing downwards.After doing that, I realized that i was still getting tons of false positives, but also it had stopped responding to human beings. For example, it will pickup cars going in the street, but will not pickup any humans who move towards your house. If you walk more than 5 miles/hour speed, this camera will not pick it up. I also noticed that if the person walked really fast to the door and did something and walked away, it will only capture the portion of them moving away. All of these captured on my video stream and the support personnel also looked at that. I removed the washers and the problems still continued. During this time, I had a security incident where someone approached my house, came to the door and slid an envelope thru the door. The RING did not catch any of that.It was basically going to sleep and would not wake up until the door bell was rung. After the bell was run, it will stay awake for few hours and then go to sleep.The stupidest reason given to me by Ring Community Support→ if you don't answer the motion, the door bell thinks that you are home and don't need any more alerts so goes to sleep. WTFrench??? Is this for security or not? If it is, it should be sending alerts out EVERY TIME. Not go to sleep. It defeats the purpose of the security if it goes to sleep.After yelling and screaming, the support personnel, they sent me a replacement. Still it was Ring and not the ring pro.During this time, my stickup cam located in my backyard, never had any problems. Always caught the motion, only human beings and alerted me properly. I also did not have to check it regularly (meaning, i didn't have to check it every time thinking it may go to sleep. It worked beautifully).Lets talk about replacement.The replacement worked great for 4 days before showing the exact same behavior. Going to sleep, not picking up human motion or humans. Staying active for few hours after the door bell is rung. Only going active when the doorbell is rung so no video of before someone ringing the door-bell. I did not even bother putting up the washers since I knew this to be a software bug and not hardware or anything else.Right now, it is not picking up anything for 15-30 seconds. Then it picks up that something is at the door. But by that time, it is too late.When I tried to ask for a refund, they told me that it can only be done thru paypal. Now I have to go to Amazon and try to get a refund from them.Final Recommendation → DO NOT BUY. This product is not ready for primetime. They have major issues with software glitch and they know it. The software is based on infrared motion sensing. But it does not want to pick up anything that is smaller than car (since cars generate lots of heat, it will pick that up easily). I know that it may work for some of you, but I am terribly disappointed in this product. I will steer anyone I know away from this product as this is the worst thing I have seen. Sad part is that their techs know about these issues but they have not bothered to fix them.

title: Standard and Pro are both worth your consideration; Excellent customer support; Enhance your Home Security
body: I have both a Ring Doorbell and a Ring Doorbell Pro, and I enjoy both of them very much now that they are installed and operational. I first ordered a certified refurbished standard Ring doorbell thinking no big deal, things get refurbished all of the time and I can save a few bucks. After a whole lot of frustration during setup with the refurbished model, I returned the refurb and paid extra dollars for a new in box product. Whatever was wrong with the refurb was clearly a hardware issue; my replacement product installed in a matter of minutes and was working decently. Depending on product placement and construction of your house, you may need to consider a Wi-Fi extender. I was making due without an extender, but I'd find myself experiencing network latency issues that caused choppy video playback and broken sound. Because I strive for a smart home that truly works as advertised, I added the Netgear EX2700 that Ring recommends in their support bulletin. The way I see it, if I'm going to drop $200 on a doorbell, I may as well drop another $30 to get my network up to speed so that it can support said doorbell.[...]There are some very distinct differences between the standard model and the Pro version that are important to consider when purchasing your Ring.HD Camera - Pro will give you 1080p; standard renders 720p.Battery - Pro uses a power pack that requires additional installation; standard uses a built-in battery. Both can make use of existing wiring.Live View - With both the Pro and the standard models, you can view your camera feed on-demand by pressing the Live View button in your app! This is a great feature that they have added, but it only exists when your setup is hard-wired to a power source. Without, the continuous power supply, the setup defaults to preserving the battery life of your doorbell so that it will maximize your security benefits.Audible Alert - Pro requires that you purchase Chime ($30) for a doorbell to still ring within your home. Standard will continue to ring your doorbell's buzzer/chime/ding-dong-sound-maker without installing any additional hardware.Personally, if I didn't care about having the higher resolution camera on the front of my house (to catch license plates of vehicles driving by) I would have opted for the standard model at both the front and the back entrances of my house. The hardware setup of the standard model is overall an easier process and the battery is built-in, so there's not much need to mess around with wires.I have to give praise to their support department. I've worked in the technology field for over 15 years and I needed to contact them for assistance in setting up my Pro. Due to my experience in the field, it turned out that my network password was slightly stronger than the product is designed for during the setup process. Their support was easy to reach and friendly, and their first level support had immediate access to second level support. That last part is a big deal to me because it means that you will not be bounced around from person to person or team to team over the course of days while waiting for your problem to be resolved. They also came through with the appropriate solution in a reasonable timeframe, so I was happy with the fact that the company stands behind its product.Pros: Small footprint works with existing wiring in order to bring life (and smart-home automation through IFTTT) to a product that we never realized was capable of more. Price point is reasonable and competitive for video doorbells. Customer support is easily reached and proved to be beneficial when needed.Cons: In order to save any of your streaming videos online, you need to pay the annual $30 subscription fee for each device. This is pricey when compared with Ring's competitors, and it can add up quickly. I'd like to see them offer a discount for multiple devices, and I'd really love to see them add capabilities to redirect your videos to a network attached storage device on the local network. While I don't envision them offering the ability for local storage, I do think they will come around to offering a multiple product discount in the future.Overall: Ring Doorbell gives you access to a business-grade security doorbell at a reasonable price for the standard home user. Installation could be tricky at times, but the product is backed by effective customer support. Adding this product to your existing home can help your feel like you're stepping into the future with a fun accessory, but it can also become a great addition to your home security network.

title: Answer the Doorbell from Anywhere
body: I've had our Ring installed for about 8 months now and my feelings on it are mixed. I really like the idea of being able to answer your doorbell from where ever you are. I also really like that Ring acts as a motion detector based on settings the user controls. (It is very easy to set the areas and distance you want monitored.) The app is very user friendly and can be set up by anyone. Literally - just looking through the app will familiarize you with everything you need to know. It isn't necessary to read an instruction manual or remember complicated settings. Straightforward, without additional crap to waste space or confuse you.The Ring itself is attractive and is incredibly easy to install. The manufacturer has made a couple of videos that will walk you through installation, whether you are hard wiring it, or just attaching it to your wall. I'm glad they offer the option of not connecting it to your old doorbell (hard wiring) as I can't - the recent siding project my HOA did makes that impossible. Measuring, drilling, and placing the unit are super easy - I watched the video, pausing it as I needed to. Everything but a drill is included in the package - you won't need to search for a drill bit, level, or even screwdriver! The Ring unit is secured by two little "special" screws at the bottom. You would never notice they are there, but they provide security for your unit. The screws are the final step of mounting the unit and are all you need to remove when you need to charge the unit (if you don't hard wire it). The kit includes the "special" screwdriver. After installing my Ring (which took about 20 minutes), I placed everything back in the molded plastic tray in the box and stuck the box in my utility closet. It's a great way to keep everything together.When someone presses the button, your cell phone is notified. The chime you get when someone actually rings the bell is different than the chime that goes off when the unit detects movement. You also get a message on the phone - "Someone is at your front door" or "There is motion detected at your front door". ("Front Door" is what I named the unit - you can name the units anything you want, which is helpful when you have more than one unit installed.) I have included a screenshot of the activity list the app keeps for you.What I don't like about Ring...the biggest issue is the time lapse between someone pressing the button and the video showing up on my screen. There is nothing but a black screen approximately 3 out of 5 times. If I waited for the video to show up before answering the chime, visitors would be gone before I said anything. I don't know why, but the delay is significant. It's easy to review video from the past, but I cannot get the video to show up in an acceptable amount of time. This part REALLY bugs me and I feel it renders the instant security part of the unit useless. If someone rings the bell and only a black screen shows up on my phone, what's the point?I also had a hard time setting the sensitivity for the motion detector. I live in a townhouse and the unit kept alerting to people and cars going by on the street. When I reduced the sensitivity I would only get notifications if someone was right up on my front porch. It was like the unit had difficulty monitoring my front path (which goes straight out from the unit). This resulted in constant alerts, which drained the battery and forced me to charge it every month. Although it is very easy to charge the unit, it does take some time (at least overnight) and the whole unmounting/mounting thing was just a pain when you have to do it that often. Now that the detection area and I have reached an agreement, the unit doesn't go off when my flowers sway in the wind and I have not had to charge the unit for the last 6 months.Would I purchase this again? Yes. Customer service is top notch - my unit stopped working and I couldn't figure it out. I called customer service and they had me back up and running very quickly (apparently an update installation problem). You have to pay to use the cloud service (although you do get the first 30 days free), but it's a small fee and easily justifiable when you consider what you get. If the video transmission was faster I would definitely give this 5 stars. Since I have trouble with that part of the program, I deducted a star. It would be AWESOME if the system was reliable and instantly pulled up the video.

title: Ring stands behind their replacement policy
body: I bought Ring about a month ago via a web site that accepts payments through Amazon but wasn't purchased via Amazon prime. That was probably my first mistake. I received the Ring device about $10.00 cheaper than what I was seeing on Amazon at that time. I received the device quickly and it included the complete installation kit: Micro USB charging cable, Drywall screws, Screwdriver, Screwdriver drill bit, wood screws and masonry anchors, weather sealant, mounting bracket and level. In our case the install was the more complicated option since we needed to install it in our brick front house and connect it to our existing digital doorbell wiring. My husband ended up having to drill out the door bell wiring a bit more so that we could accommodate installing it with the existing doorbell wiring which was recessed a little too far into the wall. Unfortunately what should have been an easy install turned into a very difficult install experience. The directions are fairly straightforward if you are just doing a normal install. The Ring app has a very nice video that gives you step by step instructions even for a case like ours where we needed to use a diode to wire it up to our existing digital doorbell chimes. After following the directions first problem we had was that the diode did not work and fell apart ( I guess it was too old). So we decided to just install Ring without connecting it to the doorbell. No joy there either. After testing it out, we found that Ring would alert us through the app that someone was at the door but when we attempted to answer, no one could hear us and we couldn't hear them. Interestingly enough later we pulled the video from the cloud and found that it was recording every word of our conversation but we just couldn't hear each other. Obviously a problem with the speaker on the device. So, I called Ring support and asked for help. Their help line was very busy and suggested we leave our number so that they could call us back when an operator was free to help. We left a message but no one called back. I tried the online method next and was able to get someone to answer by email the next day. The tech suggested we basically take Ring back to its factory settings (The standard reboot the device) and try to install again. (We had already done this ourselves a couple of times at this point). When we removed the back cover one of the pieces of the doorbell electronics literally fell out. We put it back in and were able to reboot the device and set it up again with the app. Still no sound on either end during testing. The next day I got an email from a different tech again requesting we hit the big orange reset button on the back of the Ring device. I complied but complained that we'd already done that previously and it didn't help. At this point, I was getting impatient for it to work since we were planning a trip out of town in a week and Ring is something we wanted to put in place as part of our home automation/security installation project. I asked the Ring tech if we couldn't just get a replacement device. Again all of the communication took place by email. No one EVER called us back. A week goes by and we finally got a brand new device from Ring but this time with no install kit. (Good thing I kept the install kit from the failed device we had). This time we were able to get Ring to work without any problems. I can only assume that the first device we had was a bad one. Although it took longer than I wanted, Ring is running and is working without any problems. The video quality is very good. You can set the motion detector on it as sensitive as you want and it will tell you every time a car goes by your home (annoying) or you can scale it back to only notify you when someone is in your driveway. You get 30 days of cloud recording for free and after that it costs $30.00 per year. So that's how Ring is affording to replace devices if they are defective or get stolen. They aren't making money from the sale of the device itself as much as they will off that yearly service fee. The Ring app for my Android phone works without any problems on mine and my husband's phone. We get notified when anyone is on our doorstep or driveway without fail (as long as the wireless is working in your house which is how it is networked). Overall, I'm happy with this device and glad to report that Ring stands behind their replacement policy. I'll update here if we have any problems with it over time.

title: Great product, great support, tricky installation
body: Well, it’s finally installed and working well, but it was a struggle. Kudos to Ring Support team for proactive help through the process. My wife and I really like the device, it’s perfect for our sprawling house on an island, and we’ll likely get another for the side door. Here’s what happened:First, hardware installation was straightforward. I used the wires from our old, no longer functioning doorbell to attach to the Ring. I wasn’t sure which wire went where, but Support responded rapidly and said it didn’t matter. That information should be in the very brief manual. The only tricky part was getting the Ring to seat firmly on the back plate. The secret is to press in as well as down so it snaps into place on the plastic hooks. (I actually did the hardware install last, but I’m getting it out of the way here.)The software installation was much harder, and eventually Ring sent a new device out to me when they somehow saw the original was connected to my home network but still not working properly. Our house has a basement (with our FIOS router) plus three more floors. We have a dual band AmpedWireless router bridged to the FIOS router on the top floor. DHCP comes from the FIOS router. We’re also using Powerline connected to access points to spread the wifi through the house. To get a strong Wi-Fi signal at the front door, I put a Netgear Powerline module near the door and attached a TP-Link Access Point (TL-WA901ND, $37 from Amazon). This only needed to be single band, because the Ring works on the 2.4Ghz band. The Ring Chime device plugged into an outlet in our inside hall uses the same SSID off the Access Point. Having an indoor chime is convenient in a big house.With the strong signal from the nearby access point, the replacement device installed on the first try--well, almost on the first try. You have to clip along rapidly in the install process or it will time out. Our Wi-Fi password is long, with upper and lower case characters, and I timed out a couple of times before successful completion. Ring should consider lengthening the time limit to make a connection.I recommend signing up for the $3 per month option for cloud storage of the videos of any doorbell incidents. I someone comes when you are away, and you are not near your phone to get the notification in time to interact with your visitor, you won’t know who came to your door. Signup happens through the Ring app on your phone. However there was a programming bug: the software put nice spaces between credit card number groups, but didn’t allow for those spaces when counting characters. I could not enter the last few digits of my credit card number. Fortunately you can also sign up on the Ring website.I’m not sure why the first device didn’t work and why we needed a replacement. It may have been the device, but it also could have been the network. When you do the installation, you associate the device with a particular SSID and password. If anything in your network changes so that address is not available, for whatever reason, your device is unresponsive. It needs a stable, strong network signal, not an unreasonable requirement.The user experience: When someone rings the video doorbell or activates the motion detector, the device evidently communicates with the Ring servers, presumably in the cloud somewhere. You get a notification on your mobile phone. You then need to open your phone, open the Ring app (you’ll want it on your first app screen) so the video call can proceed, and accept the call. This can take several seconds, so a good signal really helps. The video on my iPhone 5s is sharp and the voice communications with the person at the door are clear and loud. Some visitors may freak out, not realizing at first that you can see and hear them.The new packaging is very good.I score this product 4 out of 5 only because getting it installed was such a hassle. The product is excellent and so is the support. Highly recommended.

title: Exceeds expectations
body: I purchased and installed the Ring doorbell device about four weeks ago. Here are my thoughts and impressions (in random order)1. The packaging was excellent2. The install went real well. I have a techy and mechanical background so that might play into the results. At a couple points I had to infer what the next step was since it did not match exactly what the instructions indicated. I have to do this all the time in my job, so it wasn't a big deal to me.3. The field of view and picture quality is is pretty good considering all the wireless connections involved. In some cases I'm miles away at a restaurant or some other location and I get a decent streaming video feed. People are recognizable if they are standing relatively close to the camera.4. The audio quality is fine if the person at the door speaks clearly and with a normal speaking volume. The speaker volume (what the person at the door hears) can be sketchy so it seems you need to speak quite loudly into your phone. This could be a bit odd if you're located in a public place and don't want to disturb others nearby you (for example, sitting in a restaurant).5. The motion settings are adjustable with six different zones to turn on or off and it includes an overall sensitivity level setting. This turns out to be a not so useful feature for me. I live on a street that is part of the city bus route. We get buses about 2-3 times per hour passing by our house. At first I had the settings at about mid range with all the zones turned on and it was picking up buses about half the time. This can be annoying so I throttled the settings way back and turned off most of the zones and I was still getting a few "bus" events. I worked with the Ring support team and they had me install the latest firmware which has improved the situation quite a bit, but still not to a point where I can leave the motion detection on all day. There would still be too many "bus" events so I just leave it off all together.6. The support team was real good to work with. I had two chat sessions with them to try resolving the motion detection issue. The support person was very helpful and understood the issue. In the end we did not find a solution to the motion detection problem, but despite the outcome I was very happy with the level or support. It was clear that the development team at Ring is fully aware of the issue with city buses (and large garbage trucks) and is actively working to improve the algorithm that they use for motion detection. So this gives me hope that a solution is in the works.Wish ListNow that I've used the device for about a month here are some features that I wish it included.1. It would be great if I could initiate a video recording session at any time. Right now it only records when it detects motion or when someone pushes the doorbell button.2. The default setting when you are receiving a call (someone pushes the doorbell button) is to have the microphone and speaker turned on. In some cases I've wanted to mute the mic right away because I want to check out what's going on at my door before I decide if I'm going to speak to the person or not. If I were in a noisy environment, say a sports event, and I hit the Accept button to take the call, the person at my door is going to hear the noise on my end even if I don't start speaking right away. There is an icon on the screen that allows you to mute the mic, but in the split second that it takes to tap on that icon the person at the door is going to hear something. That's where it would be nice to have the option to accept the call with the mic already muted.3. I wish there was a way to adjust the mic settings on the device. The ambient noise level is rather loud and I wonder if they could add the ability to adjust a "noise cancellation" level on the mic.

title: Useful but has warts
body: I like my Ring Video doorbel (l [[ASIN:B00N2ZDXW2 Ring Wi-Fi Enabled Video Doorbell]] ) usually; it is amusing and useful. But it isquite sensitive to your internet speeds, and the delayed and choppy videos reduce its utility. I got my Ring in December 2015, and I have it hardwired and use it without a Chime and without integrating with any other home automation tools.* Pros- Can see when people get home and leave- Can talk to people at the front door (e.g. "please wait, I'm coming" or "hi, kids!" or I can ignore them if I'm not interested)- Nice videos for setup and install- Fairly easy to do basic install- Email tech support is responsive- Nice Mac OS X application* Cons- Strong wifi and fast internet are required, and the doorbell is quite sensitive to this- Motion sensor tuning is tricky- Delayed video starts means I miss who came in the door- Poor video / audio quality occasionally (not always)- "Trickle charge" from wired doorbell isn't enough to keep the doorbell charged, and video quality deteriorates as the charge drains- Button on doorbell can be sticky; if you don't press it in the center, then the press sometimes doesn't register, or registers only enough to ring the internal chime but not enough to send a notification and video to my phone- No Windows 7/8 or Linux application support- No battery level indicator for hardwired doorbell- Some key facts I needed for troubleshooting were hard to find (e.g. reset procedure)I got it to see who's ringing the doorbell interrupting dinner and decide whether to go downstairs or not. For that, the response time is usually too slow to be useful; if I wait to see the video, I'll either seem rude or they'll think I'm not home. But I've twice spoken to someone at the door to ask them to wait while I get to the door or similar; that surprises people but has been very useful.The motion detection has also been useful; my kids loved it when I spoke to them as they got home from school, while I sat in the airport waiting for my flight home. I can see exactly when the cleaners arrive at and leave the house, and when someone enters the first floor apartment.The 'trickle charge' that my wired doorbell gets is not enough to keep the doorbell charged; eventually it runs down. And when the charge winds down, I find that the doorbell motion sensor is less responsive and Live View stops working. So every couple months I have to take the doorbell down to charge it overnight.Tuning the motion detection is a pain. During the summer the reflection of cars driving by (outside the range I told the doorbellto respond to) keeps setting off the motion sensor.There is a notable delay before the motion sensor video starts; too long to see who it is when someone gets to the door and opens it with a key, unless they pause for a while to fumble with bags. Often all I see is the door closing.The doorbell is very sensitive to the wifi effective bandwidth. Shortly after getting my Ring, I upgraded my router and relocated it to be closer to mydoorbell - it simply wouldn't work without doing that. Even with those changes, the video from the doorbell is often slow to start and of low quality, even though my internet speeds are over the suggested minimums. Sometimes the video is fine though, and it isn't entirely clear why. Keeping the doorbell fully charged helps. Twice I've done a full reset on the doorbell (at tech support's suggestion) to try to improve things. My tested speeds using their preferred speed tester are 28Mbps down, and 2Mbps up; that should be plenty, and usually is. But losing the first 4-5 seconds of video and then getting choppy or low resolution video makes it harder to use the doorbell as I had hoped.

title: Very good Doorbell, highly recommended, with room for improvements
body: I was looking for a doorbell which I could see who was outside at the door without going to the door, especially at night when is quite difficult to see trough the spyhole.I have the doorbell for two weeks now and I’m very pleased with it so far, that’s why I decided to give it a review.This doorbell does the job very well. The setup is straightforward, just download the free app and follow the instructions. Took me less than 5 minutes to setup to my Wi-Fi.It also comes with all the tools necessary to install the doorbell, 5 stars on this.I did a lot of research before buying the Ring doorbell, there are other on the market but this is the only one with battery, I don’t have a wiring setup for my doorbell. There was a bit of mix reviews about the quality of the doorbell and the time between someone ringing the doorbell and you getting the notification on the phone.I found no problems with the quality of the doorbell, looks tough enough to survive the winter weather conditions, I live in the UK.About the latency, time between someone ringing the doorbell and me getting the notification on my phone, I found no problems here so far. It takes no more than 3 seconds to get the notification. You need good bandwidth and more than 1Mb upload speed. I have about 2Mb upload speed and in 2 weeks I never had any problems with latency.When someone rings the doorbell you can see, hear and talk to person at the door, even if you not at home. The quality of video and sound are good. The sound quality can suffer a little in windy conditions. The night vision picture quality is ok, probably some room for improvement in this area.There is also a motion detection feature. This is a very clever feature, people don’t need to ring the doorbell but the doorbell will detect people and send you a notification, you then can see them and talk to them. It’s a pretty cool feature to put off thieves. This can be over sensitive, I don’t see it as a bad thing because even when it’s just people passing by your house you can see what’s going on.Ring states that the battery last about a year, so far in 2 week my doorbell is 80%, so I doubt will last one year, but it doesn't take too long to charge, so this is not an issue to me.A very good selling point is the lifetime purchase protection, if your doorbell gets stolen Ring will replace it for free, you just need to send a police report.One feature that is missing is the ability to stream video on demand. The doorbell must be pressed, or the motion sensor must be triggered before you can see what is going on. I read that Ring is working on this so hopefully they will implement soon.Another improvement I would like to see is the ability to integrate with Smartthings or other connected home platforms, such as Apple Homekit, and Nest.Ring now works with IFTTT, so you can create recipes, integrate your Ring doorbell with Philips Hue lights and more.If you don’t have a wiring connection, like me, you might want to consider buying the Ring Chime so you can hear the door when you're not by your phone. If you have a wiring connection than you have the added bonus of your mechanical doorbell also chiming.Another positive about Ring is their customer support service. Twice I had contact them and twice I had a reply in a couple of hours. The last time I contact them was on a Sunday, in about 2 hours I had a reply. Their customer support service is very quick and helpful.The reason I don’t give it 5 stars is because being a new product there’s still some improvements to be made. But in 2 weeks with the Ring doorbell I already had updates e improvements made to the app and the integration with connected home platforms.Everyone at home is very happy with the Ring doorbell.

title: Initial Thoughts - Love it though audio and app responsiveness could be better.
body: I purchased the ring pro from a local box store but could not get it to connect to my wifi. Customer service very nice, but said I probably had a bad unit so returned to the store and swapped it out for a new one. Same issue with the second unit. Customer service and I believe it is due to the transformer between the door bell button and the chime on my 23 year old house is not allowing enough power (nothing wrong with my wifi). I was unable to locate the transformer so I returned the ring pro and tried the ring 2 (I love the concept). Something to think about if you try ring pro and are unable to get it to connect to your wifiBoth units are well packaged and come with everything you need to install - to include a masonry bit in case you have to install onto brick, wedges to adjust the viewing angle, screwdriver, two different colored face plates, extra wire, etc. These guys thought of everything. Due to the ring 2 being wider than the ring pro, I had to install to my brick exterior wall as opposed to replacing the space on my doorframe covered by my old door bell button - so I made use of the masonry bit. But you need to drill four holes to secure the plastic ring 2 to the wall. This is partly where ring 2 loses a star as getting four holes perfectly placed in masonry is a bit tricky - measure 10 times and drill once! I was also afraid that if the holes were slightly off that securing the screws may result in breaking the ring 2's plastic screw holes. Got the holes close enough but I can see this being an install nightmare.I also attached the unit to my old doorbell wires, so my old doorbell chime rings when the button is pressed. Plus, I read on ring's site that this allows the battery to be recharged from the doorbell power. Time will tell if I ever have to charge the battery.Once installed the video is fantastic and the field of view is more than wide enough to give a clear picture of my front house. Daylight is color; low light/night is black and white. Very clear in all conditions.I'm still playing with the motion settings. The motion sensor has a narrower (vertically) field of view - basically goes straight out and down. You are supposed to mount 4 feet off the ground but I have steps to my front door so my unit is much higher than four feet. As a result, right now I am picking up moving cars from the street 35 feet distant from the ring 2. Currently with maximum sensitivity set, every time a car passes I get a wind chime alert and the video rolls. I can then tap the green button to continue recording, or the red button to end the session. The video is then saved and listed for me to share (facebook, mail, text, or via nextdoor.com), delete, or keep. I can do the two finger split to zoom into the video to see that it was the squirrel that caused the motion alert. Very slick.Audio quality is still a work in progress, and this is where it loses part of a star. A lot of audio feedback on my iphone 6 until I turned the volume of the phone down. People on both ends are able to converse fine, but the feedback is currently annoying. Will have to research this part.Another slight negative - when someone rings the doorbell, there is a delay until the app wakes up and you are able to see and then take action (talk, decline, or just watch). When I playback the video, I notice I capture what I had missed while waiting for the app to wake up. But the delay from doorbell ringing to being able to see/talk to someone will take some getting used to.Bottom line - a few minor annoyances but I am happy with the product from opening the box, dealing with customer service, to its performance. Highly recommended.

title: The Good the Bad The Ugly BUT! It works
body: The Good, The Bad, The Ugly!The Good.It Works! Seriously It works. Now there is a bit more to it that I will get into in the bad but I does work. The packaging was fantastic, and I know what your thinking the packaging really your going to talk about the packaging. How many times have you ever purchased a home item be that a doorbell or camera, or light switch or anything that needs to be mounted and wired? How many times did you have to go back to the hardware store to purchase a tool or a drill bit or screws or something else that wasn't included with what you purchased? Ring gives you everything! I mean it everything screws anchors and even a drill bit that works perfectly for brick stucoo or concrete! I installed mine into brick granted I needed to use my impact drill because of the brick but the included bit created a perfect whole for the anchors to mount Ring. Wiring was simple just took the wiring from my old door bell and connected it simply to the ring back plate. I have had NO issues with battery, and it gets a charge from my old doorbell wiring, and I mean old. The App is very simple to use, and setup was a breeze. Its great to be able to see everyone who comes close to my front doorThe BadWhen It was first installed and connected to my network the video was bad the responsiveness from connecting from my phone to the ring was bad it was about to come off the wall and sent back to the store. That said this wasn't an issue with the Ring this was an issue with me, well no it was also some what rings fault. In rings videos and promo material it looks super simple and easy to use right? They dont tell you when your WIFI router is on one side of the house and the ring on another side of the house and even outside of the house it doesn't work too well. My router was pretty far away and it caused the ring to have a less then stellar connection to my network which resulted in the issues I was having. Now none of the information provided by ring in the box tells you this and this may be why a lot of people give it one star simple put unless your have a strong router with a great single outside of our house your going to have issues. I only determined this through Rings support section of their website. What I did was buy a 30 dollar netgear wifi extender place it in an outlet close to my front door and bam super strong single = awesome video and response time. I wish they would tell you this, and I hate that I had to buy the extender but now I have strong single all over my house so that's the silver lining?The uglyAfter getting the video and audio fixed with the extender I only have three complaints.1. I cant tune in any time I want.
