Now I hear this will be updated in a firmware update but I would like to take a peak to whats going on outside of my front door whenever I want is that too much to ask for? Is this a deal breaker no, not really at all its just nitpicking because at the end of the day if the ring doesn't detect motion outside why would you wanna tune in just to watch some leaves blowing around?2. I hate the wind angle tunnel picture that you don't see in the promo videos. Make no mistake the Promo video on rings website is not what you see through the ring camera it has a tunnel look like the camera is back too far in the housing or the wide view is just so wide it captures the ends of the casing. That is pretty disappointing.3. No support for Smarthosue now this may change in the future but right now Ring is just that Ring, it doesn't work for any smart lock yet!All in all It is great fun way to spy on your mailman.

title: Much better than Skybell and works well, but some reservations
body: I first tried the Skybell video doorbell, mainly because I had read somewhere that it was compatible with Apple's HomeKit (which, it turned out, it is not!). Skybell was unsatisfactory in several ways...difficult to install, poor video quality, inconsistent motion detection....and I finally gave up on it and returned it. Then I ordered a Ring, and I am delighted with it. First, installation was relatively easy, taking only a few minutes. All of the necessary tools are included except of course a power drill. The only hitch I ran into was that when snapping the unit onto the mounting plate, it was difficult to push down securely onto the mounting tabs, but with perseverance I was able to do it. The performance of the unit is excellent. Video quality, even though it does give a fish-eye rounded view, is much, much better than Skybell. Motion detection is reliable, and I love the way it is adjustable as to both sensitivity and the regions to activate. Even the night vision is good, and faces can be recognized, in contrast to Skybell. Plus, the unit itself looks really nice. Since it will run on the installed battery, I did not have to wire it to my doorbell wires, which would have necessitated mounting it well off to the side of the door. Of course using the battery instead of the doorbell wires does mean that it will not ring my regular doorbell, but since it does ring my iPhone instantly, I don't foresee this being a problem. The only feature I wish it had is video on demand, so that I could turn on the video even if no one is at my door (Skybell does have this feature) , but I'm told that this is a feature that will probably be added in the near future. All in all, I am happy with this purchase. Should it have problems in the future, I will update this review accordingly.Update: Well, unfortunately I must lower my rating from 5 stars down to 3. The reason is that after a couple of months I've started having trouble with the motion sensor. It was working fine until I turned off this feature for awhile, to avoid having it send alerts every time I walked out onto my front porch. Then I turned it back on when I went on vacation, and then it worked very inconsistently. Many times it would not send an alert when there was motion. I contacted the company about this, and they responded that they were doing a system update, which should fix the problem. Then it started sending false alerts, when there was no motion. This became annoying and I again turned off the feature. I'm disappointed in this, but am still happy with the basic doorbell features.Another update: Ok, I have re-activated the motion sensor feature, and I now find that it is working just fine. Evidently they have improved this feature. Everything about the Ring doorbell is working properly and accordingly, I am raising my rating back to a full five stars. I am happy with the product!And yet one more update: After using this product for almost two years, I am disappointed in the battery life. I find that I have to recharge the battery about every couple of months. I am also disappointed that Ring has been saying for a year or longer that they plan to add a Live View feature, but as of now it still has not been added to the doorbells that work on battery (they have added it to the ones that are hard wired). And also, they have been saying that it will soon become compatible with the Apple HomeKit, but this has not happened either. I am lowering my rating from 5 stars to 4 stars, because of these facts.

title: Solid product; I look forward to some future improvements.
body: There are GREAT things, GOOD things, and COULD-BE-IMPROVED things about the Ring doorbell. On the whole, this is a very good product, and well worth the cost.I'll go in order of my experience:ORDERING/RECEIVING the doorbell - GREAT. It arrived super-quickly, with terrific packaging - I could not believe the lovely little canvas "wrap" of tools that came with it. That was an unexpected and very helpful surprise.APPEARANCE - GOOD. The four color options are probably enough. I chose the dark bronze color and I am very happy with it. It's not too big and it doesn't look odd on the house.INSTALLATION - COULD-BE-IMPROVED. this was the trickiest part, but I have to say the support options were really impressive. There were all kinds of videos and it was very easy to find the specific video for my purpose ( = replacing a wired doorbell on a brick wall). The video itself left out a couple of very basic instructions (uh, need a hammer drill to drill into brick, and the masonry bit that came with the doorbell wasn't quite adequate with my house's hard brick), and the visuals were poor - the installation process was obscured by the installer so I couldn't see what was going on at a couple of specific junctures. Despite that, got the thing installed without too much effort. The package even contained the wall anchors and screws I needed!SOFTWARE - GREAT. Really nice design, very intuitive. Super easy install, had no trouble connecting the Ring to the house wifi. Adjusting the settings (for example the range/zones for motion sensing) is super easy.VIDEO /AUDIO - GREAT. Terrific quality.UTILITY? - GOOD. The doorbell alerts reliably (although there are occasional times when I would have expected an alert from motion sensing, but didn't get one). There is sometimes a delay between the alert and the video popping up on the phone (the phone alerts, I touch the notification on the screen and the phone says "Opening...." for a while). Once the video comes up, you have a little time to decide whether to Accept or Deny the "call" and while you are deciding you can zoom in to see what's going on in the video if you like. If you Accept the call, the sound turns on. If you Deny the call, the sound doesn't turn on. You can also ignore/close the notification on your phone very easily (for example, when you are leaving your house and the thing alerts), so "false positives" are not annoying. As for the cloud storage, 30 dollars a year is a bargain IMO. I am not sure if the videos store on your phone without it - if not, that would be pretty disappointing.FUTURE IMPROVEMENTS I WANT: (1) the ability to turn on the video/audio (or at least JUST the video) without the doorbell initiating it. This is a serious and obvious missing feature. I have heard that it is in the works, and I really hope so. (2) the ability to choose the alert tone. The silvery-bell thing is fine, but variety is nice.Bottom line: if you want a video doorbell, I would recommend this one. It's attractive and easy to use, and you will not regret the purchase. Everyone who has seen this on my house or whom I have told about it on Facebook wants one - I will probably be sprinkling these around come Christmastime :) I will also probably be getting a second one for the back door.I've been using it for about a month now; I'll give it a few more months and come back to update if I have more to say.

title: Great customer service but product has some shortcomings
body: We installed the Ring doorbell 2 weeks ago and overall, we like the unit. Installation was fairly easy. We have it hardwired onto a brick wall. Getting the unit attached to the back plate is a little tricky so I'm glad it's hardwired and the battery won't need to be recharged since it's constantly getting power.My router is upstairs but I do have a WiFi extender downstairs that is fairly close to where the Ring is mounted. I've done a few speed tests and I'm well above the minimum 1 mb recommended upload speed.The picture is decent. It is pixelated sometimes but nothing too bad. The picture (background) can be too bright at times but I think it may be due to the fact that I have the doorbell installed under a covered porch facing west. I've talked to customer service about the brightness and they said they made an adjustment but it didn't really make a difference. If the USPS/FedEx/UPS guy parks his truck in front of my house, you can barely see it. All you'll see is a white background. (I've attached a picture.) The background does gets better in the afternoon.When the doorbell is pushed, I am notified almost immediately on my phone and since it's hardwired, I get an audible ring inside my home. I love that I can talk to the person without having to open my front door.The problem I have is with the motion detection. I've worked with customer service many times on this issue and have given up mainly because after trying different things (adjusting the range (# of feet it detects something), the frequency (# of times it notifies me when it detects something), and the latency (I think this is the speed that the doorbell communicates with the network), nothing helps. The customer service guy attributes my problem to the way my porch is configured. I have a porch railing that may be the problem but I'm still not 100% convinced that it is. I want the Ring doorbell to start recording as someone is walking up to my porch. However, it starts recording when the person is either already at my front door or when they've already turned around and walking away. When I set the range all the way to the max (30 ft), I get notified when every single passing car drives by but it won't detect a human being that's maybe 5-10 feet away. The customer service explained that it has something to do with the heat signature (cars give off more heat than humans) which I understand but I still think it should detect a human more quickly than it actually does.Another thing I think can be improved is to be able to change the motion settings without having the physically push the doorbell. If for some reason I had to change the motion settings while away from home, I would not be able to do so because I wouldn't be physically there to push the button for the settings to save.Ring's customer service is excellent. Because of my motion detection issues, I've called, chatted and emailed with the customer service reps and have received nothing but excellent service. Each and every person that I've dealt with really tried to resolve my issues.I would have given it 5 stars if the motion detection worked the way I would like. I had thought about returning it because of the problems with the motion detection but despite its shortcomings, I decided to keep it because I believe having the Ring enhances our home security.

title: Great idea with easy setup
body: My experience so far with the Ring doorbell has been good. I was a little concerned about installing the doorbell, only because I have never installed a doorbell before and wasn't sure about how the wiring would work, or if it would work with our existing doorbell chime. One issue I ran into was the size of the Ring and the size of the wood area where my existing doorbell is located. The wood size is smaller and I wasn't able to get all the screws on the Ring mounting bracket in, although the ones I do have in seem to allow the ring to feel secure. Ring has a new Pro version of their doorbell out, for fifty dollars more, that is thinner than the orig8inal version I have. In the future when prices come down I might upgrade to the Pro version. The setup of the device was easy. Setting up the Ring, before installation, was simple and intuitive. I had it up on my wireless network and ready for installation in no time at all. To install the Ring, I removed my old doorbell, only 2 screws, disconnected the two wires (red and white), and mounted the Ring bracket. The Ring comes with all the tools you need to install the doorbell. I had a few issues connecting the Ring to the bracket, again because of the size of the wood and a brick wall next to the wood. My location didn't leave much room to easily connect the Ring onto the bracket. In a normal setup, the Ring should connect to the bracket easily. Once I was able to maneuver the Ring to align with the bracket, and attach it to the bracket correctly, I received power to the Ring from my existing wiring. You can go without using your existing wiring, but that would require you to run the Ring off of its internal battery. You can tell the Ring is receiving power from your wiring when the Ring button gloss a soft LED light.Once connected to the bracket and to my wiring, I tested out the doorbell and it worked with our existing chime and the application shows that Ring is receiving power from the hardwire power. At first the notifications on my Apple iOS phone were not being received. I had to uninstall the application and try again. Eventually the notifications to the phone of activity and ringing of the doorbell worked. I was able to use the application to add my wife and son to notifications on their phones. The application can send family members an email where they can accept to receive the notifications. I have my Ring setup to notify from both the doorbell ringing and from motion detected by the Ring doorbell. To start I only setup my Ring to see activity within 5 feet of the doorbell. Based on testing I think I will have to expand that a little bit as on the 5 foot setting, it didn't seem to detect when I walked near the doorbell. I just don't want to set the motion detector too far out as then it will notify everyone too often.Overall, I'm happy with the Ring but need to see it in every day use for a longer period of time. I feel the setup is easy, despite my tight installation location issues. In the future, if the Ring works out for me, I can see myself upgrading to the slimmer version. I really like the concept of the Ring and the cloud recording option. The Ring comes with 30 day trial of the cloud recording and to signup for a year is only $30. I think that is a reasonable charge compared to other cloud video recording options from other companies.

title: Now this device is ready for prime time!
body: Please read my favorable update to this review below.I've had this device for 2 days now and I agree with another reviewer, Rog. It looses WIFI connection completely making it totally useless. I have to reset my router to get it to work. As I write this, it is out again after reseting the router two hours ago. Also, as mentioned, if you don't click on the alert within a few seconds, you have no access whatsoever to what's going on until it is finished recording. By that time whatever was going on has finished. It can take as long as 60 seconds to show anything and not 30 seconds as some had mentioned. When it works, it works well. I get a lot of pixelation for unexplained reasons. My router is about 25 feet away and I have 75 download and 75 upload speeds. When adjusting the motion detection zones, it asks that you make the adjustment, then go out and ring the doorbell to save it. Silly. One other thing, when you click on accept to view the detected motion, the microphone is already on. Whoever is out there can hear you immediately whether you want them to or not. It should come up muted by default until you want someone to hear you. They may pickup chatter between you and someone else. I've complained to tech support and they are sending a replacement. If that doesn't work then it goes back. I really like the concept but so far, it is not ready for prime time.This is an update to my previous reviewHere is my updated review of the Ring doorbell. My initial experience was full of disappointment on what promised to be an extremely useful device. It fell short in many areas as I mentioned. Today I give it 4 stars. Tech support was extremely helpful with assisting me. Although it took three devices to finally get a working product, I am quite pleased with it now. The picture quality although not full HD is pretty good. The audio quality is very good. Motion detection works but still lacks consistency and requires a lot of tweaking. My only gripe at this point is that there is too much delay between the initial response and when you are connected. It can be as long as 30 to 40 seconds but there are less frequent times when it is immediate. One of my recordings of a solicitor came out near perfect and mimicked the demo on the Ring website almost perfectly. I was able to see and speak to the solicitor while I was 200 miles away. One very important point should be made for those of you who have a cellular service that is less than about 10mbps. You will be disappointed with the connection and may not see anything on your device although you can hear audio when motion is detected. I did have to upgrade my data service to a faster speed for an additional $10 monthly with t-mobile. If you are in need of a product such as this, I would highly recommend this device. Once you have it, you won’t want to be without it. Aside from the delay at times, my only other gripe is the way long lists of detected motion videos are handled. It is too cumbersome to delete hundreds of video shorts using the software as it is now. Going forward I would like to see two things: a panic button that could either set off your alarm system or a button that would immediately call the police after a quick confirmation that this is what you want to do. To summarize, I am very pleased with the device now thanks to the awesome technical support crew.

title: It's the best security device for $200
body: As is so often the case, people sometimes have unrealistic expectations. Here's my story.The reason I have a 4 star and not 5 is twofold. One, because the first unit was defective; there was no audio, and after several tests customer service said to return it. Second, because it can at times have a mind of its own. You just have to be patient, learn to delete the device from your app and re install (takes 5 minutes), and don't be afraid to call customer service because they are the absolute best you may ever encounter. They will literally do anything to help you get your Ring functioning properly.Once I got in my second unit, I found the audio to be crystal clear. I have a decent wi-fi setup, U-Verse. Our house is only 2,500 SF and it works perfectly all over. I was about ready to mount it after some tests, and then the motion detection system died. I deleted the device, reinstalled, and it seemed fine.I tested several locations, using double sided tape. I found that the existing doorbell location (long dead) worked just as well, maybe better, than installing it on the double door, facing directly into the street. Even with the range pulled back, beyond the covered porch it's pretty much whited out during the daytime, but on the side where the bell was located there's enough sun filtering that you can see enough to matter.There are complaints about the motion detection system not working quickly enough for the UPS or FedEx guy, and it doesn't. Next time one comes, time them. If they're slow, they're on and off your porch within 5 seconds. Of COURSE you'll see their backside! I used my iphone stopwatch and timed it. Once you get within about 5-6 feet of the device, you can actually hear the relay close, and that's when the recording starts. Within 10 seconds, you'll be connected with your iPhone. That's 15 seconds and most delivery people are back in the truck by then.That's not why I bought the device. I bought it for security, and anyone who shouldn't be there is going to hang around a lot longer than 15 second, and they WILL be recorded.The video is quite clear, certainly clear enough to identify someone. The fish eye lens if properly mounted will not allow someone to hide in a corner--you'll see them on the porch. The day vision is great, the low light vision I tested without a porch light is very good, the night vision with a porch light is very good too.The iPhone app is great. The customer service is outstanding, ask me how I know, and likely you will too. One thing that stumped me--I could not affix the device to the mounting plate. The young guy on the other end of the phone told me to sand down the plastic a bit. I used my wife's emory boards and bingo, it worked perfectly.So, some say not ready for prime time. I cannot fully disagree, there are still some glitches, but it's good enough for now and they will be issuing firmware updates as they become available. I trust that they are improving this constantly; I believe this new unit is a LOT better than the original issue.Given that there's no wiring, the outstanding customer service, the great app, and the quality of the video and audio, I'm glad to recommend this unit.

title: Good solution for answering doorbell from anywhere in my house (plus video)!
body: I bought a newly constructed house last year. As is often the case with a new house, I had lots of contractors and deliveries to greet as my new house became my home. In the first few months, I found the builder-installed traditional mechanical doorbell couldn't be heard in the bedrooms or bathrooms, especially when the room doors were closed. Indeed, even if I heard the doorbell, in our impatient modern times, folks sometimes I assumed that I wasn't home before I got to the door. I started searching for solutions and decided to try Ring since it would signal my iPhone and iPad via WiFi and also serve as an intercom (and, as it turns out, a nice additional feature is that the apps work whether or not you're on the same WiFi network as the Ring or on cellular data). Though I wasn't looking for the feature at the time, the bonus feature of Ring is its built-in high def video camera that works like a 21st Century security peep hole and (for an extra charge) can cloud save video when it detects motion or when someone presses the doorbell.Installation was pretty easy. Ring provided the correct size drill bit for the mounting plate -- the tough part was getting my cheap battery-powered cordless drill to get the holes in the stucco and cinder block deep enough. The electrical hook up was a matter of turning off the power to the old doorbell, removing the two screws that held the doorbell to the wall, loosening the two screws holding the electrical wires on the old doorbell and then attaching those wires to the Ring's mounting plate, and, of course, restoring power. Ring also provided putty to fill the old doorbell's screw holes. Downloading the Apps and WiFi setup were all very straight forward as well. The added steps I took that weren't in the instructions were to 1) caulk all around the mounting plate because, given stucco, the mount cannot sit flush against the wall no matter how deep I was able to sink the included screw anchors for the mount; and 2) cover wires (and, later, other exposed contacts) with black electrical tape. (Ring can be installed without connecting to a mechanical doorbell because of its internal lithium ion battery; apparently Ring will notify you when the battery is low requiring it to be recharged by removing it from the mount and charging via an included micro usb cable.)I had some problems with my unit and Ring.com customer service was great. They got a replacement unit out to me without hassle.As an early adopter of new technology, some issues are not unusual. Indeed, relying on WiFi is subject to all the usual hiccups. On the whole though, in the 5+ months since I decided to get Ring, it's done the job of letting me answer the door from most places, and, indeed, provided some additional nice-to-have features that now I can't imagine being without again. Ring.com has provided a few upgrades to the firmware and features just in the months I've had the device, and hopefully more will roll out soon (like the ability to turn on the camera even when no one has rung the bell which has been discussed on the company's blog). That elusive 5th star from me could only be earned if I couldn't think of ways the product could be further improved.

title: A video doorbell system that really works
body: I've been searching for a long time for an easy and reliable video doorbell system so I could see who was at the door before approaching or opening the door. I purchased this Ring doorbell directly from the manufacturer and used it to replace my previous standard wired door bell. It was easy to set up and install. My original doorbell transformer provides the power and the device still rings my original doorbell. This Ring device requires that you have a good WiFi signal at the door you are mounting it to. You may want to consider a WiFi extender if your signal is weak by your door. After using it for the past four months, these are my observations:1. The doorbell really works the way they say it will. When someone presses the doorbell button, all the devices that have the Ring software installed (android, ipad, and Windows 10 computer in my case) will alert you to the door button push and give you an opportunity to see and talk to the person at the door, while recording the entire episode. There is about a 5 to 10 second delay between the time of the doorbell button press and when the software alerts comes through.2. To use as a home security application you really need to subscribe to their Cloud Video Recording which costs $30 per year. This is really quite reasonable. They will store any video for 6 months and you can always download the videos you want to keep onto your home computer or tablet. Other webcam security systems I've tried require my home computer be on all the time to capture video. No need with the Ring system. You only need your WiFi to be up all the time.3. Video quality is satisfactory but not great. This is why I really give it only 4 1/2 stars. You get a nice fish-eye view of the entire area in front of the Ring device. They have their new Pro model with higher resolution camera that I plan to try next. Sound quality was fine.4. Motion detection feature can be customized for sensitivity. It uses an infrared signature to detect motion, so items that inherently give off heat (people, cars) will trigger it. You can roughly set how sensitive it is in each visible quadrant. This works reasonably well. I always get an alert whenever the garbage truck goes by - but it's a good indication that everything is working for me.5. Video on demand was recently added. This greatly increases the usefulness of this device for me, since now I can see what my door bell camera is seeing whenever I want (and it gets recorded too). If I'm on vacation and want to see what the weather is doing at my house, no problem. I plan to add a second camera to my garage service door so I can keep tabs on my driveway.Update 9/7/17: This original Ring camera is still working fine. I've had the Ring Pro installed on my side door now for the past few months. For the extra money, the Pro version is a much nicer camera. The extra resolution is great and the ability to define regions of interest for motion detection is something I really like. I plan to replace my original Ring front door camera with the Pro version soon. I've dropped my rating of the original Ring to 4 stars, since they set the bar so much higher with their Ring Pro.

title: Change of review for the Ring
body: First let me say that my son loves his. It seems to work every time. I purchased The Ring from the Ring Website. It worked for awhile, but then I started having problems--the same ones that have been posted/reviewed by others. I set the motion detection to 5'. It picked up cars going by at 30'. Sometimes it appeared to alert to no motion. In addition, there was a lag as to when it started to record and alert me. Sometimes you could see the backside of someone leaving the house (not worth anything if you can't see who was there) and sometimes it didn't even start recording until the UPS guy was gone! I contacted their customer support and was told it appeared my wi-fi signal was possibly the issue and they sent me a link to do a test. I couldn't get back to them until 3 days later, which the rep said was fine and that she would be waiting for my email. Did the test (appeared to me there wasn't a problem) and sent it to her. No response. Sent another message. No response. Put in a new ticket and got a response from another rep saying the lady was not in that day and that he would help me. I sent him the wi-fi test. At that point, the Ring stopped working altogether. I flapped my arms, ran around, went up and down the porch. Nothing. I sent an email to the rep telling him I now had a serious issue. No response. I guess he went home for the day. That's it. I decided to send it back. Looked on the website. No information on where to send anything. Did an online chat and got the address and an RMA #. Got a message from the lady rep the following day apologizing for not being there the day before. In addition she said "Sorry we wasn't able to help you.". Seriously? So...no suggestion that I had a bad unit. No suggestion as to sending me a replacement to try. Then I received an email from their Community Support Rep asking me about my experience. I explained all of the above and mentioned twice in my response to him that no one had offered a replacement...to which...he replied that they take their customer service seriously and they would work on training the fellow who left me hanging. This guy didn't offer me a replacement unit either. I think HE needs to be trained. The fact that no one offered to replace the unit says that this type of faulty detection is a common occurrence and no replacement is going to solve the issue. WASTE OF MONEY! Their technology is not ready for prime time...they need more testing and a better detection solution. Plus...you need to buy their cloud service to keep the videos in case you want to see them later.UPDATE: 4/8/17: I was contacted by the Ring Community Manager to address my problem. She ensured that my refund for the equipment I had purchased was quickly processed and she offered me a free Ring unit and Chime to try again. I took her up on the offer as I REALLY want this to work. I charged the new unit and sync'd it with my wi-fi. It worked a couple of times then stopped. I did an online chat with their help people and this time...they were actually helpful and able to resolve all the issues. The unit works great! Wonderful customer service this time--very impressed.

title: Several minor flaws but still a very useful upgrade for my house
body: Here are my initial observations after allowing my cats to bench-test the Ring indoors, and having it installed as my doorbell button for 24 hours.First, a few caveats:- Strong WiFi inside of your house is essential. If you don't have that, either acquire it or don't buy this product. EDIT - There are ways to improve it, such as relocating your router, reorienting its antennas. I was having connection issues and ended up buying a Ring Chime Pro, which is a combination door chime and WiFi repeater. Things are working MUCH better now.- For the mobile app to work acceptably, you must have good bandwidth end-to-end especially a strong signal at the mobile end. Alerts may work but Live View will absolutely not if you are in a location with a weak signal.- The motion sensing configuration is not very granular. As others have noted, I am getting a lot of hits from passing cars. Better to have false alarms than misses, IMO.- The instructions don't say this explicitly, but configuration will go more smoothly if you fully charge the internal battery first. That takes several hours (up to 12 or maybe 16) connected to USB.- There is a lag between the device detecting motion and starting to record. I get the impression this has been improved - I have gotten many acceptable videos of delivery people and of myself coming and going.- If you don't "answer" a ring or motion event pretty quickly, you can't get to the live video (while the device is recording.)Now some happy points:- Configuration is not difficult if you follow the instructions (but charge the battery FIRST.)- The free Android mobile app is intuitive and easy to set up.- Physical installation is easy, with useful tools provided. You may need to provide your own hand drill depending on the material you are attaching the back plate to.- I like the fact that the Ring is a straightforward replacement for a traditional doorbell button. It is rated to work on anything from 8 to 24 volts AC, provided that there is a load in the circuit (e.g. your existing mechanical door chime.)- Having a video and audio recording of myself leaving the house in the morning provides positive assurance that I remembered to lock the front door (or that I forgot to and need to go back home.)- The camera provides decent resolution for such a small and wide-angle lens. The view is close to 180 degrees.- The camera has infrared LEDs and works OK in the dark.- As I expected, I'm getting very good information about deliveries. Should a package thief show up, that would be captured as well.I recommend subscribing to the cloud storage for $30 per year. In the event someone comes up to your door and puts tape over the camera or rips it off of the wall, you will have some video of the event.I am going though a big security upgrade at home. The Ring serves both as an adjunct to a security camera system, and a prompt alert system to notify me of people at or near my front door.No regrets so far.

title: Almost perfect -- very happy with Ring doorbell
body: Overall, I'm happy with the Ring purchase and really like the product (so close to 5 star, but there are a few improvements still needed). I was a little hesitant to make the purchase because it was newer technology and at one point I had read some pretty strong negative reviews, but I took the leap and hoped for the best.Things I like:- Being able to hard-wire the doorbell is great. I likely would not have purchased it otherwise (If I add the Stick-up camera that is separate from the Ring I will purchase the solar panel option).- I haven't used the Cloud storage viewing much recently (I did enjoy checking them out at first), but for the most part, it's because I know who's actually causing the motion / doorbell many of the times. I really do like the Cloud storage and I think the price is very reasonable ($3 a month or $30 per year). Because the Cloud storage was so reasonable, that's one of the main reasons I went ahead and purchased it. Should I need the video for any reason, I'm glad that it's storing things as we go. Much better than my DLink camera which could only email me pics.- Design is decent enough. I imagine it will start to look dated, but I'm not too concerned about that -- I do like that there are different color options for the exterior.- Fish eye view -- I like this more than I thought I would. I believe it's 180 degree view and it works well for the location it's in..- Replacement policy for stolen Ring -- that also helped me make the purchase -- I could picture someone trying to steal it.- Being able to listen in and/or talk to someone at your door is awesome!Things that could be improved:- The ability to activate the camera whenever I'd like (I heard from the Ring folks and apparently this is being rolled out soon)- The time it takes for the motion / doorbell to activate my phone is not very consistent. I imagine there are a lot of handshakes happening to have the doorbell notify my phone (and likely some latency that you really is not the Ring doorbells fault -- like wi-fi issues, etc, but the delay can be a little annoying when sometimes it's a good 5-8 seconds delay). Most the time it's fairly quick, though.- I haven't tinkered with the motion settings to make them more sensitive, but I do feel like it starts recording a little too late. This is likely just something I need to resolve by changing my motion distance (I just don't want it to trigger with passing cars / walkers).Things I worry a little about since this is technology (and new technology):- I can just picture my Ring doorbell suddenly not working in a year or so. It gets pretty cold (and hot) in my locale and it also gets a peek at the sun for an hour or so a day. I really hope it will last longer than just a year or two because I don't particularly want to buy a replacement that often.Regardless, this is one of the better technology purchases and security purchases I've made and have been very happy with the product. 5 stars once the live look-in feature is released!

title: Nice device but some setup and support issues
body: This functions nicely once installed. The doorbell rings to my phone and my computer, from which I can see who is there and have a conversation. It records activity. And you can set motion as an alert in addition to the button press being an alert if desired. A clever concept and good functionality.I won't give a detailed review of how it functions since that's covered elsewhere. However, I gave one star off because install and setup were more difficult than they should have been, and tech support is a bit backed up - signs of some growth pains at the company most likely (having been in some startups, I can envision a bit of chaos trying to keep up with growth back at their HQ).Here's what happened:First PC I tried to connect to the device with didn't connect (saw the Ring wifi server in the list but then with several tries couldn't connect). There was not adequate support info to know what the problem was. I had a laptop available as well and that one did connect and went through the rest of the connection steps pretty easily.Next though I installed the Ring app on my android phone. It indicated that it was connected to the frontdoor doorbell, etc. Rang the bell (it gave the proper indication on the doorbell that it was ringing) but nothing happened on the phone. Also tried the ring.com page on a pc - same situation.I didn't see anything helpful on the website for this, but clicked to contact live chat and called for support. At the same time I called support.On the online chat, I posed my question and it said I should wait for an agent and I'm number 12 in the queue. No indication of how long that would be, and no apparent countdown updating the queue position - a nit, but frustrating to not know how long it will be and if you can leave the room or have to wait.The support phone line meanwhile has a marketing message from the founder about how he came up with the concept (cute but not a great idea - when calling support we're frustrated not interested in cute messages). And then offers to press 1 to get into the callback queue instead of holding. I did that but it just goes by caller ID. Problem is I'm on a business line with an extension and no way to indicate that so they won't be able to get back to me. I called in from a cell too. Pressed 1 to get into the callback queue. No indication of expected wait time here either.After about 30 mins, I got a call from support. She was knowledgeable and walked me thru a few steps and then fixed the problem with a remote firmware update. Given that this was shipped last week, firmware really shouldn't be out of date, but good she could fix it. Support agent indicated though that it was good I reached her since not all agents could fix that issue remotely.None of these issues are huge and ultimately it's a nicely working product, but collectively they do have the feel of a startup that's not entirely on top of its game to handle growth yet. I expect they'll get there.

title: We tried the Doorbot and had major issues, now we're on Ring and all seems to be good (at least for what we needed it for)
body: I'm a professional AV / Networking / Home Automation installer in the Chicagoland area. I've done a number of jobs where the question of a video doorbell has been raised. I never had a good answer that didn't cost upwards of > $1000 until last year.Last year two new products came on the marker, the Doorbot and the Skybell WiFi Doorbells. I followed both closely through development and release stages and waited for reviews to begin trickling in. First, Skybell was first to set a release date, which they missed by a mile. Then Doorbot hit the market. It seemed that although Skybell offered more features.... none of them worked (based on early customer reviews). Doorbot seemed to be pulling ahead in reviews, which is when I started to offer the product to my customers.July or August of 2014 I installed my first Doorbot for a customer. The home was a new construction so I was able to put in a mechanical doorbell chime (a requirement of both Doorbot, and the new model, Ring doorbells). I can't speak to the hassles of making this product work with a electronic door chime. I installed the Doorbot and had it up and running, with minimal tinkering, within an hour. As with other reviewers, there were issues including push notifications not working unless the app was open, and no audio to or from the device.As Doorbot transitioned to Ring, I continued to work with support over a 6 month period to rectify the issues with the Doorbot. Eventually support threw in the towel and provided a Ring doorbell as a replacement. I was unhappy with the length of time needed to rectify the issue but was at the mercy of the Ring release cycle.I went by my customer's house last week, February 2015, and installed the Ring. The holes I had drilled in the brick didn't match up, which was unfortunate, but there was never any expectation that they would. Oh well. The Ring, being wider, covered the holes I had drilled for the Doorbot.The Ring App (Android) was easy to install and walked through the setup steps. One advantage to the Ring over the Doorbot was that is worked in WiFi N standard (802.11N) instead of B only. Who has a Wireless B network anymore? I had to install a Wireless B access point just to get the Doorbot working on the initial install. Now the Ring doorbell works on Wireless N. Thank you! The setup process seemed to continuously fail when connecting the Ring to the network. It never gave a SUCCESS message. After 20min of retrying I gave up and just pressed the doorbell's button. Amazingly, it worked! The phone rang, the video was clear, and the audio worked... despite the setup in the app never giving a completion/success message.I finished mounting the doorbell and headed out for the day. It's been a week and I've heard no complaints from the client.I'll begin notifying my other customers that we're ready to start installing this product.Doug BuchanBuchan Consulting LLC

title: The video calling is invaluable, but there are a number of quirks
body: I recently installed this as my doorbell. The video calling is invaluable, but there are a number of quirks that have reduced my rating below a perfect five stars. Read on.INSTALLATIONMy house has stucco walls. You need a special drill bit to go through stucco/concrete, which I happen to have, but it's damn near impossible to get your holes lined up perfectly. The Ring backplate has no slack for where the screws go through the plate. If you're off, you lose. (I have a similar complaint about many other gadgets. You'd think they could have made an L-shaped channel to give you maximum flexibility for where your screw holes end up, but no.) In my case, I ended up surrendering and using Gorilla Glue to attach the backplate to my wall. It took a day for the glue to really cure up, but now the backplate is properly aligned and not going anywhere without the use of a chisel. The doorbell, itself, still comes off easily with two screws.The electrical and WiFi aspect of setup is relatively painless -- no more difficult than setting up most other "Internet of Things" devices.CONFIGURATIONThe motion sensors have configurable range for when they'll trigger recordings to begin. You have to then "ring" the bell to save these settings. This completely failed to work, and I've had an ongoing dialog with Ring service about what appears to be a software bug on their end. Even when I had the range set very close, cars going by on the street (a good distance away from my door) would trigger the video recordings and phone alerts. Having my phone alerting me multiple times per day, all false positive, was completely unacceptable. After some extensive back-and-forth with the tech support at Ring, we somehow got it to disable the motion alerts yet still record motion-triggered video. It's not that you can't do this from the UI. It's that the UI is seriously buggy and takes multiple tries to get your settings to stick. Very frustrating.BUT WHEN IT WORKSIt's fantastic when somebody comes to your door, rings your doorbell, and you can respond to them from halfway around the world. This is the killer feature that makes this product invaluable. The fact that it leaches power from your existing doorbell wiring is immensely useful. Install it and forget it.WISHLIST- Better configurability with the motion sensors (e.g., have somebody walk by and get a real-time meter of what the sensors are seeing).- Web browser access, not just smartphone access.- The Ring is a hefty device. It would be nice if it were half the thickness. Also, the mounting plane needs to have more slack to help you align it with holes that aren't drilled precisely.IN SUMMARYGreat concept. Good-but-not-great hardware engineering. Beta-quality software that's still very much a work in progress.

title: EDIT: No false positives with down angled block, still not great video quality
body: EDIT: I'm bumping from 2 stars to 4. Customer service at Ring saw my review and reached out to me directly, over two weeks or so they called me multiple times to make tweaks to the settings and angle of the Ring. I was discouraged and frustrated and wanted to wash my hands of the entire thing and move on with my life, but Jennifer at Ring customer service would not allow this! She sent me a few angle blocks to try out, next day air, free of charge and had Junior and Orlando call me probably four or five times to make sure I had it right. I found the left/right angled blocks made my problem worse, but the down angled blocks literally do exactly what I want. I get every person who walks past my house, and ZERO cars. The Ring is working exactly as I want it to now!Three complaints:- The video quality is not great, it's good enough, but it's not the HD quality I was expecting.- The blocks kind of look bad. They are white plastic with nipples (teeheehee) of plastic sticking out of the corners on the front so you can mount multiple blocks to get a more steep angle, but these nips seems to cause the ring mounting bracket to stick out slightly from the block, so there's this weird empty space between the block and the mounting bracket. I'm planning to cut a hole in the siding next to my door for the Ring to fit into, but I'll need to find my own frame so you can't see the cuts becayse one was not provided. Still, pretty stoked I didn't have to pay $10 to try the blocks.- Still wanna link it to my dropbox to save videos and not spend the $3 a month, but I get the business agenda of not opening that up.Pretty disappointed by the Ring. My front door faces the street, it is not a busy street, but a car goes down it every 15 minutes so. People walk by fairly often as well, maybe 1 or 2 per hour. It has no problem detecting every single car that goes by, but it has literally never detected someone walking by. I've tried adjusting the motion sensitivity but the results are always the same. Lots of cars, no people.The quality of the video is pretty awful as well. I thought maybe I had it set to SD only, but I can't find anyway to change the quality. Maybe if somebody walked up and put their face directly in front of it and held still for 5 seconds you'd get a quality image of a face. Going to give it two stars because it's nice to be able to answer the door and pretend that I'm inside when I am not, but that doesn't seem like $200 worth of features. The $3 a month is kind lame as well, although if it worked better I'd be happy to pay it.A dropbox cloud option would be nice, I already pay for that and would love to sync my videos there. As of now I'm not sure that I even own the videos ring captures, seems like I probably should since I own the device, it's on my property, and it's generally recording me more often than anything else.

title: This is an amazing product and I have been extremely impressed with RING's ...
body: This is an amazing product and I have been extremely impressed with RING's customer support.I did a lot of reading about this product, and competitors' products, on CNET and other sites before buying the RING. Security was my primary concern more than having a cool gadget that would allow me to communicate visitors at my door.Everything about the product, from the packaging to the instructions to the ease of installation is first class. However, I can't give a 5 star review because I ran into extensive problems with the RING when it came to its use with our home Wifi network. The video and audio quality was very inconsistent: Audio was dropping and video was pixelated and freezing. I spent many hours with customer support, even going out to purchase a WiFi extender thinking it would help. (It didn't). Ultimately, the first RING I bought stopped working (it started making static when the motion sensor was triggered). But, thanks to the really expert customer support, they rushed me out a new unit and they did extensive testing of both units as well as helping me check my internet speeds. The new unit works about 90% better than the first unit worked initially. But, presumably due to the thickness of our home's 1926 interior and exterior walls, or the intermittent quality of our relatively high speed DSL internet service, there are occasional times when the RING doesn't quite work as smoothly as is shown on some of the user's videos posted on their website.But---these are nitpicks at this point because of the coolness of this product. The adjustable motion sensor feature is fantastic. The up-loadable videos stored on the cloud are exactly what I wanted in terms of security. If anyone were to steal a package from our front door, I'd have the evidence.The most amazing feature is nicely summed up in their slogan: "Always Home." It is a "wow" factor. I can be anywhere in the world on my iPhone and get an alert, and I can within see and chat with the person at my front door--whether they rang the RING doorbell or not.Of course, being connected to one's home all the time is not unlike having a phone with you at all times. It means that you are always home (even if you're trying to get away from home) and you are always reachable to others (even if you don't want to be), unless you put your phone down or disable the app. For people younger than me (I'm 50) who have lived their entire life being connected at all times, this won't seem strange at all. But again, the advantages of the RING far outweigh the occasional glitches or any other issues.Hopefully in the future, RING will update its firmware with a feature that will allow users to see the view from the RING even when it isn't activated by button or motion sensor. I imagine that is coming and that will be another added value.

title: Edited & Improved: Motion detection is inconsistent (thus unreliable).
body: I've had my ring 1 week now while I worked from home so I could do a solid evaluation. Bottom line, inconsistent motion detection. Just for example, I had two packages delivered today, both left within a foot of the doorbell and neither delivery men were detected, yet when the garbage truck drove by on the street (30 feet away and out side my "detection zone') it recorded that! It doesn't detect the mailman - most of the time, nor neighbors cars as they drive by - most of the time (my range is set to about 15'), but it did detect a gecko climbing on the brick next to the door bell.In short, its simply unreliable, especially for the cost. Great concept, poorly executed!Edit: Within a day after posting this review, Ring contacted me asking if I would give them an opportunity to make my purchase right. I agreed and a call was set up for the next day I was home in order to troubleshoot. I was assigned a neighborhood advocate or something like that and over the phone we attempted a few fixes. By the end of this, she had expedited an order for me that included a Ring 2 doorbell, a wifi extender chime and a wedge kit.It was a week later that I had the chance to install the new device and begin testing it. At first, disappointment did not abate as response was very slow. I tried several configurations with and without the wifi extender (my router is very close to my front door) and I also used the 5 degree down wedge to reduce alerts from cars on the road. Of greatest disappointment was there was no two way communication between my phone and the doorbell. I was gone again the following week and while I did get alerts, there was rarely anything there. On Tuesday, my landscaper arrived to do some work and it picked up the motion, but still no sound. Then the very next day there was suddenly sound. Wasn't sure it was two way, but at least i could hear and it recorded sound from the doorbell.I returned home on Thursday night and tested the sound and there was two way communication. I had left with the wifi extender not in the mix, but on Friday I decided to try again with the extender. I was able easily to reconfigure the setup and to my delight I still had two way communication. I have tested it throughout the weekend and it seems to be working fine. I even had a delivery Friday that was picked up by the doorbell. With the Ring 2, the video quality is much better than the Ring.I will say that Ring did make a strong effort to correct the issue. I have upgraded my rating from two stars to 4, primarily due to their customer service effort. The doorbell is working now although I'm not sure why two way communication suddenly began to work - perhaps a firmware update? I can't say I have total confidence in the device, but will continue to monitor and when I do, I will update this review.

title: UPDATE 11/06/2019: Improved with help, but still on the fence.......
body: I've played with the settings for about a year now, and there are still times where we'll get motion notices on our Ring apps, only to see absolutely nothing going on outside. The app is a little slow to open, but overall it and the Doorbell do what they says they will.I also ran into an issue this past winter where it ran out of power and I never got a notification about it. Ring never did have a great answer for why that was. We got this one as a Black Friday discount; I'd say definitely shop around and see what else is also out there before deciding on Ring. I don't think you'll go wrong with Ring, but I wonder if other competitors have better products.UPDATE 11/06/2019: A Ring representative reached out to me after my initial review and helped troubleshoot the issue. He was kind enough to send out a set of the angled wedges on the hypothesis that the direct angle facing the street was resulting in 'false alarms'.The Ring-brand wedges were kind of tough to work with; I broke the heads off of three of the screws trying to install a couple of them, resulting in only being able to get one fully installed. This was with my drill-driver at low torque; I can appreciate the slimness of the screws so that you're not drilling huge holes in things, but they're really not made for strength.In getting everything set-up, I noticed again that my Ring had low battery, and again I wasn't alerted via the app or email about this, as I've got enabled in the settings. This is particularly frustrating, as the unit's supposed to last on the order of months without needing charging, but without manually checking every so often I don't feel like I'll get a reliable notice that it's time to charge it up.However, all of that aside, it does appear that we're getting less false alarms with the wedge installed. It's not a huge angle, either, but apparently just enough that we're not getting notices that someone's at our front door when it's just a car driving by. We're about 15-20 feet from the road, with a single step in front of our small porch, and I've got the settings in the app as restrictive as I can make them.Also, installing the Rapid Ring app has helped with receiving notices. It's a lot faster to alert me than the full-sized app (which I've also still got installed for full functionality); it brings up the Live View a little slower than I'd expect, I'm using a OnePlus 3, which isn't the newest phone on the block but shouldn't lag terribly. If I needed to talk to someone on the other end of the doorbell, tho, it'd only take a few seconds to bring that up so it's not the world's biggest issue.Overall, I'm adding a star to my review because of Ring's customer service and the improved situation described above. It's not perfect, but certainly better than it was.

title: An Almost Perfect Product (that could be even better with smaller sensitivity zones)
body: I actually LOVE my Ring doorbell. I would have given it five stars were it not for one very specific issue I have with it, which I will address later in this review.First, the good stuff:I think the RING doorbell is absolutely brilliant. There are a lot of things I just love about it – down to the chime sound it makes when there's movement outside my door. Very pleasant and an apt signal for motion near the door.I’m really impressed with the way the Ring doorbell lets me see a real time image on my iPhone of the area outside my door whenever I want. And I love being able to answer the door no matter where I am in my studio – often buying myself time to get to the door before my visitor gives up and goes away. Even more impressive: I really enjoy being able to answer the door without even being home. That has already come in very handy several times. The very first time someone rang my bell, I was not only away from home, but I was out of town. My visitor was very surprised to hear my voice saying “Hi, Hugh! I’m actually in New York right now. But thanks for coming by. Feel free to use your key to let yourself in...”Now for the one negative:I have one complaint. Just one. I wish there was a way to fine-tune the size of the motion sensitivity zones. I'd love to be able to narrow down alerts to a shorter distance than the allowable 5 feet. Perhaps 2 or 3 feet would be more meaningful for me.Without the ability to go smaller than the inner-most 5 ft semi-circular zone, the motion detection feature remains not very useful to me. My door is indoors and is quite close to another door which leads to a restroom facility. Thus I get motion alerts all day long just tracking people entering and exiting that door. Not only does the large number of alerts render the feature pointless, it drains my battery very quickly because of all the massive amount of automatic recording that's happening.The motion detection feature would become extremely useful for me if the option were added to reduce the circumference of that center "Zone 6" area by just a few more feet. Or, alternatively, if that zone could be split into two (or more) pie pieces that I could choose from, this would also make the feature tremendously useful for me.To Summerize: I love the RING doorbell and all of its features, but I wish they would go smaller with the allowable sensitivity zones.I feel encouraged, however, because the company has been very responsive to my inquiries about this issue, and they have also shown signs of willingness to re-think some of the software design of the project to make it more useful for their customers. I’m keeping fingers crossed that the option I’m asking for will be added soon.

title: As usual with these kinds of products, mixed feelings, but mostly positive so far
body: There are things to like about it, things to not like about it. I got this to put by our garage door, because that's where deliveries come, got tired of not seeing FedEx, UPS, USPS trying to deliver then claiming I wasn't home.The good:It really just worked. Connected to my (hidden) WiFi, strong signal. Push the button, my iPhone tells me so and I can see and talk with whoever is there. I really like the motion detection, I can tell when the delivery truck rolls up, but see below. I think that many people that have had issues probably have it too far from their WiFi router and so have intermittent connectivity. Unproved, just my working hypothesis. Notifications seem to arrive on both my iPhone and iPad within a couple of seconds, fine for answering.The not-so-good:If you don't want to just run on its internal battery, you have to have doorbell wiring, or add a doorbell transformer and jump thru some minor hoops to use that. You can't (according to Ring support, not sure I believe them) keep it constantly connected to a USB port for charging. (Why don't I believe them? I'm an electronics engineer. It uses a Lithium-ion battery. You can't charge one without a charge controller. If it can charge from the 24vac, and recharge from the USB port, it should be able to be on that USB port all the time.).The motion detection is somewhat flaky. It will spot my dog walking by, but miss the UPS truck driving right up into the detection zone. Hmm. Well, that was one reason for getting it in the first place. Also, if you use motion detection at the most sensitive setting, it reduces your time between charges. I don't know what the issue is, but I'm starting to think the UPS truck (brown) doesn't have enough contrast with my driveway (black) to be detected. It spots the FedEx truck all the time. It also triggers without anything visible in the detection zone. It seems to happen at the same time each day. The camera is pointed due East, I'm wondering if it's the sun being in a specific position that triggers it. Finally, it's pretty darn expensive for a doorbell. Plus, if you want a record of the video, pay them an additional $30/yr for their online backup.Conclusion:I think I like it. Maybe. :) It does solve my doorbell problem. It almost solves my motion-detection desire, maybe some more tweaking. I'll update after I've lived with it a bit longer.Update 10/03/15: I've now hardwired it to 24 VAC, motion detection seems to work more reliably. BTW, according to Ring's instructions for hooking it up without a doorbell, just straight wiring from the transformer to the Ring, they call for an absurd 50 watt resistor. Forget it. Use a 100 ohm 5 watt wirewound. That's perfectly adequate, works fine.

title: Good, not perfect
body: The Ring Video Doorbell is a good device. It has a lot of useful features that makes life a bit easier and more secure.ProsI like having the ability to answer the door from anywhere (as long as I have my phone).Night vision works pretty well.The app is really responsive.Installation was a breeze.ConsThe video quality isn't as good as I would have hoped.You can't just peek out on the camera. Someone has to press the doorbell or trip the sensor before you can see what's happening outside.Doesn't support local recording (to my own computer).Mine really likes garbage trucks. (They trip the sensor despite being very far away)Subscription fee!I'll totally recommend the Ring Video Doorbell (RVB). All of the features work as advertised, installation and setup were a breeze almost everything I needed to install it was included in the box( I did have to use my own wood drill bit instead of the masonry one provided in the box). Night vision works pretty good, I was almost unrecognizable but you can totally tell that someone is there.There are only a few things that I don't like about the RVB.1. The video quality isn't as good as I would have hoped. It's nowhere as good as it is in the advertised video. I've attach some screen captures.2. It would b great if you could just look out to see what was happening at any moment. Unfortunately the only way to see what's going on outside is to answer the door using the app after someone has either rung the bell or tripped the motion sensor.3. The RVB doesn't let me personally manage my own recordings. Everything gets sent up the the cloud (out of my control) where the company stores it (as long as you pay the fee)4. Despite having turned down the sensitivity to about 5 feet this thing will send alerts when a garbage truck drives by. At least we'll know when the garbage has been picked up.5. I'm not much of a fan of paying subscription fees. $3/month or $30/year for cloud recording.Update 7/12/16I had been having a ton of trouble with my Ring. Everyday I was needing to add it back to my network just to have to it all over again the next day. After a few calls to support they decided that my unit was defective and they would send me out a replacement. After installing the replacement everything has been smooth sailing the doorbell just sits there and does it's thing with no additional input from me.Recently, Ring added the ability to live view on the doorbell. It's great that they added this bit of functionality it really shows that the company is continuing to improve the service.

title: Very handy!
body: Currently, the doorbell works well. The video loads a little slowly, but once loaded I am able to view and communicate with a person outside my door. The wide-angle lens lets me see most of the porch and of course almost all of the front yard. When I got my first video doorbell, I was pleasantly surprised to find out that I could get a professional to install the doorbell for me at no charge. The professional I called responded quickly. He appeared a little uncertain about HD model that I had got but assured me that he knew exactly how to install it. I found this setup with the app. See to accomplish it in no time at all I was able to view my porch and communicate through the intercom. The next challenge was bandwidth. I had an old router and while the doorbell worked fine, the other devices I had connected to my network didn't work well at all. The doorbell chime also wasn't very loud and sometimes I missed hearing it. The router problem was resolved by buying a newer model router and then everything worked fine. A while later, I received an email for ring color explaining that they saw my doorbell has stopped working. They said they were sending me a new one and would let me know when it shipped. That was only a short time later and said I have a new doorbell in hand. This one did not offer free installation so I asked my handyman to take care of that for me. He did and suddenly my old doorbell chime had started working! I like this, but I wasn't sure that was how it's supposed to be. I contacted Ring customer service and Hal explained to me that yes, the doorbell is designed first outside with a pleasant chime, then ring the old doorbell chime, and, if I had a Ring Chime device, that would ring third. So it turns out that the professional had not installed the doorbell correctly after all. This solves my problem is not being able to hear the doorbell rang. All is well now and I have found it so convenient to be able to tell someone to wait, I'll be at the door in a minute, or to just leave a package if the visitor is someone making a delivery. Update 10/6/16: just installed my Chime bell. Installation was very easy. I just plugged it in and then opened the Ring app on my smartphone. I added the device, clicked when I was told to, and wah-lah, it was ready to go. The volume is adjustable but was plenty loud when I installed it. I was a little concerned that since I was installing the Chime in my garage, the wifi would not work that far away. It did take a few minutes to link up, but once it did, no problem. This will come in handy when I'm doing things in the garage, where I might not hear the fronr doorbell. Problem solved! :-)

title: If it doesn't connect at one persons home it may connect just fine at another
body: I'm getting tired of reading reviews of internet-connected devices that claim the devices won't connect to wifi. Look, everyone's home is a unique wifi environment made of a combination of specific wifi hardware and interference from housing material, other electrical/wifi sources, etc. If it doesn't connect at one persons home it may connect just fine at another.So far, the home automation devices I've tried all connect just fine at my house, including this doorbell. That doesn't mean it will work at all at your house. You'll have to try it and see.I get an alert as soon as the doorbell is pressed. There is no appreciable lag. The app works the way it was designed. At my home environment, the motion sensor is too sensitive and picks up passing cars even at the lowest sensitivity level. The motion sensor might work fine at your house.I gave it 4 stars instead of 5 because the software still needs some refinement. Right now, when the doorbell rings the app is activated and you have a window of opportunity to respond to the doorbell. If you don't respond within that window, the app shuts off and you can't receive any video or use the intercom unless the caller presses the doorbell again. That's not the way it ought to work. It should work as follows: 1) Someone rings the doorbell 2) Your phone alerts you. Completely independent of the doorbell ring, you should be able to turn on the video/audio functionality and see/talk to your caller, via the app.From a usability standpoint, the video/audio capability should be separate from the doorbell. That's how non-internet intercoms have been working for more than 50 years.I am guessing that the design decision to tie the doorbell and intercom is related to Ring's video archive service. It would probably be very expensive to record unlimited video. Perhaps they'll update their software in future to allow users to turn on the video/audio at will.**added comment** Beyond the expense of recording unlimited video, the doorbell was designed to operate independently, via its own battery for 6 months to a year. But if it was addressable at will via the app the device would have to be listening to the web at all times, which would likely run down the battery, as would heavy uploading of video. In other words, although people would be happy with the feature set if the video could be accessed from the app, they'd be extremely unhappy if the doorbell had to recharged every week or so. I guess I understand their design decision now.In the meantime, the doorbell works the way it was designed to work, and I am glad I have it.

title: Ring Video Doorbell is Pretty Impressive So Far
body: Updated 12/18/2017:Decided to upgrade to the Ring Pro. Experienced a problem with the basic Ring related to power consumption. Even though hardwired, the primary source of power appears to be the battery, which is depleted quickly with frequent motion alerts and frequent use of "live view". The battery should be able to continually charge, but apparently it wasn't charging fast enough. Experienced "poor" signal strength and weak battery. Ring customer support is very responsive by phone and email.Purchased the Ring (basic) video doorbell from an Amazon third-party seller. We had just returned the Skybell after a very brief and unfortunate experience, including a lack of Skybell customer service. Ring seemed like the only other inexpensive option. I’m probably going to be disappointed when the 30 day free trial of video storage expires, but overall, Ring is doing exactly what we wanted. It was very easy to set up and the instructions were accurate. The set up takes place before attaching the unit to the wall. That was a good idea and we were able to test the video and bell before installing it. We hardwired it to an existing doorbell, but there is a built in battery that is said to last about a year, so this is a good option for locations without an existing doorbell. I have the motion range set nearly to the maximum and have received alerts only when someone is on the property, in the driveway, or at the door. It has not been triggered by activity at the next door neighbor’s property, which is actually within video view, but outside of the set motion range. It also hasn’t been triggered by street activity. The motion detection is perfect so far. Some reviews have said that the connection is too slow to see people when they’re approaching, but that’s not been our experience. We are alerted as soon as there is nearby activity and the video seems to be in real time. The sound quality is also good. Not perfect, but more than adequate for a doorbell. Video and audio quality may be related to having high speed internet and a whole house mesh network to cover outside devices. I wish the video storage was free. Even if it was only for a few days of storage. That would be long enough to review video in the event of a problem. I don’t plan to subscribe to video storage. The app is very basic. It would be nice if the chime could be changed. There’s a volume control, but no ability to change the sound. My husband is partially deaf and he doesn’t always hear the chime’s particular tone. I rated it as four stars because of the inability to change the chime and because there’s no free, temporary video storage.

title: Overall, a very nice doorbell
body: It's an odd thing to mention, but I have to bring up the product packaging. I mean, usually you get a box and rip it open and throw it out. All the stuff included (screws, anchors, a freaken LEVEL) feels like a lot of thought and design went into the packaging alone.Anyways, that aside, installation was pretty straight-forward. I was replacing a hard-wired doorbell button that was mounted on a 3 inch wide strip of aluminum trim, so I was kind of worried about the width. But, as the picture I've added illustrates, mounting it worked out extremely well. I was done installing in less than an hour.I gave it four stars for a couple of reasons. While the hardware is fantastic, the software is a bit lacking...The Android app is a bit bare-bones. Can't quite figure out why I can't just view live video rather than viewing either a ring or motion detection. Seems like it should be an easy feature to add. There is no Kindle Fire app either, so I have to run it on my Samsung S6, which is fine, but it would be nice to have a Kindle version. I did try to side-load the app into my Kindle, and while it kinda-sorta worked, it closes after 2 seconds with a complaint that the Kindle doesn't have Google Play services. That seems a bit odd to me; not sure why this device/app would be so dependent upon Google Play services.I set the motion sensor range to the shortest setting, apparently approx. 5 feet, as that would cover the depth of my porch, but sometimes a vehicle on the road, 50 feet away, will trigger motion alerts. It only takes a second or two to unlock my phone and open the alert, but by that time, the motion has passed and all I see is empty space. I can view the cloud recording and see "oh yea, it's the mailman", whose truck has disappeared by the time I view the alert. Seems like a lot of latency between motion and ring sensing versus alerts.Sometimes I don't get motion detection OR ring signals. I can go into the app or web site and see that it's recorded those events, but my phone did not receive notification. The only explanation I can think of is a weak WiFi signal. So I git a 9db antenna with 9 meter extension cable, to bring one of my routers three antennas out to an area with a wider open space. Also tried two different range extenders (one a NetGear and one a TP-Link). Doesn't quite always work. Sometimes the door will ring and I get no alert on my phone. This is kinda the biggest point for me. I might be in a part of the house where I can't hear the doorbell, but I'm more likely to have my phone. But if I just don't get the alert, what's the point?

title: Exceeded this nerd's expectations
body: As an engineer, I go nuts over techie toys like this but am not easily pleased by marketing hooplah and fancy packaging alone. So far, I'm pleased to report that the Ring doorbell's performance has exceeded my expectations of what a Smart doorbell should be. I'm satisfied with the lag (~1 to 4 secs in my testing) from pressing the doorbell to getting a phone notification message. Daytime/nighttime (has 2 IR emitters) video quality is better than expected. 720p resolution is very good. Viewing angle is very good. 2 way audio quality and volume is reasonable for most outdoor applications (making this in App adjustable would be great feature though!). It's faults are already well known: motion sensing feature not currently enabled, no ability to remotely activate the viewer in the current App, no settings to turn off the in-unit fake doorbell sound, no setting to temporarily disable interior doorbell sound (unlike Skybell). I do have a high speed internet connection and strong Wifi coverage to support the HD quality video feed. Your results might differ if you don't have these by your front door location.I do hope the Ring App designers release an update soon to fix some of the limited capabilities, but I am quite satisfied with even the current functionality of the device. I've never used the Doorbot (the predecessor to Ring) and based on its horrid reviews I was scared to even give Ring a try. Well, I generally think this is a better looking and higher performing product than Skybell and I'm glad I took the gamble to try it out.3/11/15 Edit: Motion Detection feature is now enabled and worked pretty reliably for the few days that I tested it. I disabled it again since I don't like generating an alert every time I enter and exit through the front door. I played a little bit with my wifi router placement and noticed that the Ring doorbell really needs a strong signal for reliable performance. When I moved my router further, my video recordings would get choppy and my notification lags would range from 2-10 seconds. Sometimes, it simply could not connect meaning you would get no video recordings and no notification of a visitor other than the hardwired existing doorbell chime. I contacted Ring support and they remotely decreased the resolution of the video to better cope with a low bandwidth/poor wifi situation but that didn't help enough. I would suggest that if you have performance issues with this doorbell that you increase your wifi signal by better router placement or install a signal extender and see if that corrects your problems. Overall, I'm still happy with this product.

title: Fixed the Problems we were Having by Buying wifi extender.
body: Ring responded pretty quickly after they saw my Amazon review and I was able to schedule a troubleshooting call with one of their advisors. Before the call, on Black Friday, Amazon recommended the wifi extender below to me. I purchased it and now the doorbell works! I think it has something to do with the firewall on the extended network.
