There are two pop-up marketing panels on the front of the box for retail sales. They cover a majority of the features, but not all.The kit is the most comprehensive installation kit I've ever seen. A snap-in level in included to ensure the doorbell is completely straight. A screwdriver with double-sided bit (Philips head as well as their proprietary bit for the final securement of the doorbell unit to the plate,) an installation plate, screws with weatherproof o-rings (plus one extra- thanks guys!) and anchors for certain installation, and a DRILL BIT. Seriously, I've never seen any product include so much.The installation was a piece of cake. We have a standard wired doorbell installed onto our wooden door frame. It's wide enough to accommodate the Ring. Since it's wood, the drill bit and anchors were not required. You can even use their hand screwdriver to install the screws, but I used a power drill on a low torque setting once I determined it was level.You may want to caulk any existing screw holes or where the electric cables come through in case of moisture or weather.I decided to charge the Ring before installation even though it's not necessary, as it trickle charges from the doorbell wiring. I secured the cables to the proper screws on the plate.BEFORE putting the Ring on the installation plate, you must go through the app and pair the bell with your router/wifi. The app shows you exactly what to do. You only need to do this on one device, as you'll add additional users after installation.Once you've paired the Ring, it's time to put it on the install plate. There are four small plastic L hooks, where you have to align it perfectly and then push down from the top until it clicks into place. This was the only area I have anything to say about it- it required FAR more force than I liked, simply because it is an electronic device and using force with plastic usually doesn't end well. After two failed tries, I triple checked all four brackets were in the corresponding holes and the ring was completely flush with no gaps. I had to push HARD from the top and it finally clicked into place. I understand there is a balance between the tolerances and security vs. the weakness of the plastic. I am glad it installs rock solid when pushed into place but I imagine this is a point of weakness in the design, especially if the hooks are not completely in the four holes. My suggestion would be to manufacture the plate out of some type of metal, but I realize this would greatly increase the cost of manufacturing to be passed down to the end user.Function:It works! I set up my Ring to alert to any activity. I set the sensitivity high because we are far from the street and neighbors with a large front yard. The camera quality is acceptable, similar to FaceTime on wifi using an iPhone 6S.The motion and bell alerts have virtually no delay on wifi, and the audio quality is supurb. The volume at the door is loud without being too loud, and the clarity on the iPhone is excellent.The camera is wide angle and was so good, it even showed when we tried to hide away from it.We wanted to test the infrared camera vs. the regular daylight function. We found the Ring determines which mode to use at the beginning of the event and will not switch modes once an event has begun (both motion detection and a doorbell push.) This really would only affect performance if the porch light were to go on or off during the recorded event, or during sunrise or sunset. I don't see this really being an issue but I did want to mention it.One other feature we discovered is we can both answer the doorbell at the same time and both of our voices will come through. I can see both the advantages and drawbacks to this, and I'm not sure if this was an intended feature as I didn't find this mentioned in any literature for the Ring.The ring looks great and the camera is actually somewhat difficult to see on approach. We chose the Nickel finish as it matches our door hardware.My only complaint is confusion in the installation booklet. It provides minimal information, but the most confusing part is the wording regarding using the included diode and a transformer. Most people have a regular doorbell and I spent 15 minutes researching whether I would need to go through that part of the installation. This could easily be fixed by making two installation sections, one for a standard wired doorbell and one for an electronic doorbell.The literature included really is lacking in information. They don't go over any features or functions and this should be included.I give the Ring 5 stars, especially since they offer an additional door chime for my bedroom. I can't hear the doorbell in the room and frequently mute my devices.They also are pre-selling their security cameras which tie in to their app. This solves our security system issue. We have a hard wired alarm system but our ultimate goal is to have a full camera security system as well. Our home alarm is expandable but we don't like the equipment, interface, or the exhorbitant monthly cost.The Ring cloud service is by far the most affordable system we have found that meets our requirements. At only $3 a month or $30 a year, it's a no-brainer. We are extremely happy with our purchase.

title: Terrific device, great company
body: I purchased one of these in November 2015 and it is now early March. I decided to wait to post a review so I could get a better understanding of how the device functions in real world conditions.The Ring device is a small, attractive box, about 5" (12cm) L x 2.5" (6cm) W x 0.9" ((22mm) thick. It has only one user operated button. It is mounted on a plate which is screwed to the wall. Once the plate is mounted, the Ring is attached and locked with a barely visible security locking screw from the bottom. This screw is intended to prevent theft and a special screwdriver is included. The company claims they will replace it if it is stolen, but I have not had an opportunity to test this.Internally the Ring has a rechargeable battery. That battery allows it to be used in two ways. First, it can be mounted standalone to any wall. It does not require power but in that mode it will need to be recharged occasionally via its USB port. When a visitor presses the button on the Ring in this mode, it sends a signal to any device running the iOS or Android app. Or, the Ring can replace your current doorbell switch. The wires that used to connect to the doorbell switch are connected to the back of the Ring. In that mode, when a visitor presses the button on the Ring, it closes a relay which causes the existing doorbell to ring, and in addition it sends a wireless signal as in standalone mode. Doorbells in the US typically use a 24 Volt AC transformer for power which is available to the Ring whenever the doorbell is not actually ringing. The Ring draws a very small current from this source and uses it to keep the battery charged.Ring also offers an optional plug-in Chime for $30. The ring plugs into any AC outlet and connects to the Ring via Wifi. You can have multiple chimes associated with the same Ring. The app and the chime(s) can work at the same time.Once mounted, the Ring and optional Chime(s) need to be connected to your local wireless network. This is done in the app and could not be simpler, basically just press a button in the app. The Ring natively supports 802.11b, g, or n. If you have an 802.11ac network it will also connect but at non-native speeds. WPA and WPA2 wireless security is supported. Once connected the Ring functions as a doorbell, internet camera, and motion activated security camera. As soon as someone approaches the door the Ring notifies the owner via the app installed on any mobile phone or tablet. I have used both the Android app on a phone and the iOS app on a tablet, and both work similarly. The app provides a two way live audio feed and a one way video feed. This works whether you are on the local Wifi network or on the other side of the planet. As long as both ends are connected to the internet, it works. So you can see who is at the door and you can have a conversation with them, but they can't see you.The video and audio quality are excellent. Video is claimed to be 720p and images are crisp and well focused. There is some visible image distortion which is an artifact of the very wide lens. This is not a defect, rather it is a consequence of the physics of light interacting with the wide angle lens. That lens provides a field of view that is sufficiently wide to prevent someone from approaching the Ring undetected, say from the side. As an example, at a distance of 6' (1.8m) from the Ring, the view is about 25' (7.5m) edge to edge.The Ring employs computer vision to segment the video frame which allows the user to independently set the motion sensitivity of each segment. Sensitivity can be set both with regard to the object size / distance at which detection occurs, and also the degree of motion required. There is a "smart" setting which prevents repetitive false alarm events - say, a car passing in the street - from triggering the motion detector. All of these features prevent false alarms caused by spurious moving objects in frame. I had to adjust the sensitivity to get the device to detect people at the door reliably without false alarms from, in my case, branches moving in the wind while backlit by the setting sun. Once I adjusted it, the device worked very reliably in both winter (snow) and non-winter conditions and it has been operating for four months without further adjustment.The video and audio appears live in the app. Ring offers a paid service that allows you to record the video and audio in the cloud, so it remains available for later viewing. This is useful if you want to show that someone visited you, or in the case of a break in or other crime it acts like another HD recording security camera. The data is held for six months but other than that there does not appear to be any storage limit. The service costs $3 month and I have used it since I bought my Ring. It works well and I think it's reasonable value for the money.I had a couple of opportunities to check out the company's support group, once by email and once by phone. The email was answered the same day and I received a usable answer to my question on the first try. The phone was answered in less than a minute by a native english speaking agent who I later found out was US based. The support agent had an immediate answer to my question. The combination of US support and rapid response times left me very satisfied.I bought my Ring because I am home most days and I live in a large multi-story home. It is often difficult to get to the door before someone assumes I am not home and leaves. "The postman always rings twice" but the water meter reader does not :-) Since purchasing the Ring I have found it to be useful in other ways. For instance, i receive a lot of packages and more than once I have missed the post man because I'm not home. If it requires a signature they leave a form that you have to sign, put in the mail box, wait for them to pick it up, and then return with the item. With the Ring I can just tell the guy I am not home and to please redeliver the next day. The Ring has also allowed me to respond to the floating contractors who pass through town from time to time (for instance, the driveway sealing guy) who I would otherwise miss if I am not home.In closing, I think the Ring is a very well thought out, well implemented device. It provides a focused set of functions and does so very well. The company is young, well organized, and very helpful. I would most certainly buy this again.

title: Great Gadget, Horrible Security Device
body: I've had the Ring for about a month now. I didn't buy it from Amazon; it was an impulse purchase from a big box store when it was on sale. I've known about this product for a long time as it's constantly advertised on a few podcasts I watch. I'm a long time Dropcam/Nest Cam user so I didn't really care about the camera but liked the idea of making my doorbell 'smart'. After a month of use, I feel comfortable giving a review and laying out the use-case - which is not security by any means... but, that's not necessarily a bad thing.Buying the Ring, my goal was 'connecting' my doorbell. I was intrigued by the idea of having a camera in it as well but that wasn't my main reason for buying it. So my review may be skewed with those expectations a bit. I rent, so I don't have a great deal of control over my doorbell. My doorbell was far too quiet to hear it everywhere in the house but I wasn't about to replace the whole bell and mechanism. My hope was to use the Ring and connect the Wifi 'chimes' in a couple of places. For that purpose, it's exceeded my expectations. It works great. It never fails and has worked every single time without any problem. When the bell is pressed I get a notification on the 2 chimes I installed and my computers and phones. I really can't miss a doorbell press now. Problem solved!Most people likely buy the Ring because of the camera and this was a real surprise to me. The camera is great! It's nowhere near as good as a Nest cam but wow is it impressive considering how small and low-powered it is. The quality is easily good enough to monitor a front door, see who's coming and going and monitoring activity. The lens is very wide-angle and clear. I'm truly impressed.So I have all this praise, then why do I say it's a horrible security device? Motion. The motion detection on this thing is garbage. Not only is the motion detection poor to begin with, but the way they've implemented motion decisions is also very poor. It's understandable really. This is a very low powered camera. In order to keep the battery from needing to be replaced constantly, they have to err on the side of battery life over security when deciding when to turn it on and record and how long to record when motion is detected. Unfortunately, this is a deal breaker for a camera such as this, as it's entirely dependent on motion since it doesn't record consistently.This results in constant motion detection when there is no motion and many, many missed motion situations - constantly. It's bad enough that I've turned off motion alerts entirely. Motion alerts still get recorded in the cloud, but I don't get notified of them actively. The alternative is that my phone and computers would be going off all day. And unfortunately, they miss a lot of motion. I know this because I get UPS/FedEx deliveries almost daily. The delivery drivers, unfortunately, don't ring my bell half the time. So as a test the past month I've gone and looked for a motion recording after realizing they've left a package, and half the time, at least, there is none. It's also missed my husband and I coming in the door quite a bit.Also, and maybe most damning, if there is motion detected it will record, but then stop after a minute. So imagine, something is happening outside of your door that you'd absolutely want to be recorded... but, after a minute, the recording just stops... even if the motion continues. It will not start to record again until the motion stops, time passes, and it again detects motion. So, imagine a crime is happening outside your door - you'll get the first minute or two of activity but even if it continues for another 30 minutes you'll only have that first couple of minutes. Motion sensing is incapable of telling the difference between people or anything else. Everything is motion. Clouds moving, shadows, trees blowing in the breeze, a cat, rain, dust, light changes... everything is motion. Compare that to Nest cam which is rock-solid; It never misfires and knows the difference between a person and anything else. The technology available today is far superior to the motion detection abilities of the Ring.Motion detection aside, physical button presses have been 100% flawless. When the button is pressed, chimes always sound and videos are always recorded. Though it will also cut off at a minute or two. So, if someone rings your bell and then stands at your door and hacks at you with an ax for 15 minutes, only the first minute or so will be recorded. Keep this in mind if you plan to be murdered at your front door and this is your only camera.With that knowledge, you may be very happy with the Ring, as I am. It's a definite buy for what it is.Pros:Great camera considering size and power, very wide angle and clearFantastic Wifi antennaVery easy to install and wireNever misses a ring, also rings your existing wired chimeEasy to add wireless chimesBattery lasts forever (wired installation)Cheap cloud recording ($30yr)Doorbell button keeps lit at nightAbility to view live anytimeGood apps for PC, iPhone & AndroidCons:Mostly useless motion detectionRecordings cutoff seemingly arbitrarily, usually within 1-2 minutesNot particularly attractive. The new design looks much better but...**I intentionally bought the last generation Ring as all the reviews I've read about the new generation are very bad in comparison. I haven't tested it to compare but it's pretty much agreed upon universally that the new one has far worse viewing angle, camera and wifi antenna.

title: The Ring doorbell is great! We have dozens of connected products in our ...
body: The Ring doorbell is great! We have dozens of connected products in our home and this is one of the best. I've used motion sensors in the past to detect movement around the house but with the Ring we are able to actually see what is going on and save/share the video (very low annual cost compared to alternative methods). It's helped with knowing when packages have been delivered and when someone walks on the porch (using the motion sensor). We have also been able to respond remotely to visitors who have pushed the button on the doorbell (using the video/voice calling). Some things to note when considering the Ring doorbell for your set up:1) If you install Ring using your existing doorbell wiring your existing doorbell chime will likely still work (this was confirmed when I recently installed a Ring at relatives house).2) If you install Ring as battery operated (which is how I use it) the battery life will vary depending on how you have it set up. For example, If you use the motion sensor setting and have frequent motions detected the battery life will be reduced and I have been able to get 1-2 months prior to a recharge. If you have it set up so it only turns on when the button is pushed you will likely get much longer battery life.3) The motion sensor has the potential to pick up motion at greater distances than you set in the app, depending on the location it is installed. For example, I have Ring installed on a covered porch (shaded) and the App is set up for a 5’ radius. I get multiple false alarms throughout the week as it picks up motion from large vehicles (garbage trucks, FedEx, UPS, etc) that drive on the road which is about 20’ from the Ring. I believe it has to do with the shadows or focusing of the camera from under a covered porch. This can be annoying but the motion setting is valuable to know if someone actually goes near the Ring so it is worth keeping this setting on.4) Currently (December 2015) you are unable to do “on-demand” viewing of the doorbell. This means that if you wanted to see what was going on outside through the app you will not be able to unless motion is detected and/or someone pushes the button on the Ring. This feature may be available at a later date but I would imagine it could influence the battery life for non-hardwired Ring’s.5) There is a delay from when the Ring detects motion to when it records video. For example, if FedEx runs up to my porch to deliver a package there are times when I get the motion alert, open the app, and see the truck driving away. If I look back at the video recording (in app or on the website) it’s clear that the motion was detected, the camera turned on, you can see it begin to focus but by that time the drive has already walked back to their truck (~20feet) and began to drive away. It’s not a big deal for package delivery and you will 100% be able to see someone if they were standing near the camera for any length of time, it is just something to note that you may get motion video clips where you see something leaving when you may have expected it to begin earlier. This could be changed if you increase your field of view (5’ to 20’) but in my case this would create many more false alarms that do not outweigh the benefit of seeing someone come deliverer a package in my scenario.6) This isn’t the fault of Ring but if you do not recharge the battery of the doorbell and you let the unit completely drain you will need to re-sync your email/account through the app when it gets recharged and any associated Chime devices. This was an oversight on my part where I did not act on the low battery notification. Simple fix, recharge the battery when you get the low battery notification.7) The cloud recording is a nice feature. This is the first company I have went forward with paying an annual service plan for. The $30/year price tag is not too inflated (compared to other monthly fees for cloud recording cameras ~ $8-10/month) and it keeps more footage on the activity tracker. It should be noted, you do not need to pay for the cloud recording and you can watch the motion/ring alerts live on your phone without any plans. But... in the event that motion is detected or someone rings the bell... and you do not have your phone on you, you will not be able to go back and re-watch those videos.Overall the Ring is a great product. The device will not prevent someone with intent to break into your house or come onto your property but it does have a presence that may keep people away. The Ring is noticeable as a camera and we have received comments from visitors/guests as they walk by it. As you can see from the social media posts from Ring there have been attempted burglaries caught on a Ring in which the person notices the camera and walks away. For $200 the peace of mind of either preventing a crime or the ability to document what is happening at your house is worth it, in my opinion, and so is the cloud recording.

title: Well worth it
body: I spent a lot of time researching what product I wanted as part of a in-home security system. While ring is not necessarily integrated as part of that system, it is a nice addition to the house. After deciding I wanted to get Ring, I flip-flopped for awhile on whether or not the price tag of $199 + tax was a great idea, especially for a doorbell. My first thought was "It is a doorbell, you press a button, and it makes a sound, someone is at your door". Eventually I figured as part of a smart home, one of the things we would want is the ability to at least have a camera outside if someone is scoping out the area. Unlikely, but it would be nice to have if anything ever did happen.Come to find out after having it for about 2 weeks, that our UPS and USPS drivers frequently go to our door, and just leave stuff on the doorstep. One of our neighbors let us know that a few of their packages had said "delivered" but when they got home, no package was there. Seems like the reason being that the drivers in our area just do a drive and ditch, not even ringing the doorbell, so even if someone is home, you'd never know. I'm certainly not saying that all of my stuff from Amazon would have been jacked by this guy (or gal) who had been stealing in our neighborhood, however, it does make me feel better when I can either run home, or let a neighbor know that I have a package and to get it if available.I got mine recently, in October, so I can't speak to the motion settings beforehand, but with the newer version everything appears to function as intended. One minor issue I have is the inability to change the distance based on Zone. That is, you have a "field of vision" arrangement on your phone, to which you can turn off and on zones, and select the distance, but you can't put Zone 1 to 15 feet, and Zone 2 to 30 feet, or vice-versa. All of them have to be in a single zone. For me, it wasn't a huge deal since my yard extends to the end of the 30 feet, but if you have a smaller or non-existent front yard, you might be getting a ton of motion alerts.There still isn't the ability as of right now to turn on the camera by itself. That would be a great feature if you're waiting for someone and you're out of range of the doorbell, or just want to do something like unlock your door.Pros:- Quick response time on an iPhone 6+, normally within 10 seconds of opening the App, even on 4G. Have yet to try it on 3G or slower internet, however.- Camera is well enough that you can easily identify people as they come to the door, although this may change based on your home wireless connection- Motion settings have a few times saved me, knowing that either someone was there, or a package was delivered without a doorbell ring.- It was pretty easy to set up, other than our transformer being backwards from the previous homeowners, it probably took 30 minutes total.- Having the ability for two people (or more I assume) to answer the call is awesome.- Cloud recording is cheap, and each motion sensor is 40 seconds long, and generally starts recording before you get the alert- Set-up was very...very easyCons:- Depending on your internet speed, it may not work as well. We're on a 150/150 Mbps line here, but it seemed to work well with 105/15 as well.- No ability to turn the camera on independent of alerts- Motion sensor is an all or nothing thing for distance. You can turn off the Zone entirely, but if you set it to 25 feet, it's 25 feet if the zone is on.- No option for local storage- Price tag. I could see how $200 might be a turn off for some.Neither:- I don't believe I have had to go through a Firmware update yet. I can't speak to how well that did, or did not go for me. Given that when I talked to customer support they did mention that they were always improving their product, so a Firmware update might hit your product resulting in some issues.- Wink integration is not great if you have it. I'm not sure if that is Wink not allowing better support, or Ring, but I just thought I'd throw it in there if anyone was using the same thing.- Thickness. For us it looks pretty good on our house, but depending on the frame of your door, it may not fit.All in all this product is great. If you can afford the price tag, it's well worth it. Their customer service answered all of the questions I had previous to purchasing it, and after I did. It rings in the house, soon to a Ring Chime, and then to mine and my wife's phone as well. Even if she missed a call while she is at work, you can use the website to go back through and look at who came to the door. Originally I had pretty low expectation for the product (probably in the 2-3 star range, and didn't see a reason for a $200 doorbell) but after having it, I absolutely love it. As we add on to the house, we plan on purchasing more!

title: A for Effort (and Anticipation)
body: The Ring device sets up quickly, just by following the printed directions and the downloadable app. (If you're into those sorts of things.) Maybe 30 minutes if you include getting the drill out of the garage. Everything (except the drill) is in the box. Good build quality and packaging—although the faceplates are not interchangeable; if you don’t like the color, you must exchange the whole unit. The company even includes a little level that snaps onto the mounting plate, which I couldn’t figure out how to use (because they inconveniently placed a sticker on the mounting plate about six inches from my nose that said, “Insert Level Here”); as a result I’ve spent the past month incessantly asking my wife, “Does it look crooked to you?”The first caveat—and as a consumer you are informed of this right up front—the device requires 1mbps of upload speed. And that's not to make it work GREAT; that's to make it serviceable. I previously had .8mbps up and that was NOT enough. The highest level of service available at my location is 2mbps (that means it could be as low as 1.6 mbps with my provider). As part of the change of service, my ISP also had to make a housecall ($65), and I also had to purchase a new modem ($99), to accommodate “banded” service. (I’m told the ISP actually runs two accounts to the home, banding them together and thereby doubling the upload capability.) Even with my new upload capability, things weren’t anything to write home about. However, the company worked with me, tweaking things on their end, apparently releasing new software, and the video is now somewhere between acceptable and Netflix. If it doesn't get any better, I'd be happy. But based on my experience with the company (see headline), I think it will get better.But if something does go wrong, the other thing I like about Ring is that its CSRs seem empowered to fix a lot of things—both technically and business-wise— that other companies would have you hold for, speak with a supervisor regarding, transfer the call, call you back or, if they’re really stumped, drop the call. That is, when you can get ahold of them. I have NEVER called Ring support or customer service and spoken to someone right away. Then again, someone very knowledgeable and helpful has always called back in 2-3 hours. And he or she is not outsourced; they are based in the U.S., and English appears to be their 1st language. (Sorry, Amazon.) And let’s face it, it’s not like I was dialing 911 or ordering a live event.Re: reliability, the motion-detector alerts appear on my iPhone almost instantaneously when I leave the application open in the background. Every time (well, almost). Still, I’ve had monitored systems go out for DAYS at a time. Pretty much the moment a visitor’s vehicle enters the wide-angle frame, my phone sounds a pleasant, distinctive wind chime. The camera follows them up the steps and, if they ring the Ring doorbell, my phone rings. And if I accept the call, I “answer” the door. The person at the door can hear me as if I were on an intercom system inside the house. The frequency of the alerts and the area subject to the motion detection feature are highly adjustable.The cloud service and application also work well together. Each activity is saved, easily accessed, and capable of producing screen shots. I read a review that saved video could not be accessed via computer, but that feature recently was added to the company’a website. Now this is easily accomplished on the user’s account as well as by smartphone. And the cloud service is reasonably priced at $3mo/$30yr per device. (Without the cloud service, the notifications and live video would still work to my phone, but I would lose the ability to replay/screen-shot.)I waited years before subscribing to an alarm service, thinking I would someday wire the home myself. Then I was burglarized. Then I subscribed to Simply Safe. Then I could never get them on the phone (and they never called back). Then I bought a Dropcam. Then they were purchased by Nest, and a lot of things I liked about that previous company went away with the acquisition. I may find time to supplement my home security with wired security cameras someday but, until that day (and a manned Martian landing) comes, I recommend Ring. I recently purchased two more devices and a new Chime device, all of which I plan to install level.----------------Update: I installed the two new devices and chime, remounted the old one, connected everything to my wifi and cloud this afternoon--all without a hitch. Everything is working great--a CSR explained to me the reason I missed an alert had to do with my alert-frequency setting--and I definitely feel safer about leaving the home in the morning. And this time I reached support on the first try. These folks and their product deserve 5 stars...fwiw, I'm amending my rating rating from 4 stars to 5.

title: Ring Doorbell
body: With my RING doorbell, so far, so good.Before receiving this product I downloaded the app and created an account. When I received it I was shocked that nothing was working until I realized that the battery on the Ring device was 100% depleted (the instructions say that the doorbell comes with about 50-70% battery, mine did not). It takes 5-10 hours to fully charge a battery and then after that, it stays charged for 6-12 months. Of course factors will make this vary -- if you have your motion detector set to sensitive and it's always video-taping motion and people walking in and out of your house, then the battery goes down even more. When I first got it, my husband and I were fooling around with it so much that we reduced the battery significantly... Of course if you opt to connect the doorbell to your existing wires then you'll never have to charge it plus your old doorbell will ring. We don't have any wires at all (so strange) so we have to use it as a wireless doorbell.A couple of things that I don't like about this bell. (1) You HAVE to take your phone with you everywhere in the house if you want to answer the doorbell. I'm not tethered to my cellphone. When I come home from work I charge it up to 100% and then turn it off until the next day. Not any more -- if I want to answer the doorbell, now I must have my phone with me. Unless, of course, I buy the additional [[ASIN:B011MYEMKQ Ring Chime - A Wi-Fi-Enabled Speaker for Your Ring Video Doorbell]] that I can plug in anywhere (not battery operated, you must plug it in). This enables me to talk to a person at the door but there is no video.The volume and type of ring on the doorbell and the chime cannot be altered. I live in a quiet neighborhood and as my husband and I were installing it and playing around with it, each time we rang the doorbell, the people across the street would look over! Luckily, once someone presses the doorbell it only rings twice on the device outside, and then the blue light around the button continues to spin until you answer the doorbell on your app. If no one answers then the spinning stops. If the person outside does not understand what your doorbell is (highly likely) then they will try to press the doorbell again but there will be no sound on his or her end -- nothing will happen until you answer your doorbell. If you do not have your phone then later on, you'll see that you have a "missed call" on your phone's notification panel.If you shell out the thirty dollars to have the yearly service, then you can always go back into your phone and click on "motion detected 9:12am" and see a recording of what was around your door. If you don't pay for the service, then you'll just see a notification that there was motion outside your door at 9:12am.I have security cameras of different brands that all use an SD card to record, so why can't this device have it? Because this is how the company makes more money. They will probably come out with another generation of the Ring device that will have SD recording capabilities, and it will cost more money to make up the difference. I can also view my security cameras anytime I please from within the app, but not with this device because I am using the wireless option. However -- if you choose to wire your doorbell then you will have access to the Live View at any time. I believe in the instructions they say that there is no Live View for the wireless option because it would drain the battery almost immediately which yeah, makes sense.I also like that there is IFTTT integration. I have it set so that when someone comes to the door, my [[ASIN:B0161IJ5F0 LIFX Color 1000 A19 Wi-Fi Smart LED Light Bulb, Adjustable, Multicolor, Dimmable, No Hub Required, Works with Alexa]] flash pink once. This is incredibly helpful for those of you who (like me) do not carry their phones everywhere. It's unfortunate that the Chime only comes with sound and not some kind of flashing light -- what about the deaf and hard of hearing? They would have greatly benefited from that (if they didn't have their phone with them).Oh, one more thing that was annoying -- adding users. When I connected the doorbell to my phone and tried to add my husband, it said "user not exists." Okay, aside from the atrocious grammar, what does that mean? I found out that every user you want to add must create their own account FIRST, and then you can add them by entering their email address. Then they're sent an invitation, blah blah. I just wish that instead of saying "user not exists" that there was a message saying "users must create their own account first." Something simple like that.I hope this review was helpful!

title: The Ring Video Doorbell is a Best-in-Class Product
body: From my research and personal experience, I am convinced that the Ring Video Doorbell is the best product in its class. While not perfect, the Ring is an awesome high-tech product with very impressive capabilities, and it has a user-friendly free IOS (or Android) app interface to control the Ring. I believe that Ring as a company is dedicated to continually improving this Ring Video Doorbell by adding new features and ironing out existing issues (with firmware updates), and its technical support department is extremely responsive if a problem arises.I purchased two of these units for my home several months ago, and at first they both worked great. After a few weeks, one of the units started to experience technical issues (i.e., it would no longer sense motion and send me alerts). I contacted Ring's tech support, and they were very prompt and thorough in troubleshooting my unit. They determined that I needed a replacement unit, and a new one (free) arrived in a few days. In order to get that Ring unit working properly, I also needed to purchase a WiFi extender since the existing WiFi signal to that part of the exterior of the house wasn't strong enough to allow the Doorbell to consistently upload the video and audio data from various events that triggered the Doorbell alerts. Once the WiFi extender was setup, the connectivity of that Ring unit was vastly improved, and it now works great. The motion detection feature of the Ring Doorbell is extremely useful, and serves as an effective yet inexpensive HD security camera (including night vision) for your home. The Cloud Recording option (there is a free trial period, and an annual subscription costs $30 per unit) provides high quality HD video of Ring Doorbell ringing events and motion detection events, and these videos can be downloaded and emailed, saved, etc.From a style standpoint, the Ring Video Doorbell comes in four different colors, so that homeowners can match their decor. Also, if the Ring Video Doorbell is stolen (the unit is attached to a base with proprietary screws to make it difficult to remove without the provided screwdriver head), Ring will provide a replacement Doorbell for no charge, which is actually very impressive, since the Doorbell costs $199.The installation and setup process for the Ring Video Doorbell is much easier than you would think. A very handy mini tool belt pouch comes with the Ring, and includes pretty much everything needed for the installation, including a level. However, the drill bit included is too large, and so I needed to use a smaller one so that the hollow wall anchor could be securely attached to my home. Installation on solid surfaces like wood is easier than installation on an exterior brick surface.The capabilities included with the Ring Video Doorbell are very impressive, and provide peace of mind to the homeowner no matter where they are located. You will always know when someone rings your Ring Video Doorbell or when someone is standing around your door but does not ring the Doorbell, since an alert appears on your smartphone, and invites you to answer the ringing and to see and actually speak to the person outside your door. While the video communication is just one-way (outsiders can't see the homeowner), the audio communication is two-way. There is a slight lag between when the Doorbell is rung and when the alert appears on your smartphone, but it is less than 10 seconds. The Ring Doorbell contains a cool looking LED circle that lights up either white or blue outside, depending on the status of the Doorbell. The Ring Doorbell can use your home's existing doorbell wiring (in this case, the Ring Doorbell's battery will not need to be charged, as it will constantly receive a trickle charge from the doorbell wiring current), and even if you don't have any existing doorbell wiring, you can still use the Ring Doorbell and receive alerts on your smartphone. These customers without doorbell wiring will probably want to purchase the soon to be released Ring Chime, which will plug into an electric outlet inside your home and let you know when someone rings the Doorbell.All in all, I am extremely satisfied with my Ring Video Doorbells, and I highly recommend the Ring Video Doorbell to homeowners looking for a best-in-class high-tech video doorbell with sophisticated communication and motion detection capabilities, as well as an impressive and free IOS or Android app that provides a user-friendly interface for making adjustments to the Doorbell or viewing activities recorded by the Doorbell.

title: Great Way to Improve Safety at Home
body: ------------------- Update 06/10/2015 ------------------Comment on battery charging issue:Originally, when I read some of the reviews regarding this doorbell, I read there some people experiencing an issue where the battery would not charge to 100%. I recall on my original charge that it charged to about 97% and that was it. I charged my doorbell last night when it hit 12% capacity . I charged it for appx 9 hours, until the light around the button turned off. I tested the doorbell and it connected to my phone. I checked the capacity of the battery and it was at 100%. I'll comment and update again if this changes.------------------- Original Review ------------------I have had the Ring Door Bell for approximately 2 months. I have to say I am very satisfied and I am glad that I took the plunge and purchased it here on Amazon.Here are the Features you’ll get with the Ring Door Bell:- Wifi Connection via your home wifi network- Motion Activation- Two-Way Talk when someone presses your doorbell or motion alert is activated and you choose to answer- Mobile Access via a mobile device and notifications of doorbell presses or motion alerts- Dual Power Option via built-in battery or your existing doorbell wiring- Cloud Recording if you choose to purchase it- HD Camera with night vision- Lifetime Purchase Protection from the manufacture (i.e. if it gets stolen)Main Benefits of the Ring Door Bell:Safety- You can talk to someone at your door without physically answering it. This prevents the possibility of perpetrators pushing your door in when you answer it.- You can answer your door when you’re not home via a mobile device to give the appearance of being home. This is a good way of making thieves believe you are home if they case your neighborhood during your work hours. This is especially effective if they don’t press your doorbell but your motion settings catch them nosing around your porch Recorded Evidence- If someone takes your delivery packages or cases your home and you miss the alert on your phone, you can have recordings of those actions via the cloud service- Note that recording of audio occurs only when you choose to accept a notification from your Ring doorbell Multiple Installation Options- You can mount in where your original doorbell was and use the existing wiring to power your doorbell and connect it your existing chime- You can also mount it to your home, front door, or security gate and power it via the built-in battery. Convenient Mobile App- Access your doorbell or its recordings anywhere via your Android device or Iphone- Multiple mobile devices can have access to the same doorbell- If you’re using the built-in battery, the App will also show the current level of the batteryI chose to mount my Ring doorbell on my security gate (see my attached pic). I’m currently powering it by battery, but would like to get power to it eventually. I have had a really good experience with the device. I have only one negative experience with the device. When I first purchased it, I experienced a number of sudden shut downs of the Android App. However, it might be a problem with my Moto G as my wife’s Galaxy S4 has not experienced one shut down. I consulted with Ring Customer Service regarding this issue. In the end, they could not find out why I was experiencing the shut downs, but the agent who worked with me, Jeff, worked very hard and made sure to follow up with me every few days to see if the app was working better for me. After the last update, the app has shut down on me about 2 or 3 times the past 2 or 3 weeks. A big improvement!A number of reviews here on Amazon have noted having a lot of false motion activations. I did have that in the beginning, but I can’t say I remember having one in the past 4 weeks. I did spend a lot of time tweaking the motion settings in the beginning.In conclusion, I think the Ring doorbell is an excellent way to help improve safety at your home without having to run wires everywhere as you would with a conventional security camera. The best part is that it allows two-way-talk via your mobile device. Many of my friends who have visited me since I have installed it really liked the device and want to get one.

title: Brand new, but so far VERY pleased!!!
body: UPDATE 5/30/2017 - Have now had our Ring doorbell for almost a year. Even on battery power it works EXACTLY as promised! We get prompt video notifications when someone is at the door, no matter where we are. In fact, the other day, we had an accidental FALSE alarm (on our ADT system) and the local police showed-up--as we had the Ring, and were away from the house, we could answer the door ring and tell them all was well. A very handy gizmo, works exactly as promised! I usually charge it once every 6 - 8 weeks. We've had a great experience with it.UPDATE 9/21/2016 - I have now used Ring Doorbell for over a week and I remain very enthusiastic about this product. I am using it with battery power (as our front door wasn't wired with a DC current doorbell) and it has been performing pretty much flawlessly! Even using a WiFi extender I get doorbell rings to my phone within seconds and a very clear picture -- and audio -- of who is at the door. Sound & picture quality is excellent. The motion alert seems to work very well, as well. One minor quibble: I do get 3-4 'false' motion alerts per day. I have the Ring set to low motion sensitivity (maybe a 10-12' foot range, but not the lowest sensitivity), maybe I'll lower it a bit more, but I'd prefer a few false alerts than to miss something when I need it. You needn't respond to every alert. They are all stored in Ring's Cloud for 30+ days (for a modest $30 annual fee + 1 free trial month), so you can review & delete them whenever you want. Overall, I'd say the Ring Doorbell exceeds my expectations -- thought it would be far more glitchy, but it's not. Main thing to stress: be sure you have a solid/robust wifi connection before purchasing, Ring very much relies on that! Works FINE with an extender, I have an older Netgear WN3000RP.---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------I just purchased the Ring Doorbell Cam (not from Amazon as I normally do, but from my security company, ADT). I've only had it a few hours, and I know the reviews here are mixed, but so far my experience has been great. Unfortunately my main router is in the back of our house, but I have an older Netgear WiFi extender (purchased on Amazon!) in the middle of the house that works quite well. I was worried how well the Ring Doorbell would play with the WiFi extender. Well, right out of the box the Ring immediately connected -- just carefully follow the prompts (and there's a helpful accompanying set-up video) -- and it immediately found my network, connected, setup my account, etc. Flawless. The ADT guys mounted the RING on my masonry front door (using the supplied masonry bit), it's just two screws, but drilling into masonry/concrete is always a pain, so I let them do it. (ADT just sells the ring, doesn't really service it.) Anyway, the RING is maybe 25 - 30' from the wifi extender -- and outside the house, of course -- but immediately it appears to be working exactly as advertised! I am notified on my phone when someone rings the bell, no matter where I am. I have it set to limited-range motion detection (abt 10 feet). We happen to live on a very busy street, but so far I am not receiving ANY false alarms from passing cars or pedestrians. I am attaching a screen capture of our real-time daytime view. Considering it's wireless & battery-powered, it's quite good.As one popular review said, the RING is probably only as good as your WiFi signal and, I guess, your upload speed. We have a pretty fast network and, as I said, an older Netgear extender. Nothing very fancy -- but so far, RING is working a lot better than I expected, as my expectations, based on some of the mixed reviews, were quite low. When it works, it appears to work very well!One point to note: as other reviewers have pointed out, unless you are hard-wired to DC current, which our RING is not, you do not get a live-stream picture. On battery power, it's only activated via a doorbell ring or motion detection. If you happen to have a wired doorbell then it's quite easy to hardwire the RING to it, but you'll still need a good/robust wireless connection. I will update once we've used it for a while...

title: It's a wifi video doorbell and it works pretty well.
body: In a few words: Attractive, solid product that does what it set out to do and more.In a few more words: Easy to install and integrate into existing doorbell. Has a "locking" feature to prevent theft - you first mount a plate on your wall using standard screws and then you slide the Ring itself onto the mounting plate and "lock" it in with a pair of tiny allen set screws. But should a thief rip the Ring off your wall, fret not, because Ring will replace it free of charge for life!Modern looking and attractive in my opinion, have received lots of comments from folks who notice it and for those who don't they sure do when I start talking to them through the app!App functionality is on par for this "class" of device. When someone rings the doorbell, if you have a wired bell it's instantaneous just as you'd expect. What's not so instantaneous are the app notifications. They are quick mind you, but can be inconsistent; sometimes being very quick and other times having as much as a few second delay. Then once you get the notification you have to wait for the app to load the video stream which CAN take a couple more seconds. (I do have an Asus RT-AC3200 less than 20 feet away so I cant imagine its my wireless network causing the lag) Having a video doorbell offers you some insight on the behavior of people who visit your door. Most people you find ringing your door will wait very little time before leaving - most of the time you can't catch these guys with live video/audio. Others like salesman will stick around - I have caught a few of these and informed them politely I am not interested all from my office upstairs! But any actions that occur at your door will of course be recorded for review later. But a word on this... if at first you get a notification and you don't immediately check the app and you open it maybe a few seconds later you will not be able to see the recorded activity for sometime while the Ring uploads the data to the cloud - this can sometimes be frustrating but just requires patience. Which also brings me to streaming video which this does not have - what I mean by that is you cant stream live video from the doorbell at will. There has to be some "activity" detected in order to see the stream. It would be nice to just be able to login and see your door at any time but I'm told this is coming in a future update.Once you do get a notification you have two options, to either accept or deny the "call". If you don't do anything, the live stream will timeout and take you back to the main screen which can be a pain. If you deny the same thing happens. If you accept, you get more options on the screen to mute your mic/speaker. But once you accept, your mic defaults to ON, so you have to be conscience of that fact if you don't want your visitor to hear you right away once you accept their "call"Video quality is okay for streaming - expect typical artifacts that are sometimes apparent with streaming videos on low quality streams but recordings do seem to have a much better quality. What shocked me the most was the focal length of the lens or how wide the field of view is on the camera. In fact it is so wide that the edge of the screen shows part of the housing. So in essence you're recording is probably something like 20% useless because your seeing the inside of the housing on the Ring itself on the left and right sides of recordings/streams.Motion detection works. But, there are settings to set thresholds at various distances that does not appear to work at all on my doorbell. I constantly pick up things happening that aren't motion at my doorstep at all but are things like cars and people about 30 ft away in the street.Overall I'm very pleased with this doorbell and the company overall. The customer service is very good as well as the complementary product to this one known as Chime. Ring is constantly updating the app and the doorbell always adding new features and integrations into services like IFTTT which just opens the door to lots of possibilities.

title: Setup A Bit Challenging, but WELL worth the effort!
body: When I first set up my Ring device, I was frustrated at how poor or even non-functional the video was, despite plugging in a router extender less than 8 feet away from the front door! I called Ring tech support, didn't have to wait very long to reach a live, American tech support person, and was able to learn from her that my bandwidth was seriously lacking, even though my Verizon FIOS subscription boasts 75 mbps up AND download speeds. You have to remember, that's hard wired speed. Wireless is a whole different ballgame. My wireless signal was barely downloading or uploading at 2 or 3 mbps and my router extender was even worse, chugging along at 0.1 mbps to 1.2 mbps. No WONDER! I thanked the tech and realized I had another call I needed to make.Getting a hold of a live person at Verizon was more difficult, but they determined that my wifi security method ( the old WEP) and another setting in my router choosing compatibility mode over performance mode was seriously throttling my wireless speed. That, and some of the other 21 wireless devices I have running. Long story short, once I reconfigured my router, disconnected a couple of wireless devices that I'm not even using and set both the router and range extender to WPA2 encryption, upload and download speed on the wireless rose significantly and the Ring device has been working pretty much flawlessly (see last paragraph), ever since and I am very pleased with it. I DO have my range extender just about 8 feet away from the device and I cannot stress how important a strong wifi signal is.You can easily test your wireless signal strength by going on a wireless device connected to your wifi and going to a site like http://www.bandwidthplace.com. I'm in California and the Ring tech suggested I connect to the Washington D.C. server to test my bandwidth availability. I wound up going from .01 to 1 or 2 mbps from the original test to 10-15 mbps after the Verizon router tweaks and that's more than enough to operate Ring correctly.As far as the whole installation kit goes - very impressive, except the little bubble level is rendered all but useless because the mounting plate is made of PVC or some other sort of plastic and the top surface of the plate itself is NOT perfectly straight all the way across. Use a traditional level if you really want to make sure it's being installed straight.My only other real gripe is being locked into having to subscribe to Ring's "Cloud" in order to retrieve recorded footage of front door activity. Not because I find it cost prohibitive, but because I find it an invasion of my privacy. Ring wants to know my exact street address during setup (for reasons that are unclear) and I also fear their cloud being hacked to show someone our daily routine habits... What time we go for a walk, for instance. I would much prefer the option to store data on my own local computer or FTP server, as I do with the rest of my security cameras.I originally gave this product a four star rating due to the lack of the aforementioned local video storage capability, but changed it to a five based on the outstanding customer support that I have received from Ring both times I have called in. I was noticing that when motion was detected, I'd receive an alert on my cell phone, but there would be no video, just a swirling blue circle outline in the middle. However, video WAS being sent to the cloud for recording, because I could play back the event and see what took place after the fact. I just wasn't seeing it in real time. I called Ring support for the second time this morning to troubleshoot this problem. I got through within just a minute or two of hold time, clearly explained the trouble I was having to the tech, and she made some adjustments on her end that fixed the problem remotely. Now, when motion is detected, I am notified within a second or two at most, with clear video kicking in no more than a second after that. Now, the Ring works PERFECTLY and I am absolutely thrilled.

title: Ding Dong - Yes, I see you!
body: As with most new technology products, I go a little overboard with introducing myself to its features obsessively everyday. This product is no different. The installation was a bit tricky for me only because I had to drill into stucco with blue paint and had to make absolutely sure my leveling was correct (thank goodness for the included level and all other necessary tools!). After using the included mollies and getting the backplate on correctly, the rest was a breeze. From installing the Android app, to configuring the doorbell, to getting the first few motion sensing and doorbell rings sent to my phone, I couldn't have made the process any easier. Looking at the videos right from the app was great and even better on the PC from the www.ring.com site where you can login with your account information.There are a lot of great things about this doorbell. Answering a doorbell ring with a video feed helps my family feel more secure in that we don't have to go to our glass door to see who is standing there. Once they see you near the glass door, that person ringing either knows you're in the house and refuses to answer or may see you moving around wondering whether you will answer. So this helps give us the opportunity to see how it is before we even make the move towards the door. I can be in my bedroom, living room, lanai, etc. Then, if I chose, I can talk to them through the app and let them know I'm coming or to leave my property. The tests I've done have been rock solid! Or even better...I can be at work and see that a package was left for me by a mail carrier and can see who that person was just in case they throw the package instead of lay it down. Again, away from the home I can also talk to that person through the doorbell. I may freak them out, but it may get them to leave.Along those same lines, the motion sensing (which does need tweaking and some adjustments down the road) is a great feature too. I can see if someone is trying to break in or spy around my house by using the heat sensing capabilities. You can start at 5ft and increase the range to 30ft. I have it around 15ft at the moment so any time someone is in that range my phone will get an alert that motion was at the front door. I also get a notice if cars come by because they are sensed by heat as well...not a bad thing...but sensitivity adjustments can be made for alert frequency from the app.Cloud storage is available and either $3 a month or $30 for the entire year. I find this of incredible value both for the price and the peace of mind if anything is caught I need to show the authorities. You get 30 days free to start and then need to pay afterwards. It stores 6 months worth of video at a time. So far, it's working like a charm and it's fun to play back videos of those unsuspecting "victims."There are a few things that can be improved that they are working on. The iris opening after motion is sensed or the doorbell rings is a bit delayed between 2 - 3 seconds. In that same regard, sometimes you will only see the back of a person once they leave the door instead of when they come right up to it. That is being worked on as well. The capability to check the video feed live at any time is not possible right now, but I was told it is in the works (a good thing!). Sometimes the video can lag on the app, too, but that may be due to connection problems. However, it is nice to see it on-the-fly.I would recommend this product to anyone who wants to feel safer knowing that there is always an "eye" watching your front door. And get this, if someone tries to destroy it or remove it (which shouldn't be possible with proprietary screws during installation) they will give you a new one FREE! We definitely feel safer at home and am so glad we decided to give our doorbell ring more character...and now both eyes and ears. A MUST BUY in my opinion!!!

title: Highly recommended
body: I struggled to decide if this was 4 stars or 5, and decided on 5. I'm giving it some leeway because these kinds of products are *fairly* new and this one has exceeded my expectations (but it is the only video doorbell I have). Of course, months or years from now (early 2016), these products will be smaller and better, but I'm *very* happy with this video doorbell.I installed it on part of my door frame where my existing hard-wired doorbell was located on a small piece of wood door frame. I had to add a piece of wood to create a space wide enough to mount the whole device, but it was simple carpentry. I took a spare piece of wood, a couple of cuts to trim the wood to fit, some screws to attach the wood piece to the existing door frame and then some paint to match the surrounding door frame.The Ring worked fine with my existing doorbell wires and existing door bell. So Ring is always recharged and my doorbell sounds the same as it always did.I do not have the extra chime you can purchase extra. My existing doorbell works exactly as it used to, plus all my phones and tablets ring when the Ring doorbell is pushed. That's plenty of notification that someone is at the door, and it's all the notification I had - and more.Installation was pretty easy. They included all the tools you need (although I used some of my own since they have bigger grips than the ones included and I have big hands). I tested it inside my house for a while before installation, to make sure everything was OK before I started to install it.The Ring doorbell works very well. There is a few seconds of lag between pushing the button and capturing audio; the video starts right away. The audio delay is noticeable but doesn't bother me because it's capturing video right away. That's great.Motion detection is adjustable and catches everyone walking on the sidewalk 20 feet from my door (as I set it up to do) as well as anyone coming all the way up to the door. I've had it for less than a full year and I noticed the motion detection was a *little* less sensitive at first (summer). It's very accurate now (winter). I wondered if it's an issue with the temperature (summer vs. winter temperatures having an effect on how the Ring determines if the motion is from a person or just wind shaking a branch), or if the Ring has updated firmware - I don't know. But it's fine right now during the winter months, and I'll know if the issue is season-dependent after the summer of 2016 (my second summer with the Ring).Ring requires a $30/year to store videos, and I think that's a very reasonable price. The alternative would be to buy a security camera plus disk storage if I wanted to store my own videos, plus setup time and maintenance, so I don't begrudge Ring a nominal fee to store videos on their servers. The apps and web site gives me access to the stored videos anytime, from anywhere.If you have the Ring-stored video, it would be nice if the iOS app had a multi-select to delete stored videos faster. I've actually just gotten in the habit of not deleting anything, and I just let Ring clean up on their schedule (videos are supposed to be stored for 6 months, but I haven't checked if this is exactly the clean-up schedule). I haven't had any issues with the video storage service or accessing any video. You can save or share any stored videos (if you have the paid storage service). If you don't have the service, you only get notifications and real-time video.I was worried people would be confused what to press to ring the doorbell - I didn't want them to press the camera eye. That has not been a problem. It's clear what to press during the day and the Ring's button has a glowing ring of light at night, so that was a concern I had, but it's not been an issue.

title: Totally Happy! It is not only activated by ringing the ...
body: I looked at several different Wifi Doorbells and decided to go with Ring (original version, Ring HD wasn't out yet). Totally Happy! It is not only activated by ringing the bell button but it is a motion detector too. The motion detection "area" can be small or wide. I don't get a lot of false readings/detections either. With motion detection and/or when the doorbell is rung (and if you do not "answer" either of them on your phone on computer) a 40-second (or so) video will be captured and stored in Ring's Cloud. If you "answer" the notification on your phone or computer, you can view live video and talk to the person who rand the doorbell or look around "live" to see what set off the motion detection. I haven't had any scary situations so far but I have had a lot of fun watching people's reactions when they start looking at the bell and possibly realizing it's a camera too. When a camera is available, people start acting goofy! So much fun! LOL! Check out Ring's video channel on youtube to see actual security-related videos that aided in capturing the "bad guys/gals." You can download any video to your phone or computer and it's simple to do. You can also Share a link to a video (many methods, email, message, save to dropbox, etc.) via phone app or computer.The Ring device itself is easy to install. There are paper instructions included and videos available online. You can have Ring hard wired or totally on battery power. The battery power only is what I used and the battery life is excellent so far. Supposed to last 6 months (give or take) before a recharge via MicroUSB cable (you'll have to unscrew the Ring device and take it off the wall). The Ring device is securely attached on your house with proprietary screws (they provide a screwdriver and MicroUSB cable plus more). If your Ring was ever stolen then they will replace it for free (as long as you file a police report)! That's definitely a plus even though it will be somewhat hard to get off the wall! I also have a Ring Chime which is an A/C powered wifi remote doorbell which can be placed in any outlet. You can buy multiple if you need to hear your doorbell say in your house and in the garage, etc. It is pretty loud too (Chime volume is adjustable)! When motion is detected you hear a wind chime like notification and when the doorbell is rung it is a different bold sound. Super simple with the Android or iOS app to add your Ring Doorbell and/or Chime to your Wifi Network. When you are out and about it is so easy to "answer" your doorbell to see and talk to the person ringing the bell (or not talk, just watch to see what happens next). As long as you answer that notification, the video will continue to record. Speaking of the "cloud recordings" Ring co. gives you a free 30-day trial then it costs $30.00/per year (for each Ring Doorbell you have, you don't pay for the Chime) or you can pay monthly for $3.00/month. Pretty cheap to me considering the cost of a cup of coffee these days! RING offers "Live View" for hard wired doorbells which is like an IP Webcam. They will be rolling out this feature for Battery powered Rings but understand that the more you use it, the more your battery will be eaten up. I'm really anxious to get and try this functionality!Lastly, Ring has GREAT customer service. I have even gotten responses over the weekend. They are quick to respond (no more than a day, sooner in other cases). Get a Ring and you will have an extra measure of security at your place and some "fun" too! I got my Ring and Chime from c-0-s-t-c-0 but you know if you can't get there or they don't have it, Amazon does (and you must know Amazon has world class customer service if you need help)[[ASIN:B00N2ZDXW2 Ring Wi-Fi Enabled Video Doorbell]]

title: Good piece of mind and day or night!
body: I have been testing the Ring Doorbell and Chime for a while now and for a product about $200.00, it has many of the hardware and software features of higher priced recording systems that are above what many people can afford.We found a great deal that combined the Ring Doorbell with the Ring Chime- which is a receiver that can be plugged into any home power socket and emits a chime sound whenever the Doorbell is activated.Setup for me was smooth, but I have a lot of experience setting systems up. If you get stuck, Ring's (Great support so far) Support and video help is only a click away. After you download your app, Android or IPhone, you can add your device and name your doorbell location; set how sensitive the motion detection is; set active zones for motion detection; and more. You can also set up your chime location; match it to your doorbell; choose if the chime will ring on doorbell, motion detection, or both; and set the chime volume.The camera has excellent color and quality. The wide-angle lens gives a fish-eye effect and sees wall to wall. There is a fishpond to the left of my door and it sees that too. The camera faces south from my covered porch and as a result there is some back lighting from the direct sun, however the camera does well and shuts down to concentrate on the correct lighting for a person at the door. When the lights go out, there is black and white infrared coverage and since I have other cameras with infrared lights in the area, the system uses that light to enhance the view.The Sound is great both ways, however be careful not to have your phone or other device too close to the doorbell while talking or you will get feedback. You can have the sound off, listen without talking, or have a conversation with whoever is at the door from anywhere in the country where you have at least two bars of 4g coverage.WI-FI service with the doorbell and the chime depends on several things. You should be as close to your Wi-Fi router of range extender as possible, because the stronger the signal, the better the service connection times, especially for motion detection activations. As with all motion detection recorders, there is a delay between the motion detection and recording. This may only be 4 seconds, but if you can get it lower, it could be the difference between seeing a face, or a back.With the purchase of the Ring Doorbell I received a thirty day free trial for cloud recording. Each recording I had was over a minute long on playback. After the trial the cost for cloud recording is $30.00 a year per device (In case you want two doorbells or other Ring hardware). Considering what is recorded and the space required on a hard drive, this is a bargain to me. The recordings have a long time keep limit, but they will eventually go away. Anything you want to keep should be downloaded to your computer. And if you have a recording you no longer want you can delete it from the list with your app.Remote use of the software works well with my AT&T connection, as long as I have two strength bars of 4g. I have used it a number of times while away and it was fun to catch sales people and family at the door and talk with them without them knowing we weren’t home.The Chime came with my package, but is usually a separate piece of hardware that can be used to alert you that there is an alert or motion detection when your phone or other app is not available. Since my Ring Doorbell is hardwired to my old doorbell power wiring, it rings my old doorbell in the house when the button is pushed, but not motion detection. The chime also can be used to announce motion detection only in my case.The Ring Doorbell gives me more piece of mind and is a great addition to my systems.

title: Great for budget conscious and novice users | I returned mine and upgraded it.
body: I ordered this during Black Friday week and got it at a discounted price. I used it to replace a competitive video doorbell from a security company. The biggest weakness of the security company doorbell was the lack of a Wi-Fi chime and the cost to upload video clips per month. Therefore, I also picked up the Ring Chime to help provide some additional coverage space for the notification.The product was easy to unpack. I like the fact that screws, a screw driver, and a USB cable were provided for recharging. I do recommend a pair of wire-strippers for wired doorbells - the leads on my doorbell were very short and were not long enough to wrap around the screw posts. If you are replacing a wired doorbell, the AC voltage provided by your doorbell transformer seems to "trickle charge" the built-in battery. I couldn't find any specifications on how long the internal battery will last - which makes me wonder if a doorbell replacement will be necessary even with hardwired power at some point in the future.For those of you with wired doorbells, I do recommend initially charging the doorbell first to 100% before you install it. The trickle charge is just that - don’t expect the wired doorbell transformer to charge it to 100% for you. It might - it’ll just take a long time.Setup is incredibly simple and requires a smartphone. It seriously took longer to wire and install than it did to program the doorbell module. The built-in videos within the Ring app were easy to follow and did not require much prior research to get started.The Ring Chime was also incredibly simple to configure and get connected to the doorbell. For those of you on the fence wondering if the chime is worth it - it absolutely is for larger homes. Our mechanical chime is not loud enough to hear throughout our home, and digital wired chimes cost more than Ring’s adapter. There are a number of built-in chimes you can choose from. I didn't see any method of adding your own, or Ring having the ability to download some from their website.The install was not a perfect experience on my part though, but was pretty darn close. Here are the two things that I ran into that might help some of you.First, my mailbox was located near where the doorbell has been typically placed. It was a Wi-Fi shield even though my router is a room away behind the door along the same outer wall. While the Ring app stated that my Wi-Fi was "OK", it was choppy enough that I had issues connecting to the doorbell and getting good picture quality. I moved my mailbox and the signal dramatically improved - video quality and connection are instantaneous now. Don’t accept OK for your Wi-Fi connection. It needs to be “Good” or better for a pleasant experience.Second, my FiOS router had the 2.4 Ghz Wi-Fi channel set to a static channel assignment. Ring did not like this for some reason even though I have a solid, symmetrical 100Mb connection (up and down) and this was after the mailbox experience ;-). I could not get notifications on my device or to the Ring Chime module. I had to set the channel assignment to auto, and got everything working perfectly.All in all, it was well worth the money. However, I ended up returning it. In my housing plan, EVERYONE has a wireless router with 2.4ghz turned on. There was way too much congestion to have the Ring keep a good solid signal Given no 5ghz WiFi is supported with this specific model, I returned it and exchanged it for a Ring Doorbell Pro model.For those of you not in crowded neighborhoods, this may work well for you. However, technophiles should go with the ‘Pro’ model.

title: Their Customer Service is as Good as Their Product
body: I write few reviews. This product merits props/credit.First, for those who want a quick review, I love this product and the customer service is as good as I have seen - better. I would buy it again. It is a great security/keep an eye on things, product for a reasonable price. That said, I will save the best for last.I bought the Ring Doorbell (from now on "Ring") about a month ago. I am not a tech savy guy but I was able to in to install it and get it running. It was not difficult, but you need to pay attention to detail.You can't just put it up in 5 minutes while you are simultaneously watching your TV and drinking a beer (or Chardonnay). You need to take your time, read the instructions and watch the multiple videos about the Ring The videos tell you how to install it. The videos will show you the way. I watched the short videos several times before the Ring arrived.When the ring arrived, I smiled when i saw the Ring box included, not only the the Ring, but a special Ring screw driver, plus the drill bit for installing it into masonry. (You know, like - how do I get a screw into stucco? Don't worry - use the included drill bit) The box contained the Ring and the drill bit, screws, and plastic inserts to put into the wall to hold the screws. I thought I would have to wait for the Ring to arrive, figure out what screws it needed, get the screws, the drill bit and the everything else. Oh no. When the Ring arrived, it was all in the box.When you install the Ring, you don't need an existing doorbell with current running to it. You really can just put it anywhere. In the garage, the hallway, maybe somewhere in your girlfriend's house so you can keep in touch. It can run off of battery. The battery lasts 6 to 12 months, depending on how many times it is activated. You can watch the app on your phone to monitor the battery. When time to recharge. You just use the Ring screwdriver. Remove the Ring and charge it overnight with the included charger and it is good for another 6 to 12 months. Mine is hardwired. Connected to the doorbell wires, so no need to recharge. That, would seem the way to go it you have an existing doorbell.When I installed, it went ok. It was not something for a 5 year old, but looking back, everything that went wrong was user error and easy to fix. To install the Ring, you first must install the base plate to you porch wall. The instructions tell you to install it so that it is flat and the Ring sits squarely on it. I did not. I did not take my time. When it attached the Ring to the base plate, I could see gaps between the Ring and base plate. I ignored it and the Ring worked fine - for awhile.
